写在前面的话

辨认语言的正与变

朱绩崧

《卖花女》之前，我给《再会，契普斯先生》写的序通篇是忆旧抒情。本篇我想谈一点对语言学习的粗浅看法。

《再》《卖》二书乍看是没有任何联系的，但葛先生晚年读了那么多书——《英语写作》自序称所读近人著作数以千计——独独挑拣出这两本来详注的用意，在前言里说得很明白，概括起来就是一、有助于学习口语，二、学到雅俗不同的语言，三、体验名作家萧伯纳的作品。

我再浓缩一下：学习口语，分辨雅俗，亲近经典。

若还要简单，那就一个字吧——辨。

因何要辨？因为我在中国看到的是多数学习者没有“分别心”，说出的英语像念报告，从句套从句，写下的英文却很通俗，你都怀疑他中学没毕业。细究起来，其实他们对汉语也没有这方面的意识。可以说，虽然学习的是语言，而且花费的精力足令欧美教育界叹为观止，但对于语言本身的特点却茫然不知，只是一头扎在背单词、做考卷的泥潭里。

雕虫不易，雕龙尤难。辨别口语和书面语、文雅的表达和粗鄙的表达，乃至伟大的作品和平庸的作品，这些都需要下工夫才能习得。

上世纪末，《故宫博物院院刊》载有一篇《光绪帝朱批述评》，其中提到年幼的清德宗在广西巡抚奏折上朱批：“知道了。你们作督抚的，务尽心民事，感召天和……”在“你们作督抚的”六字边，注有小字“尔等身膺疆寄”，显然是他的大学士师傅们在教导他如何在文字中“辨”，方不失帝主之尊。可惜，存世十八万件光绪朝朱批有多少处是帝师修改，已不得而知。否则编写文言文教材，倒是有了绝佳素材。英文方面，幸而还有《卖花女》这样一部（以我寡见，也仅此一部）真材实料造就的教材，引领我们窥见语言在教科书的模版之外，于实际生活中还有更多纷呈异彩的“变态”。

其实，整个故事大概也就是为了打破“模版至高无上、涵盖一切”的错觉而写的吧。按我愚陋的理解，萧伯纳是想说，这种自我表达的“变态”才是语言的常态、社会的常态吧——究其本质，乃天赋予人之自由是也。

前言

“读什么？”，“怎样读？”　这是我们经常听到学习英语的同学提出的两个问题。学习英语，除阅读之外，当然还有听、说、写、译。这五者是相辅相成、不可偏废的，但阅读是基础。尤其在应用方面，我国极大多数从事涉外工作的人员最需要的还是阅读。阅读，对于自学者来说，更是学好外语的重要途径。

读什么？　现代英美人写的英语。作为精读材料，最好选择一些现代英美作家的作品。要新而不“怪”，就是说，语言既不陈旧，又没有很多奇怪的句子结构和俚语、方言之类；艺术表现手法新，又必须是现实主义的，既不是传统小说的平铺直叙，纯粹的讲故事，又不是“新”得出奇，变成了梦呓式的“天书”。总之，选择读物要着眼于最能起到帮助学习英美语言（进而研究英美文学）的实际效果……

怎样读？　读有精读和略读之分。两者也是相辅相成、不可偏废的，只是要求有所不同。略读从“量”来要求，广泛浏览，达到见多识广，可以容许“不求甚解”；精读则从“质”来要求，务必一词、一句都弄得清清楚楚：每个词的正确发音、它的词性、在文中的意义、色彩、搭配、用法，以及在句子中的作用；每个短语、每个从句在文中的作用和相互关系；每个句子的语法结构，有否倒装或者省略等，句型有无习惯上的特殊之处，以及句子的精确含义。这一切都弄清楚了，才能弄清每一章节的意思和各章节之间的联系。这才算是语言方面真正读懂了，才能理解全文讲的是什么，是怎样讲的，才谈得上文艺欣赏、文艺评论和翻译。

这本注解本是为精读而编写的，也可以说，是试图回答“怎样读”的问题的一个探索性的答案。

精读读懂以后，还希望能反复诵读，因为对于外语学习来说，懂和熟是两回事。懂而不熟，可以说并没有完成学习任务。“熟读唐诗三百首，不会吟诗也会吟。”熟读不但可以加深理解，提高阅读水平、鉴赏水平，而且直接有助于写和译，间接有助于听和说——达到全面学好英语的目的。

上面这段话是我们在合注的《再会，契普斯先生》一书“前言”中所说的。继《契普斯》之后，今又选择了萧伯纳的《卖花女》加以详注，都是试图用实例来解答“读什么？”和“怎样读？”的问题。

《契普斯》是小说，《卖花女》是剧本。这回选用一个剧本的原因是希望它在大体上帮助读者学习英语之外，还有助于学习口语。借助阅读学口语，通常采用会话书。从会话书中固然可以学到许多现成的日常口头用语，但往往比较刻板，太“规范化”，而实际生活中说的要随便得多、灵活得多。戏剧家模拟人物在实际生活中的对话，像录音一样记录成文字，比一般会话书中的对话更接近实际、更活泼。从不同人物口中，我们可以学到雅俗不同的语言，这对于听懂不同阶层的人所说的英语，也有帮助。

但是，应该强调说明，我们选用这本剧本，决不光是为供学习口语之用的。我们选用这个剧本，首先是因为萧伯纳的语言精练明快，是英国文学史上继斯威夫特（Jonathan Swift）以来第一人，而且这个剧本无论从题材还是思想性、艺术性方面来说，作为学习英语的读物都是比较理想的。

关于萧伯纳　萧伯纳（George Bernard Shaw）是现代爱尔兰戏剧家，鲁迅称他为“世界的文豪”，于1856年出生在爱尔兰都柏林一个小公务员的家庭。中学毕业后，因家贫，没有能够上大学，在一家公司里当职员。二十岁时，迁居伦敦。当时英国社会主义运动高涨，他在参加社会活动的同时，开始文艺创作，先是写小说，所著《一个脱离社会的社会主义者》（An Unsocial Socialist），尖锐批判资本主义。九十年代，他受了易卜生的社会剧的影响，并鉴于英国戏剧界的萎靡不振，决心从事戏剧创作。到1950年萧伯纳以九十四岁的高龄逝世，半个多世纪里他写了五十一个剧本。他惯用似非而是的俏皮话，无情揭露、批判资本主义社会种种不合理现象。资本主义的政治、经济、宗教、文化无不是他抨击的对象，其影响之大，遍及全世界。然而，他本身毕竟是个改良主义者，所以始终不能指出彻底改造社会的道路。

萧伯纳曾在1933年访问我国，受到宋庆龄、鲁迅等进步人士的热情接待。鲁迅有专文记述，极言“萧的伟大”，说“我是喜欢萧的”，“他说的是真话”，“他说的是直话”（《南腔北调集》）。萧的好多作品很早就在我国有译本介绍。在1956年萧伯纳诞生一百周年时，人民文学出版社曾出版他的戏剧集，1963年又出版了《萧伯纳戏剧三种》。

萧伯纳的主要剧作有《鳏夫的房产》（Widowers' Houses, 1892）、《华伦夫人的职业》（Mrs Warren's Profession, 1893）、《武器与人》（Arms and the Man, 1894）、《魔鬼的门徒》（The Devil's Disciple, 1897）、《凯撒和克丽奥佩拉》（Caesar and Cleopatra, 1898）、《人与超人》（Man and Superman, 1903）、《巴巴拉少校》（Major Barbara, 1905）、《卖花女》（Pygmalion, 1912）、《伤心之家》（Heartbreak House, 1919）、《回到玛士撒拉》（Back to Methuselah, 1921；旧译《千岁人》）、《圣女贞德》（Saint Joan, 1923）、《苹果车》（The Apple Cart, 1928）等。

关于《卖花女》剧本原名Pygmalion　Pygmalion原是希腊神话中的塞浦路斯国王，善于雕刻。他刻了一个象牙美女，名为Galatea，对她迷恋不置，祈求爱神赋予她生命，愿与她结为夫妻。爱神玉成了他的祈求，两人终成眷属。在本剧中，萧伯纳用Pygmalion比喻语言学家Higgins。Higgins用六个月的工夫训练一个满口伦敦土音的街头卖花姑娘，居然使她学会了上层社会的文雅口音、语言和风度。他带她出席大使馆的招待会，她那雍容大方的仪态使四座震惊，被疑为某国公主光临。故事的结局则与神话不同：Pygmalion娶Galatea为妻，而萧伯纳却在剧中以卖花女Eliza（即Liza）和Higgins教授之间的矛盾分歧收场，并在剧本后面加上了一条小品体裁的长尾巴（sequel，本书未收入），写Eliza最后离开Higgins，嫁了一个热爱她的穷小子Freddy。

萧伯纳别出心裁地拿语言学问题作为剧本的题材，讽刺上层社会所谓文雅的语言和风度，鲜明地显示劳动人民淳朴的本质远远高于上层社会的空虚外表。Eliza不能容忍上层社会熙来攘往的浮华无聊，不能容忍Higgins目空一切的高傲、任性，更不能容忍自己成了一个假人。Eliza最后离去，正表示她同上层社会的决裂。她宁愿嫁个穷小子，因为她深信，她和Freddy之间有真正的爱情，他们在一起可以有真正的生活。

萧伯纳在剧本序言中说，在Higgins身上有英国著名语言学家Henry Sweet（1845–1912）的影子。但这与剧情和剧本的中心思想没有多大关系。

《卖花女》写于1912年，1914年首次上演，1916年出版，是萧伯纳最有趣的一个剧本。1938年修改，并添上了一些场景，拍摄电影，也很成功，曾在我国放映。王佐良先生在《萧伯纳戏剧三种》的“译文序”中称《卖花女》“情节有趣，发人深思。至今显得十分新鲜。”这是很恰当的评语。《剑桥英国文学史简编》（The Concise Cambridge History of English Literature）也承认《卖花女》是“可喜的喜剧”，但不喜欢它的结局，这正是资产阶级的偏见。1956年美国抒情歌曲作家Alan Jay Lerner把萧伯纳的这部剧本改编成音乐喜剧《窈窕淑女》（My Fair Lady），把原来的结尾改成Higgins和Eliza“大团圆”，并拍摄了电影，轰动一时。

关于注解　和前注《契普斯》“前言”中所说的一样，“我们是从语言角度进行注解的，力求详尽。如果你觉得有些讲解是多余的，那就说明这些你已经懂了，可以跳过去。相反，如果这样详尽讲解了，你阅读还有困难，那说明这本书也许暂时对你还太深了些，可以先读一些简易读物，作为一个阶梯。注解的详略要正好符合所有读者的要求是不可能的。我们希望读者能够看得出，我们是认认真真地做了这本书的注解工作的，煞费苦心，总想切实有助于我国的英语学习者，尤其是自学者。”

剧本因为都是对话，句子结构和所用词汇比其他体裁的文学作品简单。然而口语常有特殊的表现方法，本书的讲解在这方面特别注意，尽量指出各种惯用法和阐明有些句子比较含蓄的涵义。萧伯纳的语言是现代英国语言的典范，既如上述。剧中在模拟卖花女未受训练前的语言时，出现少数伦敦土语、土音（主要是在第一幕中），都一一注出其相应的规范英语和正规读音。另外，萧伯纳特意在拼法中把ain't、don't、haven't等中的撇号（’）删去，写成aint、dont、havnt等；同样，把that's、let's、you'd、you're、it'll写成thats、lets、youd、youre、itll等。但是I'll、she'd、we'll、it's等中的撇号不删，以免与Ill、shed、well、its等相混淆。I'm中的撇号也不删，因为删了之后似应读成[ɪm]而不是[aɪm]了。对此，我们在这里作一总的说明，注中不再分散地解释了。

本剧有杨宪益先生译的英汉对照本（中国对外翻译出版社公司出版，1982）。杨译极好。如把详注对照译文研习原著，可以更加加深对原文的理解，更充分地欣赏原文、欣赏译文，学习翻译。

最后，申明一下：为了帮助读者精确了解原文，学好英语，我们的注解旨在讲清原文词义，明析原文的语法结构。注文不同于译文。翻译追求“信、达、雅”，而我们的注文常难免读来佶屈聱牙。好在没有人会把注文当作汉语规范，不会通过注文来学习汉语。但我们还是恳请读者谅解我们的苦心。

我们这样详尽的注解是否有必要，以及注解不当和错误之处，诚望英语学者和读者们予以批评指教。

葛传椝　俞亢咏

一九八五年五月

于汉光院

目 录

写在前面的话

前言

第一幕　剧情提要

ACT I

第二幕　剧情提要

ACT II

第三幕　剧情提要

ACT III

第四幕　剧情提要

ACT IV

第五幕　剧情提要

ACT V

第一幕　剧情提要

夏夜大雨。剧院散场，众人在教堂廊下避雨，一面抢着叫车子。Freddy在纷乱中撞翻了卖花女Liza的花篮，Liza要他赔钱，又缠着一位中年绅士，要他买花，说的是满口土话。语言学家Higgins教授背着众人在笔记本上记录Liza的发音。周围的人还当他是警察局的密探，以为他把她当做坏女人，要找她的麻烦，纷纷不平，引起一番争吵。后来Higgins才说出他是以研究语音学为职业的。他对Liza说，经过三个月的语音训练，他能使她冒充公爵夫人，参加外国大使的招待会，还说能使她在上等人家找到个保姆或者店员的工作。Liza要他买花，他扔了一把钱币在她篮子里，这一把钱里不但有好些银币，还有一枚金币。

那位中年绅士Pickering上校是研究印度方言的。他是远道而来、专程寻访Higgins教授的。Higgins对他也慕名已久。如今他们不期而遇，喜出望外。Higgins把地址告诉了他，请他来家一叙。就是这一对独身的语言学家，闹出了一场好戏。

撞翻花篮的Freddy是个破落户子弟。他在这一幕里只匆匆过场，后面可成了一个意想不到的角色。

ACT I

London at 11.15 p.m.1 Torrents of heavy summer rain2. Cab whistles3 blowing frantically in all directions. Pedestrians running for shelter4 into the market and under the portico5 of St. Paul's6 Church (not Wren's cathedral7 but Inigo Jones's church8 in Covent Garden vegetable market9), among them a lady and her daughter10 in evening dress11. All are peering out gloomily at the rain, except one man with his back turned12 to the rest, who seems wholly preoccupied with a notebook in which he is writing busily.

The church clock strikes the first quarter13.

THE DAUGHTER [in the space between the central pillars, close to the one on her left14] I'm getting chilled to the bone15. What can Freddy be doing all this time?16 He's been gone17 twenty minutes.

THE MOTHER [on her daughters' right] Not so long18. But he ought to have got us a cab by this19.

A BYSTANDER [on the lady's right] He wont get no cab not until half-past eleven20, missus21, when they come back after drop-ping their theatre fares22.

THE MOTHER But we must have a cab. We cant stand here until half-past eleven. It's too bad23.

THE BYSTANDER Well24, it aint25 my fault, missus.

THE DAUGHTER If Freddy had a bit of gumption, he would have got one at the theatre door.26

THE MOTHER What could he have done, poor boy?27

THE DAUGHTER Other people got cabs. Why couldnt he?

Freddy rushes in out of the rain from the Southampton Street28 side, and comes between them29 closing a dripping umbrella. He is a young man of twenty, in evening dress, very wet around the ankles.

THE DAUGHTER Well, havnt you got a cab?

FREDDY Theres not one to be had for love or money30.

THE MOTHER Oh, Freddy, there must be one31. You cant have tried32.

THE DAUGHTER It's too tiresome33. Do you expect us to go and get one34 ourselves?

FREDDY I tell you35 theyre all engaged36. The rain was so sudden: nobody was prepared37; and everybody had to take a cab. Ive been to Charing Cross one way and nearly to Ludgate Circus the other38; and they were all engaged.

THE MOTHER Did you try Trafalgar Square39?

FREDDY There wasnt one at Trafalgar Square.

THE DAUGHTER Did you try?

FREDDY I tried as far as Charing Cross Station40. Did you expect me to walk to Hammersmith41?

THE DAUGHTER You havnt tried at all.

THE MOTHER You really are very helpless42, Freddy. Go again; and dont come back until you have found a cab.

FREDDY I shall simply43 get soaked44 for nothing45.

THE DAUGHTER And what about us46? Are we to stay47 here all night in this draught48, with next to nothing on49? You selfish pig –

FREDDY Oh, very well: I'll go, I'll go. [He opens his umbrella and dashes off Strandwards50, but comes into collision51 with a flower girl who is hurrying in for shelter, knocking her basket out of her hands. A blinding flash of lightning52, followed instantly by a rattling peal of thunder, orchestrates53 the incident.]

THE FLOWER GIRL Nah then, Freddy: look wh' y' gowin, deah.54

FREDDY Sorry. [He rushes off.]

THE FLOWER GIRL [picking up her scattered flowers and replacing55 them in the basket] Theres menners f' yer!56 Tə-oo banches o voylets trod into the mad.57 [She sits down on the plinth of the column, sorting her flowers, on the lady's right. She is not at all a romantic figure58. She is perhaps eighteen, perhaps twenty, hardly older59. She wears a little sailor hat60 of black straw that has long been exposed to the dust and soot of London61 and has seldom if ever62 been brushed. Her hair needs washing rather badly63: its mousy color can hardly be natural64. She wears a shoddy65 black coat that reaches nearly to her knees66 and is shaped to her waist67. She has a brown skirt with a coarse apron. Her boots are much the worse for wear68. She is no doubt69 as clean as she can afford to be70; but compared to the ladies71 she is very dirty. Her features72 are no worse than theirs; but their condition leaves something to be desired73; and she needs the services of a dentist74.]

THE MOTHER How do you know that my son's name is Freddy, pray?75

THE FLOWER GIRL Ow, eez yə-ooa san, is e?76 Wal, fewd dan y' dəooty bawmz a mather should, eed now bettern to spawl a pore gel's flahrzn than ran awy athaht pyin.77 Will ye-oo py me f'them?78 [Here, with apologies, this desperate attempt to represent her dialect without a phonetic alphabet must be abandoned as unintelligible outside London.79]

THE DAUGHTER Do nothing of the sort, mother80. The idea!81

THE MOTHER Please allow me82, Clara83. Have you any pennies84?

THE DAUGHTER No. Ive nothing smaller than sixpence85.

THE FLOWER GIRL [hopefully] I can give you change86 for a tanner87, kind lady.

THE MOTHER [to Clara] Give it to me. [Clara parts88 reluctantly.] Now[to the girl] this is for your flowers.

THE FLOWER GIRL Thank you kindly, lady.89

THE DAUGHTER Make her give you the change. These things are only a penny a bunch.

THE MOTHER Do hold your tongue90, Clara. [to the girl] You can keep the change.

THE FLOWER GIRL Oh, thank you, lady.

THE MOTHER Now tell me how you know that young gentleman's name.

THE FLOWER GIRL I didnt.

THE MOTHER I heard you call him by it91. Dont try to deceive me.

THE FLOWER GIRL [protesting92] Who's trying to deceive you? I called him Freddy or Charlie93 same as94 you might yourself95 if you was96 talking to a stranger and wished to be pleasant97.

THE DAUGHTER Sixpence thrown away! Really, mamma, you might have spared Freddy that98. [She retreats in disgust behind the pillar.]

An elderly gentleman of the amiable military type rushes into the shelter99, and closes a dripping umbrella. He is in the same plight as Freddy, very wet about the ankles. He is in evening dress, with a light overcoat100. He takes the place left vacant101 by the daughter.

THE GENTLEMAN Phew!

THE MOTHER [to the gentleman] Oh, sir, is there any sign of its stopping102?

THE GENTLEMAN I'm afraid not103. It started worse than ever104 about two minutes ago. [He goes to the plinth beside the flower girl; puts up his foot on it; and stoops to turn down his trouser ends.]

THE MOTHER Oh dear! [She retires sadly and joins her daughter.]

THE FLOWER GIRL [taking advantage105 of the military gentleman's proximity106 to establish friendly relations with him] If it's worse, it's a sign it's nearly over.107 So cheer up, Captain108; and buy a flower off a poor girl109.

THE GENTLEMAN I'm sorry. I havnt any change110.

THE FLOWER GIRL I can give you change111, Captain.

THE GENTLEMAN For a sovereign112? Ive nothing less113.

THE FLOWER GIRL Garn114! Oh do buy a flower off me, Captain. I can change half-a-crown115. Take this for tuppence116.

THE GENTLEMAN Now dont be troublesome117: theres a good girl118. [trying his pockets119] I really havnt any change – Stop120: heres three hapence121, if thats any use to you. [He retreats to the other pillar.]

THE FLOWER GIRL [disappointed, but thinking three halfpence better than nothing122] Thank you, sir.

THE BYSTANDER [to the girl] You be careful123: give him a flower for it124. Theres a block125 here behind taking down every blessed word126 youre saying. [All turn to the man who is taking notes.]

THE FLOWER GIRL [springing up terrified] I aint done nothing wrong127 by speaking to the gentleman. Ive a right to sell flowers if I keep off the kerb128. [hysterically] I'm a respectable129 girl: so help me130, I never spoke to him except to ask him to buy a flower off me. [General hubbub131, mostly sympathetic to the flower girl, but deprecating her excessive sensibility132. Cries133 of Dont start hollerin134. Who's hurting you? Nobody's going to touch you. Whats the good of fussing?135 Steady on136. Easy, easy137, etc.138, come from the elderly staid spectators139, who pat her comfortingly. Less patient ones bid her shut her head140, or ask her roughly what is wrong with her141. A remoter group, not knowing what the matter is142, crowd in143 and increase the noise with question and answer144: Whats the row145? What-she do? Where is he? A tec taking her down.146 What! him?147 Yes: him over there148: Took money149 off the gentleman, etc.]

THE FLOWER GIRL [breaking through them150 to the gentleman, crying wildly] Oh, sir, dont let him charge151 me. You dunno what it means to me152. Theyll take away my character153 and drive me on the streets154 for speaking to gentlemen. They –

THE NOTE TAKER [coming forward on her right, the rest crowding after him] There!155 There! There! There! Who's hurting you, you silly girl? What do you take me for156?

THE BYSTANDER It's aw rawt: e's a genleman: look at his bə-oots157. [explaining to the note taker] She thought you was a copper's nark158, sir.

THE NOTE TAKER [with quick interest] Whats a copper's nark?

THE BYSTANDER [inapt at definition159] It's a – well160, it's a copper's nark, as you might say161. What else would you call it?162 A sort of informer.163

THE FLOWER GIRL [still hysterical] I take my Bible oath164 I never said a word –165

THE NOTE TAKER [overbearing but good-humored] Oh, shut up, shut up. Do I like a policeman?

THE FLOWER GIRL [far from reassured166] Then what did you take down my words for? How do I know whether you took me down right167? You just shew168 me what youve wrote169 about me. [The note taker opens his book and holds it steadily under her nose, though the pressure of the mob trying to read it over his shoulders170 would upset a weaker man171.] Whats that? That aint proper writing.172 I cant read that.

THE NOTE TAKER I can. [reads, reproducing173 her pronunciation exactly] 'Cheer ap, Keptin174; n'baw ya flahr orf a pore gel175.'

THE FLOWER GIRL [much distressed] It's because I called him Captain.176 I meant no harm177. [to the gentleman] Oh, sir, dont let him lay a charge agen me178 for a word like that. You –

THE GENTLEMAN Charge! I make no charge179. [to the note taker] Really, sir, if you are a detective, you need not begin protecting me against molestation by young women until I ask you.180 Anybody could see that the girl meant no harm.

THE BYSTANDERS GENERALLY181 [demonstrating against police espionage182] Course they could183. What business is it of yours?184 You mind your own affairs185. He wants promotion, he does.186 Taking down people's words!187 Girl188 never said a word to him. What harm if she did?189 Nice thing190 a girl cant shelter from the rain without being insulted, etc., etc., etc.191 [She is conducted by the more sym-pathetic demonstrators back to her plinth, where she resumes her seat and struggles with her emotion192.]

THE BYSTANDER He aint a tec. He's a blooming busybody193: thats what he is. I tell you, look at his bə-oots.

THE NOTE TAKER [turning on him genially194] And how are all your people195 down at Selsey196?

THE BYSTANDER [suspiciously] Who told you my people come from197 Selsey?

THE NOTE TAKER Never you mind198. They did.199 [to the girl] How do you come to be up so far east200? You were born in Lisson Grove201.

THE FLOWER GIRL [appalled] Oh, what harm is there in my leaving Lisson Grove? It wasnt fit for a pig to live in202; and I had to pay four-and-six203 a week. [in tears204] Oh, boo-hoo-oo –205

THE NOTE TAKER Live where you like206: but stop that noise.

THE GENTLEMAN [to the girl] Come, come!207 He cant touch you: you have a right to live208 where you please.

A SARCASTIC BYSTANDER [thrusting himself between the note taker and the gentleman] Park Lane209, for instance. Id like to go into the Housing Question210 with you, I would211.

THE FLOWER GIRL [subsiding into a brooding melancholy212 over her basket213, and talking very low-spiritedly to herself] I'm a good girl, I am.

THE SARCASTIC BYSTANDER [not attending to214 her] Do you know where I215 come from?

THE NOTE TAKER [promptly] Hoxton216.

Titterings217. Popular interest218 in the note taker's performance in-creases.

THE SARCASTIC ONE [amazed] Well, who said I didnt?219 Bly me!220 you know everything, you do.

THE FLOWER GIRL [still nursing her sense of injury221] Aint no call to meddle with me, he aint.222

THE BYSTANDER [to her] Of course he aint. Dont you stand it from him223. [to the note taker] See here224: what call have you to know about people225 what226 never offered to meddle with you227?

THE FLOWER GIRL Let him say what he likes. I dont want to have no truck with him.228

THE BYSTANDER You take us for dirt under your feet229, dont you? Catch you taking liberties with a gentleman!230

THE SARCASTIC BYSTANDER Yes: tell him where he come from231 if you want to go fortune-telling232.

THE NOTE TAKER Cheltenham, Harrow, Cambridge, and India.233

THE GENTLEMAN Quite right.

Great laughter. Reaction in the note taker's favor234. Exclamations of235 He knows all about it.236 Told him proper.237 Hear him tell the toff238 where he come from239? etc.

THE GENTLEMAN May I ask, sir, do you do this240 for your living241 at a music hall242?

THE NOTE TAKER Ive thought of that.243 Perhaps I shall some day.244

The rain has stopped; and the persons on the outside of the crowd begin to drop off245.

THE FLOWER GIRL [resenting the reaction] He's no gentleman, he aint, to interfere with a poor girl.246

THE DAUGHTER [out of patience247, pushing her way rudely to the front248 and displacing249 the gentleman, who politely retires to the other side of the pillar] What on earth250 is Freddy doing? I shall get pneumownia251 if I stay in this draught any longer252.

THE NOTE TAKER [to himself, hastily making a note of her pronunciation of 'monia'253] Earlscourt254.

THE DAUGHTER [violently] Will you please keep your impertinent remarks to yourself.255

THE NOTE TAKER Did I say that out loud256? I didnt mean to257. I beg your pardon.258 Your mother's Epsom259, unmistakeably.

THE MOTHER [advancing between the daughter and the note taker] How very curious260! I was brought up261 in Largelady Park262, near Epsom.

THE NOTE TAKER [uproariously amused263] Ha! Ha! What a devil of a name264! Excuse me265. [to the daughter] You want a cab, do you?

THE DAUGHTER Dont dare speak to me266.

THE MOTHER Oh please, please267, Clara. [Her daughter repudiates her268 with an angry shrug and retires haughtily.] We should be so grateful to you, sir, if you found us a cab.269 [The note taker produces270 a whistle.] Oh, thank you.271 [She joins her daughter.]

The note taker blows a piercing blast272.

THE SARCASTIC BYSTANDER There! I knowed273 he was a plain-clothes copper274.

THE BYSTANDER That aint a police whistle275: thats a sporting whistle276.

THE FLOWER GIRL [still preoccupied with277 her wounded feelings278] He's no right to take away my character. My character is the same to me as any lady's279.

THE NOTE TAKER I dont know whether youve noticed it; but280 the rain stopped about two minutes ago.

THE BYSTANDER So it has.281 Why didnt you say so before? And us losing our time listening to your silliness282! [He walks off towards the Strand.]

THE SARCASTIC BYSTANDER I can tell where you come from. Youcome from Anwell283. Go back there.

THE NOTE TAKER [helpfully] Hanwell284.

THE SARCASTIC BYSTANDER [affecting great distinction of speech285] Thenk you286, teacher287. Haw haw288! So long289. [He touches his hat290 with mock respect291 and strolls off.]

THE FLOWER GIRL Frightening people like that! How would he like it292 himself?

THE MOTHER It's quite fine now, Clara. We can walk to a motor bus. Come. [She gathers her skirts above her ankles and hurries off towards the Strand.]

THE DAUGHTER But the cab –293 [Her mother is out of hearing.] Oh, how tiresome! [She follows angrily.]

All the rest have gone except the note taker, the gentleman, and the flower girl, who sits arranging her basket, and still pitying herself in murmurs294.

THE FLOWER GIRL Poor girl! Hard enough for her to live without being worrited and chivied.295

THE GENTLEMAN [returning to his former place on the note taker's left]How do you do it, if I may ask?296

THE NOTE TAKER Simply phonetics.297 The science of speech298. Thats my profession: also my hobby. Happy is the man299 who canmake a living by his hobby! You can spot an Irishman or a Yorkshireman by his brogue300. I can place any man within six miles.301 I can place him within two miles in London. Sometimes within two streets.302

THE FLOWER GIRL Ought to be ashamed of himself, unmanly coward!303

THE GENTLEMAN But is there a living in that?304

THE NOTE TAKER Oh yes. Quite a flat one305. This is an age of up-starts306. Men begin in Kentish Town307 with £80 a year, and end in Park Lane308 with a hundred thousand. They want to drop Kentish Town309; but they give themselves away310 every time they open their mouths. Now I can teach them –

THE FLOWER GIRL Let him mind his own business and leave a poor girl –311

THE NOTE TAKER [explosively312] Woman313: cease this detestable boohooing314 instantly; or else315 seek the shelter of some other place of worship316.

THE FLOWER GIRL [with feeble defiance] Ive a right to be here if I like317, same as318 you.

THE NOTE TAKER A woman who utters such depressing and disgusting sounds has no right to be anywhere319 – no right to live.Remember that you are a human being with a soul and the divine gift of articulate speech320: that your native language321 is the language of Shakespear and Milton and The Bible322; and dont sit there crooning like a bilious pigeon323.

THE FLOWER GIRL [quite overwhelmed324, looking up at him in mingled wonder and deprecation325 without daring to raise her head] Ah-ah-ah-ow-ow-ow-oo!326

THE NOTE TAKER [whipping out his book327] Heavens!328 What a sound!329 [He writes; then holds out the book and reads, reproducing her vowels330 exactly.] Ah-ah-ah-ow-ow-ow-oo!

THE FLOWER GIRL [tickled331 by the performance, and laughing in spite of herself332] Garn!

THE NOTE TAKER You see this creature333 with her Kerbstone English334: the English that will keep her in the gutter to the end of her days335? Well, sir, in three months I could pass that girl off as a duchess336 at an ambassador's garden party337. I could even get her a place as lady's maid338 or shop assistant339, which requires better English.

THE FLOWER GIRL What's that you say?340

THE NOTE TAKER Yes, you squashed cabbage leaf, you disgrace to the noble architecture of these columns, you incarnate insult to the English language: I could pass you off as the Queen of Sheba.341 [to the gentleman] Can you believe that?

THE GENTLEMAN Of course I can. I am myself a student of Indian dialects342; and –343

THE NOTE TAKER [eagerly] Are you? Do you know Colonel Pickering344, the author of Spoken Sanscrit345?

THE GENTLEMAN I am Colonel Pickering. Who are you?

THE NOTE TAKER Henry Higgins346, author of Higgins's Universal Alphabet.

PICKERING [with enthusiasm] I came from India to meet you.

HIGGINS I was going to India to meet you.

PICKERING Where do you live?

HIGGINS 27A Wimpole Street347. Come and see me tomorrow.

PICKERING I'm at the Carlton348. Come with me now and lets have a jaw over some supper349.

HIGGINS Right you are350.

THE FLOWER GIRL [to Pickering, as he passes her] Buy a flower, kind gentleman. I'm short for my lodging351.

PICKERING I really havnt any change. I'm sorry. [He goes away.]

HIGGINS [shocked at the girl's mendacity352] Liar353. You said you could change half-a-crown.

THE FLOWER GRIL [rising in desperation] You ought to be stuffed with nails, you ought.354 [flinging the basket at his feet] Take the whole blooming basket for sixpence.355

The church clock strikes the second quarter.

HIGGINS [hearing in it356 the voice of God, rebuking him for his Pharisaic want of charity357 to the poor girl] A reminder358. [He raises his hat359 solemnly; then throws a handful of money into the basket and follows Pickering.]

THE FLOWER GIRL [picking up a half-crown] Ahow-ooh! [picking up a couple360 of florins361] Aaahow-ooh! [picking up several coins] Aaaaaah-ow-ooh! [picking up a half-sovereign362] Aaaaaaaaaaaahow-ooh!!!

FREDDY [springing out of a taxicab] Got one363 at last. Hallo! [to the girl] Where are the two ladies that were here?

THE FLOWER GIRL They walked to the bus when the rain stopped.

FREDDY And left me with a cab on my hands!364 Damnation!365

THE FLOWER GIRL [with grandeur366] Never mind, young man367. I'm going home in a taxi. [She sails off368 to the cab. The driver puts his hand behind him and holds the door firmly shut against her369. Quite understanding his mistrust, she shews him her handful of money.] A taxi fare aint no object370 to me, Charlie371. [He grins and opens the door.] Here. What about the basket?

THE TAXIMAN Give it here.372 Tuppence extra.373

LIZA374 No: I dont want nobody to see it375. [She crushes it into the cab and gets in, continuing the conversation through the window.] Goodbye, Freddy.

FREDDY [dazedly376 raising his hat] Goodbye.

TAXIMAN Where to?377

LIZA Bucknam Pellis378 [Buckingham Palace].

TAXIMAN What d'ye mean – Bucknam Pellis379?

LIZA Dont you know where it is? In the Green Park380, where the King381 lives. Goodbye, Freddy. Dont let me keep standing there.382 Goodbye.

FREDDY Goodbye. [He goes.]

TAXIMAN Here? Whats this about Bucknam Pellis?383 What business have you at Bucknam Pellis?

LIZA Of course I havnt none.384 But I wasnt going to let him know that385. You drive me home.

TAXIMAN And wheres home386?

LIZA Angel Court, Drury Lane, next Meiklejohn's oil shop387.

TAXIMAN That sounds more like it388, Judy389. [He drives off.]

Let us follow the taxi to the entrance to Angel Court, a narrow little archway between two shops, one of them Meiklejohn's oil shop390. When it stops there, Eliza391 gets out, dragging her basket with her.

LIZA How much?

TAXIMAN [indicating the taximeter392] Cant you read?393 A shilling.

LIZA A shilling for two minutes!!

TAXIMAN Two minutes or ten: it's all the same.394

LIZA Well, I dont call it right395.

TAXIMAN Ever been in a taxi before?396

LIZA [with dignity] Hundreds and thousands of times397, young man.

TAXIMAN [laughing at her] Good for you398, Judy. Keep the shilling399, darling, with best love from all at home400. Good luck!401 [He drives off.]

LIZA [humiliated] Impidence402!

She picks up the basket and trudges up the alley403 with it to her lodging: a small room with very old wall paper hanging loose in the damp places404. A broken pane in the window is mended with paper. A portrait of a popular actor and a fashion plate of ladies' dresses405, all wildly beyond poor Eliza's means406, both torn from newspapers407, are pinned up408 on the wall. A birdcage hangs409 in the window; but its tenant410 died long ago: it remains as a memorial411 only.

These are the only visible luxuries412: the rest413 is the irreducible minimum of poverty's needs414: a wretched415 bed heaped with all sorts of coverings that have any warmth in them416, a draped packing case417 with a basin and jug418 on it and a little looking glass over it419, a chair and table420, the refuse of some suburban kitchen421, and an American alarum clock422 on the shelf above the unused423 fireplace: the whole424 lighted425 with a gas lamp with a penny in the slot meter426. Rent: four shillings a week.427

Here Eliza, chronically weary428, but too excited to go to bed, sits429, counting her new riches430 and dreaming and planning what to do with them431, until the gas goes out432, when she enjoys for the first time the sensation433 of being able to put in another penny without grudging434 it. This prodigal mood does not extinguish her gnawing sense of the need for economy sufficiently to prevent her from calculating435 that she can dream and plan in bed more cheaply and warmly than sitting up436 without a fire437. So she takes off her shawl and skirt and adds them to the miscellaneous bedclothes438. Then she kicks off her shoes and gets into bed439 without any further change440.

1 London at 11.15 p.m.　这是名词词组构成的单部句（one element sentence），可称为名词句，后面三句同。11.15是英国式；11:15是美国式。

2 Torrents [ˈtɒrənts] of heavy summer rain夏季大雨的倾注；夏雨倾盆。Torrents作“倾注”解，用复数。

3 Cab whistles [ˈwɪslz]叫车的哨子。Cab Taxi-cab.出租车（兼指出租的马车或机动车）。

4 running for shelter　奔去躲避。Shelter作“掩蔽”解，是不可数名词。

5 portico　[ˈpɔːtɪkəʊ]有圆柱的门廊。

6 St. Paul's [sntˈpɔːlz] church圣保罗教堂，即St. Paul's Cathedral。

7 Wren's [renz] cathedral [kəˈθiːdrəl] （由）Wren（设计建造）的大教堂。Christopher [ˈkrɪstəfə] Wren (1632–1723)，英国建筑家。cathedral大教堂（内设主教法座）。

8 Inigo [ˈɪnɪgəʊ] Jones's [ˈdʒəʊnzɪz] church （由） Inigo Jones（设计建造）的教堂。Inigo Jones (1573–1652)，英国建筑家兼舞台设计师。

9 Covent [ˈkʌvənt] Garden vegetable market Covent Garden在伦敦中部地区，也指那里的菜市场或剧场the Covent Garden Theatre。

10 among them a lady and her daughter　among them there being a lady and her daughter.是个简略的独立结构（absolute construction）。

11 evening dress　晚礼服。注意：是个不可数名词，前面不用冠词，也不可用复数。

12 turned过去分词，其作用等于being turned。

13 the first quarter　这里指十一点钟的第一刻（的末尾），即上文所说的11.15 p.m.。

14 close [kləʊz] to the one on her left靠近她左边的廊柱。one = pillar.

15 chilled to the bone　冻得彻骨。chilled过去分词，作表语。

16 What can Freddy be doing all this time? Freddy（去了）这么久，会在干些什么呢？这里can有“究竟”的意思。

17 He's been gone　注意有been，指“离开此地而尚未回来”的情况。这里gone是形容词。

18 Not so long　He's not been gone so long.口语中很多用省略句。

19 ought to have got us a cab by this到这时候应该给我们弄到一辆出租车了。ought后接完成时不定式表示应该已经做到而没有做到。by this by this time, by now.

20 He wont get no cab not until half-past [ˈhɑːfpɑːst] eleven He won't get any cab not until half-past eleven.没受过教育的英美人常使用双重否定（double negative）；这里是用了三重否定。

21 missus　[ˈmɪsɪz] = missis [ˈmɪsɪz].太太。这是没有受过教育的人的用语；正规英语称madam [ˈmædəm]。

22 when they come back after dropping their theatre fares在他们送掉了剧院散场的客人们以后再回来的时候。这个句子可以说是和half-past eleven同位，也可以说是until的另一个宾语。dropping使下车。fares　乘客。这里不作“车费”解。

23 It's too bad　这（在这里站到十一点半）太糟了。

24 Well　随口的感叹词，这里表示无可奈何。

25 aint ain't，俚语，= isn't。

26 If Freddy had a bit of gumption [ˈgʌmpʃən], he would have got one at the theatre door.如果Freddy有点办法，他准已在剧场门口叫到一辆出租车了。had是虚拟语气，实际是因为Freddy没有办法，所以车子没有叫到。不用had had而用had，因为有没有办法是一个人的本领，而不是某一时刻的情况。比较：If he had had a chance, he would have got one at the theatre door.“碰到个机会”便是一时的情况。Freddy [ˈfredi]男子名。gumption精明能干，魄力，是口语词。

27 What could he have done, poor boy?　他能怎么样呢，可怜的孩子？poor boy与he同位，指Freddy。

28 Southampton [saʊˈθæmptən] Street伦敦一街道。

29 comes between them　来到他们中间。them指the mother和the daughter，即Freddy的母亲和姐妹。

30 for love or money　无论是讲交情还是讲价钱，无论如何。这是习语，只用于否定，这里上文有not one。

31 there must be one　一定有一辆车的。

32 You cant have tried你不可能已经努力（找）过。can + have +过去分词，表示过去的可能。

33 tiresome　招人讨厌的，恼人的。

34 expect us to go and get one准备让我们去叫辆车子。expect这里是“料想，准备”的意思，不是“盼望，希望”。go and get = go to get，是口语。

35 I tell you　我告诉你们，你们该相信我，的确是这样。

36 theyre (= they were) all engaged车子都被人叫去了。

37 The rain was so sudden: nobody was prepared so sudden后面的冒号相当于英语的连词that的作用。prepared有准备的，是形容词作表语。

38 Ive been to Charing Cross [ˈtʃærɪŋ ˈkrɒs] one way and nearly to Ludgate [ˈlʌdgeɪt] Circus the other我一头到过Charing Cross，另一头到过Ludgate Circus。Ive been to我到过（某处）。Charing Cross是伦敦中部地区地名；... Cross前面不用the。Ludgate Circus是伦敦地名。circus英国十字路中的圆形广场；... Circus前面不用the。one way in one direction. the other = the other way = in the other direction.

39 Trafalgar [trəˈfælgə] Square伦敦中区广场名；前面不用the。

40 Charing Cross Station　Charing Cross车站；... Station前面不用the。

41 Hammersmith　[ˈhæməsmɪθ]伦敦地名，在泰晤士河北岸。

42 helpless　没用的，帮不上忙的；无效的。

43 simply　简直。

44 soaked [səʊkt]被雨水湿透；过去分词作表语。

45 for nothing没来由地，白白地，徒然。

46 what about us　我们怎么样。what about ...口语中常用，意思是“……怎么样”。

47 Are we to stay Should we stay.

48 draught　[draːft]穿堂风，风口。

49 with next to nothing on几乎不穿衣服（极言衣着单薄）。next to nothing几乎一无所有。on被穿着。

50 Strandwards　[ˈstrændwədz]朝the Strand方向。the Strand原指沿泰晤士河的河岸，现在是伦敦商业区（the City of London）与威斯敏斯特教堂（Westminster Abbey）之间的干道，注意用the。

51 comes into collision [kəˈlɪʒən] with和……相撞，撞着。

52 blinding flash of lightning　[ˈlaɪtnɪŋ]使人目眩的闪电。

53 orchestrates　[ˈɔːkɪstreɪt]原义是“为……配交响乐”，这里转义指“（雷电）用它的声响配合（Freddy撞翻卖花女的花篮这一事情，即the incident）”。

54 Nah then, Freddy: look wh' y' gowin, deah.　= Now then, Freddy: look where you're going, dear. Now then注意啊！喂！look where you're going （你怎么不）看看你走到哪里去了。dear犹言“朋友”。

55 replacing　放回；这里不作“以……代替”解。

56 Theres menners f' yer!　= There's manners for you!那就是你的礼貌！其实是说：“你竟然没有礼貌！”manners礼貌；具有抽象名词性质，虽是复数形式，作单数用，所以前面用There's，而不用There're。

57 Tə-oo banches o voylets trod into the mad. = Two bunches of violets (were) trod into the mud.不用were，是名词句。

58 romantic figure　楚楚动人者。

59 hardly older　不大会（比二十岁）年纪更大。

60 sailor hat　（妇女戴的）低顶草帽。

61 exposed to the dust and soot of London听任暴露于伦敦的尘土和煤灰中（势必沾满灰尘）。

62 seldom if ever　即便曾经有过也是极其难得地；实际意义= seldom or never。

63 needs washing rather badly极需要洗。need后面用主动形式的动名词，表示被动的意思。注意：不能说need being washed。badly口语中常作“非常”解。

64 its mousy [ˈmaʊsi] color can hardly be natural她头发的灰褐颜色不大可能是天然如此。can这里作“可能”解，不是“能够”的意思。

65 shoddy质量差的，以次充好的（如充羊毛的）。

66 reaches nearly to her knees　几乎齐膝。reach to伸延到……。

67 shaped [ʃeɪpt] to her waist裁制得适合她的腰身。

68 the worse for wear　穿得破旧了的；破旧不堪的。the是副词。

69 no doubt的确，固然；注意下面用but ...。

70 as she can afford to be尽她所能地干净；be后省略clean。

71 the ladies　上流社会妇人那一类人物。用the指“……那一类人物”。

72 Her features她脸庞的各部，她的容貌。

73 their condition leaves something to be desired　它们（her features）的状况还需改善；她的容貌还需修饰。leaves something (much, nothing) to be desired都是习语，分别作“还需改善”（“还需大大改善”、“无需改善，完美无缺”）解。

74 the services of a dentist牙医服务（指修补、矫正、洗洁等）。注意用复数形式services。

75 How do you know that my son's name is Freddy, pray?　你怎么知道我儿子的名字叫Freddy？卖花女对此没有回答。再次问她后，她才回答（见后）。pray = please.请问。

76 Ow, eez yə-ooa san, is e?　= Oh, he is your son, is he?

77 Wal, fewd dan y' də-ooty bawmz a mather should, eed now bettern to spawl a pore gel's flahrzn than ran awy athaht pyin. = Well, if you'd (= you had) done your duty by him as a mother should, he'd (= he would) know better than to spoil a poor girl's flowers and then run away without paying.唉，假如你尽到了一个母亲应尽的（教育子女的）责任，他应该懂得道理，不至于弄坏了一个穷苦女孩的花，钱也不给就跑掉了。这是虚拟句：if you had done ... , he would know ... ，假如你过去做了……，他现在就懂……了；事实是你过去没有做，所以他现在不懂。done your duty by him尽了你对他的责任；注意用by。know better than （后接带to的不定式）懂得，明白，因而不至于……。

78 Will ye-oo py me f'them?　= Will you pay me for them (= the flowers)?

79 Here, with apologies [əˈpɒədʒɪz], this desperate [ˈdespərət] attempt to represent [reprɪˈzent] her dialect without a phonetic alphabet [ˈælfəbet] must be abandoned as unintelligible outside London.至此以后，将放弃这种不用音标，勉强描述她一口伦敦方言的做法，因为在伦敦以外（的人）无法理解，抱歉。注意with apologies中用复数。

80 Do nothing of the sort.　决不要干这样的事；一个钱也不要给她。

81 The idea!（亏她想得出这样的）好主意！岂有此理！

82 Please allow me　= Please allow me to do something of the sort.让我给一点钱。

83 Clara [ˈkleərə]女子名。

84 pennies便士。penny有两个复数式：指一个一个便士的钱币时用pennies；计值用pence。

85 sixpence　[ˈsɪkspens]六便士，这里指英国当时六便士面值的银币。当时英国一镑＝二十先令，一先令＝十二便士；六便士即半先令。现已取消先令，一镑=一百便士。

86 change　[tʃeɪndʒ]找头。

87 tanner （俚语）英国旧时的六便士银币。

88 parts　（口语）出钱，付款。

89 Thank you kindly, lady.　多谢你，女士。用kindly来修饰自己对对方的道谢，在意义上讲是不妥当的，但事实上用得很多。lady这里用来称对方，现在不是正规的用语。

90 Do hold your tongue　住嘴。Do hold加重hold的语气；Do要重读。You can keep the change你不用找我钱了。keep the change是惯用语；不说Do not give me the change。

91 call him by it　用Freddy的名字叫他；注意用by。

92 protesting　抗议；辩白。protest动词读[prəˈtest]，名词读[ˈprəʊtest]。

93 Charlie　[ˈtʃaːli] Charlie和Freddy等都是很普通的男子名。

94 same as正规英语用the same as或just as。

95 you might yourself = you might yourself call him Freddy or Charlie.

96 if you was正规英语应作if you were；这里是虚拟语气。

97 be pleasant　[ˈplezənt]使人喜悦；这里是“表示亲热”的意思。

98 you might have spared Freddy that　你大可把那个（白白扔掉的六便士）省下给Freddy。spare可以接双宾语，spare Freddy that比spare that to Freddy普通，尤其如果这里不用that而用了the sixpence。

99 rushes into the shelter　冲进躲避处。比较：前面有过running for shelter，那shelter是抽象名词，作“掩蔽”解；这里的shelter是普通名词，是具体的掩蔽的地方，所以用冠词the。

100 light overcoat　薄大衣。厚大衣称heavy overcoat。

101 the place left vacant被空出的地方。left是过去分词，修饰the place; vacant是补语。

102 any sign of its stopping　它（雨）停的任何迹象。

103 I'm afraid not　= I'm afraid there's not any sign of its stopping.

104 It started worse than ever情况比原来更糟了。这里指雨开始下得更大了。

105 taking advantage [ədˈvɑːntɪdʒ] of利用，趁……的机会；是习语。

106 proximity [prɒkˈsɪməti]靠近；接近。

107 If it's worse, it's a sign it's nearly over.　sign后面省略that；第一和第三个it代替rain，第二个it是虚词（expletive），和that it's nearly over同位。

108 Captain　是“队长”或“陆军上尉”、“海军上校”。这里是卖花女对军人的胡乱称呼。

109 buy a flower off a poor girl从穷苦女孩子手里买一枝花。注意用off，有“让我卖掉一枝花”的意思。

110 change　零钱；是不可数名词。

111 change　找头；是不可数名词。

112 For a sovereign　[ˈsɒvrɪn]? = Can you give me change for a sovereign? Sovereign是当时的一金镑硬币。

113 Ive nothing less = I've nothing less than a sovereign.我没有比一金镑小的钱币。

114 Garn　英国俚语，表示不信、轻蔑等意，犹言“去你的！”、“得了吧！”，是从go on转讹而来。

115 change half-a-crown　兑开两先令六便士的硬币。当时英国有五先令的硬币，称crown；也有两先令六便士的硬币，称half-a-crown。

116 Take this for tuppence [ˈtʌpəns].出两便士买这个吧。

117 Now dont be troublesome　[ˈtrʌbəlsəm]好啦，别纠缠不清了。Now这里是语气词，引出命令、请求、劝慰等。troublesome使人感觉麻烦的；讨厌的；不是“感觉麻烦的”。

118 theres a good girl这才是个好姑娘。

119 trying his pockets　在口袋里摸摸看。

120 Stop等一等看。

121 heres three hapence [ˈheɪpəns]这里有一个半（⅓ × 3）便士。hapence半便士；正规写作halfpence（也读[ˈheɪpəns]），是halfpenny [ˈheɪpni]的复数。halfpennies计值用halfpence。一个半便士是一个数额，所以动词用单数。

122 better than nothing　比没有好些；聊胜于无。

123 You be careful　比Be careful语气重。

124 it　指three hapence。

125 block （俚语）家伙（只指男人）。

126 every blessed [ˈblesɪd] word每一个词。blessed这里用以加强every的语气，带有俚俗的口气，但比说every damned word文雅些。

127 I aint done nothing wrong　= I haven't done anything wrong / I've done nothing wrong.

128 keep off the kerb　对街沿石保持距离；不走到街沿石。kerb = kerbstone.人行道的镶边石，街沿石；是英国用语，在美国称curb(stone)。

129 respectable正派的；这里不作“可敬的”解。

130 so help me也作so help me God，是赌咒用语；犹言“天晓得”，表示千真万确的。

131 General hubbub　['hʌbʌb]众人的喧闹。

132 deprecating ['ˈdeprəkeɪt] her excessive sensibility责怪她过分的敏感（神经过敏）。

133 Cries是后面come from the elderly staid spectators的主语。Cries of后面的Dont start hollerin到Easy easy, etc.是你一声、我一声Cries的内容。

134 hollerin = hollering.吵闹。土语中总将-ing读作-in。

135 Whats the good of fussing?　大惊小怪有什么好处？good作名词，是“好处”、“用处”的意思。

136 Steady on　[ˈstedi ɒn]且慢；停下来。

137 Easy, easy Easy是Be easy、Take it easy之略，意思是“不要急”、“定心”。

138 etc. [ˌetˈsetərə]是拉丁语et cetera的缩写，也写作&c, = and so on，等等。这里etc.指上文众人所说的种种话以外还有其他的话，并不是说话中有这个词。

139 the elderly staid [steɪd] spectators年老持重的旁观者们。

140 shut her head　住嘴，= shut her mouth.比较：open one's head，（美国俚语）开口，说话。

141 what is wrong with her她出了什么事。注意：用with。

142 not knowing what the matter is　不知是什么事情。这里the matter是字面的意义：这事情。比较：not knowing what is the matter是“不知出了什么事”的意思，这里的the matter是习语，作“麻烦”、“毛病”解，基本上= wrong，具有形容词的性质。

143 crowd in拥挤到人群中来。

144 question and answer　照理，question和answer都是可数名词，应作questions and answers，但两词用and连接，成了一对，故可用单数。后面许多话都是回答的内容。

145 Whats the row [raʊ]?吵嚷什么？What-she [ˈwɒtʃɪ] do? = What does she do?这里What-she连写，表示说话的人把两个词念在一起。

146 A tec taking her down.　= A tec is taking her down.一个侦探在把她（说的话）记下来。tec俚语，= detective；指剧本开首的那个one man，即the note taker。take down记下，录下；是习语。

147 What! him?什么！他吗？这是What! Is it he?的省略句。照理him应作he，主格作表语，但口语中常用宾格。

148 him over there = It's him over there. over there他正在那边。

149 Took money　= She took money.

150 breaking through them　从人群中挤出去（或冲出去）。

151 charge　控告。

152 You dunno [ˈdʌnəʊ] what it means to me.你不知道这（控告我）意味着什么。dunno口语，= don't know.

153 Theyll take away my character　[ˈkærəktə]他们会拿掉我的品行证明书；毁我的名声。They泛指警察局人员。

154 drive me on the streets把我赶到街头去流浪。

155 There!　感叹词，用以引起注意、表示安慰或加强语气等。

156 take me for...　当我是……

157 It's aw rawt: e's a genleman: look at his bə-oots　= It's all right: he's a gentleman: look at his boots.没有问题，他是个上等人，看他的皮鞋。意思是从他的皮鞋可以看得出，他不是警察手下的密探（见下句）。

158 She thought you was a copper's nark　她原以为你是警察手下的密探。was是were之讹。copper警察；是俚语。nark （警察手下的）密探；也是俚语。

159 inapt at definition　不善于下定义。inapt作“不熟练于某事的”解时，介词用at；作“对某事不恰当的”解时，用for。

160 well语气词。It's a后面应该说出个copper's nark的定义来，但是说不出，打了个顿，含糊地说了声well，接下去还是个copper's nark。

161 as you might say　有如你们（如果说到这种人时）会这样称呼的。

162 What else would you call it?还能叫它什么呢？it指copper's nark这个名称。

163 A sort of informer. You would call it a sort of informer.informer现在只作“告密者”解；“报告者”用informant。

164 I take my Bible oath　我对《圣经》发誓。take an oath = swear.发誓这里后面省略引起同位从句的连词that。

165 never said a word –没有说过一句（不正经的或违法的）话。这句话没说完，被人喝住了（见下句）。

166 far from reassured　还是不放心的；极不放心。far from习语；极不。from虽是介词，后面可接形容词；也可作far from being reassured。

167 took me down right　正确地把我记下来。right这里是副词，= rightly；在动词后面通常用right。

168 shew　是show的古老拼法，萧伯纳爱用此式。

169 youve wrote是you've written之讹。

170 trying to read it over his shoulders　想从他肩背后看他写的东西。这个分词短语修饰the mob。

171 would upset a weaker man　是虚拟语气，= would upset him if he were a weaker man than he is。would upset的主语是the pressure。

172 That aint proper writing.那不是正规文字（因为那是些特殊的缩记符号）。

173 reproducing　重新念出；模仿。

174 Keptin　= Captain.

175 n'baw ya flahr orf a pore gel = and buy a flower off a poor girl.

176 It's because I called him Captain.　卖花女误会，以为因为她称人Captain被认为犯了错误。

177 meant [ment] no harm没有恶意。

178 lay a charge agen (= against) me告我一状；控告我；指责我。

179 Charge! I make no charge.　Charge!在这里是名词，承接上文lay a charge。make no charge没有控告；没有指责。

180 you need not begin protecting me against molestation by young women until I ask you.　you need not begin protecting me ... until I ask you，在我请求你之前，你不必保护起我来。protecting me against molestation by young women保护我不受年轻女子的缠扰。protect后表示不受重大损害用against，表示不受一般损害用from。

181 the bystanders generally　一般旁观者；generally指作者“笼统地”说“旁观者”。

182 police espionage [ˈespɪənɑːʒ]警察局的密探行为。espionage一般指“间谍活动”。

183 Course they could　= Of course they could. they could承接上文Anybody could see ...。Anybody是单数，但因概念上是许多人，所以英美人往往用复数they作anybody的代词。对于everybody也是这样。

184 What business is it of yours? of yours修饰business。全句= What's your business?这关你什么事？

185 You mind your own affairs你管你自己的事就是了（可不用管别人的事）。

186 He wants promotion, he does.　加he does强调He wants promotion。

187 Taking down people's words!这是动名词短语构成的单部句。

188 Girl　= The girl.

189 What harm if she did? = What harm is there if she said a word to him?

190 Nice thing　= It's a nice thing that ... .这里nice是反语：糟糕的。

191 etc., etc., etc.　犹言“等等，等等”，相当于and so on and so forth。

192 struggles with her emotion　跟她自己（激动）的情绪挣扎；竭力使自己镇静下来（一时还镇静不下来）。

193 a blooming busybody　[ˈbɪzɪbɒdi]太爱管闲事的人。blooming这里是俚语，与blessed一样是bloody、damned等诅咒语的委婉的说法，此处用以加重busybody的语气，没有具体意义。

194 turning on him genially　和蔼地转向他（说）。

195 your people你的家属。

196 down at Selsey　[ˈselsi] Selsey，地名，所在不详；down，说“南方”、“乡村”、“内地”都用down。

197 come from　出生于（某地）。

198 Never you mind　= Never mind.用you加重语气。mind = mind who told me that your people come from Selsey.

199 They did. = They（人们）told me that your people come from Selsey.

200 come to be up so far east　向东跑这么远到城里来。come后接不定式，表示“终于”或“开始”。up从乡村到城市用up。

201 Lisson [ˈlɪsn] Grove地名，看上文当在伦敦以西郊区。

202 It wasnt fit for a pig to live in　那地方（很糟，）给猪待在里面都不行。

203 four-and-six　四先令六便士。（现行英国币制已废除先令，见前。）

204 in tears在流泪。

205 boo-hoo-oo –哭闹声。因而boohoo作“哭闹”解，名词读作[ˈbuːhuː]，动词读作[ˌbuːˈhuː]。

206 Live where you like　你爱住在哪里就住在哪里。

207 Come, come!　Come作感叹词，这里表示不耐烦，也可用以引起注意或表示鼓励等。

208 have a right to live　有居住的权利。也可说have the right to live；也可说have a (或the) right of living，但不如用不定式普遍。

209 Park Lane　伦敦一街，是高级住宅区。那里当然轮不到卖花女居住；这是讽刺the note taker和the gentleman对卖花女说的她有权利要住哪里就住哪里。

210 go into the Housing [ˈhaʊzɪŋ] Question研究住房问题。go into探讨；深入研究；是习语。the Housing Question住房问题是当时英国正争议着的一大社会问题，用大写字母开头，表示专题。

211 I would　句末加I would，重复句首的Id (= I would)，是强调语气。

212 subsiding [səbˈsaɪdɪŋ] into a brooding [ˈbruːdɪŋ] melancholy [ˈmelənkəli] （从原来的哭闹）平静下来，转入闷闷不乐。melancholy是不可数名词，这里用a，是“一种……”的意思。

213 over her basket低头对着她的篮子。

214 attending to　注意；理会。

215 I　斜体，要重读，强调“我”，表示“你认得出别人是哪里人，能说出我是哪里人吗”的口气。

216 Hoxton　[ˈhɒkstən]地名，所在不详。

217 Titterings　（周围的人）嗤嗤的笑声。

218 Popular interest大众的兴趣。

219 Well, who said I didnt?　= Well, who said I didn't come from Hoxton?这是反问（rhetoric question），强调表示“唉，一点不错，我确是Hoxton人。”

220 Bly me!俚语；表示惊讶；也作Blimey [ˈblaɪmi]；是由(God) blind me转讹而来。

221 nursing her sense of injury　感觉很受伤。

222 Aint no call to meddle with me, he aint.句首的Aint = He aint (= He hasn't)；口语中有时省略主语。call理由；必要。Aint no call是双重否定，应作Has no call。

223 Dont you stand it from him　= Dont stand it from him.中间用you，加强语气。stand it from him忍受他那一套。it并无具体所指，有些语法家称为“it的泛用（the vague use of 'it'）”。

224 See here　也说Look here，是引起注意的口头语，意思是“听我说”，犹如说I say。

225 know about people　知道关于人们的事情。

226 what应作who或that。

227 offered to meddle with you要来干预你。offered表示“主动”、“起劲”的意思。

228 I dont want to have no truck with him.　双重否定；= I'll have no truck with him. truck交往；交道；是口语。

229 take us for dirt under your feet　把我们当作你脚底下的垃圾；极度蔑视我们。dirt统指肮脏东西；是不可数名词。

230 Catch you taking liberties with a gentleman!　料你不敢跟（有身份的）绅士胡来！（言下之意你只敢欺侮卖花女等。）Catch somebody doing something有“发现某人做某事（决不放过；料你不敢）”的意思，如Catch me smoking again!你瞧着吧，我决不再抽烟！take liberties（也作liberty）with对……放肆；随意对待。

231 come from　应作comes from。

232 go fortune-telling　进行算命。go doing通常作“出去做某事”解，如go shopping，出去买东西；也作“进行……”、“继续……”解，如Don't go telling me lies，别继续对我撒谎。

233 Cheltenham [ˈtʃeltənəm], Harrow [ˈhærəʊ], Cambridge [ˈkeɪmbrɪdʒ], and India [ˈɪndɪə].出生于Cheltenham，在哈罗公学、剑桥大学念的书，在印度工作过。Cheltenham，英格兰西南部Gloucester [ˈɡlɒstə]郡的一个城市，是旅游胜地和文化中心；Harrow，英国的著名公学，哈罗公学、伊顿（Eton）公学的学生大多是上层子弟；Cambridge，剑桥大学，它和牛津（Oxford）大学是英国历史最悠久的著名大学；India指此人当时是英国在印度的殖民地官员。

234 Reaction in the note taker's favor　有利于the note taker的反应，群众佩服起他来了。这是省略谓语的名词句。

235 Exclamations of ……的惊叫（惊叹）声。后面不单是接He knows all about it，而是接到etc.为止的一段话。

236 He knows all about it.　他对这些情况全都知道。it泛指人们的种种情况。

237 Told him proper. = He told him proper. him指the gentleman. proper这里作副词，= properly；是方言。

238 Hear him tell the toff him指the note taker。toff　上流人士；时髦人物；是英国口语；这里指the gentleman。

239 come from应作comes from，已见前。根据上文，这里既指“出生于（何地）”，也指“受教育于（什么学校）”、“从（什么地方）来”。

240 do this　指讲出别人的出生地和在何处上学、何处工作等。

241 for your living借以为生。living生计。

242 music hall　表演歌舞和杂耍的游艺场。在美国往往指“音乐厅”。

243 Ive thought of that.　我想到过那个。that指上文讲的到游艺场去替人算命，借以为生。

244 I shall some day. = I shall do this for my living（或for a living）at a music hall some day. some day将来有一天。

245 drop off　一个个离去。

246 He's no gentleman, he aint, to interfere with a poor girl.　他要干涉一个贫苦的女孩子，（当然）不是个上等人，他（决）不是（上等人）。no = not a. interfere干涉，是不及物动词，后面用介词with。

247 out of patience　[ˈpeɪʃəns]不耐烦的。这是习语，作定语，不能作状语。说“对……不耐烦”时，后面介词用with。

248 pushing her way rudely to the front粗鲁地挤到前面。

249 displacing　取代……的位置。

250 What on earth　究竟什么。on earth习语，加强what的语气。

251 pneumownia [njʊˈmaʊnɪə] = pneumonia [njuːˈməʊnɪə].肺炎。土音把[əʊ]念作[aʊ]。

252 any longer　再……下去；是习语，用于否定、疑问或假定。

253 making a note of her pronunciation of 'monia'　把她说pneumonia时“monia”的发音记录下来。

254 Earlscourt [ˈɜːlzkɔːt]地名，所在不详；反正把pneumonia念成pneumownia是Earlscourt的土音。

255 Will you please keep your impertinent [ɪmˈpɜːtɪnənt] remarks to yourself.请你不要对别人胡说八道！Will you please...原是客气的问句形式，但这里却是不客气的命令。keep your remarks to yourself不要说出口；不要对人说。impertinent不恰当的；无礼的。

256 say that out loud　大声说出（Earlscourt）那个地名。

257 I didnt mean to = I didnt mean to say that out loud.我并不是有意大声说出那个来。

258 I beg your pardon.我请你原谅；对不起。这句话是常用的口语，在听不清对方说什么，要求再说一遍时，也有此话，有时简略作Beg pardon。

259 Your mother's Epsom　[ˈepsəm]你母亲是Epsom人。Epsom在伦敦之南的城市，其西南郊外有大赛马场，著名的Derby赛马就在此举行。

260 curious　稀奇的；难以理解的。这里不作“好奇的”解。

261 was brought up被抚养大；被教育。

262 Largelady [ˈlaːdʒˌleɪdi] Park所在地不详。

263 uproariously [ʌpˈrɔːrɪəsli] amused感到有趣得大声嘻嘻哈哈起来；哄然失笑。

264 What a devil [ˈdevəl] of a name （Largelady Park）是多怪的一个名字呀！a devil of a ...是习语，表示“讨厌”、“奇怪”、“有趣”，其中的devil（原义“魔鬼”）没有具体意义。

265 Excuse me　原谅我。因为他脱口而出用了devil这个不文雅的词，有失礼貌，所以请人原谅。

266 Dont dare speak to me　别放肆对我说话。dare有“胆大妄为”的意思。

267 please, please　表示“劝阻”、“安慰”等意，犹如说：“好了，好了。”

268 repudiates [rɪˈpjuːdɪeɪts] her拒绝她；不听母亲的劝阻。

269 We should be so grateful to you, sir, if you found us a cab.　这是虚拟语气。found us a cab = would find us a cab.注意found可接双宾语。

270 produces　拿出。

271 thank you.　The mother看见the note taker拿出哨子替她叫车子，所以谢谢他。

272 blows a piercing blast [blaːst]吹出尖锐刺耳的哨子声。blast喇叭、汽笛或哨子等的声音。

273 knowed　早知道，是knew之讹。

274 plain-clothes [ˈpleɪnkləʊðz] copper便衣警察。

275 police whistle警笛。

276 sporting whistle裁判员或教练员用的哨子。

277 preoccupied with　被……缠住，被……迷住。

278 wounded [ˈwuːndɪd] feelings被伤害的感情；认为被冤枉的感觉。

279 as any lady's　= as any lady's character to her.

280 I dont know whether youve noticed it; but...　= I dont know whether you have noticed it that ...英美人常在这样的语气中用but，如： I am sorry, but I cant accept it.对不起，我不能接受。

281 So it has.　= Yes, it (= the rain) has stopped.雨是停了。这里也可承上文说So it did = Yes, it stopped two minutes ago.注意：这和倒装形式So has it等涵义不同，如：He has learnt it; so has she.他学会了，她也学会了。

282 And us losing our time listening to your silliness而我们却浪费时间（在这）听你胡说。us不可改作we。

283 Anwell　是Hanwell [ˈhænwəl]的讹读，土话中不但常脱落[h]音，且有时会在没有h的元音前凭空加上[h]的音，如Alex讹读作[ˈhælɪks]。

284 Hanwell　在伦敦西郊，有疯人院。作者用此地名，可能有取笑the note taker是疯子之意。H斜体，表示说话的人强调[h]音，纠正前面说话人的发音错误。

285 affecting great distinction of speech　做作地发音非常清晰。

286 Thenk you Thank you之讹。

287 teacher　用teacher是因为the note taker纠正了the sarcastic bystander的发音之故，含有讽刺之意。

288 Haw haw [ˈhɔːˈhɔː]伦敦某些上流人士的笑声。

289 So long再见。正常用于极熟的亲友间。

290 touches his hat举手到帽檐致敬。

291 mock respect假装的敬意。mock假装的。常有揶揄之意。

292 it　承上文，指being frightened。

293 But the cab –　可是出租车（就不叫了吗）？

294 pitying herself in murmurs　嘀嘀咕咕地自觉可怜。

295 Hard enough for her to live without being worrited and chivied.　她不受烦扰，不受折磨，生活已够苦恼（再要受到烦扰和困惑，更无法生活了）。Hard是It is hard之略；it是虚词，引起同位语to live ...。worrited是worried之讹。chivied，其原形动词是chivy [ˈtʃɪvi]，也作chivvy或chevy [ˈtʃevi]，作“折磨”解，是英国用语。

296 How do you do it, if I may ask?　假如我可以问的话，（我要请问）你如何做到（说出各人来自什么地方）的？这里的if是特殊用法。类似的例子如： We met once at a party, if you remember.你如果记得的话，（你该记得）我们有一次在聚会上见过面。“见过面”是客观事实，并不是假如他记得才见过面，假如他不记得就没有见过面。if的这种用法很普遍。

297 Simply phonetics.　= It's simply phonetics.或I do it simply by phonetics.名词句。

298 The science of speech是phonetics的同位语，独立成为一个名词句。

299 Happy is the man　是The man is happy的倒装形式，一方面着重Happy的语气，一方面使the man紧跟后面的定语从句who can make a living by his hobby。

300 spot an Irishman [ˈaɪərɪʃmən] or a Yorkshireman [ˈjɔːkʃəmən] by his brogue [brəʊɡ]从土音中确定（说话的人）是爱尔兰人或约克郡人。约克郡（York或Yorkshire）位于英吉利东北部。brogue爱尔兰土音；（泛指）土音。

301 I can place any man within six miles.　我能说出任何人的出生地（或生活的地方），误差不出六英里。place这里是及物动词，作“说出……的位置”、“确定”解。

302 Sometimes within two streets.　这是省略句，承上句，= Sometimes I can place him within two streets in London.

303 Ought to be ashamed of himself, unmanly [ʌnˈmænli] coward [ˈkaʊəd]! Ought前省去主语He, = He ought; unmanly coward是He的同位语，也可把全句理解为倒装句，= The unmanly coward ought to be ashamed of himself.ashamed of himself为他自己害臊。注意ashamed后用of。unmanly coward字面意义是“没有男子气的胆小鬼”，这里称“可鄙的人”。

304 But is there a living in that?　可是在那（说出各人来自什么地方）里面有生计（可寻）吗？能以此为生吗？

305 Quite a fat one.　= There's quite a fat living in that.在那个行当里有大好的生计。fat修饰living，犹如汉语中“肥差”、“肥缺”中的“肥”字。quite a也可说a quite，口语中多说quite a。

306 an age of up-starts [ˈʌpstaːts]暴发户时代。upstart作动词时读[ʌpˈstaːt]，作“（使）突然跃起”，“使崛起”解。

307 Kentish Town　伦敦西北部的工业区。

308 Park Lane已见前。

309 drop Kentish Town　改掉Kentish Town的口音。

310 give themselves away　（说出土音）露出马脚。

311 Let him mind his own business and leave a poor girl –　him指the note taker。leave a poor girl –话未说完，后面该有alone; leave ... alone是习语，是“让……去”，“不管”，“不干预”的意思。

312 explosively　爆发似地；大发雷霆地。

313 Woman用woman作称呼是极粗野无礼的。

314 boohooing [buːˈhuːɪŋ]哭闹。boohoo作名词时读[ˈbuːhuː]，见前注。

315 or else　否则；要不然。与or同义，语气较重。

316 seek the shelter of some other place of worship找个另外的教堂去躲避（不要在这儿St. Paul教堂前胡闹）。place of worship做礼拜的地方；教堂。

317 if I like　= if I like to be here.

318 same as you　正规英语是the same as you。

319 has no right to be anywhere　任何地方都没有权利待。不作“无权到处去，而只能在某些地方”解。

320 the divine [dɪˈvaɪn] gift of articulate [ɑːˈtɪkjələt] speech神圣天赋的声音，清楚的语言。这个词组和上面a soul同属介词with的宾语。

321 native language　本国语言。

322 the language of Shakespear [ˈʃeɪkspɪə] and Milton [ˈmɪltən] and The Bible莎士比亚、弥尔顿和《圣经》的语言。这种语言被认为是英国语言的典范。Shakespear，通常作Shakespeare; William Shakespeare (1564–1616)是英国戏剧家兼诗人。John Milton (1608–1674)是英国诗人，《失乐园》（Paradise Lost）的作者。The Bible，此处指1611年承英王James一世之旨英译出版的《圣经》。A and B and C比普通说A, B, and C语气强。

323 crooning like a bilious [ˈbɪlɪəs] pigeon像生气的鸽子那样叽里咕噜。crooning原义是“低调哼（消沉的）调子”。

324 overwhelmed　[ˌəʊvəˈwelmd]被（the note taker的一通训斥）压倒；弄得不知所措。

325 in mingled wonder and deprecation [ˌdeprɪˈkeɪʃən]在惊愕和反抗相混杂的心情下。deprecation不以为然；反对。

326 Ah-ah-ah-ow-ow-ow-oo!　在无奈中发出的呼叫声，没有具体意义。

327 whipping out his book　倏地拿出他的笔记簿来。这里book = notebook。

328 Heavens!天哪！啊呀！也作Good（或Great, Gracious）Heavens，一般说，heavens指“天空”，常用大写开首的heaven指“天堂”、“上帝”。但Heavens这个感叹语中用复数。

329 What a sound! = what a sound it is!

330 reproducing her vowels　重新读出她的元音。模仿她的Ah-ah-ah-ow-ow-ow-oo的声音。

331 tickled　原义是“被使觉得痒”，转义为“被逗得发笑”。

332 laughing in spite of herself不由自主地大笑起来。

333 creature　人（含有怜爱或轻蔑之意）。

334 Kerbstone English街头英语；俚俗的英语。kerbstone见前注。

335 keep her in the gutter to the end of her days　使她终身颠沛街头。gutter街沟，也作“贫民区”、“贫民窟”解。

336 pass that girl off as a duchess　让那个姑娘冒充公爵夫人。duchess也指“女公爵”。pass ... off as ...使……冒充……，是习语。这里的as也可用for。

337 garden party　游园会。

338 get her a place as lady's maid给她找一个伺候贵妇人的女仆的差事。 lady's maid在贵妇人身边主要侍候梳妆的女侍；这里作为一种职位，放在as（作为……身份；担任）后面，所以省略冠词。

339 shop assistant　店员。这是英国说法，在美国称(store) clerk，或salesman、saleswoman。

340 What's that you say?　= what's that that you say?第一个that指上面the note taker说的话；第二个that是关系代词，是从句中say的宾语；作宾语的关系代词可以省略。

341 Yes, you squashed [skwɒʃt] cabbage leaf, you disgrace to the noble architecture [ˈɑːkɪtektʃə] of these columns, you incarnate [ɪnˈkɑːnət] insult to the English language: I could pass you off as the Queen of Sheba [ˈʃiːbə].前面三个you是呼唤语（vocative），cabbage leaf、disgrace和insult分别为它们的同位语，体现了说话人激动和咄咄逼人的气势。全句直译为：“是啊，你这压烂了的菜瓣皮，你这使高贵的廊柱建筑丢脸的东西，你这对英国语言而言活生生的污辱——我可以让你冒充示巴的女王。disgrace丢脸；丢脸的人。 these columns指St. Paul教堂的廊柱，剧中人在这些廊柱间躲雨。incarnate化身的；人体化的；这里如作动作“赋予形体”、“体现”语法上也可通，但与全句语气不符；注意如作动词，重音在前，读[ˈɪnkɑːneɪt]，也有读[ɪnˈkɑːneɪt]的。the Queen of Sheba 《旧约圣经·列王纪上》所载亚拉伯西南部示巴的女王，曾朝觐以色列王所罗门（Solomon）。

342 a student of Indian dialects　[ˈdaɪəlekts]研究印度方言的人。student一般指大、中学生（不指小学生，小学生称pupil），但也指研究某项学问的人，甚至可能是专家、学者，像这里的gentleman就是后者。

343 and – and后面话没有说完，就被打断了。

344 Colonel [ˈkəːnəl] Pickering [ˈpɪkərɪŋ] Pickering上校。

345 the author of Spoken Sanscrit　[ˈsænskrɪt]《梵语口语》的作者。照例书名应作斜体或加引号，但萧伯纳常用正体，也不加引号。Sanscrit = Sanskirt. （印度的）梵语；梵文。

346 Henry Higgins　[ˈhɪgɪnz]本剧主人公之一，对卖花女进行语言训练，使其成为“贵妇人”，犹如希腊神话中雕刻出一个活美人的Pygmalion，所以剧名为Pygmalion（见前言）。Higgins's [ˈhɪgɪnzɪz] Universal Alphabet [ˈælfəbet] 《希氏通用字母表》，书名。

347 27A Wimpole [ˈwɪnpəʊl] Street英美习惯，门牌号码写在前，街名在后。

348 the Carlton　[ˈkaːltən]指Carlton Club。是英国保守党的俱乐部，1832年由威灵顿（Wellington）公爵创立。

349 have a jaw over some supper一面吃点晚饭，一面谈谈。jaw喋喋不休地说话；是口语。some supper用some，有“随便吃点什么”的意思；注意前面用over，同样的用例如“over a cup of tea”。

350 Right you are　= You are right.是口语，= Right!或Right oh!，都是表示同意、赞成。

351 short for my lodging　[ˈlɒdʒɪŋ]没有足够的钱付房钱；注意用for。

352 mendacity　[menˈdæsəti]虚假；说谎。卖花女刚才说能兑开half-a-crown（两先令六便士的硬币，见前注），怎么现在又说付不出房钱，分明在说谎。

353 Liar [ˈlaɪə]说谎的人。这里可以当作是呼唤语，也可当是You are a liar之略。

354 You ought to be stuffed with nails, you ought.　你该被塞满一肚子的钉子。肚子里塞满钉子，当然是活不成了，所以也就是“该死”的意思。句末重复you ought，是加强语气。

355 Take the whole blooming basket for sixpence.　（你）出六便士把整篮（花）拿去（买去）。blooming见前注。

356 it　指教堂的时钟敲起的钟声。

357 Pharisaic [ˌfærɪˈseɪik] want of charity法利塞人般没有慈悲心的。法利塞人（Pharisee [ˈfærɪsiː]）是犹太教的一派，《圣经》中称他们是言行不一的伪善的人。

358 A reminder　= It is a reminder.教堂的时钟是提醒我（应该慈善）的信号。

359 raises his hat举帽（脱帽）致敬。

360 a couple　[ˈkʌpl]一对，即“两个”，但口语中常泛指“两三个”，甚至“几个”。

361 florins　[ˈflɒrɪnz]两先令的银币。

362 half-sovereign　[ˈhaːfˈsɒvrɪn]半英镑的金币。这使卖花女大为惊奇，所以“哇哇”地大叫起来。

363 Got one　= I've got one或I got one. one指a taxicab。

364 And left me with a cab on my hands!　而把一辆出租车留在我手上了。And紧接上面卖花女说她们去乘公共汽车而言，所以后面left前的主语也省去了。leave somebody with something是“把某物留下给某人”的意思。on my hands由我照管（或处理），有“棘手”之意，这是习语；比较另一习语on hand：即待处理的，手头现有的；在美国还作“即将发生”、“在场”等解。注意两个习语中hand的单复数。

365 Damnation [dæmˈneɪʃn]!动词damn的名词形式，这里作感叹词，是“该死！”的意思。

366 grandeur　[ˈgrændʒə]庄严；伟大；富丽堂皇。

367 young man用以称呼人，犹言“小伙子”。

368 sails off　昂然走去。sail作“傲慢（或潇洒）地行走”解，常用于女人。

369 puts his hand behind him and holds the door firmly shut against her手伸到背后，紧紧按住关着的门，不让她上车（因为看她是穷苦的卖花女，料她乘不起出租车）。shut这里是过去分词，作宾语the door的补足语。

370 aint no object　是“双重否定”，= is no object. no object不成问题；是习语，尤其用于广告文字中，如Money no object：报酬不计。

371 Charlie [ˈtʃaːli]这是一个极普通的男子名，卖花女并不认识the driver，是随口乱叫的，同她前面叫别人Freddy一样。

372 Give it here.　把它（这一篮花）交给我，放在这里。

373 Tuppence extra.另加两便士（车钱）。Tuppence = Twopence.都读[ˈtʌpəns]。

374 Liza　[ˈlaɪzə]卖花女的名；她的姓是Doolittle。Liza是Elizabeth [ɪˈlɪzəbəθ]的昵称。这里突然不用the flower girl而用Liza，是表示她摆起架子，丢掉了卖花女的“低贱”身份。

375 dont want nobody to see it = don't want anybody to see it或want nobody to see it. it指一篮花。

376 dazedly　[ˈdeɪzɪdli]被弄得头昏眼花地；茫然。

377 Where to?　= Where are you going (to)?或Where am I to take you to?

378 Bucknam Pellis　[ˈbʌknəmˈpelɪs]是括号中Buckingham Palace [ˈbʌkɪŋəmˈpælɪs]的误读。白金汉宫，是现今英国王宫。

379 What d'ye (= do you) mean – Bucknam Pellies?你说白金汉宫是什么意思？一个卖花女说要到王宫去，自然使人大惑不解。

380 Green Park　英国王家花园，是个小丘，树林特别青翠茂盛。

381 the King指英王乔治六世，1936–1952年在位；萧伯纳改写出版电影版是在1941年，这一段文字是在1941年改写本中插入的。

382 Dont let me keep standing there.　别让我使你老站在那里。我把你叫来的却难以处置的车子乘走了，你可以去了。

383 Whats this about Bucknam Pellis?　你要去白金汉宫，这是怎么回事？下面Liza的回答很清楚：她说要去白金汉宫是吹牛，是说给Freddy听的。

384 I havnt none.　= I have none. = I have no business at all at Bucknam Pellis (Buckingham Palace).

385 that　指I have no business at all at Buckingham Palace。

386 wheres home　承上文drive me home开车送我回家而问，= where is "home" =where is your home。

387 Angel[ˈeɪndʒl] Court, Drury [ˈdrʊəri] Lane, next Meiklejohn's [ˈmɪkldʒɒnz] oil shop （家在）Drury Lane路上的Angel Court，就在Meiklejohn油店隔壁。英美地址的说法，一般是“门牌，街道，城镇，国家”，先小后大。Angel Court不可考，可能是虚构的。Drury Lane伦敦街名，旧时贵族住宅区，著名的大剧场Drury Lane Theatre所在地。Meiklejohn's oil shop英美企业常用企业主的姓氏为名。next也可说next to；单用next，是介词；next to中的next是形容词，有时也作副词，如：He sits next to me.

388 That sounds more like it　那（指上文Liza所说的地址）听起来（比起先说的白金汉王宫）像话多了；那个地方才比较像是你这种人住的了。

389 Judy [ˈdʒuːdi]普通的女子名，是Taximan随便乱叫的，正像Liza对Taximan乱叫Charlie一样。

390 one of them (the two shops) Meiklejohn's oil shop them后省略being，是独立结构（absolute construction），是伴随说明的短语。

391 Eliza　[ɪˈlaɪzə]即Liza，同是Elizabeth的昵称。

392 taximeter　[ˈtæksɪˌmiːtə] （出租车凭以计算车费的）计程器。

393 Cant you read?你看不懂计程器吗？read读数。

394 Two minutes or ten: it's all the same.　ten = ten minutes. it指two minutes or ten。all the same习语，作“（虽然……）还是、依然”解。这里是不管时间是两分钟或十分钟，车费都一样。注意：不能因为这里是说两者相同而用both the same。

395 I dont call it right　我并不认为这（两分钟和十分钟车费相同）是合理的=我认为不合理。但是不说I call it not right。

396 Ever been in a taxi before?　= Have you ever been in a taxi before?你以前乘过出租汽车吗？

397 Hundreds and thousands of times　几百几千次；许许多多次。省略主语和谓语动词，= I have been in a taxi hundreds and thousands of times.注意：这里hundreds和thousands用复数，但several hundred（或thousand）times中的hundred和thousand直接修饰中心词times，用单数。

398 Good for you　你干得好；你运气好。是口语，这里有讥讽的意思。

399 Keep the shilling （看你苦恼）还是留着这一先令（别付车钱了）吧。（有轻视的意味。）

400 with best love from all at home　带着我全家至诚的爱。这类的话也常用作书信结尾的问候语。

401 Good luck! = I wish you good luck!常用在告别时，表示祝愿。其实Good-bye也表示祝愿，它是从God be with you （愿上帝与你同在，即保佑你）衍变过来的。

402 Impidence　是Impudence [ˈɪmpjədəns]的误读；意为“大胆妄为；无礼”，指Taximan对穷人的轻视态度。

403 trudges [ˈtrʌdʒɪz] up the alley [ˈæli]沿着巷子吃力地走进去。up这里是介词。

404 hanging[ˈhæŋɪŋ] loose in the damp places在这几个潮湿处快要脱落下来。不及物动词hang的现在分词修饰wall paper（糊墙纸）。

405 fashion plate of ladies' dresses　一件件女服的时式样板。fashion plate有时也借喻作“服装时髦的人”解。

406 all wildly beyond poor Eliza's means　都远远超过贫穷的Eliza的资力（根本买不起）。从语法上讲，all是代词，指dresses，整个词组是省略了分词being的一个独立结构，即= all being wildly beyond poor Eliza's means。也可说all修饰beyond，即“（不是某一件衣裳超过，而是）全部都超过（她的资力）”。beyond ... means修饰dresses。wildly相差极远，犹如上海话说“野豁豁”。

407 both torn from newspapers两者（portrait和fashion plate）都是从报纸上扯下来的。结构同上条注。可以认为both后省略being；也可认为both修饰分词torn, torn修饰portrait和fashion plate。

408 are pinned up　被用大头针钉起来。主语是portrait和fashion plate。

409 hangs　悬挂；这里是不及物动词；它也可作及物动词，如hang a birdcage in the window；它的过去式和过去分词都是hung。hang作“绞死”、“吊死”解时，过去式和过去分词都是hanged，间或也用hung。

410 its tenant [ˈtenənt]鸟笼的居住者（指原来关在笼中的那只鸟）。

411 memorial　[məˈmɔːrɪəl]纪念物。鸟死笼空，只好把空笼子挂着作纪念。注意：亲友间赠送的纪念品不能说是memorial，应该说keepsake。

412 visible [ˈvɪzəbl] luxuries [ˈlʌkʃəriz] （眼前）看得见的（非生活必需的）奢侈品。把报上扯下的画像和空鸟笼称为“奢侈品”是幽默地说明卖花女的贫穷。看得见的“奢侈品”是这些，另外不在眼前、看不见的“奢侈品”，也可想而知。

413 the rest其他不属“奢侈品”的东西；生活必需品。the rest可作单数，也可作复数，视其所指是单数或复数而定。这里后面的表语是minimum（单数），故作单数。如拿后面具体列举的许多家具作表语，the rest就作复数，如：The rest are a bed, a packing case, a chair and table, etc.

414 irreducible minimum [ˈmɪnɪməm] of poverty's needs贫苦（生活）必须的无可减少的最小极限；即使生活贫苦也缺一不可的必需品。注意：这里needs用复数。

415 wretched　[ˈretʃɪd] bed破旧不堪的床。它后面的packing case、chair and table等都是进一步说明上面the rest所指的具体内容，也可说是the rest的同位语。

416 all sorts of coverings that have any warmth in them各式各样只要能保暖的覆盖物（如被头）。all sorts of也可说all sort of, = all kind(s) of。have any warmth照字面讲，不通，因覆盖物本身里面没有温度，故应理解为“（对人体）有保暖（御寒）作用”。

417 draped packing case　遮着布的装货（粗板）箱。

418 a basin and jug　面盆和水壶。这两样东西作为一套用具，故合用一个冠词。

419 a little looking glass over it箱子上面（放着）一面小镜子。looking glass和basin and jug并列，是with的介词宾语。over it在箱子的上方（墙上）。

420 a chair and table　一对桌椅。作为一组，故合用一个冠词。和bed及packing case并列。

421 the refuse of some suburban [səˈbɜːbən] kitchen不知哪家郊区（人家）厨房（不要而丢掉）的垃圾。这个名词词组是a chair and table的同位语。注意refuse在这里是名词，读[ˈrefjuːs]；作“拒绝”解的动词读[rɪˈfjuːz]。

422 an American alarum [əˈleərəm] clock美国货闹钟。“闹钟”在美国称alarm clock，现在在英国也多称alarm clock。

423 unused　[ˌʌnˈjuːzd]不用的。这个词也作“没有用过的”，即“新的”解，这里不是此义。另外，它又与to连用作“不习惯于……的”解，读[ʌnˈjuːst]，如：He is unused to society.他不善于交际。

424 the whole整个房间。前面讲了许多零星家具用品，现在讲整个房间，故用the whole。

425 lighted被照亮着；是过去分词，可理解为因在说明词中，所以前面省略了was，也可理解为前面省略了being, the whole being lighted ... slot meter是个独立结构。

426 a gas lamp with a penny in the slot meter在自动收费计（的投钱口）里投入了一枚一便士硬币（因而点燃着）的煤气灯。

427 Rent: four shillings a week.　= The rent is four shillings a week.在说明词中可以用公式的写法，省略动词；Rent前冠词也省略。

428 chronically weary　[ˈwɪəri]极度疲劳。chronically作“剧烈地”、“顽固地”解，是英国用法。

429 sits主语是Eliza。

430 her new riches　她新得来的财富，即Higgins扔给她的一把钱币。

431 planning what to do with them计划着怎样处置它们（这些钱币）。what to do with是习语，除“怎样处置”、“怎样对付”外，还可作“怎样与……相处”、“怎样忍受”解。

432 the gas goes out　煤气灯熄灭。投入一便士，使用时间已结束。

433 sensation　激动的感觉。

434 put in another penny without grudging [ˈgrʌdʒɪŋ] it毫不吝惜地再投入一枚一便士钱币（使煤气灯继续点下去）。it指another penny。

435 This prodigal [ˈprɒdɪgəl] mood does not extinguish her gnawing [ˈnɔːɪŋ] sense of the need for economy sufficiently to prevent her from calculating这种（不在乎一便士的）挥霍心情不足以完全消除她想到需要节俭的痛苦意识，（不足以）使她不盘算着……。sufficiently修饰extinguish; does not extinguish sufficiently，消除得还不够。gnawing原义“咬的”，转义“令人痛苦的”。

436 sitting up　坐着不就寝。

437 without a fire不生火；没有生火炉。

438 adds them to the miscellaneous [ˌmɪsəˈleinjəs] bedclothes把它们（shawl和skirt）加盖在杂七杂八的被褥上面。比较：add to和add ... to的不同用法，例如：This adds to the expense.这要增加费用。

439 gets into bed　上床。注意：和go to bed以及前三行的in bed一样，bed前都不用冠词。

440 without any further change　没有任何其他的变化；只踢掉一双鞋子，不另换什么睡衣之类。

第二幕　剧情提要

卖花女Liza昨天得到Higgins扔给她的一把钱，以为出得起给语音学专家Higgins教授的学费，今天一早就登门来请他授课。Higgins问她打算付多少钱。她说：“人家请真正的法国先生教法语，一个小时给一先令半；你教我本国语言，我至多给一先令。”Higgins替她计算，她出一先令抵得上百万富翁出六、七十镑！当然，他不要她的钱，但是他要用她作试验。他说能在三至六个月里使她冒充公爵夫人参加大使馆的招待会。Pickering和他打赌：如果试验成功，他愿承担这次试验的一切费用。

Liza的父亲Doolittle得知Liza在这里，找上门来。他是个倒垃圾的工人，愤世嫉俗，玩世不恭。他说：“两位先生看上了我的女儿，可不能白占我便宜。”他要Higgins给他五个英镑。Higgins给他十镑，他只要五镑。他说：“五镑正够和老伴去吃喝玩乐一番，钱一多，就舍不得乱花，乐不起来了。”所谓“老伴”，算是Liza的后娘，却一直没肯和他正式结婚。Doolittle发表了一大套言论，Higgins认为他大可当个牧师或议员。

Higgins开始了对Liza非常严格的语音训练。Liza既是他的学生，又成了任他呼来唤去的女管家。

ACT II

Next day at 11 a.m. Higgins's laboratory1 in Wimpole Street2. It is a room on the first floor3, looking on the street4, and was meant for5 the drawing room. The double doors6 are in the middle of the back wall; and persons entering7 find in the corner to their right8 two tall file cabinets9 at right angles to one another10 against the walls. In this corner stands a flat writing-table11, on which are a phonograph12, a laryngoscope13, a row of tiny organ pipes with a bellows14, a set of lamp chimneys for singing flames with burners attached to a gas plug in the wall by an indiarubber tube15, several tuning-forks16 of different sizes, a life-size image of half a human head, shewing in section the vocal organs17, and a box containing a supply of wax cylinders18 for the phonograph.

Further down the room19, on the same side, is a fireplace, with a comfortable leather-covered easy-chair at the side of the hearth nearest the door20, and a coal-scuttle21. There is a clock on the mantelpiece22. Between the fireplace and the phonograph table is a stand for newspapers23.

On the other side of the central door, to the left of the visitor, is a cabinet of shallow drawers. On it is a telephone and the telephone directory24. The corner beyond, and most of the side wall, is occupied by a grand piano25, with the keyboard at the end furthest from the door26, and a bench for the player27 extending the full length of the keyboard28. On the piano is a dessert dish heaped with fruit and sweets, mostly chocolates29.

The middle of the room is clear30. Besides the easy-chair, the piano bench, and two chairs at the phonograph table, there is one stray chair31. It stands near the fireplace. On the walls, engravings32: mostly Piranesis33 and mezzotint portraits34. No paintings.35

Pickering is seated at the table, putting down some cards36 and a tuning-fork which he has been using. Higgins is standing up near him37, closing two or three file drawers which are hanging out38. He appears in the morning light as a robust, vital, appetizing sort of man39 of forty or thereabouts40, dressed41 in a professional-looking42 black frock-coat43 with a white linen collar and black silk tie. He is of the energetic, scientific type44, heartily, even violently interested45 in everything that can be studied as a scientific subject, and careless about himself and other people, including their feelings46. He is, in fact, but for his years and size47, rather like a very impetuous48 baby 'taking notice' eagerly and loudly49, and requiring almost as much watching50 to keep him out of unintended mischief51. His manner varies52 from genial bullying when he is in a good humor53 to stormy petulance when anything goes wrong54; but he is so entirely frank and void of malice55 that he remains likeable even in his least reasonable moments56.

HIGGINS [as he shuts the last drawer] Well, I think thats the whole show57.

PICKERING It's really amazing. I havnt taken half of it in58, you know.

HIGGINS Would you like to go over59 any of it again?

PICKERING [rising and coming to the fireplace, where he plants himself60 with his back to the fire] No, thank you: not now61. I'm quite done up62 for this morning.

HIGGINS [following him, and standing beside him on his left] Tired of63 listening to sounds?

PICKERING Yes, It's a fearful strain64. I rather fancied myself65 because I can pronounce twenty-four distinct vowel sounds; but your hundred and thirty beat me66. I cant hear a bit of67 difference between most of them68.

HIGGINS [chuckling, and going over to the piano to eat sweets] Oh, that comes with practice69. You hear no difference at first; but you keep on listening, and presently you find theyre all as different as A from B70. [Mrs Pearce71 looks in: she is Higgins's housekeeper72.] Whats the matter?73

MRS PEARCE [hesitating, evidently perplexed] A young woman74 asks to see you, sir.

HIGGINS A young woman! What does she want?

MRS PEARCE Well, sir, she says youll be glad to see her when you know what she's come about75. She's quite a common girl76, sir. Very common indeed. I should have sent her away, only I thought77 perhaps you wanted her to talk into your machines78. I hope Ive not done wrong79; but really you see such queer people sometimes – youll excuse me, I'm sure80, sir –

HIGGINS Oh, thats all right, Mrs Pearce. Has she an interesting accent81?

MRS PEARCE Oh, something dreadful82, sir, really83. I dont know how you can take an interest in it84.

HIGGINS [to Pickering] Lets have her up85. Shew her up86, Mrs Pearce. [He rushes across to his working table87 and picks out a cylinder to use88 on the phonograph.]

MRS PEARCE [only half resigned to it89] Very well, sir. It's for you to say90.[She goes downstairs.]

HIGGINS This is rather a bit of luck. I'll shew you how I make records91. We'll set her talking92; and I'll take it down first in Bell's Visible Speech93; then in broad Romic94; and then we'll get her on the phonograph95 so that you can turn her on as often as you like96 with the written transcript97 before you.

MRS PEARCE [returning] This is the young woman, sir.

The flower girl enters in state98. She has a hat with three ostrich feathers99, orange, sky-blue, and red. She has a nearly clean apron, and the shoddy coat has been tidied a little. The pathos of this deplorable figure100, with its innocent vanity and conse-quential air101, touches Pickering, who has already straightened himself in the presence of Mrs Pearce102. But as to Higgins, the only distinction he makes between men and women103 is that when he is neither bullying nor exclaiming to the heavens against some feather-weight cross104, he coaxes women as a child coaxes its nurse when it wants to get anything out of her105.

HIGGINS [brusquely106, recognizing107 her with unconcealed disappointment108, and at once, babylike, making an intolerable grievance of it109] Why110, this is the girl I jotted down111 last night. She's no use112: I've got all the records I want of the Lisson Grove lingo113; and I'm notgoing to waste another cylinder on it. [to the girl] Be off with you114: I dont want you.

THE FLOWER GIRL Dont you be so saucy.115 You aint heard what I come for yet116. [to Mrs Pearce, who is waiting at the door for further instructions117] Did you tell him I come in a taxi?118

MRS PEARCE Nonsense119, girl120! What do you think a gentleman like Mr Higgins cares what you came in?121

THE FLOWER GIRL Oh, we are proud!122 He aint above giving lessons123, not him124: I heard him say so. Well, I aint come here to ask for any compliment125; and if my money's not good enough I can go elsewhere126.

HIGGINS Good enough for what?

THE FLOWER GIRL Good enough for yə-oo127. Now you know, dont you? I'm come to have lessons128, I am. And to pay for em tə-oo129: make no mistake130.

HIGGINS [stupent131] Well!!!132 [recovering his breath with a gasp133] What do you expect me to say to you?134

THE FLOWER GIRL Well135, if you was a gentleman, you might ask me to sit down136, I think. Dont I tell you I'm bringing you business137?

HIGGINS Pickering: shall we ask this baggage138 to sit down, or139 shallwe throw her out of the window?

THE FLOWER GIRL [running away in terror140 to the piano, where she turns at bay141] Ah-ah-oh-ow-ow-ow-oo! [wounded142 and whimpering] I wont be called a baggage when Ive offered143 to pay like any lady.

Motionless, the two men stare at her from the other side of the room, amazed.

PICKERING [gently] But what is it you want?144

THE FLOWER GIRL I want to be a lady in a flower shop stead of sellin145 at the corner of Tottenham Court Road146. But they wont take147 me unless I can talk more genteel148. He said he could teach me. Well, here I am ready to pay him – not asking any favor149– and he treats me zif I was dirt150.

MRS PEARCE How can you be such a foolish ignorant girl as to think you could afford to pay151 Mr Higgins?

THE FLOWER GIRL Why shouldnt I?152 I know what lessons cost as well as you do153; and I'm ready to pay.

HIGGINS How much?

THE FLOWER GIRL [coming back to him, triumphant154] Now youre talking!155 I thought youd come off it156 when you saw a chance ofgetting back a bit of what you chucked at me last night157. [confidentially158] Youd had a drop in159, hadnt you?

HIGGINS [peremptorily160] Sit down.

THE FLOWER GIRL Oh, if youre going to make a compliment of it –161

HIGGINS [thundering162 at her] Sit down.

MRS PEARCE [severely] Sit down, girl. Do as youre told.163

THE FLOWER GIRL Ah-ah-ah-ow-ow-oo! [She stands, half rebellious, half bewildered164.]

PICKERING [very courteous165] Wont you sit down? [He places the stray chair near the hearthrug between himself and Higgins.]

LIZA166 [coyly] Dont mind if I do.167 [She sits down. Pickering returns to the hearthrug.]

HIGGINS Whats your name?

THE FLOWER GIRL Liza Doolittle168.

HIGGINS [declaiming gravely]

Eliza, Elizabeth, Betsy and Bess169,

They went to the woods to get a bird's nes'170:

PICKERING They found a nest with four eggs in it:

HIGGINS They took one apiece171, and left three in it.

They laugh heartily at172 their own fun.

LIZA Oh, dont be silly173.

MRS PEARCE [placing herself behind Eliza's chair] You mustnt speak to the gentleman like that.

LIZA Well, why wont he speak sensible174 to me?

HIGGINS Come back to business175. How much do you propose to pay me for the lessons?

LIZA Oh, I know whats right. A lady friend of mine176 gets French lessons for eighteenpence177 an hour from a real French gentleman. Well, you wouldnt have the face to ask me the same for teaching me my own language as you would for French178; so I wont give more than a shilling. Take it or leave it.179

HIGGINS [walking up and down the room180, rattling181 his keys and his cash in his pockets] You know, Pickering, if you consider a shilling, not as a simple shilling, but as a percentage of this girl's income182, it works out as fully equivalent to183 sixty or seventy guineas184 from a millionaire185.

PICKERING How so?186

HIGGINS Figure it out.187 A millionaire has188 about L-End} £150189 a day. She earns about half-a-crown.

LIZA [haughtily] Who told you I only –

HIGGINS [continuing] She offers me two-fifths190 of her day's income for a lesson. Two-fifths of a millionaire's income for a daywould be somewhere about £60. It's handsome.191 By George192, it's enormous! it's the biggest offer193 I ever had.

LIZA [rising, terrified] Sixty pounds! What are you talking about? I never offered you sixty pounds. Where would I get –194

HIGGINS Hold your tongue195.

LIZA [weeping] But I aint got sixty pounds. Oh –

MRS PEARCE Dont cry, you silly girl. Sit down. Nobody is going to touch your money196.

HIGGINS Somebody is going to touch you, with a broomstick197, if you dont stop snivelling. Sit down.

LIZA [obeying slowly] Ah-ah-ah-ow-oo-o! One would think you was my father.198

HIGGINS If I decide to teach you, I'll be worse than two fathers to you199. Here200! [He offers her his silk handkerchief201.]

LIZA Whats this for?202

HIGGINS To wipe your eyes.203 To wipe any part of your face that feels moist204. Remember: thats your handkerchief; and thats your sleeve. Dont mistake the one for the other205 if you wish to become a lady in a shop.

Liza, utterly206 bewildered, stares helplessly207 at him.

MRS PEARCE It's no use talking to her like that208, Mr Higgins: shedoesnt understand you. Besides, youre quite wrong: she doesnt do it that way at all209. [She takes the handkerchief.]

LIZA [snatching it] Here! You give me that handkerchief.210 He gev it to me, not to you.211

PICKERING [laughing] He did.212 I think it must be regarded as her property, Mrs Pearce.

MRS PEARCE [resigning herself213] Serve you right214, Mr Higgins.

PICKERING Higgins: I'm interested. What about the ambassador's garden party?215 I'll say youre the greatest teacher alive if you make that good216. I'll bet you all the expenses of the experiment you cant do it217. And I'll pay for the lessons.

LIZA Oh, you are real good218. Thank you, Captain.

HIGGINS [tempted219, looking at her] It's almost irresistible. She's so deliciously low220 – so horribly dirty221 –

LIZA [protesting extremely] Ah-ah-ah-ah-ow-ow-oo-oo!!! I aint dirty: I washed my face and hands afore I come222, I did.

PICKERING Youre certainly not going to turn her head with flattery, Higgins.

MRS PEARCE [uneasy] Oh, dont say that, sir: theres more ways than one of turning a girl's head223; and nobody can do it better than Mr Higgins, though he may not always mean it. I dohope, sir, you wont encourage him to do anything foolish224.

HIGGINS [becoming excited as the idea grows on him225] What is life but a series of inspired follies?226 The difficulty is to find them to do. Never lose a chance: it doesnt come every day.227 I shall make a duchess of this draggletailed guttersnipe228.

LIZA [strongly deprecating this view of her229] Ah-ah-ah-ow-ow-oo!

HIGGINS [carried away230] Yes: in six months231 – in three if she has a good ear and a quick tongue232 – I'll take her anywhere and pass her off as anything233. We'll start today: now! this moment!234 Take her away and clean her, Mrs Pearce. Monkey Brand235, if it wont come off236 any other way. Is there a good fire237 in the kitchen?

MRS PEARCE [protesting] Yes; but –238

HIGGINS [storming on239] Take all her cloths off and burn them. Ring up Whiteley or somebody for new ones.240 Wrap her up in brown paper241 til242 they come.

LIZA Youre no gentleman243, youre not, to talk of such things. I'm a good girl, I am; and I know what the like of you are244, I do.

HIGGINS We want none of your Lisson Grove prudery245 here, young woman. Youve got to246 learn to behave like a duchess. Take her away, Mrs Pearce. If she gives you any trouble, wallop247 her.

LIZA [springing up and running between Pickering and Mrs Pearce for protection] No! I'll call the police, I will.

MRS PEARCE But Ive no place to put her248.

HIGGINS Put her in the dustbin249.

LIZA Ah-ah-ah-ow-ow-oo!

PICKERING Oh come250, Higgins! be reasonable.

MRS PEARCE [resolutely] You must be reasonable, Mr Higgins: really you must. You cant walk over everybody251 like this.

Higgins, thus scolded, subsides252. The hurricane is succeeded by a zephyr of amiable surprise253.

HIGGINS [with professional exquisiteness254 of modulation255] I walk over everybody256! My dear Mrs Pearce, my dear Pickering, I never had the slightest intention of walking over anyone. All I propose is that we should be kind to this poor girl. We must help her to prepare and fit herself for her new station in life257. If258 I did not express myself clearly259 it260 was because I did not wish to hurt her delicacy, or yours261.

Liza, reassured, steals back to262 her chair.

MRS PEARCE [to Pickering] Well, did you ever hear anything like that, sir?

PICKERING [laughing heartily] Never, Mrs Pearce: never.

HIGGINS [patiently] Whats the matter?

MRS PEARCE Well, the matter is, sir, that you cant take a girl up like that263 as if you were picking up a pebble on the beach.

HIGGINS Why not?264

MRS PEARCE Why not!265 But you dont know anything about her. What about her parents? She may be married.

LIZA Garn!266

HIGGINS There!267 As the girl very properly says, Garn! Married indeed!268 Dont you know that a woman of that class looks a worn out drudge of fifty a year after she's married269?

LIZA Whood marry me?270

HIGGINS [suddenly resorting to271 the most thrillingly beautiful272 low tones in his best elocutionary style273] By George, Eliza, the streets will be strewn with the bodies of men shooting themselves for your sake274 before Ive done with you275.

MRS PEARCE Nonsense, sir. You mustnt talk like that to her.

LIZA [rising and squaring herself276 determinedly] I'm going away. He's off his chump277, he is. I dont want no balmies teaching me.278

HIGGINS [wounded in his tenderest point by her insensibility to his elocution279] Oh, indeed!280 I'm mad, am I?281 Very well, Mrs Pearce: you neednt order the new clothes for her. Throw her out.

LIZA [whimpering] Nah-ow.282 You got no right to touch me.

MRS PEARCE You see now what comes of283 being saucy. [indicating the door] This way, please.284
