LIZA [almost in tears285] I didnt want no clothes. I wouldnt have taken them.286 [She throws away the handkerchief.] I can buy my own clothes.

HIGGINS [deftly retrieving the handkerchief and intercepting her on her reluctant way to the door287] Youre an ungrateful wicked288 girl. This is my return289 for offering to take you out of the gutter290 and dress you beautifully and make a lady of you.

MRS PEARCE Stop, Mr Higgins. I wont allow it. It's you that are wicked291. Go home to your parents, girl; and tell them to take better care of you.

LIZA I aint got no parents292. They told me I was big293 enough to earn my own living294 and turned me out295.

MRS PEARCE Wheres your mother?

LIZA I aint got no mother. Her that296 turned me out was my sixth stepmother297. But I done without them298. And I'm a good girl, I am.

HIGGINS Very well, then, what on earth is all this fuss about299? The girl doesnt belong to anybody – is no use to anybody but me300.[He goes to Mrs Pearce and begins coaxing.] You can adopt her301, Mrs Pearce: I'm sure a daughter would be a great amusement to you. Now dont make any more fuss. Take her downstairs; and –

MRS PEARCE But whats to become of her302? Is she to be paid anything?303 Do be304 sensible, sir.

HIGGINS Oh, pay her whatever is necessary305: put it down in the housekeeping book306. [impatiently] What on earth will she want with money? She'll have her food and her clothes. She'll only drink307 if you give her money.

LIZA [turning on him308] Oh you are a brute309. It's a lie310: nobody ever saw the sign of liquor311 on me. [to Pickering] Oh, sir: youre a gentleman: dont let him speak to me like that.

PICKERING [in good-humored remonstrance] Does it occur to you, Higgins, that312 the girl has some feelings313?

HIGGINS [looking critically at her] Oh no, I dont think so. Not any feelings that we need bother about.314 [cheerily] Have you315, Eliza?

LIZA I got my feelings same as anyone else316.

HIGGINS [to Pickering, reflectively] You see the difficulty317?

PICKERING Eh? What difficulty?

HIGGINS To get her to talk grammar318. The mere pronunciation is easy enough.

LIZA I dont want to talk grammar. I want to talk like a lady in a flower-shop.319

MRS PEARCE Will you please keep to the point, Mr Higgins.320 I want to know on what terms321 the girl is to be here. Is she to have any wages322? And what is to become of her when youve finished your teaching? You must look ahead a little323.

HIGGINS [impatiently] Whats to become of her if I leave her in the gutter? Tell me that, Mrs Pearce.

MRS PEARCE Thats her own business, not yours324, Mr Higgins.

HIGGINS Well, when Ive done with her, we can throw her back into the gutter; and then it will be her own business again; so thats all right.

LIZA Oh, youve no feeling heart325 in you: you dont care for nothing but yourself326. [She rises and takes the floor327 resolutely.] Here! Ive had enough of this.328 I'm going [making for the door329]. You ought to be ashamed of yourself, you ought.

HIGGINS [snatching a chocolate cream330 from the piano, his eyes suddenly beginning to twinkle with mischief331] Have some chocolates, Eliza.

LIZA [halting, tempted332] How do I know what might be in them333? Ive heard of girls being drugged by the like of you334.

Higgins whips out his penknife335; cuts a chocolate in two; puts one half into his mouth and bolts336 it; and offers her the other half.

HIGGINS Pledge of good faith337, Eliza. I eat one half: you eat the other338. [Liza opens her mouth to retort: he pops339 the half chocolate into it.] You shall have boxes of them, barrels of them, every day. You shall340 live on them341. Eh?342

LIZA [who has disposed of343 the chocolate after being nearly choked by it] I wouldnt have ate it344, only345 I'm too ladylike346 to take it out of my mouth.

HIGGINS Listen347, Eliza. I think you said you came in a taxi.

LIZA Well, what if I did? Ive as good a right to take a taxi as anyone else348.

HIGGINS You have349, Eliza; and in future you shall have as many taxis as you want. You shall go up and down and round the town350 in a taxi every day. Think of that, Eliza.

MRS PEARCE Mr Higgins: youre tempting the girl. It's not right. She should think of the future.

HIGGINS At her age!351 Nonsense! Time enough to think of the future when you havnt any future to think of352. No, Eliza: do as this lady does353: think of other people's futures; but never think of your own354. Think of chocolates, and taxis, and gold, and diamonds355.

LIZA No: I dont want no gold and no diamonds. I'm a good girl, I am. [She sits down again, with an attempt at dignity356.]

HIGGINS You shall remain so357, Eliza, under the care of358 Mrs Pearce. And you shall marry an officer in the Guards359, with a beautiful moustache360: the son of a marquis, who will disinherit him for marrying you361, but will relent362 when he sees your beauty and goodness –

PICKERING Excuse me, Higgins; but363 I really must interfere. Mrs Pearce is quite right. If this girl is to put herself in your hands364 for six months for an experiment in teaching, she must understand thoroughly what she's doing365.

HIGGINS How can she?366 She's incapable of367 understanding anything. Besides, do any of us understand what we are doing? If we did, would we ever do it?368

PICKERING Very clever369, Higgins; but not to the present point370. [to Eliza] Miss Doolittle371 –

LIZA [overwhelmed] Ah-ah-ow-oo!

HIGGINS There! Thats all youll get out of Eliza.372 Ah-ah-ow-oo! No use explaining.373 As a military man374 you ought to know that375. Give her her orders376: thats enough for her377. Eliza: you are to live here for the next six months, learning how to speakbeautifully, like a lady in a florist's shop. If youre good378 and do whatever youre told, you shall sleep in a proper bedroom, and have lots to eat, and money379 to buy chocolates and take rides in taxis. If youre naughty and idle you will sleep in the back kitchen among the black beetles380, and be walloped by Mrs Pearce with a broomstick. At the end of six months you shall go to Buckingham Palace in a carriage, beautifully dressed381. If the King finds out youre not a lady, you will be taken by the police to the Tower of London382, where your head will be cut off as a warning to other presumptuous383 flower girls. If you are not found out, you shall have a present of seven-and-sixpence to start life with as a lady in a shop384. If you refuse this offer you will be a most ungrateful wicked385 girl; and the angels will weep for you386. [to Pickering] Now are you satisfied, Pickering? [to Mrs Pearce] Can I put it more plainly and fairly387, Mrs Pearce?

MRS PEARCE [patiently] I think youd better let me speak to the girl properly in private388. I dont know that I can take charge of her or consent to389 the arrangement at all. Of course I know you dont mean her any harm390; but when you get what you call interested in people's accents391, you never think or care what may happen to them or you392. Come with me, Eliza.

HIGGINS Thats all right. Thank you, Mrs Pearce. Bundle her off393 to the bathroom.

LIZA [rising reluctantly and suspiciously] Youre a great bully394, you are. I wont stay here if I dont like. I wont let nobody wallop me. I never asked to go to Bucknam Palace395, I didnt. I was never in trouble with the police, not me.396 I'm a good girl –

MRS PEARCE Dont answer back397, girl. You dont understand the gentleman. Come with me. [She leads the way to the door, and holds it open for Eliza.]

LIZA [as she goes out] Well, what I say is right. I wont go near the King, not if I'm going to have my head cut off398. If I'd known what I was letting myself in for, I wouldnt have come here.399 I always been a good girl; and I never offered to say a word400 to him; and I dont owe him nothing401; and I dont care402; and I wont be put upon403; and I have my feelings the same as anyone else404 –

Mrs Pearce shuts the door; and Eliza's plaints are no longer audible405.

Eliza is taken upstairs to the third floor greatly to her surprise406; for she expected to be taken down to the scullery407. There408 Mrs Pearce opens a door and takes her into a spare bedroom.

MRS PEARCE I will have to put you here. This will be your bedroom.

LIZA O-h, I couldnt sleep here, missus. It's too good for the likes of me.409 I should be afraid to touch anything. I aint a duchess yet410, you know.

MRS PEARCE You have got to make yourself as clean as the room: then you wont be afraid of it. And you must call me Mrs Pearce, not missus411. [She throws open the door412 of the dressingroom, now modernized as a bathroom413.]

LIZA Gawd414! Whats this?415 Is this where you wash clothes? Funny sort of copper I call it.416

MRS PEARCE It is not a copper. This is where we wash ourselves417, Eliza, and where I am going to wash you.

LIZA You expect me to get into that and wet myself all over! Not me.418 I should catch my death419. I knew a woman did it420 every Saturday night; and she died of it.

MRS PEARCE Mr Higgins has the gentlemen's bathroom downstairs; and he has a bath every morning, in cold water421.

LIZA Ugh!422 He's made of iron, that man.423

MRS PEARCE If you are to sit with him and the Colonel and be taught424 you will have to do the same425. They wont like the smell of you if you dont426. But you can have the water as hot as you like. There are two taps: hot and cold.

LIZA [weeping] I couldnt427. I dursnt428. It's not natural: it would kill me. Ive never had a bath in my life: not what youd call a proper one429.

MRS PEARCE Well, dont you want to be clean and sweet and decent, like a lady? You know you cant be a nice girl inside if youre a dirty slut outside430.

LIZA Boohoo!!!

MRS PEARCE Now stop crying and go back into your room and take off all your clothes. Then wrap yourself in this [taking down a gown from its peg and handing it to her] and come back to me. I will get the bath ready431.

LIZA [all tears432] I cant.433 I wont.434 I'm not used to it.435 Ive never took off all my clothes before. It's not right: it's not decent.

MRS PEARCE Nonsense, child. Dont you take off all your clothes every night when you go to bed?436

LIZA [amazed] No. Why should I?437 I should catch my death438. Of course I take off my skirt.

MRS PEARCE Do you mean that you sleep in the underclothes you wear in the daytime?

LIZA What else have I to sleep in?439

MRS PEARCE You will never do that again as long as you live here440. I will get you a proper nightdress441.

LIZA Do you mean change into cold things and lie awake shivering half the night?442 You want to kill me, you do.

MRS PEARCE I want to change you from a frowzy slut to a clean respectable girl fit to sit with the gentlemen in the study. Are you going to trust me and do what I tell you or be thrown out and sent back to your flower basket443?

LIZA But you dont know what the cold is to me444. You dont know how I dread it.

MRS PEARCE Your bed wont be cold here: I will put a hot water bottle445 in it. [pushing her into the bedroom] Off with you and undress.446

LIZA Oh, if only I'd a known what a dreadful thing it is to be clean I'd never have come447. I didnt know when I was well off.448 I – [Mrs Pearce pushes her through the door, but leaves it partly open lest her prisoner should take to flight449.]

Mrs Pearce puts on a pair of white rubber sleeves, and fills the bath, mixing hot and cold450, and testing the result with the bath thermometer451. She perfumes it with a handful of bath salts452 and adds a palmful of mustard453. She then takes a formidable looking454 long handled455 scrubbing brush and soaps it profusely456 with a ball of scented soap457.

Eliza comes back with nothing on but the bath gown huddled tightly round her458, a piteous spectacle of abject terror459.

MRS PEARCE Now come along. Take that thing off.460

LIZA Oh I couldnt, Mrs Pearce: I reely461 couldnt. I never done462 such a thing463.

MRS PEARCE Nonsense. Here: step in and tell me whether it's hot enough for you.

LIZA Ah-oo! Ah-oo! It's too hot.

MRS PEARCE [deftly snatching the gown away and throwing Eliza down on her back464] It wont hurt you. [She sets to work465 with the scrubbing brush.]

Eliza's screams are heartrending.

Meanwhile the Colonel has been having it out with466 Higgins about Eliza. Pickering has come from the hearth to the chair and seated himself astride of it with his arms on the back467 to cross-examine him.

PICKERING Excuse the straight question468, Higgins. Are you a man of good character469 where women are concerned470?

HIGGINS [moodily] Have you ever met a man of good character where women are concerned?

PICKERING Yes: very frequently471.

HIGGINS [dogmatically, lifting himself on his hands to the level of the piano472, and sitting on it with a bounce] Well, I havnt.473 I find that the moment I let a woman make friends with me, she becomes jealous, exacting, suspicious, and a damned nuisance474. I find that the moment475 I let myself make friends with a woman, I become selfish and tyrannical476. Women upset everything. When you let them into your life477, you find that the woman is driving at478 one thing and youre driving at another.

PICKERING At what, for example?479

HIGGINS [coming off the piano restlessly] Oh, Lord knows!480 I supposethe woman wants to live her own life481; and the man wants to live his482; and each tries to drag the other on to the wrong track483. One wants to go north and the other south484; and the result is that both have to go east, though they both hate the east wind485. [He sits down on the bench at the keyboard486.] So here I am487, a confirmed old bachelor488, and likely to remain so489.

PICKERING [rising and standing over him490 gravely] Come, Higgins! You know what I mean. If I'm to be in this business491 I shall feel responsible for that girl. I hope it's understood that492 no advantage is to be taken of her position493.

HIGGINS What! That thing!494 Sacred, I assure you.495 [rising to explain] You see, she'll be a pupil; and teaching would be impossible unless pupils were sacred496. Ive taught scores of497 American millionairesses how to speak English: the best looking women in the world498. I'm seasoned499. They might as well be blocks of wood500. I might as well be a block of wood. It's –

Mrs Pearce opens the door. She has Eliza's hat in her hand. Pickering retires to the easy-chair at the hearth and sits down.

HIGGINS [eagerly] Well, Mrs Pearce: is it all right501?

MRS PEARCE [at the door] I just wish to trouble you with a word, if I may502, Mr Higgins.

HIGGINS Yes, certainly. Come in. [She comes forward.] Dont burn that503, Mrs Pearce. I'll keep it as a curiosity504. [He takes the hat.]

MRS PEARCE Handle it carefully, sir, please. I had to promise her not to burn it505; but I had better put it in the oven for a while506.

HIGGINS [putting it down hastily on the piano] Oh! thank you. Well, what have you to say to me?

PICKERING Am I in the way507?

MRS PEARCE Not in the least508, sir. Mr Higgins: will you please be very particular what you say before the girl509?

HIGGINS [sternly] Of course. I'm always particular about what I say. Why do you say this to me?

MRS PEARCE [unmoved510] No, sir: youre not at all particular when youve mislaid anything or when you get a little impatient. Now it doesnt matter before me511. I'm used to it512. But you really must not swear before the girl513.

HIGGINS [indignantly] I swear!514 [most emphatically] I never swear. I detest the habit515. What the devil516 do you mean?

MRS PEARCE [stolidly] Thats what I mean517, sir. You swear a great deal too much518. I dont mind your damning and blasting, and what the devil519 and where the devil and who the devil –

HIGGINS Mrs Pearce: this language from your lips520! Really!

MRS PEARCE [not to be put off521] – but there is a certain word522 I must ask you not to use. The girl used it herself when she began toenjoy the bath. It begins with the same letter as bath523. She knows no better524: she learnt it at her mother's knee525. But she must not hear it from your lips.

HIGGINS [loftily] I cannot charge myself with526 having ever uttered it, Mrs Pearce. [She looks at him steadfastly. He adds527, hiding an uneasy conscience with a judicial air528.] Except529 perhaps in a moment of extreme and justifiable excitement530.

MRS PEARCE Only this morning531, sir, you applied it to your boots532, to the butter533, and to the brown bread534.

HIGGINS Oh, that!535 Mere alliteration536, Mrs Pearce, natural to a poet537.

MRS PEARCE Well, sir, whatever you choose to call it538, I beg you not to let the girl hear you repeat539 it.

HIGGINS Oh, very well, very well. Is that all?540

MRS PEARCE No, sir. We shall have to be very particular with this girl as to personal cleanliness.

HIGGINS Certainly. Quite right. Most important.

MRS PEARCE I mean not to be slovenly about her dress541 or untidyin leaving things about542.

HIGGINS [going to her solemnly] Just so.543 I intended to call your attention to544 that. [He passes on to Pickering, who is enjoying the conversation immensely.] It is these little things that matter545, Pickering. Take care of the pence and the pounds will take care of themselves546 is as true of personal habits as of money547. [He comes to anchor548 on the hearthrug, with the air of a man in an unassailable position549.]

MRS PEARCE Yes, sir. Then might I ask you550 not to come down to breakfast551 in your dressing-gown, or at any rate not to use it as a napkin to the extent you do552, sir. And if you would be so good as not to553 eat everything off the same554 plate, and to remember555 not to put the porridge saucepan out of your hand on the clean tablecloth, it556 would be a better example to the girl. You know you nearly choked yourself with a fishbone in the jam557 only last week.

HIGGINS [routed from the hearthrug558 and drifting back to the piano] I may do these things sometimes in absence of mind559; butsurely I dont do them habitually. [angrily] By the way560: my dressing-gown smells most damnably of benzine561.

MRS PEARCE No doubt it does, Mr Higgins. But if you will wipe your fingers –562

HIGGINS [yelling] Oh very well, very well: I'll wipe them in my hair in future563.

MRS PEARCE I hope youre not offended, Mr Higgins.

HIGGINS [shocked at finding himself thought capable of an unamiable sentiment564] Not at all, not at all. Youre quite right, Mrs Pearce: I shall be particularly careful before the girl. Is that all?

MRS PEARCE No, sir. Might she use565 some of those Japanese dresses you brought from abroad566? I really cant put her back into her old things567.

HIGGINS Certainly. Anything you like.568 Is that all?

MRS PEARCE Thank you, sir. Thats all. [She goes out.]

HIGGINS You know, Pickering, that woman has the most extraordinary ideas about me. Here I am569, a shy, diffident sort of man570. Ive never been able to feel really grown-up and tremendous571, like other chaps572. And yet she's firmly persuaded573 that I'm an arbitrary overbearing bossing kind of person574. I cant account for it575.

Mrs Pearce returns.

MRS PEARCE If you please576, sir, the trouble's beginning already.Theres a dustman downstairs, Alfred Doolittle577, wants578 to see you. He says you have his daughter here.

PICKERING [rising] Phew!579 I say!580

HIGGINS [promptly] Send the blackguard up581.

MRS PEARCE Oh, very well, sir. [She goes out.]

PICKERING He may not be582 a blackguard, Higgins.

HIGGINS Nonsense. Of course he's a blackguard.

PICKERING Whether he is or not583, I'm afraid we shall have some trouble with him584.

HIGGINS [confidently] Oh no: I think not585. If theres any trouble he shall have it with me, not I with him.586 And we are sure to get something interesting out of him.

PICKERING About the girl?587

HIGGINS No. I mean his dialect.

PICKERING Oh!

MRS PEARCE [at the door] Doolittle, sir.588 [She admits Doolittle and retires.]

Alfred Doolittle is an elderly but vigorous dustman, clad in the costume of his profession589, including a hat with a back brim covering his neck and shoulders590. He has well marked and rather interesting features591, and seems equally free from fear and conscience592. He has a remarkably expressive voice593, the result of a habit of giving vent to his feelings without reserve594. His present pose is that of wounded honor and stern resolution595.

DOOLITTLE [at the door, uncertain which of the two gentlemen is his man596] Professor Iggins597?

HIGGINS Here. Good morning. Sit down.

DOOLITTLE Morning598, Governor599. [He sits down magisterially600.] I come about a very serious matter, Governor.

HIGGINS [to Pickering] Brought up601 in Hounslow602. Mother Welsh603, I should think. [Doolittle opens his mouth, amazed604. Higgins continues.] What do you want, Doolittle?

DOOLITTLE [menacingly] I want my daughter: thats what I want. See?

HIGGINS Of course you do605. Youre her father, arnt you? You dont suppose606 anyone else wants her, do you? I'm glad to see you have some spark of family feeling left607. She's upstairs. Take her away at once.

DOOLITTLE [rising, fearfully taken aback608] What!

HIGGINS Take her away. Do you suppose I'm going to keep your daughter for you609?

DOOLITTLE [remonstrating] Now, now, look here610, Governor. Is this reasonable? Is it fairity611 to take advantage of a man like this? The girl belongs to me. You got her. Where do I come in?612 [He sits down again.]

HIGGINS Your daughter had the audacity to613 come to my house and ask me to teach her how to speak properly so that shecould get a place in a flower-shop. This gentleman and my housekeeper614 have been here all the time. [bullying him] How dare you come here and attempt to blackmail me? You sent her here on purpose.615

DOOLITTLE [protesting] No, Governor.

HIGGINS You must have.616 How else could you617 possibly know that she is here?

DOOLITTLE Dont take a man up like that618, Governor.

HIGGINS The police shall take you up619. This is a plant620 – a plot621 to extort money by threats. I shall telephone for622 the police. [He goes resolutely to the telephone and opens the directory623.]

DOOLITTLE Have I asked you for a brass farthing624? I leave it to the gentleman here625: have I said a word about money?

HIGGINS [throwing the book aside and marching down on Doolittle with a poser626] What else627 did you come for?

DOOLITTLE [sweetly] Well, what would a man come for?628 Be human629, Governor.

HIGGINS [disarmed630] Alfred: did you put her up to it631?

DOOLITTLE So help me632. Governor. I never did. I take my Bible oath633 I aint seen the girl these two months past634.

HIGGINS Then how did you know she was here?

DOOLITTLE ['most musical, most melancholy'635] I'll tell you, Governor, if youll only let me get a word in636. I'm willing to tell you. I'mwanting to tell you. I'm waiting to tell you.637

HIGGINS Pickering: this chap has a certain natural gift of rhetoric638. Observe the rhythm of his native woodnotes wild639. 'I'm willing to tell you: I'm wanting to tell you: I'm waiting to tell you.' Sentimental rhetoric!640 Thats the Welsh strain in him641. It also accounts for his mendacity and dishonesty.642

PICKERING Oh, please643, Higgins: I'm west country myself.644 [to Doolittle] How did you know the girl was here if you didnt send her645?

DOOLITTLE It was like this646, Governor. The girl took647 a boy in the taxi to give him a jaunt648. Son of her landlady, he is.649 He hung about650 on the chance of651 her giving him another ride home652. Well, she sent him back of her luggage653 when she heard you was654 willing for her to stop here655. I met the boy at the corner of Long Acre and Endell Street656.

HIGGINS Public house.657 Yes?

DOOLITTLE The poor man's club658, Governor: why shouldnt I?659

PICKERING Do let him tell his story, Higgins.

DOOLITTLE He told me what was up660. And I ask you, what was my feelings and my duty661 as a father? I says to the boy, 'You bring me the luggage, ' I says662 –

PICKERING Why didnt you go for it yourself663?

DOOLITTLE Landlady wouldnt have trusted me with it664, Governor. She's that kind of woman: you know. I had to give the boy a penny afore665 he trusted me with it, the little swine666. I brought it to her just to oblige you like667, and make myself agreeable668. That's all669.

HIGGINS How much luaggage?

DOOLITTLE Musical instrument670, Governor. A few pictures, a trifle of671 jewelry, and a bird-cage. She said she didnt want no clothes. What was I to think from that672, Governor? I ask you as a parent what was I to think?

HIGGINS So you came to rescue her from worse than death673, eh?

DOOLITTLE [appreciatively: relieved at being so well understood674] Just so, Governor. Thats right.

PICKERING But why did you bring her luggage if you intended to take her away?

DOOLITTLE Have I said a word about taking her away? Have I now?

HIGGINS [determinedly] Youre going to take her away675, double quick676. [He crosses to the hearth677 and rings the bell678.]

DOOLITTLE [rising] No, Governor. Dont say that. I'm not theman to stand in my girl's light679. Heres a career opening for her680, as you might say681; and –

Mrs Pearce opens the door and awaits orders.

HIGGINS Mrs Pearce: this is Eliza's father. He has come to take her away. Give her to him. [He goes back to the piano, with an air of washing his hands of the whole affair682.]

DOOLITTLE No. This is a misunderstanding. Listen here –

MRS PEARCE He cant take her away, Mr Higgins: how can he? You told me to burn her clothes.683

DOOLITTLE Thats right. I cant carry the girl through the streets like a blooming monkey684, can I? I put it to you.685

HIGGINS You have put it to me that you want your daughter686. Take your daughter. If she has no clothes go out and buy her some687.

DOOLITTLE [desperate] Wheres the clothes she come in?688 Did I burn them or did you missus here689?

MRS PEARCE I am the housekeeper, if you please. I have sent for some clothes690 for your girl. When they come you can take her away. You can wait in the kitchen. This way, please.691

Doolittle, much troubled, accompanies her to the door692; then hesitates; finally turns confidentially to Higgins693.

DOOLITTLE Listen here, Governor. You and me is694 men of the world695, aint we?

HIGGINS Oh! Men of the world, are we?696 Youd better go697, Mrs Pearce.

MRS PEARCE I think so, indeed, sir. [She goes, with dignity.]

PICKERING The floor is yours698, Mr Doolittle.

DOOLITTLE [to Pickering] I thank you, Governor. [to Higgins, who takes refuge699 on the piano bench, a little overwhelmed by the proximity of his visitor700; for Doolittle has a professional flavor of dust about him701] Well, the truth is702, Ive taken a sort of fancy to you703, Governor; and if you want the girl, I'm not so set on704 having her back home again but what I might be open to an arrangement. Regarded in the light of a young woman705, she's a fine handsome girl. As a daughter she's not worth her keep706; and so I tell you straight707. All I ask is my rights as a father708; and youre the last man alive to expect709 me to let her go for nothing710; for I can see youre one of the straight sort711, Governor. Well, whats a five-pound note to you? And whats Eliza to me?712 [He turns to his chair and sits down judicially713.]

PICKERING I think you ought to know, Doolittle, that Mr Higgins's intentions are entirely honorable714.

DOOLITTLE Course they are715, Governor. If I thought they wasnt716, I'd ask fifty717.

HIGGINS [revolted] Do you mean to say that you would sell your daughter for £50?

DOOLITTLE Not in a general way I wouldnt718; but to oblige a gentleman like you719 I'd do a good deal720, I do assure you.

PICKERING Have you no morals721, man?

DOOLITTLE [unabashed] Cant afford them, Governor. Neither could you if you was as poor as me. Not that I mean any harm, you know. But if Liza is going to have a bit out of this722, why not me too?

HIGGINS [troubled] I dont know what to do, Pickering. There can be no question that723 as a matter of morals it's a positive crime724 to give this chap a farthing. And yet I feel a sort of rough justice in his claim725.

DOOLITTLE Thats it726, Governor. Thats all I say.727 A father's heart, as it were728.

PICKERING Well, I know the feeling; but really it seems hardly right –729

DOOLITTLE Dont say that, Governor730. Dont look at it that way. What am I, Governors both? I ask you, what am I? I'm one of the undeserving poor731: thats what I am. Think of what that means732 to a man. It means that he's up agen middle class morality all the time733. If theres anything going734, and I put in for a bit of it735, it's always the same story736: 'Youre undeserving; so you cant have it.' But my needs737 is as great as the most deserving widow's738 that ever got money out of six different charities in one week for the death of the same husband739. I dont need less than a deserving man: I need more. I dont eat less hearty than him740; and I drink a lot more741. I want a bit of amusement, cause742 I'm a thinking man743. I want cheerfulness and a song and a band744 when I feel low745. Well, they charge me just the same for everything as they charge the deserving746. What is middle class morality? Just an excuse747 for never giving me anything. Therefore, I ask you, as two gentleman, not to play that game on me748. I'm playing straight with you749. I aint pretending to be deserving750. I'm undeserving; and I mean to go on being undeserving. I like it751; and thats the truth. Will you take advantage of a man's nature752 to do him out of the price of his own daughter753 what754 he's brought up and fed and clothed755 by the sweat of his brow756 until she's growed757 big enough to be interesting to you two gentlemen? Is five pounds unreasonable?758 I put it to you; and I leave it to you.759

HIGGINS [rising, and going over to Pickering] Pickering: if we were to take this man in hand for three months760, he could choose between a seat in the Cabinet and a popular pulpit in Wales761.

PICKERING What do you say to that762, Doolittle?

DOOLITTLE Not me, Governor, thank you kindly763. Ive heard all the preachers and all the prime ministers – for I'm a thinking man and game for764 politics or religion or social reform same as765 all the other amusements766 – and I tell you it's a dog's life any way you look at it767. Undeserving poverty is my line.768 Taking one station in society with another769, it's – it's – well, it's the only one that has any ginger in it, to my taste770.

HIGGINS I suppose we must give him a fiver771.

PICKERING He'll make a bad use of772 it, I'm afraid.

DOOLITTLE Not me, Governor, so help me773 I wont774. Dont you be afraid that I'll save it and spare it and live idle on it775. There wont be a penny of it left776 by Monday777: I'll have to go to work same as if778 I'd never had it. It wont pauperize me, you bet779. Just one good spree780 for myself and the missus781, givingpleasure to ourselves and employment to others782, and satisfaction to you to think it's not been throwed away783. You couldnt spend it better784.

HIGGINS [taking out his pocket book and coming between Doolittle and the piano] This is irresistible785. Lets give him ten786. [He offers two notes787 to the dustman.]

DOOLITTLE No, Governor. She wouldnt have the heart to spend ten788; and perhaps I shouldnt neither789. Ten pounds is a lot of money: it makes a man feel prudent like790; and then goodbye to happiness791. You give me what I ask you, Governor: not a penny more792, and not a penny less.

PICKERING Why dont you marry that missus of yours? I rather draw the line at encouraging that sort of immorality793.

DOOLITTLE Tell her so, Governor: tell her so. I'm willing. It's me that suffers by it.794 Ive no hold on her.795 I got to796 be agreeable to her797. I got to give her presents. I got to buy her clothes something sinful798. I'm a slave to that woman, Governor, just because I'm not her lawful husband799. And she knows it too. Catch her marrying me!800 Take my advice, Governor: marry Eliza while she's young and dont know no better801. If you dontyoull be sorry for it802 after803. If you do, she'll be sorry for it after; but better her than you804, because youre a man, and she's only a woman and dont know805 how to be happy anyhow.

HIGGINS Pickering: if we listen to this man another minute, we shall have no convictions left806. [to Doolittle] Five pounds I think you said.807

DOOLITTLE Thank you kindly, Governor.

HIGGINS Youre sure you wont take ten?

DOOLITTLE Not now.808 Another time809, Governor.

HIGGINS [handing him a five-pound note] Here you are.

DOOLITTLE Thank you, Governor. Good morning810. [He hurries to the door, anxious to get away with his booty811. When he opens it he is confronted with a dainty and exquisitely clean young Japanese lady812 in a simple blue cotton kimono813 printed cunningly814 with small white jasmine blossoms. Mrs Pearce is with her. He gets out of her way815 deferentially816 and apologizes.] Beg pardon817, miss818.

THE JAPANESE LADY Garn!819 Dont you know your own daughter?

exlaiming simultaneously　[ˌsɪməlˈteɪnɪəsli]也有人（尤其美国人）读作[ˌsaɪməl-ˈteɪnɪəsli]同时叫喊。Doolittle叫喊Bly me! it's Eliza! Higgins叫喊Whats that? This! Pickering叫喊By Jove!三人的叫喊混成一片。Bly me! = Blimey!感叹词，表示惊讶。

By Jove!　指神名的发誓、诅咒语；天哪！Jove即罗马神话中的诸神之王Jupiter [ˈdʒuːpɪtə]。

LIZA Dont I look silly?820

HIGGINS Silly?

MRS PEARCE [at the door] Now, Mr Higgins, please dont say anything to make the girl conceited about herself821.

HIGGINS [conscientiously] Oh! Quite right, Mrs Pearce. [to Eliza]Yes: damned silly.

MRS PEARCE Please822, sir.

HIGGINS [correcting himself] I mean extremely silly.

LIZA I should look all right with my hat on. [She takes up her hat; puts it on; and walks across the room to the fireplace with a fashionable air823.]

HIGGINS A new fashion, by George824! And it ought to look horrible!825

DOOLITTLE [with fatherly pride826] Well, I never thought she'd clean up827 as good looking as that, Governor. She's a credit to me828, aint she?

LIZA I tell you, it's easy to clean up here. Hot and cold water on tap, just as much as you like, there is.829 Woolly towels, there is; and a towel horse so hot, it burns your fingers.830 Soft brushes to scrub yourself, and a wooden bowl of soap smelling like primroses831. Now I know why ladies is832 so clean. Washing's a treat for them.833 Wish834 they could see what it is for the like of me!

HIGGINS I'm glad the bathroom met with your approval835.

LIZA It didnt836: not all of it837; and I dont care who hears me say it838. Mrs Pearce knows.

HIGGINS What was wrong839, Mrs Pearce?

MRS PEARCE [blandly840] Oh, nothing841, sir. It doesnt matter842.

LIZA I had a good mind to break it843. I didnt know which way to look.844 But I hung a towel over it845, I did.

HIGGINS Over what?

MRS PEARCE Over the looking-glass, sir.

HIGGINS Doolittle: you have brought your daughter up too strictly846.

DOOLITTLE Me! I never brought her up at all, except to give her a lick of a strap now and again847. Dont put it on me, Governor. She aint accustomed to it848, you see: that's all. But she'll soon pick up849 your free-and-easy ways850.

LIZA I'm a good girl, I am; and I wont pick up no free-and-easy ways.

HIGGINS Eliza: if you say again that youre a good girl, your father shall take you home851.

LIZA Not him852. You dont know my father. All he come here for was to touch you for some money to get drunk on.853

DOOLITTLE Well, what else would I want money for? To put into the plate in church, I suppose854. [She puts out her tongue at him855. He is so incensed by this856 that Pickering presently finds it necessary to step between them857.] Dont you give me none of your lip858; and dont let me hear you giving this gentleman any of it neither859, or youll hear from me about it860. See?861

HIGGINS Have you any further advise to give her before you go, Doolittle? Your blessing862, for instance.

DOOLITTLE No, Governor: I aint such a mug863 as to put up my children to all I know myself864. Hard enough to hold them in without that865. If you want Eliza's mind improved, Governor, you do it866 yourself with a strap. So long, gentlemen. [He turns to go.]

HIGGINS [impressively] Stop. Youll come regularly to see your daughter. It's your duty, you know. My brother is a clergyman; and he could help you in your talks with her867.

DOOLITTLE [evasively] Certainly, I'll come, Governor. Not just this week868, because I have a job at a distance869. But later on you may depend on me870. Afternoon871, gentlemen. Afternoon, maam872. [He touches his hat to873 Mrs Pearce, who disdains the salutation874 and goes out. He winks at Higgins, thinking him probably a fellow-sufferer from Mrs Pearce's difficult disposition875, and follows her.]

LIZA Dont you believe the old liar. He'd as soon you set a bulldog on him as a clergyman.876 You wont see him again in a hurry.877

HIGGINS I dont want to, Eliza. Do you?

LIZA Not me. I dont want never to see him again, I dont. He's a disgrace to me, he is, collecting dust, instead of working at his trade878.

PICKERING What is his trade, Eliza?

LIZA Talking money out of other people's pockets into his own. His proper trade's a navvy879; and he works at it880 sometimes too – for exercise881 – and earns good money882 at it. Aint you going to call me Miss Doolittle any more?883

PICKERING I beg your pardon, Miss Doolittle884. It was a slip of the tongue.885

LIZA Oh, I dont mind886; only it sounded so genteel887. I should just like to take a taxi to the corner of Tottenham Court Road and get out there and tell it to wait for me888, just to put the girls in their place a bit889. I wouldnt speak to them, you know.

PICKERING Better wait890 til we get you something really fashionable891.

HIGGINS Besides, you shouldnt cut your old friends892 now that you have risen in the world893. Thats what we call snobbery.

LIZA You dont call the like of them my friends now, I should hope. Theyve took it out of me often enough with their ridicule when they had the chance894; and now I mean to get a bit of my own back895. But if I'm to have fashionable clothes, I'll wait. I should like to have some. Mrs Pearce says youre going to give me some to wear in bed at night different896 to what I wear in the daytime; but it do seem a waste of money897 when you could get something to shew898. Besides, I never could fancy changing into cold things899 on a winter night.

MRS PEARCE [coming back] Now, Eliza. The new things have come for you to try on900.

LIZA Ah-ow-oo-ooh! [She rushes out.]

MRS PEARCE [following her] Oh, dont rush about like that, girl. [She shuts the door behind her.901]

HIGGINS Pickering: we have taken on a stiff job902.

PICKERING [with conviction] Higgins: we have.

There seems to be some curiosity as to903 what Higgins's lessons to Eliza were like. Well, here is a sample: the first one904.

Picture905 Eliza, in her new clothes, and feeling her inside put out of step906 by a lunch, dinner, and breakfast of a kind to which it is unaccustomed907, seated908 with Higgins and the Colonel in the study, feeling like a hospital out-patient909 at a first encounter910 with the doctors.

Higgins, constitutionally unable to sit still911, discomposes her still more by striding restlessly about912. But for the reassuring presence and quietude of her friend the Colonel913 she would run for her life914, even back to Drury Lane915.

HIGGINS Say your alphabet916.

LIZA I know my alphabet. Do you think I know nothing? I dont need to be taught917 like a child.

HIGGINS [thundering918] Say your alphabet.

PICKERING Say it, Miss Doolittle. You will understand presently919. Do what he tells you; and let him teach you in his own way.

LIZA Oh well, if you put it like that –920 Ahyee, bәyee, cәyee, dәyee921 –

HIGGINS [with a roar of a wounded922 lion] Stop. Listen to this923, Pickering. This is what we pay for as elementary education.924 This unfortunate animal925 has been locked up for nine years in school926 at our expense927 to teach her to speak and read the language of Shakespear and Milton928. And the result in Ahyee, Bә-yee, Cә-yee, Dә-yee. [to Eliza] Say A, B, C, D.

LIZA [almost in tears] But I'm sayin it.929 Ahyee, Bәyee, Cә-yee –

HIGGINS Stop. Say a cup of tea.

LIZA A cappәtә-ee930.

HIGGINS Put your tongue forward until it squeezes against the top of your lower teeth.931 Now say cup.

LIZA C-c-c –932 I cant. C-Cup933.

PICKERING Good. Splendid, Miss Doolittle.

HIGGINS By Jupiter934, she's done it at the first shot935. Pickering: we shall make a duchess of her. [to Eliza] Now do you think you could possibly936 say tea? Not tә-yee937, mind938: if you ever say bәyee, cә-yee, dә-yee again939 you shall be dragged round the room three times by the hair of your head940. [fortissimo941] T, T, T, T.942

LIZA [weeping] I cant hear no difference943 cep that it sounds more genteel-like when you say it944.

HIGGINS Well, if you can hear that difference, what the devil are you crying for?945 Pickering: give her a chocolate.

PICKERING No, no. Never mind crying a little946, Miss Doolittle: you are doing very well; and the lessons wont hurt. I promise you I wont let him drag you round the room by your hair947.

HIGGINS Be off with you to Mrs Pearce948 and tell her about it.949 Think about it950. Try to do it by yourself951: and keep your tongue well forward in your mouth952 instead of trying to roll it up and swallow it953. Another lesson at half-past954 four this afternoon. Away with you.955

Eliza, still sobbing, rushes from the room.

And that is the sort of ordeal956 poor Eliza has to go through957 for months before we meet her again958 on her first appearance in London society of the professional class959.

1 laboratory　实验室；研究室。英国多读[ləˈbɒrətri]，美国读[ˈlæbrətɔːri]。

2 in Wimpole Street英国说“在……街”、“在街上”用in，美国用on。

3 first floor　在英国，“底层”称ground floor; first floor是我国所说的“二楼”。在美国first floor指“底层”，“二楼”称second floor，和我国说法相同。

4 looking on the street看出去是街景；（楼面）临街。

5 was meant [ment] for原是准备作……之用的。上下文用现在时，这里用过去时，表示原先的情况。

6 double doors　《新英和大辞典》用复数，指“双扇双关的门”或“双重门”；《韦氏国际词典》用单数，指“双扇门”，则复数当指两个或两个以上双扇门了。

7 persons entering　= persons who enter / persons when they enter.

8 corner to their right他们右边的角落。这里的to也可用at。

9 file cabinets　[ˈkæbɪnəts]文件柜。

10 at right angles to one another彼此形成直角。one another过去一般语法书上说限用于三个或三个以上事物之间，each other才用于两者之间，实际上两语通用。

11 stands a flat writing-table　= a fat writing-table stands.因句首有状语短语In this corner，所以主谓倒装。writing-table写字台（似乎着重在供写字用，未必如desk有许多抽屉）。

12 phonograph　[ˈfəʊnəɡrɑːf] （老式的）录音机。

13 laryngoscope [ləˈrɪŋgəskəʊp] （医学上或语音学、声学上用的）喉镜。

14 a row [rəʊ] of tiny [ˈtaɪni] organ pipes with a bellows装有风箱的一排小小的风琴管。风箱用以吹响琴管。注意：作“划船”解和口语中作“吵架”解的row均读[raʊ]，词源各不相同。bellows只有复数式，常用作单数。

15 a set of lamp chimneys for singing flames with burners attached to a gas plug in the wall by an indiarubber tube　一组罩住试音火的灯罩，配有（烧煤气的）灯头，灯头用橡皮管接在墙上的煤气插头上。singing flames随声音颤动而起落的煤气火焰（装置）。with burners修饰lamp chimneys。indiarubber一般分写成india rubber。

16 tuning-forks　音叉。

17 a life-size image [ˈɪmɪdʒ] of half a human head, shewing (=showing) in section the vocal organs一具同比例的半人头模型，以断面显示各种发音器官。shewing in section，这里= shewing in longitudinal（或vertical）section：以纵截面。“横截面”为cross section。

18 wax cylinders　[ˈsɪlɪndəz] （老式录音机录音用的）蜡管。

19 Further down the room　在房间里更前面些的地方。在舞台上，down指靠台前的一面，up指靠台后的一面。

20 at the side of the hearth [ˈhaːθ] nearest the door在壁炉旁边最近房门处。at the side of中的at也可用on，如下文有On the other side of ... 。hearth原指“壁炉（fireplace）的炉床”，转义为“炉边”；这里借指“壁炉”。nearest the door　最靠近门处（可能靠近门处除easy-chair外还有其他东西，而easy-chair靠门最近）。nearest the door并非修饰hearth，而是和at the side of the hearth一起修饰easy-chair。door当是指上文的double doors，虽是双扇的门，但仍是“门”的概念，故此处可用单数door。

21 coal-scuttle煤斗；和easy-chair并列，是with的介词宾语。

22 mantelpiece　[ˈmæntlpiːs] （壁炉顶上的）壁炉架。

23 stand for newspapers　放报纸的架子。

24 On it is a telephone and the telephone directory　[dəˈrektəri]柜（cabinet）上是电话和电话簿。状语短语On it放句首，故主谓倒装。这里的is按语法应用are，因为主语是两样东西，但英语中常有这种情况，可以理解为说了is a telephone，又想到the telephone directory，所谓after-thought；也可理解为= a telephone with the telephone directory，两者作为一组东西。

25 The corner beyond, and most of the side wall, is occupied by a grand piano（[pɪˈænəʊ]，也可读作[pɪˈɑːnəʊ]）这里beyond = beyond the cabinet; The corner beyond the cabinet是在舞台口的角落。and连接的前后两个位置，作为一个空间，故后面用is occupied。grand piano大钢琴（乐器名，不照字面作“大的钢琴”解）。

26 keyboard at the end furthest from the door　大钢琴背朝side wall，键盘朝外，所以说“键盘在离门（the double doors in the middle of the back wall）最远的一端”。furthest further比farther常用；后者限用于“距离”；这里是指距离，所以改用farthest也可。

27 a bench for the player　弹琴的人坐的长凳子。bench与keyboard并列，是with的介词宾语。

28 extending the full length of the keyboard （凳子）同键盘一样长。注意：extending在这里是不及物动词。the full length of the keyboard作状语。比较及物动词extend的例子：extend the length（延长长度）。

29 a dessert [dɪˈzɜːt] dish heaped with fruit and sweets, mostly chocolates [ˈtʃɒkləts]堆满水果和糖果——大半是巧克力糖的果盘。heaped是过去分词，修饰dessert dish。fruit统称“水果”，是不可数名词用单数；强调多种水果时，用复数，如the fruits in season now（现在当季的几种水果）。sweets统称“糖果”用复数；指一块、两块糖时，说a sweet、two sweets；美国称candy，可统指“糖果”，也可指一块糖，因而单复数都可用，如give the boy some candy（或candies）。chocolates这里指一块块巧克力糖，是可数名词，作sweets的同位语，chocolate作为巧克力这种物质时，是不可数名词。

30 clear　（房间）没放东西的。

31 one stray chair　一把散置的（或不成套的）椅子。用one而不用a，是强调“一把”（不是“两把，三把”）。

32 engravings　版画；雕版印刷品。它前面用逗号，省略are。

33 Piranesis　[pɪrəˈneɪzɪz] Piranesi的一些作品。Giambattista Piranesi (1720–1778)，是意大利雕刻家兼画家；这里以人名用作可数名词，是指其作品。

34 mezzotint [ˈmetsəʊtɪnt] portraits金属版印刷的肖像画。

35 No paintings. = There are no paintings. paintings油画水彩画，别于上面的印刷品。

36 cards　卡片；资料卡。

37 standing up near him　在他身旁稍后些处。up指靠后台一面，见前注。

38 hanging [ˈhæŋɪŋ] out （抽屉）开着，口子下倾；拖出在外面。

39 a robust [rəʊˈbʌst], vital, appetizing [ˈæpɪtaɪzɪŋ] sort of man一种强壮、活力充沛、令人喜爱的人。注意：sort of和kind of一样，后面的名词用单数，无冠词，很少用复数；即使sorts of和kinds of后面有时也用单数。appetizing原义是“开胃的”。

40 forty or thereabouts　四十岁左右。thereabouts一般读[ˈðeərəˌbaʊts]；在there or thereabouts（附近；左右）这一习语中读[ˌðeərəˈbaʊts]；thereabouts有时也作thereabout。

41 dressed这里是过去分词。

42 professional-looking看上去很专业的样子。professional职业的。这里指“从事专门职业（常指医生、律师、牧师）的”。“-looking”是构词成分，常接在形容词后面，表示“看上去像什么样子”的意思，如angry-looking（像在发火）。

43 frock-coat　（男子的）礼服大衣，简称frock。

44 scientific type　从事科学研究的那种人的类型；不作“科学的类型”解。

45 heartily, even violently interested强烈地、甚至狂热地感兴趣。

46 careless about himself and other people, including their feelings　不顾自己，不顾别人，包括（不顾会不会伤）别人的感情。

47 but for his years and size　除了他（已经是四十左右的）年纪和（高大的）个子的缘故；若不考虑他的年龄和个子；这是插入的状语短语，修饰is, is接后面表语rather like ... but for = except for。

48 impetuous　[ɪmˈpetjʊəs]激动的；是名词impetus [ˈɪmpɪtəs]（动力）衍生的形容词。

49 'taking notice' eagerly and loudly开始懂事，因而样样都哗啦哗啦要管；分词词组修饰baby。taking notice这里不作“注意”解，而指婴孩的“开始懂事”，因为是一种特殊的用法，故加引号。

50 requiring almost as much watching　需要几乎同样程度的看管。require watching中的watching形式是主动语态，意义是被动的，可改用不定式to be watched，却不可改用being watched。requiring almost as much watching后面省略as a very impetuous baby 'taking notice' eagerly and loudly requires to keep him out of unintended mischief。

51 keep him out of unintended mischief [ˈmɪstʃɪf]不让他无意中闯乱子。keep him out of作“使他保持在……之外”、“使免于……”，“使……不”解；这里和keep him from同义。

52 varies　[ˈveərɪz]变化不同。varies from ... to ...变化于……与……之间；一会儿……，一会儿……。这里是varies from genial [ˈdʒiːnɪəl] bullying [ˈbʊlɪɪŋ] to stormy petulance [ˈpetjʊləns] ，时而和和气气地欺辱人，时而大发雷霆。

53 when he is in a good humor　当他高兴的时候，修饰bullying。

54 when anything goes wrong当有什么事情出了毛病（不顺他心）的时候，修饰petulance。goes wrong原义“走入歧路”，转义“出岔”；是习语。

55 void of malice　[ˈmælɪs]绝无恶意的。这里void是形容词；void of malice和frank并列。

56 remains likeable even in his least reasonable moments　即使在他最不讲理的时候也还是可爱的。likeable = likable。

57 thats the whole show　要给你看的全给你看了。犹言“全本《西厢记》尽在于此”。比较：美国口语中be the whole show是“当主角”、“一手包办”的意思。

58 havnt taken half of it in　懂得不到一半。take in懂得；是习语；另外还可作“接受”、“轻信”等解。

59 go over　这个习语有许多意义。这里作“把……来一遍”解。

60 plants [plɑːnts] himself使他自己站立着；站立不动。

61 not now　= I shouldn't（也可说wouldn't）like to go over any of it now.

62 done up精疲力竭。也作done in，都是习语；这里的done是形容词。I'm quite done up for this morning这一个早晨（听下来）我累极了。

63 Tired of ...　= Are you tired of ... （你因为……而厌倦了吗？）

64 fearful strain　（听发音录音听得）非常吃力。fearful作“非常”、“极度”解，是口语。

65 fancied myself （妄）自以为（能力强）。

66 your hundred and thirty beat me　你的一百三十个（元音）弄得我稀里糊涂。beat使迷惑；使为难；是口语。hundred and thirty hundred和十位或个位数之间通常用and（在美国通常不用and）；这里hundred前加one也可。一百三十个元音见于英国语言学家Henry Sweet（1845–1912）所著语音学著作中；萧伯纳原是以Henry Sweet作为剧中人Henry Higgins的原型，见前言。

67 cant hear a bit of在……中一点也听不出。

68 difference between most of them它们（这一百三十个元音）中的大多数之间（所存在）的区别。between旧语法书中常说限用于两个之间，实际也可像among一样用于三个或三个以上之间。这里也可理解为许多元音的每两个之间，如[ɛ]和[e]，[ʌ]和[ə]。

69 that comes with practice　那（分辨一百三十个元音）是随着练习而来的；多听听就听得出来了。

70 presently you find theyre all as different as A from B　不久之后你就会觉得全像A和B那样（明显地）不同。different后一般接from，英国用法也有接to，也有（主要在美国）接than的。

71 Pearce　[ˈpɪəs]姓氏。

72 housekeeper女管家。这词也作“女主人”解；犹如hostess，是“（宴会上的）女主人”，又可作“（车、船和飞机上的）女服务员”等解。

73 Whats the matter?　出了什么事？是惯用语。the matter是wrong的意思。

74 young woman　因为来访的是卖花女，所以不客气地称woman；倘是来Higgins家的“上等”女客，就称lady了。

75 what she's (= she has) come about她为了什么事而来的。

76 common girl粗俗的姑娘。

77 I should have sent her away, only I thought ...　我本想打发她走的，可是我想…… should have sent her away是表示过去与事实相反的虚拟语气，实际上是没有sent her away。only这里是连词，= but。连在一起，可以理解为I should have sent her away if I had not thought ...，即I did not send her away, for I thought ...。

78 wanted her to talk into your machines　[məˈʃiːnz]要她对着你的录音机器说话（给你录音）。wanted是“将要”而并非“过去要”，所以用过去时，是因为它用在过去时thought引起的从句中，语法上叫做“时态的呼应”。如以为Higgins原先曾约好她来录音的，那么wanted应改为had wanted。

79 done wrong　做错；犯错误；这是习语。比较：另一习语do wrong to somebody，常作do somebody wrong：委屈（或冤枉）某人；说（某人）坏话。

80 I'm sure　我相信。语气很轻，实际上只是“大概”的意思；I'm certain才是“我确实相信”、“我肯定”的意思。

81 accent　口音。这里不作“重音”解。作“口音”解的例子还有如The French guest speaks German with a French accent.那位法国客人说德语带法国口音。

82 something dreadful　难听的东西（口音）。something在口语中也作副词用，有“相当”、“非常”的意思。dreadful原义“可怕的”，转义“糟透的”，犹如上海方言说“吓（坏）人的”。

83 really真的。不是修饰前面的dreadful，而是修饰全句，表示“我说这话是千真万确的”，另举两例：Really, that's a flat lie.真的，那是彻头彻尾的谎言。Really?（你说的）是真的吗？

84 I dont know how you can take an interest in it我不知道你怎么会对它（那种糟透的口音）感兴趣；在我看来是毫无意义的。

85 have her up　让她上来。up是宾语补足语，her up（她上来）有逻辑上的主谓关系，因此构成have的复合宾语。

86 Shew her up带领她上来。这个up是副词，修饰shew，表示“方向”、“去处”；和have her up的up不同，须仔细辨别。shew show的旧式拼法。

87 rushes across to his working table　匆匆跑到房间另一端他的工作台那里。across也可说across the room，即across the middle part of the room。working table，就是指前面讲的writing-table。

88 to use = to be used.

89 only half resigned to it　只是一半屈从于；相当勉强地听从主人的命令；听从是出于无可奈何。only修饰half。

90 It's for you to say由你说了算。It是不及物动词不定式to say的先行词。

91 records　纪录，唱片。这里是录好音的录音筒（cylinders）。record，名词读[ˈrekɔːd]，动词读[rɪˈkɔːd]。现在录音机按钮上的record字样，应读[rɪˈkɔːd]。

92 set her talking使她说话。talking是her的宾语补足语，her与talking在逻辑上有主谓关系，her talking这个主谓结构也称为是set的复合宾语。

93 take it down first in Bell's Visible [ˈvɪzəbl] Speech先用倍耳“视语音标”把它（她说的话）记录下来。Bell's Visible Speech Alexander Melville Bell（1819–1905）是生于苏格兰的美国语音学家兼教育家，1867年著《视语》，设计了一套音标表示发音器官的实际位置，后改称“器官音标”（Organic Alphabet）。

94 broad Romic　[ˈrəʊmɪk]宽式罗马字音标。这是英国语言学家Henry Sweet创制的一种用罗马字母标识的音标，分“宽”、“狭”（narrow）两式，“狭式”比“宽式”更精密。

95 get her on the phonograph　用录音器把她（说的话）录下来。on the phonograph是her的宾语补足语。

96 can turn her on as often as you like可以随时打开她的（录音）。as often as you like你想（打开）多少次就多少次。

97 written transcript　[ˈtrænskrɪpt]书写的音标。

98 in state　庄严地；是习语。

99 ostrich [ˈɒstrɪtʃ] feathers [ˈfeðəz]鸵鸟毛。

100 The pathos [ˈpeɪθɒs] of this deplorable figure凄惨动人的寒酸形象，后接touches（感动）Pickering。

101 with its innocent [ˈɪnəsnt] vanity [ˈvænəti] and consequential [ˌkɒnsɪˈkwenʃl] air带着天真的虚荣自满和自以为了不起的神气。its指这形象（figure's）。

102 has already straightened himself in the presence of Mrs Pearce　在Mrs Pearce出现的时候已经站立起来。Pickering的彬彬有礼和Higgins的任性放肆始终形成鲜明对比。

103 the only distinction he makes between men and women　他对（待）男女的唯一区别。西俗对妇女应更有礼貌，而Higgins不但不分男女，而且不分尊卑，都不重礼貌。

104 when he is neither bullying nor exclaiming to the heavens against some featherweight cross　当他既不盛气凌人，又不为了些鸡毛蒜皮的糟心事发誓赌咒时。cross从“十字架”转义为“折磨”、“苦难”。

105 coaxes women as a child coaxes its nurse when it wants to get anything out of her　像小孩子为了得到某种东西时哄保姆那样去哄女人。coaxes用甜言蜜语劝诱。its和it代child('s)；英语中常用it代baby或幼小的child，相反常用she代国家、船舶等。

106 brusquely[ˈbruːskli]粗暴地（说）。

107 recognizing [ˈrekəɡnaɪzɪŋ] her见面而认出她（就是曾经在一起避雨的卖花女）。

108 unconcealed disappointment毫不掩饰的失望；明显表现出来的失望。

109 making an intolerable grievance of it　把这（识出就是卖花女）当作是无法忍受的倒霉事，叫苦连天。

110 Why这里是感叹词，表示发现什么或不耐烦等意思。

111 the girl I jotted down　我（把她的口音）记录下来的那个姑娘，girl后省略whom。jotted简略地摘记；草草写下。

112 no use = of no use.无用的。是习语，与(of) no good同义。

113 lingo　方言；难懂的语言。犹如说某人讲的像“外国话”，带有开玩笑的贬义。

114 Be off with you　去！滚开！这里的with没有具体意义，只是与副词连用构成祈使句。

115 Dont you be so saucy.　你不要这样无礼；Dont you是加强Dont的语气。saucy （对尊长）无礼的。

116 aint (= haven't) heard what I come for yet你还没有听我说我是为什么来的。这里come也可用came。yet修饰heard。

117 instructions　作“命令”、“指示”解是可数名词，且常作复数；作“教育”、“训练”解是不可数名词。

118 Did you tell him I come in a taxi?在过去时Did tell后面的间接引语中的这个come按严格的语法应用had come，实际上通常用came。

119 Nonsense　胡说！这里作感叹词。

120 girl单独girl一词作呼唤语很不客气，与亲热地称my girl截然不同。

121 What do you think a gentleman like Mr Higgins cares what you came in?你认为像Higgins先生这样的绅士会在意你是乘什么来的吗？cares的宾语是what you came in这个宾语从句；句首的what是状语宾语（adverbial objective），修饰cares。cares把……放在心上；关心。

122 Oh, we are proud!噢，我们这种人是光明正大的；别把我们看作不自珍重的人。

123 aint (= isn't) above giving lessons并非不肯授课。above不屑……的。

124 not him　= He is not above giving lessons.重复以强调。him用宾格代替主格，常见于这种上下文的口语中。

125 I aint (= haven't) come here to ask for any compliment [ˈkɒmplɪmənt]我不是来讨你恭维的；不因为我出钱请你教课，就要你把我当个好主顾那样来奉承我。

126 if my money's not good enough I can go elsewhere　假如我（出）的钱（你嫌）不够的话，我可以到别处去（请教）。

127 yə-oo　= you.为要强调you，读成了[jəuː]。

128 have lessons　听课；上课。

129 And to pay for em tə-oo = And I'm to pay for them (the lessons), too.而且我听课是要付钱的。

130 make no mistake　别弄错；我说的话是千真万确的。

131 stupent　感到惊慌失措。

132 Well!!!这个感叹词可以表示许多意思，这里表示惊讶，后面用三个惊叹号，表示非常非常惊讶。

133 recovering his breath with a gasp （被卖花女气得）喘着粗气才能恢复呼吸。

134 What do you expect me to say to you?你道我会对你说什么？What是say的宾语。expect忖度；不是“希望”的意思，如：You're not expected.没想到你会来；不是“不希望你来”。

135 Well　这个Well和上面的“Well!!!”不同，仅是个语气词，有“那么”的意思。

136 if you was a gentleman, you might ask me to sit down这是虚拟语气，you was应作you were; you might ask是ask的委婉说法，言外有责怪Higgins的意思。

137 bringing you business　[ˈbɪznəs]给你带生意来。

138 baggage　原是“行李”的意思，这里是对女人的蔑称，犹言“贱货”、“婊子”。Higgins身为专家、教授，举止言语极其粗鲁。

139 or = otherwise.否则。

140 running away in terror　因恐惧而逃跑。

141 turns at bay处于困兽斗的状态。这是习语，常作turn to bay、stand at bay、be at bay。

142 wounded　[ˈwuːndɪd]这是由wound的过去分词变成的形容词，原义“受伤的”，转义“受了委屈的”。注意：wound（伤），不要和wind [waɪnd]（绕、绞）的过去时和过去分词wound混淆。

143 offered　提供；主动提出。

144 But what is it you want?　it后面省略关系代词that；过去的修辞学上常说句首不能用And或But，事实上这很普遍。

145 stead of sellin　= instead of selling. stead是误用；又，没受过教育的人常把-ing读作-in，即把[ŋ]读作[n]。

146 Tottenham [ˈtɒtnəm] Court Road伦敦街名。

147 take　雇用；录用。

148 genteel [dʒenˈtiːl]这里该用genteelly，意思为“斯文地”，此词常讽刺地含有“假斯文”的意思。

149 asking any favor　要求任何恩惠。ask作“请求”解时，通常限于请求无形的东西，而ask for则请求有形、无形的东西一般都可以用，如ask for a ticket、ask for help。

150 zif I was dirt　= as if I was dirt.仿佛我是垃圾；当我垃圾（不当我人）。as if仿佛；后面用虚拟语气，was也可用were。dirt垃圾。是不可数名词，不能说a dirt或dirts。

151 could afford to pay　付得起。afford作“负担得起”解时，常与can连用，后面接不定式，但有时也可直接接名词或代词，如can't afford a new car = can't afford to buy a new car。当然文中afford to pay Mr Higgins不能说afford Mr Higgins。

152 Why shouldnt I?　= Why shouldnt I think I can afford to pay Mr Higgins?为什么我不能认为我是付得起Higgins先生的（学费）呢？

153 I know what lessons cost as well as you do我同你（们）一样清楚地知道学费要多少。what是疑问代词，作cost的宾语。as well as you do = as well as you know what lessons cost这里as well as ...是“像……一样清楚（well）”；不要和习语as well as混淆，如：He gives me clothes as well as food.他不但给我吃的，还给我穿的。

154 coming back to him, triumphant　[traɪˈʌmfənt] （从钢琴处）回到他面前，得意洋洋。这里triumphant是形容词，作表语，也称主语补足语，说卖花女回到Higgins面前后的神态，倘改用副词triumphantly，那就修饰coming，是“得意洋洋地回到他面前”的意思，说的是回过来时一路上的神态。

155 Now youre talking!现在你（才像是）说人话了；你现在这样说才对（我们可以谈下去了）。言下之意，Higgins刚才蛮横无理，简直是胡闹。这是口语中的惯用语。

156 youd come off it　= you would come off it.你会不再胡闹的。come off it是口语中的习语，是“不再胡说八道”、“不再胡闹”的意思。

157 what you chucked at me last night　昨夜你扔给我的东西（即一大把金银钱币）。what是chucked的宾语。

158 confidentially [ˌkɒnfɪˈdenʃəli]私下地；不足与外人道。书信、文件上写个confidential，表示“秘密”。

159 had a drop in　喝了点酒（喝醉了，所以会给那么多钱）。

160 peremptorily　[pəˈremptrəli]命令式地；专横地。

161 if youre going to make a compliment of it –　如果你把这（叫我坐）作为一种歉意；如果你客气点叫我坐（那么我就坐）。破折号表示话未说完就被打断了。

162 thundering　怒喝（犹如雷鸣）。

163 Do as youre told.　= Do as youre told to do.

164 half rebellious [rɪˈbeljəs], half bewildered一半对抗，一半惶惑；既要反抗，又不知所措。

165 courteous　[ˈkɜːtɪəs]彬彬有礼的；殷勤的。

166 Liza　[ˈlaɪzə]这里应作The Flower Girl，因为接下去又用The Flower Girl，且方始报出Liza的名字。

167 Dont mind if I do. = I dont mind if I sit down. Dont不要误解为祈使语气而认为= You dont。mind这里作“反对”解，全句字面意思是：“假如我坐，我也不反对”，实际是“我并不反对坐下”，即“我坐下好了”。

168 Doolittle　[ˈduːlɪtl]姓氏。

169 Eliza [ɪˈlaɪzə], Elizabeth [ɪˈlɪzəbəθ], Betsy [ˈbetsi] and Bess [bes]这一行起的四行是Higgins和Pickering一唱一和拿Liza的名字开玩笑的一首打油诗。Eliza、Betsy、Bess，跟Liza一样，都是Elizabeth的昵称，诗中作为四个人。

170 bird's nes'　= bird's nest.鸟巢。也作bird-nest。这里nest省去t，为和上行末的Bess押韵。

171 apiece　各；每人。

172 laugh heartily at　因……而开怀大笑。注意：这里laugh和at不构成习语；习语laugh at作“嘲笑”、“对……付之一笑”解。

173 silly　傻的；无聊的。

174 speak sensible　= speak sensibly.合情合理地说话。

175 Come back to business　言归正传。

176 A lady friend of mine　我的一个上等女性朋友。卖花女在当时的社会中自不算“上等”。

177 eighteenpence　十八便士。当时英国币制，一先令=十二便士。这里不说a shilling and sixpence或a shilling and a half，而说eighteenpence，犹如英语中往往不说a year and a half等，而说eighteen months。

178 wouldnt have the face to ask me the same for teaching me my own language as you would for French　总没有这脸皮（总不好意思）要求我付同你教法语一样的学费来教我学母语；要你教母语总该比要你教外国语的学费低些。as you would for French = as you would ask (me) for teaching (me) French.这里的as是代词，和上面the same呼应，在它引起的从句中作ask的直接宾语（间接宾语是me）。ask接双宾语，如此处作“请求”、“要求”解外，也可作“问”解，如：ask him a question（问他个问题）。the same同样的学费。

179 Take it or leave it.　要么接受，要么放弃；（你肯收一先令）就收它，否则就不收它（我不请你教就是）。

180 walking up and down the room　在房间里走来走去。up and down是习语，虽有“上上下下”、“前前后后”等多种意义，但此处未必是说“向舞台的后部（up）和向舞台的前部（down）”来回走。

181 rattling使发出叮叮当当（或格格）的声音。

182 consider a shilling, not as a simple shilling, but as a percentage of this girl's income　把一个先令不单纯看作是一个先令，而看作是这个姑娘（全部）收入的百分之几。consider接复合宾语，中间通常不用as，如consider him a true scholar（认为他是真正的学者）；这里用as是“作为……（来考虑）”。not ... but ...不是……，而是……；是习语。注意：not和but后面的语法成分必须对等，如：He is not an actor, but a singer.他不是演员，而是歌手。不能说He is not an actor, but is a singer.

183 it works out as fully equivalent [ɪˈkwɪvələnt] to这（一先令）算起来足足抵得上……

184 guineas　[ˈɡɪnɪz]基尼；旧时英国金币，值二十一先令，也作为二十一先令的计值单位。现在作为计值单位，是一又百分之五镑，即一镑五便士（新币制一镑=一百便士）。

185 from a millionaire [mɪljəˈneə]百万富翁拿出来的。

186 How so?　怎么会是这样的？这是口语中的惯用语。

187 Figure [ˈfɪgə] it out.你把它算出来看。这里的it指Higgins刚才所说卖花女的一先令相当于百万富翁的六、七十基尼的说法。

188 has有（多少）收入；= has an income of。

189 £150一百五十英镑，读作one hundred and fifty pounds或pound one hundred fifty。

190 two-fifths　五分之二。fifth除作“第五”解外，还可作“五分之一”解，并可用复数（其他序数词也是这样）；二个五分之一就是五分之二。但二分之一是half，不是second。

191 It's handsome.　这（一课的学费六十英镑）是可观的（数目）。

192 By George发誓用语，表示“真的”；有时只是激动地感叹。这用语现在似乎已经过时了。

193 offer　出价。这里指愿付的学费金额。

194 get –　破折号表示话未说完被打断了；后面该有类似such a big sum，或至少有it等词。

195 Hold your tongue　住嘴；是习语；= Shut up。

196 touch your money　要你的钱。touch有“动脑筋取得”等意思。

197 touch you, with a broomstick　用扫帚柄打你。这里承上文所用touch，也用touch一词，而意义不同，是幽默的说法。

198 One would think you was my father.　人们会当你是我老子的（我老子对我才这样凶）。was正规英语中用were。这里用was (were)而不用are，是因为主句中用would而不是用will的缘故。

199 worse than two fathers to you　比两个老子更叫你难受。上文Liza说Higgins凶得好比是她老子，所以他这样说。

200 Here　这儿；喏；是拿东西给人时常用语。

201 offers her his silk handkerchief主动把他的丝手帕给她。handkerchief读[ˈhæŋkətʃɪf]，复数式是handkerchiefs [-tʃɪfs]，现在也读作[ˈhæŋkətʃiːf]，复数式作handkerchieves [-tʃiːvz]。

202 Whats this for?　这（手帕）作什么用？for的介词宾语是what。

203 To wipe your eyes.　= It's for you to wipe your eyes.

204 that feels moist摸上去觉得湿的。feels作“接触着感觉（如何）”解，类似用例如：The air feels cold.觉得空气冷。

205 Dont mistake the one for the other　不要把两者相互弄错；不要把手帕当袖子用、把袖子当手帕用。mistake A for B把A误作为B。

206 utterly　全然；一般修饰贬义词，如utterly ruined（彻底垮掉），utterly exhausted（精疲力竭）。

207 helplessly不知所措地；无可奈何地。

208 It's no use talking to her like that　那样对她讲没有用。It是虚词（expletive），除可以引起同位的不定式和名词性从句外，也可像这里这样引起同位的动名词，甚至名词，如：It's of vital importance education.教育是头等重要的。用动名词一般指当前的事实，用不定式一般指泛说的行为。

209 she doesnt do it that way at all　她根本不那样做的（不用手帕擦眼泪鼻涕的）。does not do用现在时表示经常的、习惯的动作。it指擦眼泪鼻涕。that way　= in that way.

210 Here! You give me that handkerchief.　不行！你把那块手帕给我！ Here!那可不行！（你抢去手帕这件事）太不应该啊！You give me ... = Give me ...祈使句中用You比不用You语气重。

211 gev (= gave) it to me, not to you.它（手帕）是给我的，不是给你的。注意：give the handkerchief to me也可用双宾语，说give me the handkerchief，但在用it的情况下，在英国比较少说give me it（往往说give it me）。这里to me和to you对照，更不能用双宾语了。

212 He did.　= He gave it to Liza.

213 resigning [rɪˈzaɪnɪŋ] herself使自己勉强服从；无可奈何。

214 Serve you right是习语，= It serves you right.它（这里是指Liza抓去手帕当自产）给了你应有的报应。也就是“活该”的意思。

215 What about the ambassador's garden party?　在第一幕中Higgins曾说要训练卖花女，使她在大使馆的游园会上冒充公爵夫人，这里问他这事怎么样。

216 make that good　实现那个话（使卖花女得以冒充公爵夫人）。make good是习语，作“实现（计划、诺言）”解。

217 bet you all the expenses of the experiment you cant do it跟你打赌——倘我输，由我负担（训练卖花女使之能冒充公爵夫人的）全部试验费用——你不可能办到这件事。在句法上，bet you的you是bet的间接宾语，you cant do it（you前省去连词that）这个从句是bet的直接宾语，expenses是状语宾语（adverbial objective）。

218 real good　= really good.口语中有将real作副词的。

219 tempted　受了诱惑，因而产生了兴趣。

220 deliciously low　美妙地低贱；低贱得可爱。这是萧伯纳的幽默之笔。修饰词和被修饰词词义褒贬相反，在修辞学上原有此一格，叫做“矛盾修饰法”（oxymoron）；英国大诗人弥尔顿（Milton）常用这种修辞手法，极端的例子可举丁尼生（Tennyson）的“and faith unfaithful kept him falsely true”（不忠的忠心使他一直虚假地真诚）。

221 horribly dirty脏得吓人；极脏。horribly在口语中可作“非常”解。

222 afore I come　= before I came. afore在古文和方言中= before，在航海用语中作“在（……）前面”解，= in front (of)。come此处应用came。

223 turning a girl's head　= turning her head with flattery.用恭维使她妄自尊大；捧得她忘乎所以。Higgins称她deliciously low和horribly dirty，当然不是恭维话。Pickering把这说成是恭维，是句俏皮话。

224 wont encourage [ɪnˈkʌrɪdʒ] him to do anything foolish不要鼓励他干出任何蠢事来。“蠢事”指训练卖花女冒充公爵夫人。

225 the idea grows on him　这想法（指训练卖花女冒充公爵夫人）在他头脑里滋长（越想越感兴趣）。

226 What is life but a series of inspired follies?除了一系列心血来潮的蠢事之外，人生是什么呢？人生只是一系列心血来潮的蠢事而已。but = except. inspired受灵感启示的；心血来潮的；兴之所致的。

227 The difficulty is to find them to do. Never lose a chance: it doesnt come every day.　困难在于找到这种心血来潮的蠢事去干。（找到也不容易）不要失掉（这种干蠢事的）机会，这种机会不是每天都有的。

228 make a duchess of this draggletailed guttersnipe　（通过语音训练）使这个破破烂烂的穷女孩变成公爵夫人。draggletailed是由draggle（拖得又脏又湿）+tail（尾巴）+ ed构成的，作“懒散邋遢的”解，用于女人，与slattern同义。guttersnipe是gutter（沟）和snipe（鹬，也作“可鄙的人”解）构成的，是对流浪儿或拾荒者的蔑称。

229 this view of her　对她的这个看法，即把她看作是破烂的穷女孩。比较：this view of hers（她的这种看法）。

230 carried away　由于激动而失去自制；得意忘形。

231 in six months = I shall make a duchess of this draggletailed guttersnipe in six months.

232 has a good ear and a quick tongue有能辨别各种发音的耳朵和能很快学会发各种音的舌头。

233 take her anywhere and pass her off as anything　带她到任何地方去，使她冒充任何（高贵的）人物。

234 now! this moment!　= We'll start now! We'll start this moment. Higgins的激动和急躁，活现纸上，如闻其声。

235 Monkey Brand　= Use（或Clean her with）Monkey Brand. Monkey Brand猴牌洗涤剂。当是去污粉之类，不是一般盥洗用的香皂。

236 it wont come off　污垢洗不掉。it这里没有先行词；泛指污垢。

237 a good fire烧得很旺的炉火。

238 Yes; but –　= Yes, there is a good fire in the kitchen, but –旺的炉火是有，可是——（话未说完。）

239 storming on　继续大发雷霆；不待Mrs Pearce话说完，他自管自大叫大嚷。

240 Ring up Whiteley [ˈwaɪtli] or somebody for new ones.打电话给Whiteley或（别的）什么人，叫他送新衣服来。Whiteley当是个成衣商。ones = clothes.

241 brown paper　牛皮纸。

242 til = till.

243 Youre no gentleman　你不是正派的上等人。这里no = not a，但又与not a有不同意义，例如：He is not a scholar.他不是学者（可能是干部、画家等）。而He is no scholar却有“此人不学无术，根本不是什么学者”的意思。youre not按语法= youre not a gentleman，而承上文，只是重复强调Youre no gentleman。

244 what the like of you are　像你这样的一类人是什么样的人。

245 Lisson Grove prudery　[ˈpruːdəri]贫民区的假正经。Lisson Grove卖花女居住的贫民区，见第一幕。prudery规矩特多、忌讳特多；过分拘谨；假正经。

246 Youve got to　你必须；你得。have got to口语，= have to；口语中还有说成got to的。

247 wallop　[ˈwɒləp]痛打；是口语，犹如我们说“揍”。

248 Ive no place to put her　不必说Ive no place to put her in。

249 dustbin　[ˈdʌstbɪn]垃圾箱。这是英国语；美国称ash can。

250 come　得了。这里有感叹词的意味，叫人注意。

251 walk over everybody　对每个人都强凶霸道，不顾人家怎样想。walk over是习语；它还有一个意义是“轻易胜过”。

252 subsides　[səbˈsaɪdz]平息下来。subside作“平息”解时，可用于The debators subsided，也可用于His anger（或excitement等）subsided。

253 The hurricane [ˈhʌrɪkən] is succeeded by a zephyr [ˈzefə] of amiable [ˈeɪmɪəbl] surprise [səˈpraɪz]照字面译为“飓风被和蔼的惊讶的微风所替代。”实际是说：Higgins原在大发雷霆，Mrs Pearce说他强凶霸道，他还不自知，所以觉得惊讶，但是他对这指责并不暴跳如雷，而是转而和蔼地作了辩解。hurricane和zephyr都是比喻。

254 professional exquisiteness　([ˈekskwɪzɪtnɪs]，现在也读作[ɪkˈskwɪzɪtnɪs])

255 of modulation专家的优美音调。modulation这里是指音调的抑扬顿挫。收音机上的FM就是frequency modulation（频率调制，即“调频”）的简写。

256 I walk over everybody!我对任何人都强凶霸道吗！（别人可能强凶霸道）我才不强凶霸道呐！

257 help her to prepare and fit herself for her new station in life　帮助她准备和适应（她作为公爵夫人等的）新的社会地位。help her后面接不定式时，英国用法往往有不定式记号to，美国不用to, prepare和fit合用反身代词herself和介词for。prepare oneself for和prepare for意义基本相同，常可互用，但应指出：（1）prepare for比prepare oneself for普遍，更口语化；（2）prepare himself for bad news（对坏消息作好思想准备）之类的用例中，不能用prepare for；（3）prepare for the examination单纯是“准备考试”的意思，而prepare oneself for the examination是指“为某种重要考试（如留学考试）作好各方面的准备”。

258 If　是“如果说”的意思。

259 express myself clearly　把自己（的意思）表达得清楚，即“表达清楚”。

260 it指I did not express myself clearly这件事。

261 hurt her delicacy [ˈdelɪkəsi], or yours伤害她的或者你们的脆弱的情感。

262 steals back to　偷偷地回到……；不声不响地回到……。

263 take a girl up like that　那样（随便）捡个姑娘（收留下来）。

264 Why not?　= Why can I not（或can't I）take up a girl like that.

265 Why not!　这里承接上文而用惊叹号，表示“还问得出为什么不！”

266 Garn!　去你的！得了吧！见第一章注。

267 There!　这里是感叹词，引起注意，等于说Look (here)!

268 Married indeed!真结婚了！真有结婚了的事！这是反话。

269 looks a worn out drudge [drʌdʒ] of fifty a year after she's married结婚一年之后看上去就像个五十来岁油尽灯枯的苦工。worn out通常写作worn-out，是从wear out（使）疲惫、（使）耗尽衍生的形容词。a year修饰after。she's = she is。

270 Whood marry me?　= Who would marry me?是虚拟语气，省略诸如even if I should like to get married的条件从句。

271 resorting [rɪˈzɔːtɪŋ] to诉诸；求助于；采取（手段）。

272 thrillingly beautiful惊人地漂亮的；悦耳之极的。

273 elocutionary [ˌeləˈkjuːʃənəri] style朗诵的语调。

274 the streets will be strewn with the bodies of men shooting themselves for your sake街上会铺满为你而开枪自杀的男人们的尸体。意思是：“多少男人会因得不到你而自杀呢”。shooting是现在分词，修饰men。

275 before Ive done with you　在我结束对你的训练之前；不消等到我把你完全训练成贵妇人（就有许许多多人争着要娶你为妻）。have done with停止，结束；是习语。在美国也作be done with。注意：不要和另一习语do with（对付；容忍）相混。

276 squaring herself　摆架势；是口语。

277 off his chump　头脑失常；发疯；是俚语；= off his head。chump原义“厚木块”、“厚肉块”，俚语中作“头”解。

278 I dont want no balmies [ˈbɑːmɪz] teaching me.我不要疯子来教我。这里dont和no是“双重否定”。balmies teaching me是动名词词组构成的复合宾语。balmies原义“香的”等，俚语中= barmy，愚蠢的；疯的。原是形容词，这里用作名词。

279 wounded in his tenderest point by her insensibility to his elocution　她对他（音调优美）的朗诵无动于衷刺痛了他（感情）的最敏感处。tender point （身体上）最易感到酸痛之处，也称tender spot。

280 Oh, indeed!　哦，当真！（表示惊讶、讽刺等。）

281 I'm mad, am I?这是一种比较少见的句型。一个陈述句后面加上一个“附加疑问句”（tag question），一般是前后两句，一正一反或一反一正；这里前句与后句都是肯定句，带有愤怒的反问口气；“我疯了，我疯吗？”其实自己不承认疯。

282 Nah-ow.　= Now.这里有感叹词的作用，可以表示各种意思，这里有“瞧你”的意思。

283 comes of　因……而得到的结果。what comes of = the result of.

284 This way, please.　= Come this way, please.这是口语中引路的常用语。

285 in tears　流着泪。注意：tear，一滴眼泪；是可数名词，读[tɪə]；作“撕”解的动词tear读[teə]。

286 I wouldnt have taken them.这是虚拟语气；（即使买了衣服来给我）我也不会拿的。

287 on her reluctant way to the door　在她并不情愿地向门口走去的途中。注意reluctant way之类不同于中国说法的“外国话”。

288 wicked　[ˈwɪkɪd]邪恶的。口语中作“讨厌的”、“令人不愉快的”解，这里就是这意思。下文Mrs Pearce称Higgins才是wicked，当然不可能说她主人“邪恶”。

289 my return　我所得到的报答。

290 take you out of the gutter把你从泥沟里拉出来。gutter原义“沟”，转义“民居住处”；the gutter再转作“贫困”、“卑贱”解。

291 It's you that are wicked　不好的是你；是你不好。这种句型的说法是强调you are wicked中的you。照语法，that是关系代词，它的先行词是It，这样，that后面的动词应用is，但习惯上常把you做that的先行词，因而that后用are；而且也有把这里的that改用who的，但不很普遍。

292 aint got no parents　没有父母。aint = haven't. haven't got（口语）= haven't.这里又是“双重否定”。

293 big大。原指体积而言，这里= grown-up / old。

294 earn my own living我自己谋生。

295 turned me out　把我赶出来。turned和前面told并列。

296 Her that　照语法该作She that或She who；但口语中一般用her而不用she。

297 stepmother　后母。前一句说没有mother是说亲生母亲已经不在。从后面剧情中知道，这个“后母”实际上当时并没有同Liza的父亲正式结婚；结婚是在他突然继承了一笔财产，成了暴发户之后的事。

298 I done without them应作I have done without them（我已经不需要他们了）。do without没有……也行；是习语。

299 what on earth is all this fuss about　这一切的大惊小怪都是干吗呀？完全无需小题大做。on earth强调what。about是介词，what是它的宾语。

300 anybody but me　除我之外的任何人。but = except.

301 adopt her　收养她；收她为养女。

302 whats to become of her　她将变成什么呢；她今后怎么样。口语中常说What has become of him（或it等）?他（这件事等）现在情况怎么样了？

303 Is she to be paid anything?要付她点钱吗？动词be（这里是Is）后接不定式表示“计划”、“规定”等。

304 Do be　Be的强调式，Do要重读。

305 whatever is necessary　需要的一切；比what (= that which) is necessary语气强。

306 put it down in the housekeeping book把它（付出的钱）记在家用账上。book账簿。簿记学叫bookkeeping。

307 She'll only drink　这里有“徒然让她去喝酒”的意思。

308 turning on him　突然（怀着恶意地）转身向他。

309 brute [bru:t]原义“畜生”；这里指“蛮不讲理的人”。

310 lie原义“谎话”，这里只是“胡说”的意思。

311 sign of liquor　[ˈlɪkə]喝酒的痕迹。

312 Does it occur to you, Higgins, that ...　Higgins，你想到过……吗。occur to you在你头脑中出现；被你想到。it是虚词，引起后面与它同位的that从句。

313 has some feelings　有情感（上的反应）。注意：这里的feelings即使前面没有some，也用复数，同hurt somebody's feelings（伤某人感情）等语中的feelings一样。

314 Not any feelings that we need bother about.　= She has not any feelings that we need bother about.她没有需要我们去烦心的情感；她的情感不关我们的事。need在否定句和疑问句中作助动词，所以bother前没有to。

315 Have you　= Have you any feelings that we need bother about?

316 I got my feelings same as anyone else　= Ive (got) my feelings just as anyone else(has his feelings). got这里不是过去时，而是在不正规的口语中作have got用，= have。same是副词，same as应用just as（这里as是连词）。

317 the difficulty　难处。指Liza不但语音是土腔，而且语法也不对，还要教她语法，这就困难了。

318 To get her to talk grammar　= The difficulty is to get her to talk grammar. talk grammar = talk grammatically.符合语法地说话。

319 I want to talk like a lady in a flower-shop.　我要讲话讲得像花店里的上等女子；像花店里的女店员一样说话，而不再用街头的卖花女的语言。

320 Will you please keep to the point, Mr Higgins.　请你不要岔开话，Higgins先生。Will you please ... = Please.所以虽是主谓倒装的疑问句形式，句末不用问号而用句号。keep to the point扣住论点；不离主题。

321 on what terms　以什么条件。注意：terms作“条件”解时用复数，前面介词用on。

322 wages　工钱；常用复数。比较：wages指支付体力劳动的“工钱”；salary指支付脑力劳动的“薪金”，pay统指“工资”，军饷称soldier's pay。

323 look ahead a little　稍微向前看看；考虑得远一些。

324 her own business, not yours　她自己的事，不是你的事（与你无关）。

325 feeling heart　同情的心；恻隐之心。

326 dont care for nothing but yourself不顾别人，只顾自己。这是“双重否定”，dont care应作care。nothing but除……之外没有其他；只有。这是习语。比较：另一习语anything but：除……之外的任何一切；根本不……。这两个习语中的but都是介词，= except。

327 takes the floor　站起来（准备走开）。take the floor有时作“发言”解，这里不是。

328 Ive had enough of this.　我对这（蛮横无理）已经受够了。

329 making for the door走向门口。make for向……前进；是习语。

330 chocolate cream　奶油夹心巧克力糖。cream（奶油）原是不可数名词，和chocolate构成复合词，是一块一块的糖，所以是可数名词。

331 his eyes suddenly beginning to twinkle with mischief [ˈmɪstɪf]他的眼睛忽然开始闪闪发出恶作剧的光芒。这是个“独立结构”，表示snatching时的状态。

332 halting, tempted　受到引诱（想吃）而停止（往门口跑）。tempted是过去分词，修饰halting。

333 what might be in them它们（指巧克力糖）里面可能会有什么。might比may表示更大的嫌疑。

334 Ive heard of girls being drugged by the like of you　我听到过姑娘们被像你这样的人用药物麻醉。girls being drugged ...是heard of的复合宾语。drug名词作“药”解，也指“麻醉药”、“毒品”。作动词用时只指“（使）服麻醉药”、“（使）受到麻醉药的毒而失去知觉”解。

335 whips out his penknife　一下掏出小摺刀（旧称“小洋刀”）。whips out已见第一幕whipping out his book一语中。

336 bolts　原义“射”、“窜”，这里作“囫囵吞下”解。

337 Pledge [pledʒ] of good faith真诚的保证；自己吃给她看，保证不是给她吃什么毒品。Pledge是It（指我吃一半）is a（或my）ledge的省略。

338 the other = the other half.

339 pops　pop原是个拟声词，表示“砰”或“啪”的一声。用作动词，表示突然发生的动作；这里是“突然投掷”、“突然塞进”的意思。

340 shall　shall用于第二、第三人称表示说话人的意向，读[ʃæl]，不读[ʃəl]，这里和后面好几处you shall中的shall都表示说话人“允诺”，有时也表示“命令”、“威吓”。

341 live on them　靠（吃）巧克力糖生活。

342 Eh?用以征求对方同意；好不好？是不是？

343 disposed of　处理掉；这里是“吃掉”。

344 wouldnt have ate it　本来不要吃的。这是虚拟语气，表示与过去的事实相反，实际上她已经吃了。ate是eaten之误。

345 only这里是连词，= but。

346 ladylike像贵妇人的；有大家闺秀风度的。

347 Listen　听（我讲）；实际上只是叫对方注意。

348 as good a right to take a taxi as anyone else　有同任何其他人一样充分的乘出租车的权利。as anyone else = as anyone else has a good right to take a taxi.

349 You have　= You have as good a right to take a taxi as anyone else.

350 go up and down and round the town　在城里来来去去兜圈子。up and down来来去去；到处；是习语。round原义“围绕着”，实际上也是“在……的各处”的意思；美国多用around。go round常是“（在……）到处兜一兜”的意思。

351 At her age!　= She should think of the future at her age! （你竟然说）她这年纪（该想想未来）！意思是“她（这年纪）才无需考虑未来呐！”

352 Time enough to think of the future when you havnt any future to think of = There is time enough .../ You have time enough ...到你没有任何未来可以考虑的时候再去考虑未来，也有足够的时间（也不迟）。“未来”就是“前途”，即：到无路可走的时候，再找出路也不迟。这是萧伯纳典型的俏皮话。you泛指“任何人”，也可理解为是对Liza说的。future未来；也就是“（自己的）前途”。

353 do as this lady does　照这位太太（指Mrs Pearce）那样做。冒号后面是Mrs Pearce的做法。

354 think of other people's futures; but never think of your own　考虑别人的未来，而决不考虑你自己的未来。Higgins说Mrs Pearce的做法是这样的，且把它说得像条处世格言，这分明是讽刺Mrs Pearce专管闲事而不想想自己的未来，可能她自己的未来还没有着落呢。futures别人各有各的未来或前途，故用复数。your own = your own future.

355 chocolates, and taxis, and gold, and diamonds　[ˈdaɪəməndz]叠用一连串and，比单用末一个and语气强。全不用and也比用一个and语气强。

356 with an attempt at dignity　要作出庄严的样子。

357 remain so　= remain a good girl.依旧是个正经姑娘。

358 under the care of在……照管下。

359 an officer in the Guards　禁卫军军官。the Guards王家禁卫军。

360 moustache　[məˈstɑːʃ]小胡子。“大胡子”是beard [bɪəd]。

361 the son of a marquis [ˈmɑːkwɪs], who will disinherit [ˌdɪsɪnˈherɪt] him for marrying you侯爵的儿子，侯爵会因为他儿子娶了你而剥夺他儿子的继承权。who前后有逗号，= and he(marquis)，它引起的从句叫做“非限制性定语从句”，别于“限制性定语从句”，如：He is the first (man) who got the news.他是第一个得到消息的人。（who前没有逗号。）for表示原因。

362 will relent　会变得仁慈；宽恕他儿子娶Liza为妻。

363 but　在Excuse me、sorry等语后常用but接所要请人原谅的事。

364 If this girl is to put herself in your hands　要是想使这个姑娘自愿把自己交托给你（由你支配）。

365 she must understand thoroughly what she's doing　必须使她彻底了解她正在做的是怎么回事。

366 How can she?　= How can she understand thoroughly what she's doing?

367 incapable of不能；不会。它同capable一样，后面接介词of。比较：able和unable后面接不定式。

368 If we did, would we ever do it?　如果我们明白（我们所做的事）的话，我们还会去做它吗？这是虚拟语气，意思是“正因为我们不明白，所以我们才会去做”。did = understood what we were（或are）doing. ever用在疑问句中加强语气。do it = do what we are doing.

369 Very clever　= That（你说的话）is very clever.

370 not to the present point无关现在的主题；说到题外去了。

371 Miss Doolittle –　Liza从没有被人称过Doolittle小姐，所以Pickering这样称呼她，她受宠若惊，Pickering话还没说下去，她听见这称呼就大叫起来。

372 Thats all youll get out of Eliza.　那（Ah-ah-ow-oo的大叫声）就是你会从Eliza那里得到的一切；你只可能得到这样的反应。

373 No use explaining.　= It's no use explaining. It是可以引起同位的动名词，已见前注。

374 As a military man作为一个军人。

375 that　指“只可能得到这样的反应”。

376 Give her her orders对她下她（该执行）的命令。这句和Give her your orders（给她下你的命令）是一样意思。

377 thats enough for her那（指“给她下命令”）对她已经足够（不必跟她解释，反正解释她也不懂）。

378 good　好好的；与下文naughty and idle对照。

379 money　和lots to eat并列，是have的宾语；money后面接to buy chocolates和(to) take rides（乘车）in taxis两个并列的不定式词组修饰它。

380 sleep in the back kitchen among the black beetles　睡在后厨房蟑螂堆里。black beetles = cockroaches [ˈkɒkrəʊtʃɪz].蟑螂。among the black beetles与in the back kitchen并列，修饰sleep。

381 you shall go to Buckingham Palace in a carriage [ˈkærɪdʒ], beautifully dressed给你坐着四轮马车到白金汉王宫去，穿得漂漂亮亮的。beautifully dressed修饰you。

382 be taken by the police to the Tower [ˈtaʊə] of London被警察抓（去关）到伦敦塔里去。the police警察局；警务人员们。the Tower of London简称the Tower，在伦敦泰晤士河北岸，是十一世纪建造的王宫，后改为监狱，现又改作博物馆，收藏历代兵器尤多。

383 presumptuous　[prɪˈzʌmptjʊəs]自以为是的；放肆的。

384 you shall have a present of seven-and-sixpence to start life with as a lady in a shop　要使你得到的赠礼是：你将以七先令六便士的收入开始，从此做一个花店里的店员小姐。

385 a most ungrateful wicked girl　一个很不领情的坏姑娘。a most很；非常；和用以表示形容词最高级的the most（最）不同。ungrateful不领情的；忘恩负义的；也作“讨厌的”解。

386 the angels [ˈeɪndʒlz] will weep for you天使们要为你哭泣（婉惜她拒绝接受这条件）。

387 Can I put it more plainly and fairly　我还能把话说得更清楚、更公平吗？这是反问，意思是“我说得再明白、公平不过了”。

388 in private　私下。

389 consent to　同意。consent是不及物动词，介词用to，不能直接接宾语。

390 mean her any harm　对她怀恶意。mean这里接双宾语。

391 get what you call interested in people's accents对人们的口音感到所谓兴趣。what you call插在get interested（感到兴趣）两个词中间，= what is called = so-called。

392 what may happen to them or you　对他们或者对你（自己）会产生什么（后果）。

393 Bundle her off　把她撵走。

394 bully　[ˈbʊli]恃强欺弱的人。

395 I never asked to go to Bucknam Palace　我从没有要求过到Buckingham Palace。事实她是说过的，但并没有真要去白金汉宫。Liza这样辩解，无非说明自己并没有做过越轨的事，是个good girl。

396 I was never in trouble with the police, not me.我从来没有和警察（局）有过纠葛，我没有过。not me口语中习惯用not me，不用not I。

397 answer back　回嘴；顶嘴。

398 not if I'm going to have my head cut off　= I wont go near the King even if I'm going to have my head cut off.即使（要是不去就要）杀我的头，我也不要到国王跟前去。have my head cut off让我的头被砍掉；cut是过去分词，cut off作宾语补足语，my head和cut off之间有逻辑上的主谓关系，my head cut off构成复合宾语。

399 If I'd known what I was letting myself in for, I wouldnt have come here.假如我早知道我会遭遇到什么，我才不会来这里呢。这里表示与过去事实相反的虚拟语气，事实是早先不知道是这么回事，所以她来了。

400 say a word　说（几）句话；a word并不真限于“一个词”，犹如Drop me a line（简单写封信给我）中的a line不真限于“一行”。

401 dont owe him nothing　是“双重否定”，= dont owe him anything或owe him nothing，一点不欠他什么。

402 dont care不放在心上；不以为意。

403 be put upon　被欺骗；上当。

404 the same as anyone else = just as anyone else has his feelings.

405 plaints are no longer audible　[ˈɔːdəbl]抱怨声不再听得见。

406 greatly to her surprise　使她大为惊异。

407 scullery　[ˈskʌləri]厨房的洗涤间；碗碟贮藏室，即前面曾见的back kitchen。

408 There指the third floor（ground floor以上的第三层楼，即我国所说的“四楼”）。spare bedroom空余没有人住的卧室。

409 It's too good for the likes of me.　这（卧室）对我这样的人是太好了。the likes of me像我这样（贫穷或不行）的人；是口语。

410 I aint (= am not) a duchess yet我还不是公爵夫人。做了公爵夫人，才配住这卧室，碰这里的东西。

411 not missus　[ˈmɪsəz]不要称我missus（因为我不是这里的主妇）。missus口语；= missis [ˈmɪsɪz]，太太。

412 throws open the door猛推开门。

413 dressingroom, now modernized as a bathroom （原来的）梳妆室，现在已被加以现代化（加上现代设备），改作浴室。

414 Gawd　= God.是土音。

415 Whats this?是指着浴盆问的。

416 Funny sort of copper I call it. = I call it a funny sort of copper.我认为这是一种奇异的洗涤槽。Funny口语，作“奇怪的”解。copper金属洗涤槽，洗涤炊具或洗衣用；是可数名词，这里因用在sort of后面，所以不加冠词。

417 wash ourselves　洗澡。

418 Not me.　= I won't get into that (= the copper) and wet myself all over.在这种句型中，常用me代替I，已见前。

419 catch my death　= catch my death of cold.得可能致死的感冒；是习语。

420 did it指洗澡。

421 in cold water　前面加逗号，有“而且是用冷水”的意思。

422 Ugh!　惊叫声，可读作[ɜː]，表示“厌恶”、“惊吓”。

423 He's made of iron, that man.他那个人是铁打的。that man是He的同位语。

424 sit with him and the Colonel and be taught　和他（Higgins）和Pickering上校坐在一起，跟他们学习（语音）。

425 do the same　= has a bath every morning.

426 if you dont　= if you dont have a bath every morning.

427 I couldnt　是虚拟语气，= I couldn't do it if I had to do it，有“叫我洗我也不洗”的意思。

428 I dursnt [ˈdɜːsnt] = I dursn't do it if I had to do it. durst = dared; dursnt = dare not.

429 not what youd call a proper one　= Ive never had what you'd call a proper bath in my life.我一生没有洗过你们称为正规的澡。从下文看，Liza过去洗澡时衣服都不脱干净，更谈不上使用正式的浴缸。

430 cant be a nice girl inside if youre a dirty slut outside　如果你外表是个邋遢婆，就不能内心是个好姑娘。slut是“懒妇”、“荡妇”的意思，是个很不好听的字眼，所以Liza听了，“Boohoo!!!”地大哭起来。

431 get the bath ready　把洗澡水准备好。ready是宾语补足语，the bath ready构成复合宾语。

432 all tears　满面是泪的；是形容词短语。

433 I cant. = I can't take off all my clothes.

434 I wont. = I won't take off all my clothes.

435 I'm not used to it. = I'm not used to taking off all my clothes.我不习惯于把所有衣裳脱得精光。

436 Dont you take off all your clothes every night when you go to bed?　Mrs Pearce这样反问Liza，是因为她认为晚上睡觉总得脱光身上的衣服，换上睡衣的。

437 Why should I?　= Why should I take off all my clothes every night when I go to bed?

438 I should catch my death.这是虚拟语气，省略if I took off all my clothes when I go to bed。

439 What else have I to sleep in?　（除了白天随身穿的内衣外）我另外有什么衣裳可穿上睡觉的呢？Liza贫困，哪里另有睡衣之类？What是have的宾语。

440 will never do that again as long as you live here　你住在这里的期间，再不用那样做（再不用穿随身的内衣睡觉）。as long as在整个……的期间；只要是……

441 nightdress　妇女或儿童穿的直统的睡衣；也称nightgown。分衣裤的睡衣称pyjama(s)，美国称pajama(s)。

442 Do you mean change into cold things and lie awake shivering half the night?　你的意思是要我换上冰冷的衣裳，半个夜里躺在床上醒着发抖吗？change和lie都是不及物动词，在语法上是把动词change和lie用作省去了to的不定式。

443 or be thrown out and sent back to your flower basket　还是被撵出去，送回到你卖花的篮子那儿去（仍旧到街头去卖花）呢？

444 what the cold is to me　冷对我是怎么回事；我多怕冷。

445 hot water bottle　取暖的热水瓶或热水袋；不是保暖用的热水瓶，后者称vaccum flask、vaccum bottle或thermos bottle、thermos flask，或单称thermos。

446 Off with you and undress.　去，把衣服脱掉。Off with you走开；滚开；是习语；这里的Off可理解为前面省略动词Be, = Be off。也可当作不及物动词，作“离开”解。

447 if only I'd a known what a dreadful thing it is to be clean I'd never have come要是我早知道要弄干净是这么可怕，我决不会来（受这罪呢）。这是表示与过去事实相反的虚拟语气，事实是事先不知，所以来了。if only要是……就好；是习语。I'd a known = I should have known.土音把have的轻音[əv]读成[ə]，所以写成a。what是it is的表语；it是虚词，引起同位语to be clean。

448 I didnt know when I was well off.　过去我过好日子的时候，还不知道（要弄干净有这样可怕）。well off富裕；光景好；是习语。萧伯纳幽默地让Liza认为过去不脱衣服洗澡，不换睡衣睡觉是“过好日子”，发人深思。

449 leaves it partly open lest her prisoner should take to flight　（Mrs Pearce）让门半开着，（可以看守Liza），怕她的俘虏逃跑。lest恐怕；以防；是连词，它所引起的状语从句中一般用should。take to flight逃走；take to有“趋于”、“采取”的意思。

450 mixing hot and cold　调匀热水和冷水。

451 testing the result with the bath thermometer [θə'mɒmɪtə]用浴缸温度计测量（冷热水调匀后的）结果（温度）。

452 perfumes it with a handful of bath salts　加把浴盐使洗澡水香。perfume香水，香（名词），读[ˈpɜːfjuːm]。这里是及物动词（洒香水于……；使香），读[pɜː'fjuːm]。bath salts浴盐（使水软而香）。

453 a palmful [ˈpɑːmfʊl] of mustard[ˈmʌstəd]一手心的芥末。浴水中放芥末，当是为使皮肤发热袪寒。
