454 formidable [ˈfɔːmɪdəbl] looking看起来模样可怕的；也写作formidable-looking。

455 long handled　长柄的；也写作long-handled，是由形容词+名词+ ed构成的。

456 soaps it profusely [prəˈfjuːsli]把它（刷子）擦足了肥皂。soap作动词，既可作“上肥皂于……”解，如这里的soap a brush，又可作“用肥皂擦洗”解。

457 a ball of scented soap一块（圆形的）香皂。soap是物质名词，“一块肥皂”说a cake(或piece) of soap; a soap是“一种肥皂”。

458 with nothing on but the bath gown huddled tightly round her　除了胡乱地紧裹在她身上的浴衣之外，一点不穿什么；裤子也不穿，只包着一件浴衣。on被穿在身上。nothing but只有；是口语；but = except。

459 a piteous [ˈpɪtɪəs] spectacle of abject [ˈæbdʒekt] terror [ˈterə]一副可怜相，显出无可奈何的惊恐。

460 Take that thing off.　把那东西脱掉。that thing指bath gown。

461 reely　really的土音。

462 I never done应作Ive never done。

463 such a thing指脱掉光身包着的浴衣；脱得精光。

464 throwing Eliza down on her back　把Eliza仰面朝天推下（浴缸）。

465 sets to work　开始工作；开始替Eliza擦洗。set to开始；是习语；set可作不及物动词，如这里的set to work；也可作及物动词，如set her to work。to是介词，work是名词，不是动词。在set to scrubbing这样的结构中，可以认为to是介词，以动名词为介词宾语，也可认为to是副词，scrubbing是现在分词，是set to的补语。

466 having it out with　与……争辩明白；是习语；也作“与……决一雌雄”解。

467 seated himself astride of it with his arms on the back　（面朝椅背）跨坐在椅子上，手臂搭在椅背上。

468 Excuse the straight question　恕我直问。excuse的宾语可以是被请求原谅的人，也可以是被请求原谅的事情。

469 good character　性格好。

470 where women are concerned在涉及女人之处；关于女人方面。

471 very frequently　= I have very frequently met men of good character where women are concerned.

472 lifting himself on his hands to the level of the piano　用手撑着（钢琴），使自己升高到和钢琴一样高。

473 I havnt.　= I havn't met a man of good character where women are concerned.

474 becomes jealous, exacting, suspicious, and a damned nuisance　[ˈnjuːsns]变得妒忌，苛求，猜疑，（变成）讨厌之极的人。and所连接的应该是词类相同的词语，但这里and前面是三个形容词，and后面是一个名词词组，并列作becomes的表语，口语中常有这种情况。如在and后加becomes则and前后词语的词类相同，但读起来反不自然。

475 the moment　相当于连词as soon as或when。

476 tyrannical　[tɪˈrænɪkl]专横的。这是从tyrant [ˈtaɪrənt]（暴君；专横的人）派生的形容词。

477 let them into your life　让她们进入你的生活；你生活中有了女人。

478 driving at　意向；意指。

479 At what, for example?　= What, for example, are the woman and you driving at(respectively)?

480 Lord knows!　天知道！谁知道！= God knows! / Heaven knows!

481 live her own life　过她自己的生活。动词live和名词life是同源的，因此这里的life叫做“同源宾语”（cognate object）。

482 live his　= live his own life.

483 the wrong track错路；指一个不是要去的方向。“错路”虽不是专指，也未必只有一条，但习惯在wrong前面用the，而不用a，如买错了一本书，说Sorry to have bought the wrong book。

484 the other south　= the other wants to go south.这里的south和前面的north一样，是副词，= southward / to the south。

485 hate the east wind　讨厌东风。可能因为在英国，春天的东风比较阴冷，所以有人不喜欢东风。hate原义“恨”，口语中常作“不喜欢”、“讨厌”解。

486 sits down on the bench at the keyboard　在琴键跟前凳子上坐下。

487 So here I am所以我在这里（Higgins的laboratory里）。

488 a confirmed old bachelor [ˈbætʃələ]一个坚定的老独身汉。可以说前面省去being。

489 and likely to remain so　而且大概将一直如此（即保持独身）。

490 standing over him　居高临下似地站在他面前。stand over带有“监督”的意思。

491 be in this business　参加这事情（指训练Liza）。business这里不作“生意”解。

492 it's understood that ...　这是被默认（或不言而明）的；大家同意。

493 no advantage is to be taken of her position不占她（贫苦无依）的处境的便宜。take advantage of趁机利用。

494 That thing!　指Pickering所说希望大家同意的no advantage is to be taken of her position。

495 Sacred, I assure you. = I assure that she is sacred.神圣不可侵犯的。

496 teaching would be impossible unless pupils were sacred　除非学生是神圣不可侵犯的，否则教学无法进行。这是虚拟语气，意思是“必须尊重学生，才能施教”。unless = if ... not.

497 scores of　几十个；许多。score原义“二十”，单复数同形；用scores时，作“许多”解；和dozen(s)的用法相同。

498 the best looking women in the world　天下最漂亮的女人；是millionairesses [ˌmɪljəˈneərəsɪz]（有百万家财的女人）的同位语。Higgins的意思是说，他对“财貌双全”的女人尚且无动于衷，决不会对一个贫穷的“邋遢婆”动什么坏脑筋。best looking最好看的，也写作best-looking，是good-looking的最高级。in the world习语；强调语气，未必实有“在这世界上”的意思。

499 seasoned　由及物动词season（给予锻炼；使适应）的过去分词演变成的形容词，作“经过风雨的”、“老练的”解，这里指“不会为女色所动的”。

500 They might as well be blocks of wood她们不妨是（等于是）一块块木头。might as well不妨；……为宜；后面还可接第二个as，如：You might as well do it by yourself as ask for his help.你求他帮助，还不如自己做。

501 is it all right　事情（指帮Liza洗澡和对她的安排）顺利吗？没有问题吧？

502 if I may　= if I may trouble you with a word.这是客气话。

503 Dont burn that　别烧掉那个（指Liza的草帽，见下面舞台指示）。

504 curiosity　[ˌkjʊərɪˈɒsɪti]珍奇的东西；古玩。

505 had to promise her not to burn it　已经不得不答应她不烧掉这帽子。

506 had better put it in the oven [ˈʌvn] for a while最好在烘箱里烘一会儿（以消毒）。had better还是……好；后接没有to的不定式。

507 in the way　有妨碍的；碍事的；是习语。

508 Not in the least　= You are not in the least in the way. Not in the least毫不；是习语。

509 be very particular what you say before the girl在姑娘面前说话要非常注意。particular讲究的；这里指说话要文雅，忌粗俗、诅咒。注意：这里particular后直接接what you say，不及下面Higgins回答的particular about what I say的说法正规。

510 unmoved　不为所动；无视她主人的严肃（sternly）责问。

511 Now it doesnt matter before me　这（指说话不注意）在我面前是无所谓的。Now语气词，引起一个话头，没有具体意义。matter有关紧要。

512 I'm used to it我对这（说话不注意）已经（听）惯了。

513 swear before the girl　在这姑娘面前诅咒（指下文所说的用the devil等诅咒语）。

514 I swear!我诅咒！意思是“我才不诅咒呢！”

515 the habit　= the habit of swearing.

516 the devil [ˈdevl]原义“魔鬼”，用作诅咒语，加强what、where等疑问词的语气，如这里的What the devil。

517 Thats what I mean　我就是指那个；指你刚才脱口而出的What the devil（之类的诅咒）。

518 swear a great deal too much诅咒得实在太多。too much修饰swear; a great deal又修饰too。a great deal = much，这里作为状语短语。

519 I dont mind your damning and blasting, and what the devil　我不介意你用damn和blast（诅咒），和用what the devil（之类的诅咒语）。要是这里在What the devil和Where the devil等加上引号，更易理解。

520 this language from your lips　从你嘴里说出这样的话。这是个名词词组，构成单部句，可称为“名词句”。this language指Mrs Pearce说了的一连串诅咒语。顺便一提，有时language一词也单独有“诅咒语”之意，= bad language。

521 not to be put off　不让被打断说话。put off是习语，有“推迟”、“搪塞”、“去掉”等多种意义。

522 a certain word某一个词；从下文看，当指bloody，原义“出血的”、“血污的”，作为诅咒语，极为恶俗，尤为妇女所忌讳（taboo）。bloody作形容词，犹如“他妈的”，又可用作副词，有“非常”的意思，当然同样是非常恶俗的字眼。

523 It begins with the same letter as bath.　这个词的首字母和bath（的首字母）相同；指bloody这个词。as bath = as 'bath' begins with. as是关系代词。

524 knows no better　不懂得更多（而只能如此）；只懂这一点。它的反义词组是know better：懂得更多（而不至于如此）；后面还能跟than，如：He knows better than to quarrel.他知道不能吵嘴。这里的better是副词。

525 at her mother's knee （抱）在她母亲膝盖上（的时候），即“从小”的意思。注意：这里knee用单数；而on one's knees（跪着）中knees是复数；这是习惯用法。

526 charge myself with　指责自己犯……的过错；认（罪）。

527 adds　（说了其他的话之后）加上一句；补充说。

528 hiding an uneasy conscience[ˈkɒnʃəns] with a judicial [dʒʊˈdiʃəl] air做出慎重的样子，掩盖内心不安（因为他自己知道确实常要诅咒）。

529 Except　Except引起的这个介词短语在语法上是紧接上面一句的。

530 extreme and justifiable [ˈdʒʌstɪfaɪəbl] excitement极度的、有道理的激动；尽管是大发雷霆，却有道理。

531 Only this morning　就在今天早晨。不是“只有今天早晨”的意思。

532 applied it to your boots对你的鞋子使用诅咒；说到鞋子就用了诅咒语。英国人所说的boot，美国人往往称shoe；而美国人说的boot，英国人往往称high boot。

533 the butter　专指家里买着的或Higgins吃的butter，所以前面用定冠词，下面the brown bread同。

534 brown bread用糙面粉做的面包；麸皮面包。

535 Oh, that!　噢，（我道是什么，原来是）那个（指诅咒boots、butter和brown bread）。

536 Mere alliteration [əˌlɪtəˈreɪʃən] （我说bloody boots、bloody butter、bloody brown bread）只是双声而已。前后诸词的首字母相同（这里是b），在作诗法中称为alliteration（头韵法；犹如我国所称“双声叠韵”的“双声”）。

537 natural to a poet对诗人是自然的。修饰alliteration。萧伯纳在本剧序言中谈到语音学家Henry Sweet，私淑大诗人John Milton，而Higgins是以Sweet为原型的。所以这里Higgins以诗人自命，是萧伯纳幽默之笔。

538 whatever you choose to call it　不管你爱叫它什么。whatever在这里= no matter what，引起表示让步的状语从句；choose to = like to; it指说bloody boots等。

539 repeat　重复说；再说。

540 Is that all?　话说完了吗？要对人说的话说完后，常说That's all.

541 I mean not to be slovenly [ˈslʌvnli] about her dress我的意思是（她）不要衣衫不整洁。注意：这里I mean后面接不定式和I mean to go（我要去）的意义不同，前者not to be slovenly的主体不是mean的主语I，而是Liza；而后者to go的主体就是mean的主语I。slovenly是名词sloven（衣衫不整的人；懒散的人）+ ly构成的形容词。dress这里是不可数名词；统称“服装”。

542 leaving things about　把（用过的）东西到处乱放（不收拾）。

543 Just so.　正是如此；是口头常用习语。

544 call your attention to叫你注意。

545 It is these little things that matter　要紧的就是这些小事情。It是虚词，以that从句作它的同位从句。

546 Take care of the pence and the pounds will take care of themselves　这是谚语，直译“如果你当心便士，英镑自会当心自己（无需你当心）”。意思是“小钱省了，大钱自省”，推而广之，“小事谨慎，大事自成”。祈使句+ and +陈述句，表示“如果……，那么……”，这是一个习惯句型。pence penny有两个复数形式，指多个便士的硬币用pennies，计值用pence。

547 is as true of personal habits as of money　（谚语）对金钱是正确的，对个人习惯也同样正确。is的主语是一句谚语。as of money = as it is true of money.

548 comes to anchor　[ˈæŋkə] （坐）定下来；是习语，也作come to anchor，原义“抛锚”。

549 the air of a man in an unassailable position处于无懈可击的地位的人的神气。

550 might I ask you　比may I ask you婉转，是客气的说法。Might you = Please，也是客气说法。

551 come down to breakfast （从楼上）下来吃早饭。breakfast是名词。

552 not to use it as a napkin to the extent you do　不要像你那样经常把它（dressinggown，梳妆时穿的晨衣）当作餐巾用。to the extent（程度）you do = to the extent to which you use it as a napkin.

553 be so good as not to　请你不要……。be so good as to ... = please；是惯用客套语。

554 eat everything off the same plate用同一只盆子吃各种东西。按正式用餐规矩，一道菜用一只盆。注意这里用介词off。

555 to remember接would be so good as，和前面的not to eat并列。remember not to put the porridge saucepan out of your hand on the clean tablecloth记住不要放手把粥锅放在干净桌布上。remember后接简单不定式，是“记住，不要忘记去做（某事）”的意思；后接动名词时，意思是“记得（过去所做的事）”，如remember meeting him some time ago（记得过去什么时候见到过他）；但用了完成不定式，又是“记得（过去所做的事）”的意思，如remember to have met him some time ago。

556 it　指if从句中所讲的内容。

557 nearly choked yourself with a fishbone in the jam　差一点被果酱里的鱼骨头梗死。可见Higgins吃饭时不按规矩，乱七八糟。

558 routed from the hearthrug　从炉前地毯上败退下来。上文刚说Higgins站定在炉前地毯上一副无懈可击的神气，现在被Mrs Pearce历数他粗野的举止，只得认输，败下阵来。

559 in absence of mind　在心不在焉的时候。

560 By the way　顺便提起。Higgins认输，总不甘心，寻找话题，又图反扑。

561 smells most damnably [ˈdæmnəbli] of benzine [ˈbenziːn, benˈziːn]糟透地尽是汽油味道。smells发出……的气味，介词用of。benzine和benzene（苯）读音完全相同。most非常。damnably该死地；口语中又作“非常”解。

562 But if you will wipe your fingers –　话未说完，意思是：然而要是你肯（用抹布之类而不用晨衣）擦手指（晨衣不会肮脏到非用汽油清洗不可了）。

563 wipe them in my hair in future　今后在我头发上擦手指。Higgins使气说蛮话了。

564 finding himself thought capable of an unamiable [ʌnˈeɪmɪəbl] sentiment发觉自己被人认为会有不和蔼的情绪（发脾气）。thought过去分词，是finding的宾语补足语；himself和thought有逻辑上的主谓关系，构成复合宾语。

565 Might she use　比May she use婉转，更客气。

566 from abroad　从国外。abroad到国外；在国外；说“从国外”，要用from abroad。

567 put her back into her old things给她重新穿她的旧衣服。things用复数，指“个人用品”。

568 Anything you like.　= You may do anything (that) you like.

569 Here I am　（在我看来，）我就是这样一个人。

570 a shy, diffident sort of man一种羞怯、犹豫的人；这是I的同位语。注意：sort of、kind of后面的可数名词不用冠词。

571 grown-up and tremendous　成熟和了不起的。tremendous原义“可怕的”，口语中作“巨大的”、“非凡的”解。

572 chaps　家伙；人；与fellow和美国人说的guy一样，有熟不拘礼的用语，有时有鄙意。

573 And yet she's firmly persuaded [pəˈsweɪdɪd]然而她被使得深信；但她深信。

574 an arbitrary [ˈɑːbɪtrəri] overbearing bossing kind of person一种武断的傲慢的发号施令的人。这里三个形容词之间既无逗号，又无and，那就该理解为bossing修饰kind, over-bearing修饰bossing kind, arbitrary又修饰overbearing bossing kind；如有逗号或and，则三个形容词就并列修饰kind。bossing由名词boss（头目；老板）演化为动词，再由动词的分词用作形容词。

575 account for it　说明它的道理。can't account for it便是“莫名其妙”。

576 If you please　一般用于请求或命令，= please，又作“信不信由你”解；这里有“你瞧，出事了”的意思。

577 Alfred [ˈælfrɪd] Doolittle卖花女Liza Doolittle的父亲；是dustman [ˈdʌstmən]的同位语。

578 wants = who wants.口语中，在There's a dustman who wants to see you的句型中who常省略。

579 Phew　[fjuː]!唷！哼！表示惊讶，反感等。

580 I say!常用引起话头，也用以表示惊讶、反感。按照美国用法，说Say!

581 Send the blackguard up　带那流氓（指the dustman）上来。Higgins惯于出言不逊。

582 may not be　可能不是；不作“不可能是”解。

583 Whether he is or not　= Whether he is a blackguard or he is not a blackguard.不管他是不是流氓。这是个状语从句。

584 we shall have some trouble with him我们将在他那里有些麻烦。注意：有麻烦（have trouble）的是我们，不是他。

585 I think not　= I think (that) we shall not have any trouble with him. = I don't think we shall have any trouble with him.

586 If theres any trouble he shall have it with me, not I with him.如果要有什么麻烦的话，（我要叫）他在我这里得到麻烦，而不是我在他那里得到麻烦。he shall第二、三人称后用shall表示说话人的意志。not I with him = I shall not have trouble with him.

587 About the girl?　= Shall we get something interesting about the girl out of him?

588 Doolittle, sir.　Doolittle是Mrs Pearce向主人通报来客的名字（用姓氏而不冠以Mr，显然不客气），sir是称呼主人。

589 clad in the costume [ˈkɒstjuːm] of his profession穿着他（倒垃圾）行业的服装；倒垃圾的打扮。clad是clothe的不规则变化的过去分词，等于规则变化的clothed。costume （各行业、时代、地区特殊的）服装。

590 a back brim covering his neck and shoulders　（帽子的）后沿（垂在脑后）遮盖着他的头颅和肩膀（为倒垃圾遮尘）。

591 well marked [mɑːkt] and rather interesting features轮廓非常清楚而且相当有趣的面貌。features面貌，即眉、目、口、鼻等各个feature的综合；用复数。

592 equally free from fear and conscience　同等地没有恐惧和良心不安（的样子）；用equally，表示既无恐惧又无不安。free from没有……的。

593 remarkably expressive voice　非常能表达感情的嗓子。

594 the result of a habit of giving vent to his feelings without reserve　无保留地发泄感情的习惯的结果。the result ...这个名词词组是voice的同位语，也可理解为the result的前面省略which (voice) is；倘不省略，便是一个非限制性定语从句。give vent to发泄；吐露。

595 His present pose is that of wounded [ˈwuːndɪd] honor and stern resolution [ˌrezəˈluːʃən].他目前是表示（感到）有损了体面和（抱着）坚定决心的姿态。that = the pose；如用one，则= a pose。

596 uncertain which of the two gentlemen is his man　吃不准两位先生中哪一位是他（要找）的人。his man指Higgins。uncertain后可用about，也可直接接疑问词。

597 Iggins　是Higgins的误读。伦敦土音常脱落[h]音。

598 Morning　把Good morning说成Morning，是很随便的说法；初次见面这样说，显然不合适，但Doolittle无视礼仪，而且他也实在没有教养。

599 Governor原义“统治者”、“行政长官”、“总督”；在英国俚语中用以称呼主人、老板等，犹言“老爷”。

600 magisterially [ˌmædʒɪˈstɪərɪəli]以主人（或长官）的神气；威严地。

601 Brought up　= He (Doolittle) was brought up.

602 Hounslow [ˈhaʊnzləʊ]在伦敦之西，Middlesex郡一城市。

603 Mother Welsh = His mother is Welsh:他母亲是威尔士（Wales）人。

604 opens his mouth, amazed　（Doolittle听了）惊奇得张开了嘴巴。

605 you do　= you want your daughter.

606 suppose　= think.

607 have some spark of family feeling left　有点对家属的感情被遗留着（没有消失）；还有父女之情。spark原义“火星”，转义“一点”。left是过去分词，作have的宾语补足语，some spark of family feeling left称作复合宾语。

608 taken aback　吃了一惊。

609 keep your daughter for you　替你养活你女儿。

610 look here　叫人注意的用语，并不一定有“瞧这儿”的意思。

611 fairity　是fair（公平的）之误。

612 Where do I come in?　我的份儿呢？这是句惯用语。

613 had the audacity [ɔːˈdæsəti] to ...胆敢。audacity大胆；放肆。卖花女来请专家教课，被视为“胆大妄为”。

614 This gentleman and my housekeeper　指Pickering上校和Mrs Pearce。

615 You sent her here on purpose.　是你有意派了她来（设计讹诈）。on purpose故意地；是习语。

616 You must have.　= You must have sent her here on purpose.

617 How else could you = Or else (= Otherwise) how could you.

618 take a man up like that　那样对待人；那样看人。

619 take you up　把你逮捕。上文说take up，这里接着也说take up，但意思不同，是个双关语（pun）。

620 plant欺诈；骗局；是俚语。

621 plot阴谋。

622 telephone for　打电话叫……来。

623 directory　= telephone directory.电话号码簿。

624 a brass [brɑːs] farthing [ˈfɑːðɪŋ]一文钱；极小数的金额。farthing英国过去四分之一便士的铜币。

625 leave it to the gentleman here这事情让这里这位先生讲一句（公道话）。

626 marching down on Doolittle with a poser　大步朝前走到Doolittle面前责问他。down朝台前方向。poser难题。这里不作“装腔作势（pose）的人”解。

627 What else　（要不是钱，又是）别的什么。

628 Well, what would a man come for?是呀，一个人到这里为啥？（总有目的的嘛；用Doolittle下面自己的话说：“总不会把女儿白白送人。”）这是虚拟语气，省略诸如if I might ask you之类的条件从句。

629 Be human要讲人情（别不讲道理而胡说）。

630 disarmed　原义“被解除了武装”，转义“火气被消了”，“平了气”。

631 put her up to it指使她做这件事；（故意）派她来的。put up to是口语；既有“唆使”之意，也有“告诉”、“指导”之意。

632 So help me　天晓得；我说的是千真万确的。已见前注。

633 I take my Bible oath我可以对《圣经》发誓；我发誓。

634 these two months past　= for the past two months.

635 'most musical, most melancholy'　“（声调）非常悦耳，非常凄婉”。这个舞台指示放在引号里，因为这里引用英国诗人弥尔顿（John Milton, 1680–1674）的抒情长诗《沉思的人》（Il Penseroso [ɪl ˌpensəˈrəʊzəʊ]）中的第六十二行诗句；这里四个词都用m开首，这叫做头韵法（alliteration）。

636 get a word in　插入一句话；插话。

637 I'm willing to tell you. I'm wanting to tell you. I'm waiting to tell you.三句话同一句型，又连用三个以w开头的动词，似乎铿锵有力，俨然雄辩家的口吻，实际上只是前面说了的I'll tell you的重复，无怪Higgins接着大加讽刺和挖苦。

638 natural gift of rhetoric　[ˈretərɪk]演说的天赋。rhetoric英国指“演说术”，美国指“修辞学”。

639 the rhythm [ˈrɪðəm] of his native woodnotes wild纯朴而奔放的诗歌的节奏。his native woodnotes wild一语出于弥尔顿《欢乐的人》（L' Allegro [læˈleɪgrəʊ]）一诗第一百三十四行。萧伯纳以语言学家Henry Sweet作为Higgins的原型，而Sweet热衷于弥尔顿（前注中已提及），所以这里Higgins随口引用了弥尔顿。woodnotes原指“林中的鸟叫”，转义为“朴素的诗（或歌）”。wild修饰woodnotes；弥尔顿常把定语放在被修饰的中心词后面。

640 Sentimental rhetoric!　感伤的浮夸的语言！这是个名词句。

641 Welsh strain in him他的威尔士的本色。前面Higgins曾听Doolittle话音，说他母亲是威尔士人。

642 It also accounts for his mendacity [menˈdæsəti] and dishonesty.这也说明他虚伪和不老实的原因。It指Welsh strain。Higgins说威尔士人虚伪，当然是他的偏见，犹如大文豪约翰生（Samuel Johnson, 1709–1784）对苏格兰人有偏见。also指Welsh strain既是浮夸的根源，又是虚伪的根源。

643 please　这里有“请别这么说”的意思。

644 I'm west country myself.我自己就是（英国）西部地方人。Wales在英国西部；Pickering出生于Cheltenham，在英格兰西南部，英格兰西部（尤其西南部）和苏格兰西部称west country。注意：这里用I'm west country = I come from the west country.

645 send her　派她（到这里来）。

646 It was like this　事实情况是这样的。

647 took给……搭车。

648 give him a jaunt　给他兜个圈子。jaunt短途游览。

649 Son of her landlady, he is. = He is the（或a）son of her landlady.口语中常有这种句型。在正常叙述中，如那房东太太只有一个儿子，son前用the；如有几个儿子，而说的是其中某一个，则用a，全不用冠词也可，因为在father、mother、son、daughter等词前常省略冠词。

650 hung about徘徊；在Liza身边打转。hung hang的过去时。hang about是习语；也作hang around。

651 on the chance of　期待；怀着……的希望。

652 giving him another ride home让他再乘一次车回家。home修饰名词ride；可说是副词作定语用。

653 sent him back of her luggage　叫他回去拿她的行李。luggage统称“行李”，是不可数名词。

654 you was you were之误。

655 stop here　逗留在这里，住在Higgins家。

656 at the corner of Long Acre and Endell Street在Long Acre和Endell Street的转角处；虽四叉路口有四个转角处，但习惯不用a corner of ...，而用the corner of ...。

657 Public house.　= You met the boy at the corner of Long Acre and Endell Street in a(或the) public house.这里如果专指某家酒店，用the public house，不专指哪一家，则用a。public house小客栈；在英国也指“小酒店”。

658 The poor man's club　= The public house is the poor man's club.这里的the都不是专指，而是泛指某一类型的人或物。The poor man's club中的the是属于poor man的冠词。

659 why shouldnt I? = why shouldnt I meet him in the public house?为什么我不能（在小酒店里遇见他）呢？也就是“为什么我不能去小酒店喝两杯呢”的意思。

660 what was up　发生什么事。

661 what was my feelings and my duty这里表语虽是复数，但主语what是单数，所以动词用was，而不用were。

662 I says　是I say之误。

663 go for it yourself　你自去拿取行李。

664 trusted me with it　放心把行李交给我。

665 afore　方言，= before。

666 the little swine那小猪崽；是the boy的同位语。

667 just to oblige you like　可以算是为你效劳。like在这里是口语中的一种用法，有“仿佛”、“可以说”的意思。

668 make myself agreeable使我讨（你的）欢喜些；讨好（你）。

669 That's all　我要说的就是这些（全在这里了）。

670 Musical instrument　[ˈɪnstrəmənt]乐器。它和下面的pictures、jewelry、birdcage是她所谓的“行李”。

671 a trifle of一些些。

672 What was I to think from that　从（她说她不用拿衣服）那句话，我当时会怎么想。

673 rescue her from worse than death　把她从比死更坏的处境中救出去（以免女儿受污辱）。worse在这里作名词。

674 relieved at being so well understood　因（他的意思）那么充分地被了解而宽慰。bring her luggage把她的行李带来。这里的her是定语，不是间接宾语。

675 Youre going to take her away　= You shall take her away = Take her away.

676 double quick加倍地快；速即。这两个词是副词，double修饰副词quick, quick又修饰take；也可以看作是个复合动词，作“快走”、“跑步”解，它还可作复合名词用。

677 crosses to the hearth　穿过（房间的中央部分）到壁炉跟前。

678 rings the bell按铃（唤Mrs Pearce来让Doolittle把女儿带走）。

679 stand in my girl's light　妨碍我女儿的前程。stand in somebody's light原义是“挡住某人的光”，转义为“妨碍某人的前程”；是习语。

680 a career opening for her放在她面前的事业；前程。Doolittle并不知道他女儿要受语音训练，所以他所谓career，并不是说她受教育可有光明前程，而是他误会Higgins看上了她，她可以交运了。

681 as you might say　有如（假如你们要说起来）你们会这么说的。

682 with an air of washing his hands of the whole affair　带着对整件事情撒手不管（今后不干他事）的神气。

683 You told me to burn her clothes.　你叫我把她的衣服都烧了。（叫她怎么出去呢？）

684 carry the girl through the streets like a blooming monkey　把女儿像只该死的（光身的）猴子一样背着穿过大街小巷。carry带；有“运载”的意思，包括“挑”、“掮”、“抱”、“背”等；与本身没有这些含义的bring（拿来）、take（拿去）不同。blooming诅咒语，已屡见前。

685 I put it to you.　我把问题向你提出；我问你怎么办。it上面一句提出的问题。

686 You have put it to me that you want your daughter.　你向我提出了要女儿嘛。这里的it引起that从句构成的同位语。

687 buy her some　= buy her some clothes = buy some clothes for her.

688 Wheres the clothes she come in?　应作Where are the clothes she came in?她来的时候穿的衣服哪去了呢？

689 or did you missus here　还是你的这位太太呢。后面省略burn them。

690 sent for some clothes　叫人去买些衣服来。在send for ...的结构中，拿东西来的可能就是派去的人，也可能派去的人只是通知，而拿东西来的是另外的人；也可以指“打电话去买或借”。

691 This way, please.　请这边走；指引去厨房的路。

692 accompanies her to the door　照字面解释，应是“陪她（或送她）到门口”，但这里accompanies实在是follows（跟随）的意思。

693 turns confidentially to Higgins　引为知己地转向Higgins。turns to除“转向……”外，还有“求助于……”的意思。

694 You and me is　是You and I are之误。

695 men of the world老于世故的人。

696 Men of the world, are we?　意即We are men of the world.这种把表语移前的说法，在口语中很普遍，尤其像这一句，Men of the world是重复对方的话，说在前面，语气紧凑而自然。

697 Youd better go你最好走开；你去吧。Higgins叫她去，一是他不准备叫Doolittle带他女儿走，因而没有Mrs Pearce的事了，另一方面可能他要跟Doolittle谈谈，恐怕Mrs Pearce在场不方便。

698 The floor is yours　现在由你发言，这是会议上的用语，Pickering在这场合说的话，带有幽默之意。

699 takes refuge　[ˈrefjuːdʒ]躲避；避难。

700 a little overwhelmed by the proximity[prɒkˈsɪməti] of his visitor因为对他的来客（Doolittle）的靠近有点受不了，这个以过去分词overwhelmed为中心的分词短语，表示take refuge的原因。

701 has a professional flavour of dust about him　他身边有一股（倒垃圾工人的）职业性的垃圾气味。Dust统指“尘土”，在英国也作“垃圾”解，都是不可数名词；a dust不是“一颗灰尘”，而是“一阵灰尘”。

702 the truth is, ...　= the truth is that ...实际情况是；实在是。

703 taken a sort of fancy to you对你产生了某种喜爱；有些喜欢你。take a fancy to对……中意；喜欢；是习语；注意介词用to。

704 I'm not so set on　我并不是那么坚决要……but what　而不……，= that not，是习语，用于否定或疑问语后面。另举一例：Nobody saw the play but what it impressed him deeply.没有人看了这戏而不深为感动的。but what也作but that，也可单用but; but what多用于口语中。I'm not so set on having her back home again but what I might be open to an arrangement.全句的意思是：“我并不是那么坚决要她重新回家去而不能商量解决的”，要她留在这里，也好商量。

705 Regarded in the light of a young woman　被作为一个年轻女人来看。in the light of作为……；是习语；也作“按照”解。

706 she's not worth her keep　她不值得被扶养。注意：her keep是“她的被扶养”；keep是名词。

707 tell you straight　直率对你讲；这里straight是副词。

708 rights as a father作为一个父亲（应有）的各种权利。

709 youre the last man alive to expect你是活在世上的最后会指望的人……；实际就是“你决不会指望”之意。be the last man to决不……；是习语；加上一个alive，犹如on earth，只是加强语气，并无具体意义。

710 let her go for nothing　白白让她走掉；女儿给人要去，而老子一无所得。

711 the straight sort　直爽的一类（人）。

712 Well, whats a five-pound note to you? And whats Eliza to me?唉，一张五镑的钞票对你算得了什么？Eliza对我又算得了什么？（而你要Eliza，我拿五镑好花用，岂不两便？）note = banknote.

713 judicially　慎重地。

714 Higgins's [ˈhɪgɪnzɪz] intentions are entirely honorable Higgins的用意是全然光明正大的；只是对Eliza进行语言训练，决无任何坏心意。说某人的intentions are honorable，一般说某人“是诚意求婚而不是想勾引”；但这里并无求婚的意思。

715 Course they are　= Of course, they (= his intentions) are entirely honorable.

716 If I thought they wasnt = If I thought (that) they weren't entirely honorable.这是虚拟语气；thought不是指过去时；wasnt是werent之误。

717 I'd ask fifty　= I would ask fifty pounds那我就将要求五十镑了。虚拟语气。这里ask也可用ask for。

718 Not in a general way I wouldnt　在一般情况下我不会（拿五十镑卖掉女儿）。这是“双重否定”。wouldnt = wouldn't sell my daughter for £50.

719 to oblige a gentleman like you为了报效像你这样的一位先生。这个不定式词组作目的状语，修饰'd (= would) do。

720 do a good deal　做一笔克己的交易。deal交易；如fair deal（公平交易）。good这里是“对对方有利”的意思。

721 morals　道德，用复数。

722 have a bit out of this　从这件事中得到一点（好处）。

723 There can be no question that ...　……是不可能有问题的；毫无问题。

724 positive crime　绝对的罪过；错尽，错绝。

725 feel a sort of rough justice in his claim　觉得他提出的要求中有一种大致上的公平（也有一点道理）。

726 Thats it　那就是了；对啦；口语中常用的习语。

727 Thats all I say.我所说的就是那个意思；all后面省略that。

728 as it were仿佛；可以说是；是习语。注意：不要和as it was混淆；as it is（过去时用was）是“现在（或过去）实际情况如此”、“事实上”、“既然如此”的意思。

729 really it seems hardly right –　似乎总不大对——话未说完。“不对”指为Liza之事而给他钱。

730 Dont say that, Governor.　that指Pickering说的it seems hardly right。Governor这里是称呼Pickering。

731 the undeserving poor　不配救济的穷人们。

732 what that means那（指to be one of the undeserving poor）意味着什么。

733 up agen (= against) middle class morality all the time老是与中产阶级伦理道德相抵触。up against面临；与……冲突；是习语。middle class在英国指贵族和雇佣劳动者之间的阶级，即资产阶级和自由职业者。萧伯纳经常抨击中产阶级伦理道德。all the time老是，= always;在英国通常指“在某一段时间内老是……”。

734 theres anything going　有什么事在进行；这里指“有什么救济或慈善活动”。going可视为形容词，作“进行中的”、“现行的”解。

735 put in for a bit of it申请一些救济。put in for申请；是习语。It指上文anything，即“救济”。

736 the same story　老一套。

737 needs　生活必需的种种；用复数。

738 the most deserving widow's最配受救济的寡妇的需要。widow's后省略needs。严格地说，这里widow's不妥，因为下面的关系代词that指widow's needs不妥（needs不大可能got money out of ...）；the most deserving widow's改作those (= needs) of the most deserving widow才对，这样下面的that指the most deserving widow，完全符合语法。

739 ever got money out of six different charities in one week for the death of the same husband　曾经因为同一个丈夫的死亡而在一个星期里从六个慈善团体得到救济金。charity作“慈善”解是不可数名词；作“慈善团体”解是可数名词。in one week同in a week，强调一。注意用ever和过去时got（不用现在时gets）。

740 I dont eat less hearty than him　我吃得并不比他少；我的胃口并不比他（的胃口）小。hearty应作heartily，痛快，胃口好。than him = than he eats.在文学中多作than he，口语中多说than him。

741 I drink a lot more　我喝酒比他多得多；more后省略than he drinks。a lot许多；这里是副词短语；修饰more。

742 cause　= because.

743 a thinking man有思想的人。

744 band　乐队。want a band有个乐队（听听音乐）。

745 feel low觉得情绪低落；不起劲。

746 they charge me just the same for everything as they charge the deserving他们对什么都要我支付和受救济的人们同样的费用；衣食住行样样需要同样的开支。charge使支付；可接双宾语。

747 Just an excuse　= It's (middle class morality is) just an excuse只是一个借口而已。

748 play that game on me对我耍那个（middle class morality的）花招。

749 playing straight with you　和你们坦率地打交道。straight这里是副词；比较：play fair（公平地比赛）和play foul（比赛犯规；作弊）中的fair和foul都是副词。

750 pretending to be deserving假装是配受救济的。deserving是形容词作表语，并不和be构成进行式。

751 like it　喜欢这样；it指to go on being undeserving或being undeserving。

752 take advantage of a man's nature　利用一个人的本性（宁肯不配救济）而占他便宜。

753 do him out of the price of his own daughter剥夺他应得的他自己女儿的代价；要他把他的女儿给你们而你们不给他钱。

754 what　应作that或whom。

755 clothed [kləʊðd]给……衣穿。

756 by the sweat [swet] of his brow靠（满头大汗地）辛勤劳动；是习语。

757 growed grown之误。

758 Is five pounds unreasonable?　五镑不合理（意即太贵）吗？five pounds作为一个金额，用单数。

759 I put it to you; and I leave it to you.　我把问题向你提出，让你回答。这口吻和前面连用I'm waiting to tell you等三句的口吻相仿，都是描摹出他“演说的天赋”。

760 take this man in hand for three months训练他三个月。take in hand拿在手上；有“承担”、“处理”等意思，这里指“进行语言训练”。

761 could choose between a seat in the Cabinet and a popular pulpit in Wales能在内阁阁员和威尔士群众布道师两者中间选择（担任）一个。

762 that　指Doolittle受三个月训练可成为阁员或布道师的设想。

763 thank you kindly　这句话很普通；有些咬文嚼字的人认为不妥，因为自称对人“谢”得kindly，有自誉为有礼貌的意味。

764 game for　对……极有兴致的；有劲。game是形容词，后面介词用for。

765 same as = the same as = in the same way as.

766 amusements　娱乐。在Doolittle看来，politics、religion、social reform等也不过都是开开玩笑的事。

767 it's a dog's life any way you look at it无论你怎么看，它总是极苦的生活。it泛指社会上一切形形式式的事物。any way = however，有连词的作用。a dog's life苦难的生活。

768 Undeserving poverty is my line.　不配救济的穷困是我的行业。

769 Taking one station in society with another拿社会上各个行当相比。another = another station in society. society作“社会”解时前面不用冠词。

770 it's the only one that has any ginger [ˈdʒɪndʒə] in it, to my taste只有这个行当才有劲，才配我胃口。it指undeserving poverty。ginger原义“生姜”，口语中转作“劲头”解。to taste配胃口的；合适的；是习语。

771 fiver　= a five-pound note.

772 make a bad use of　不好好利用；滥花（钱）。

773 so help me　天晓得；我可以保证；见前注。

774 I wont = I wont make a bad use of it（指the fiver）.

775 live idle on it　靠这五镑闲混过日子。

776 There wont be a penny of it left = Not a penny of it will be left.

777 by Monday　至迟到星期一。

778 same as if　= (just或all) the same as if.土语中same这样的用法，已屡见前注。as if后用虚拟语气。

779 you bet你可以打赌，你可以确信。

780 good spree　狂欢一场（例如痛饮一顿）。

781 the missus口语中称“妻子”，其实当时Doolittle和他所谓的missus尚未正式结婚，只是“情妇”；见前。

782 employment to others　与前面的pleasure和后面的satisfaction并列，是giving的宾语。Giving employment to others使别人有生意做。

783 throwed away　被丢掉；自费。throwed是thrown之误。

784 couldnt spend it better　后面省略than give it to me。

785 This is irresistible　这话极有说服力（无法不听从）。

786 give him ten给他十镑。Doolittle原来只要五镑，Higgins听了他那番话，心悦诚服，愿意加倍奉敬。

787 two notes两张（五镑）钞票。

788 wouldnt have the heart to spend ten　舍不得花十镑。这是虚拟语气，省略if I were given ten或I got ten。have the heart to （接不定式）忍心（常用于否定句中）。

789 I shouldnt neither　= I shouldnt have the heart to spend ten either.用了neither，变成“双重否定”。

790 feel prudent like　觉得有些拘谨（不肯随意花用了）。口语中like这样的用法已见前注。

791 and then goodbye to happiness那就向快乐告别了；那就没有愉快了。

792 not a penny more　= don't give me a penny more than I ask you to give.

793 rather draw the line at encouraging that sort of immorality　我总觉该有个界限，不能（超越这界限而）鼓励那样的不道德；如鼓励不道德，那就过分了。draw the line at也作draw a line at，是习语，后面所接的是不该做的事。这里的that sort of immorality指Doolittle和一个女人不结婚而姘居。

794 It's me that suffers by it.　由于这个情况（不结婚）而受害的是我。It's me在口语中比It's I普遍。that是关系代词，它的先行词是It，不是me，所以从句中用suffers而不用suffer。但习惯上也有不用that而用who的；既用who，先行词变成是me，从句中自不能用suffers而要用suffer了。英国语法家Quirk曾举It's me who's to blame的例子，说是informal English。

795 Ive no hold on her.　我控制不住她；我管不住她。

796 got to = have got to = have to.这是口语用法。

797 agreeable to her合她的意的；讨她喜欢的。

798 buy her clothes something sinful　= buy her rather sinful clothes.买相当邪气的衣服给她。something这里是副词，是口语用法，既可作“多少有点”解，也可作“非常”解。

799 lawful husband　合法的（正式结了婚的）丈夫。

800 Catch her marrying me!　要是她会跟我结婚，你抓住问我！即“你瞧着，她决不会跟我结婚”的意思。

801 marry Eliza while she's young and dont know no better趁Eliza年轻还不懂事的时候娶她。（待她懂得了不正式结婚的便宜，就不肯跟你结婚了。）dont know no better是双重否定；dont是doesn't之误。所以整个词组应作doesn't know better。know better是习语，作“懂得更多（因而不做某事）”，后面有时接than；这里是know better than to marry you的意思。

802 for it　因为不娶她。

803 after应说afterwards。

804 better her than you　= It's better for her to be sorry than for you to be sorry. /It's better that she (should) be sorry than you (should) be sorry.

805 dont know　是doesn't know之误。

806 shall have no convictions left　将没有信念被留存（而不被打破）。Higgins怕自己彻底怀疑起middle class morality的信念。left过去分词，作宾语补足语。

807 Five pounds I think you said. = I think you said 'five pounds.'

808 Not now.　= I wont take ten (pounds) now.

809 Another time = I will take ten (pounds) another time.我下次拿十镑。

810 Good morning　可用作上午的告别语，一般两词都重读；见面时用，一般重音放在morning上。

811 get away with his booty　拿了他要得的五镑钞票离去。booty战利品；掠夺所得。

812 exquisitely [ɪkˈskwɪzɪtli] clean young Japanese lady高雅清洁的年轻日本贵妇人；即Eliza，她穿着和服。

813 kimono　[kɪˈməʊnəʊ]和服；和服式晨衣；是日本语“着物”的音读。

814 printed cunningly精巧地印着花样；cunning有“狡猾”、“精巧”和“熟练”几个解释。

815 gets out of her way　给她让路。out of somebody's way是in somebody's way（妨碍；阻挡）的反义习语。

816 deferentially　[ˌdefəˈrenʃəli]恭敬地。Doolittle认不出自己女儿，还当是日本贵妇人，所以恭而敬之。

817 Beg pardon对不起；是(I) beg your pardon之略。

818 miss小姐；不带姓名地作为称呼，常用于仆人对主人等的场合；正规的称呼是madam。

819 Garn!　去你的！见前注。

820 Dont I look silly?　我看上去不傻里傻气吗？因众人看见她都惊叫，所以她这样问。

821 dont say anything to make the girl conceited about herself.　别说什么会使得姑娘自负的话。Mrs Pearce怕Higgins夸耀她好看。

822 Please　这是要Higgins注意，因为她又在Eliza面前说damned了。

823 a fashionable air　时髦的气派。

824 by George　指神名的发誓，诅咒语，表示千真万确。George指英国的守护神（patron）St. George。

825 And it ought to look horrible!而且它（指new fashion）应该看上去吓人！要时髦得吓人；那当然是讽刺。

826 fatherly pride　父亲的骄傲。

827 clean up弄得干干净净，面目一新。

828 a credit to me　为我增光的人。a credit增光的事物。

829 Hot and cold water on tap, just as much as you like, there is.　= There is hot and cold water on tap, just as much as you like. on tap在龙头里，随手可以放出的。

830 Woolly towels [ˈtaʊəlz], there is; and a towel horse so hot, it burns your fingers. = There are woolly towels and so hot a towel horse that it burns your fingers. Woolly羊毛般柔软的。towel horse手巾架，也称towel rack。burns烫。这里不作“烧”解。your泛指“人们的”。

831 soap smelling like primroses　闻上去像樱草花香的肥皂。soap是不可数名词。

832 ladies is　ladies are之误。

833 Washing's a treat for them.洗澡对她们是一种享乐（因为她们有那么舒适的设备）。

834 Wish　= I wish.口语中常把I wish, I hope等语中的I略去。

835 met with your approval　[əˈpruːvəl]得到你的赞赏。

836 It didnt　= It didn't meet with my approval.

837 not all of it = not all of it meet with my approval.并不是浴室的一切我都赞赏。她对浴室挂着照见她身体的镜子，大不以为然。

838 dont care who hears me say it不怕谁听见我说这话（话在下面）。

839 What was wrong　出了什么问题；有什么不对头。

840 blandly　若无其事地。

841 nothing = nothing was wrong.

842 doesnt matter无关紧要。

843 had a good mind to break it　真想砸了它（镜子）。

844 I didnt know which way to look.我不知该朝哪边看（才能不看见镜子里自己赤裸的身子）。

845 hung a towel over it　在这（镜子）上面挂条毛巾（把它遮住）。

846 brought your daughter up too strictly　把女儿管教得太严了。

847 except to give her a lick of a strap now and again　除了时而给她吃一顿皮鞭子。lick原义“舔”，俚语作“用鞭子抽打”、“猛击”解。

848 aint (= is not) accustomed to it不习惯于此（对着镜子洗澡）。

849 pick up　学会。

850 free-and-easy ways各种随随便便的习惯；随便的生活方式。

851 your father shall take you home　我就要你父亲把你带回家去。shall用于第二、第三人称，表示说话人的意向。

852 Not him　= He shall not take me home.我才不让他带我回家呢。在Not him这样的结构中，常用宾语而不用主语。

853 All he come here for was to touch you for some money to get drunk on.他来到这里，所为的就是向你要几个钱去喝个醉。All后面省略that。come是came之误。All he came here for他来到这里的全部目的，即仅有的目的。touch俚语中作“向……商借（或求事）”、“偷窃”、“敲诈勒索”等解。句末的on可有可无；to get drunk on是不定式短语作定语，修饰money；如不用on，则to get drunk是不定式短语作状语，修饰touch，说明要钱的目的。

854 To put into the plate in church, I suppose.　我想（要么）在教堂里捐献掉。教堂里做礼拜时，用盘子（plate）收集献金。church作“教堂里做礼拜”解时，前面不用冠词，如go to church、church begins at eight o'clock。

855 puts out her tongue at him　对他伸出舌头（嗤笑他说到教堂里捐钱）。

856 incensed by this　被这（伸舌头的）动作所惹怒。

857 presently finds it necessary to step between them立即觉得有必要走到他们（父女）中间（以防Doolittle打女儿）。it虚词，引起不定式短语。

858 Dont you give me none of you lip　别说无礼的话。这一句里用了“双重否定”；Dont you加强Dont的语气。lip无礼的话；多嘴嚼舌；是俚语，常用于none of your lip一语中。

859 dont let me hear you giving this gentleman any of it neither　应作dont let me hear you give this gentleman any of it either, it指lip（失礼的话）。

860 or youll hear from me about it　否则你会从我这里受到（关于你多嘴无礼的）训责。hear有“受到批评（或训责）”的意思。it指说无礼的话这件事。

861 See? = You see?你知道了吗？

862 Your blessing　你（对女儿）的祝福。

863 mug　笨蛋；是俚语；原指“嘴”、“脸”、“丑脸”。

864 put up my children to all I know myself把我自己所懂得的全都教给我的子女。put up to教……做（某事）；是习语。

865 Hard enough to hold them in without that　不那样（把我自己所懂得的全教给他们），已经够难管束他们了。Hard是It is hard之略。hold in约束；是习语。without that = without putting up my children to all I know myself.

866 do it　= improve Eliza's mind.教育她。

867 help you in your talks with her　在你对她说服教育方面帮助你。注意：help后面介词用in。talks with参照动词短语talk with，除“与……谈话”外，还作“说服”解。

868 Not just this week　= I wont come just this week.

869 have a job at a distance　在远处有工作要做。job这里指“短活”、“任务”。at a distance在附近的远处，如父亲在上海，儿子在东北工作，不能说My son is working at a distance.

870 later on you may depend on me以后你可以相信我；你放心，我会来的。

871 Afternoon　= Good afternoon.

872 maam [mæm, mәm]常写作ma'am，是口语；= madam。

873 touches his hat to　向……用手碰帽檐（致敬礼）。

874 disdains the salutation不理Doolittle的致意。salutation这里指既称Mrs Pearce为maam，又向她致敬礼。

875 fellow-sufferer from Mrs Pearce's difficult disposition　同是Mrs Pearce的难弄的脾气的受害者。

876 He'd as soon you set a bulldog on him as a clergyman.　他宁可你叫条恶狗去咬他，也不愿你叫个牧师去缠他；他对牧师比恶狗还怕。He'd = He would. would as soon ... as ...宁可……而不要……。句中的would有谓语动词的作用，后面省去连词that。set ... on指使；是习语，这里的set是动词过去时。bulldog斗牛犬（一种猛犬）。as a clergyman = as you set a clergyman on him.

877 You wont see him again in a hurry.　你短期内不会再见他。

878 collecting dust, instead of working at his trade　收集（拾）垃圾而不务正业。他的正业是挖土工（navvy），见下文。

879 Talking money out of other people's pockets into his own. His proper trade's a navvy　上文问What is his trade? Eliza接着回答：（他的行当变成是专门）把别人腰包里的钱弄到自己腰包里（骗人家的钱）了。他的正业是挖土工（倒不认真干）。navvy开沟筑路的挖土工；是英国用语。

880 works at it　做（挖土）工。it = the job as a navvy.

881 for exercise　（工作只是）为了运动运动。

882 earns good money挣大钱。

883 Aint you going to call me Miss Doolittle any more?你（听了我爹的这行径）不会再（尊敬我）称我Doolittle小姐了吧。按前文Pickering曾随口称她Miss Doolittle，使她哇哇叫起来，她实在不喜欢人家这样叫她。

884 Miss Doolittle　又叫她Miss Doolittle了。Pickering客气已成习惯。

885 It was a slip of the tongue.这（上次称她Miss Doolittle）是口误。a slip of the pen是“笔误”。

886 I dont mind　我并不介意；没关系。

887 only it sounded so genteel [dʒenˈtiːl]可是（Miss Doolittle）这称呼听起来太文雅了些。genteel高雅的；常含讽刺意，作“装斯文的”解。

888 get out there and tell it to wait for me　在那里下车，叫车子（停着）等我。

889 put the girls in their place a bit叫（那边街道的）那些姑娘安分些（不要再像过去那样来耻笑我）。put somebody in his place使某人安分，不得越规；是习语。

890 Better wait　= You had better wait.还是等……好。

891 get you something really fashionable给你买真正时髦的衣服。

892 cut your old friends　不理睬你的老朋友。

893 now that you have risen in the world因为你在社会上地位升高了的缘故。

894 Theyve took it out of me often enough with their ridicule [ˈrɪdɪkjuːl] when they had the chance她们一有机会就耻笑我，耻笑得我够多的了。take it out of以（某人）出气；使（某人）受气；里面的it无所指，是习语。Theyve took是Theyve taken之误。

895 mean to get a bit of my own back　要稍微报复报复。get one's own back报复，犹言“翻本”，是习语。

896 different　= which（指前面的some，即some clothes）are different. different后面一般介词用from，有时（尤其英国人）也用to；美国人有时用than。

897 it do seem a waste of money　这（另外给我睡衣）确实好像是浪费金钱。do是does之误。seem这里也可说seem to be。waste前面的a不用也可。

898 shew　= show.显示；炫耀；出风头。

899 never could fancy changing into cold things决不会喜欢（脱下身上温暖的衣服）换上冷的衣服（睡衣）。

900 try on　试穿。

901 She shuts the door behind her.　她（出房间）随手关门。her指Mrs Pearce，而不是Eliza。

902 have taken on a stiff job　承担了一项棘手的工作。

903 There seems to be some curiosity as to ...　看来（有些读者）会好奇地想到……

904 the first one　第一课。one指lesson。

905 Picture　（请读者们）想一想。

906 feeling her inside put out of step觉得她的肠胃被搞乱了。inside口语，作“肚子”解。put是过去分词，作宾语补足语。out of step原义脚步不齐；转义“失调”、“混乱”。

907 to which it is unaccustomed　它（她的肠胃）所不习惯那种的。

908 seated　是过去分词。如“请坐”，常说Please be seated.

909 a hospital out-patient　医院的门诊病人。

910 a first encounter一个初次的相会。比较：the first encounter，初次相会。

911 constitutionally unable to sit still　生性坐不定。

912 discomposes her still more by striding restlessly about由于他不停地大步走来走去，弄得她更加心神不宁。Eliza原已像第一次见到医生的门诊病人那样局促不安，现在更加不安了，所以用still more。

913 But for the reassuring presence and quietude [ˈkwaɪәtjuːd] of her friend the Colonel要不是她的朋友Pickering上校的在场使她心安且平静。But for要不是；是习语；含有虚拟语气，这里= If it were not for，所以下面用would run，而不用will run。

914 run for her life　拼命地跑；逃命。

915 even back to Drury Lane　Drury Lane是Liza家的所在地，见第一幕末尾（p.38）。说“即使回到老家去”，可见她把她的家看作是最可怕的去处。

916 Say your alphabet　[ˈælfәbet].背出你的字母表。an alphabet是一种语言的全套字母，不是一个字母；“一个字母”称a letter。

917 dont need to be taught　也可以说need not be taught。

918 thundering　（像打雷般）怒喝。

919 presently　马上；不久。

920 if you put it like that –　如果你那样说；如果你要我念；话未说完，没说出的意思是“我就念吧”。

921 Ahyee, bәyee, cәyee, dәyee Liza用伦敦土音把[eɪ]、[biː]、[siː]、[diː]念成[ˈɑːjiː]、[ˈbәjiː]、[ˈsәjiː]、[ˈdәjiː]。

922 with a roar of a wounded lion　用受伤的狮子（一般）的吼声；比喻“怒吼”。

923 Listen to this听这个（指Liza念ABCD的发音）。

924 This is what we pay for as elementary education.　这就是我们出钱（纳税）办的小学教育。

925 This unfortunate animal这个畜生般的可怜人；指Liza。

926 locked up for nine years in school　在学校里关了九年；受了九年教育（当指英国的义务教育）。

927 at our expense　由我们负担费用。

928 to teach her to speak and read the language of Shakespear and Milton教她说和读莎士比亚及弥尔顿（所用）的语言（规范英语）。to teach her ...这个格式在这里似乎不合理，该改作to be taught。

929 But I'm sayin it.　可我是在念你所教我念的东西嘛。其实她接下来念出来的还是Ahyee、Bәyee、Cә-yee。

930 cappәtә-ee　Liza把cup of tea念成[kæpәˈtәjiː]。

931 Put your tongue forward until it squeezes against the top of your lower teeth.　把你的舌头向前伸到顶住你下面牙齿的上端。

932 C-c-c –　Liza一下念不出[kʌp]，因此尽是[k-k-k-]的。

933 C-Cup终于念出了[k-kʌp]。

934 By Jupiter　发誓，诅咒用语，见前By Jove条注。

935 done it at the first shot一炮就打响；一试就试成（正确念出cup的音）。

936 could possibly　有可能会。用了possibly，有“是否可能竟然不会”的意思。

937 Not tә-yee = Do not say tә-yee.不要念成[tәjiː]。

938 mind注意；也作mind you。

939 if you ever say bә-yee, cә-yee, dә-yee again如果再念一声[ˈbәjiː][ˈsәjiː][ˈdәjiː]。ever有“只要有一次”的意思。

940 you shall be dragged round the room three times by the hair of your head　我要你被揪着头发在房间里拖着转三圈。注意：drag somebody by the hair之类的结构中习惯用the，不用his；又如catch him by the arm这说法与catch his arm略有不同；前者侧重抓住其人，后者仅是抓住其臂。

941 fortissimo　[fɔːˈtɪsɪməʊ]极响；极强；音乐用语；是意大利语。

942 T, T, T, T. Higgins连续发[tiː]音，教Liza学着念。

943 I cant hear no difference　我听不出（你念的和我念的T的音）有什么区别。Liza用了“双重否定”。

944 cep that it sounds more genteel-like when you say it只是你念的T听起来比较文雅。这就说明她能够听出区别来。cep = except.

945 what the devil are you crying for?　你还哭什么呢？ the devil加强what的语气，但那是诅咒语，Higgins却经常会脱口而出，使用这种语词。

946 Never mind crying a little　稍许哭哭不要紧。mind作“介意”解，后面接动名词。

947 drag you round the room by your hair　前面注中刚说过，在这样的句型中，习惯用by the hair，这里用by your hair，可能为了突出“你的”的意思。

948 Be off with you to Mrs Pearce　到Mrs Pearce那里去。Be off with也作Off with；用于祈使句；去！去掉……！

949 tell her about it.把它（指this lesson，这一课所教的）告诉她。

950 Think about it　想想它（指this lesson，这一课所教的）。

951 Try to do it by yourself独自试着做它（指这一课所教的），自己练习。

952 keep your tongue well forward in your mouth把舌头在嘴里好好（充分地）向前伸着。

953 instead of trying to roll it up and swallow it　而不是拼命把舌头卷起来吞下去。“吞下去”当然是挖苦的话。

954 half-past　一般分写，不用连字号。

955 Away with you.　去！Away with是习语。

956 ordeal　[ɔːˈdiːl]苦难；折磨。

957 go through经历；通过。

958 we meet her again　我们再次在戏中看到她出现。

959 London society of the professional class　伦敦专门职业者阶级的社交界。

第三幕　剧情提要

在Higgins教授的母亲家里。这天是她的会客日。她一向不要儿子在会客日到她家里来，因为他不拘礼仪，谈吐粗野。这回他说还将有个卖花女Liza来访，更使Higgins太太不安。Higgins把他和Pickering打赌的事情告诉她，说明Liza是先来这里做个试验的。

正在这时，Freddy和他的母亲及妹妹——Eynsford Hill太太和Clara小姐——来了。他们虽然家道中落，可母女俩始终装得一派大家风范。

接着Liza来了。她服饰华丽，举止优雅，开始时说话也确实斯文得体。可是她渐渐越说越不像话，最终街头卖花女的原形毕露。Higgins太太啼笑皆非；Eynsford Hill太太表示惊愕万分；Freddy却说这正是最时髦的语言；Clara竟当场学起“时髦”来。

训练工作自得继续进行。六个月的期限未满，而Liza竟已成功地在大使馆招待会上冒充公爵夫人。一位青年语言学家肯定她是匈牙利王族，不可能是英国人，因为英国女人没有一个能说这样正确的英语，只有外国人才能说得那么好。又有位太太则说Liza的言谈与维多利亚女王一般无二。

试验成功。Higgins教授打赌赢了。可是Liza精疲力竭，再也受不了了。

ACT III

It is Mrs Higgins's at-home day1. Nobody has yet arrived. Her drawing room, in a flat on Chelsea Embankment2, has three windows looking on the river3; and the ceiling is not so lofty as it would be in an older house of the same pretension4. The windows are open, giving access to5 a balcony with flowers in pots. If you stand with your face to the windows, you have the fireplace on your left and the door in the right-hand wall close to the corner nearest the windows6.

Mrs Higgins was brought up on Morris and Burne Jones7; and her room, which is very unlike her son's room in Wimpole Street8, is not crowded with furniture9 and little tables and nicknacks10. In the middle of the room there is a big ottoman11; and this12, with the carpet, the Morris wallpapers13, and the Morris chintz window curtains and brocade covers14 of the ottoman and its cushions15, supply all the ornament16, and are much too handsome to be hidden by odds and ends of useless things17. A few good oil-paintings from the exhibitions in the Grosvenor Gallery thirty years ago18 (the Burne Jones, not the Whistler side of them19) are on the walls. The only landscape is a Cecil Lawson20 on the scale of a Rubens21. There is a portrait of Mrs Higgins as she was22 when she defied the fashion in her youth23 in one of the beautiful Rossettian costumes24 which, when caricatured by people who did not understand25, led to the absurdities of popular estheticism in the eighteen-seventies26.

In the corner diagonally opposite the door27 Mrs Higgins, now over sixty and long past taking the trouble to dress out of the fashion28, sits writing at an elegantly simple29 writing-table with a bell button within reach of her hand30. There is a Chippendale chair31 further back in the room32 between her and the window nearest her side. At the other side of the room, further forward, is an Elizabethan chair roughly carved in the taste of Inigo33 Jones. On the same side a piano in a decorated case34. The corner between the fireplace and the window is occupied by a divan cushioned in Morris chintz35.

It is between four and five in the afternoon.

The door is opened violently; and Higgins enters with his hat on36.

MRS HIGGINS [dismayed] Henry! [scolding him] What are you doing here today? It is my at-home day: you promised not to come. [As he bends to kiss her, she takes his hat off, and presents it to him.]

HIGGINS Oh bother!37 [He throws the hat down the table.]

MRS HIGGINS Go home at once.

HIGGINS [kissing her] I know, mother. I came on purpose38.

MRS HIGGINS But you mustnt39. I'm serious40, Henry. You offend all my friends: they stop coming41 whenever they meet you.

HIGGINS Nonsense! I know I have no small talk42; but people dont mind43. [He sits on the settee44.]

MRS HIGGINS Oh! Dont they?45 Small talk indeed!46 What about your large talk?47 Really, dear, you mustnt stay.

HIGGINS I must. Ive a job for you.48 A phonetic job.

MRS HIGGINS No use49, dear. I'm sorry; but50 I cant get round51 yourvowels; and though I like to get pretty postcards in your patent shorthand52, I always have to read the copies in ordinary writing53 you so thoughtfully send me54.

HIGGINS Well, this isnt a phonetic job.

MRS HIGGINS You said it was.

HIGGINS Not your part of it.55 Ive picked up56 a girl.

MRS HIGGINS Does that mean that some girl has picked you up?

HIGGINS Not at all. I dont mean a love affair57.

MRS HIGGINS What a pity!58

HIGGINS Why?

MRS HIGGINS Well, you never fall in love with anyone under forty-five59. When will you discover that there are some rather nice-looking young women about60?

HIGGINS Oh, I cant be bothered with young women61. My idea of a lovable woman is somebody as like you as possible62. I shall never get into the way of63 seriously liking young women: some habits lie too deep to be changed64. [rising abruptly and walking about65, jingling his money and his keys in his trouser pockets66] Besides, theyre all idiots.

MRS HIGGINS Do you know what you would do if you really loved me, Henry?

HIGGINS Oh bother! What? Marry, I suppose.67

MRS HIGGINS No. Stop fidgeting68 and take your hands out of your pockets. [With a gesture of despair, he obeys and sits down again.] Thats a good boy.69 Now tell me about the girl.

HIGGINS She's coming to see you.

MRS HIGGINS I dont remember asking her.70

HIGGINS You didnt.71 I asked her. If youd known her you wouldnt have asked her.

MRS HIGGINS Indeed! Why?

HIGGINS Well, it's like this72. She's a common flower girl. I picked her off the kerbstone73.

MRS HIGGINS And invited her to my at-home74!

HIGGINS [rising and coming to her to coax her] Oh, thatll be all right. Ive taught her to speak properly; and she has strict orders as to her behavior75. She's to keep to two subjects76: the weather and everybody's health – Fine day and How do you do77, you know – and not to let herself go on things in general78. That will be safe.

MRS HIGGINS Safe! To talk about our health!79 about our insides! perhaps about our outsides!80 How could you be so silly, Henry?

HIGGINS [impatiently] Well, she must talk about something81. [He controls himself and sits down again.] Oh, she'll be alright: dont you fuss82. Pickering is in it with me.83 Ive a sort of bet on84 that I'll pass her off as a duchess in six months. I started on her85 some months ago; and she's getting on86 like a house on fire87. I shallwin my bet. She has a quick ear; and she's been easier to teach than my middle-class pupils because she's had to learn a complete new language88. She talks English almost you talk French.89

MRS HIGGINS Thats satisfactory, at all events.90

HIGGINS Well, it is and it isnt91.

MRS HIGGINS What does that mean?

HIGGINS You see, Ive got her pronunciation all right92; but you have to consider not only how a girl pronounces, but what she pronounces93; and that's where –94

They are interrupted by the parlormaid95, announcing guests96.

THE PARLORMAID Mrs and Miss Eynsford Hill97. [She withdraws.]

HIGGINS Oh Lord!98 [He rises; snatches his hat from the table; and makes for99 the door; but before he reaches it his mother introduces him.]

Mrs and Miss Eynsford Hill are the mother and daughter who sheltered from the rain in Covent Garden. The mother is well bred100, quiet, and has the habitual anxiety of straitened means101. The daughter has acquired a gay air102 of being very much at home in society103: the bravado of genteel poverty104.

MRS EYNSFORD HILL [to Mrs Higgins] How do you do? [They shake hands.]

MISS EYNSFORD HILL How d'you do? [She shakes.105]

MRS HIGGINS [introducing] My son Henry.106

MRS EYNSFORD HILL Your celebrated son! I have so longed to meet you107, Professor Higgins.

HIGGINS [gumly, making no movement in her direction108] Delighted.109 [He backs against the piano110 and bows brusquely111.]

MISS EYNSFORD HILL [going to him with confident familiarity112] How do you do?

HIGGINS [staring at her] Ive seen you before113 somewhere. I havnt the ghost of a notion114 where115; but Ive heard your voice116. [drearily] It doesnt matter. Youd better sit down.

MRS HIGGINS I'm sorry to say that my celebrated son has no manners117. You mustnt mind him.

MISS EYSNFORD HILL [gaily] I dont.118 [She sits in the Elizabethan chair119.]

MRS EYSNFORD HILL [a little bewildered] Not at all.120 [She sits on the ottoman between her daughter and Mrs Higgins, who has turned her chair away from the writing-table121.]

HIGGINS Oh, have I been rude? I didnt mean to be.122

He goes to the central window, through which, with his back to the company123, he contemplates the river124 and the flowers in Battersea Park on the opposite bank125 as if they were a frozen desert126.

The parlormaid returns, ushering in Pickering127.

THE PARLORMAID Colonel Pickering128. [She withdraws.]

PICKERING How do you do, Mrs Higgins?

MRS HIGGINS So glad129 youve come. Do you know Mrs Eysnford Hill – Miss Eysnford Hill? [Exchange of bows.130 The Colonel brings the Chipendale chair a little forward between Mrs Hill and Mrs Higgins, and sits down.]

PICKERING Has Henry told you what weve come for131?

HIGGINS [over his shoulder132] We were interrupted: damn it!

MRS HIGGINS Oh Henry, Henry, really!133

MRS EYNSFORD HILL [half rising134] Are we in the way?135

MRS HIGGINS [rising and making her sit down again136] No, no. You couldnt have come more fortunately137: we want you to meet a friend of ours.

HIGGINS [turning hopefully] Yes, by George! We want two or three people. Youll do as well as anybody else.138

The parlormaid returns, ushering Freddy139.

THE PARLORMAID Mr Eynsford Hill.

HIGGINS [almost audibly, past endurance140] God of Heaven! another of them141.

FREDDY [shaking hands with Mrs Higgins] Ahdedo?142

MRS HIGGINS Very good of you to come.143 [introducing] Colonel Pickering.

FREDDY [bowing] Ahdedo?

MRS HIGGINS I dont think you know my son, Professor Higgins.144

FREDDY [going to Higgins] Ahdedo?

HIGGINS [looking at him much as if he were a pickpocket145] I'll take my oath146 Ive met you before somewhere. Where was it?

FREDDY I dont think so.

HIGGINS [resignedly147] It dont matter148, anyhow149. Sit down.

He shakes Freddy's hand, and almost slings him on to the ottoman with his face to the window150; then comes round to the other side of it151.

HIGGINS Well, here we are152, anyhow! [He sits down on the ottoman next Mrs Eynsford Hill, on her left153.] And now, what the devil154 are we going to talk about until Eliza comes?

MRS HIGGINS Henry: you are the life and soul of the Royal Society's soirée155; but really youre rather trying156 on more commonplace occasions157.

HIGGINS Am I?158 Very sorry. [beaming159 suddenly] I suppose I am, you know. [uproariously] Ha, ha!

MISS EYNSFORD HILL [who considers Higgins quite eligible matrimonially160] I sympathize. I havnt any small talk. If people would only be frank and say what they really think!161

HIGGINS [relapsing into gloom162] Lord forbid!163

MRS EYNSFORD HILL [taking up her daughter's cue164] But why?

HIGGINS What they think they ought to think is bad enough165, Lord knows166; but what they really think would break up the whole show167. Do you suppose it would be really agreeable168 if I were to come out now with what I really think169?

MISS EYNSFORD HILL [gaily] Is it so very cynical170?

HIGGINS Cynical! Who the dickens171 said it was cynical? I mean it wouldnt be decent172.

MRS EYNSFORD HILL [seriously] Oh! I'm sure you dont mean that173, Mr Higgins.

HIGGINS You see, we're all savages, more or less174. We're supposed to be175 civilized and cultured – to know all about poetry and philosophy and art and science, and so on; but how many of us know even the meanings of these names?[to Miss Hill] What do you know of poetry? [to Mrs Hill] What do you know of science? [indicating Freddy] What does he know of art or science or anything else? What the devil do you imagine I know of philosophy?

MRS HIGGINS [warningly176] Or of manners177, Henry?

THE PARLORMAID [opening the door] Miss Doolittle. [She withdraws.]

HIGGINS [rising hastily and running to Mrs Higgins] Here she is, mother.178 [He stands on tiptoe179 and makes signs over his mother's head180 to Eliza to indicate to her which lady is her hostess181.]

Eliza, who is exquisitely dressed182, produces an impression of such remarkable distinction and beauty183 as she enters184 that they all rise, quite fluttered185. Guided by Higgins's signals186, she comes to Mrs Higgins with studied grace187.

LIZA [speaking with pedantic correctness of pronunciation188 and great beauty of tone] How do you do, Mrs Higgins? [She gasps slightly in making sure of the H in Higgins189, but is quite successful.] Mr Higgins told me I might come.

MRS HIGGINS [cordially] Quite right190: I'm very glad indeed to see you.

PICKERING How do you do, Miss Doolittle?

LIZA [shaking hands with him] Colonel Pickering, is it not?191

MRS EYNSFORD HILL I feel sure we have met before, Miss Doolittle. I remember your eyes.

LIZA How do you do? [She sits down on the ottoman gracefully in the place just left vacant192 by Higgins.]

MRS EYNSFORD HILL [introducing] My daughter Clara.

LIZA How do you do?

CLARA [impulsively] How do you do? [She sits down on the ottoman beside Eliza193, devouring her with her eyes194.]

FREDDY [coming to their side of the ottoman] Ive certainly had the pleasure.195

MRS EYNSFORD HILL [introducing] My son Freddy.

LIZA How do you do?

Freddy bows and sits down in the Elizabethan chair, infatuated196.

HIGGINS [suddenly] By George, yes: it all comes back to me!197 [They stare at him.] Covent Garden!198 [lamentably] What a damned thing!199

MRS HIGGINS Henry, please!200 [He is about to sit201 on the edge of the table.]Dont sit on my writing-table: youll break it.

HIGGINS [sulkily] Sorry.

He goes to the divan, stumbling into the fender and over the fire-irons on his way202, extricating himself with muttered imprecations203; and finishing his disastrous journey by throwing himself so impatiently on the divan204 that he almost breaks it. Mrs Higgins looks at him, but controls herself205 and says nothing.

A long and painful pause206 ensues.

MRS HIGGINS [at last, conversationally207] Will it rain, do you think?208

LIZA The shallow depression in the west of these islands is likely to move slowly in an easterly direction.209 There are no indications of any great change in the barometrical situation.210

FREDDY Ha, ha! How awfully funny!211

LIZA What is wrong with that212, young man213? I bet I got it right.214

FREDDY Killing!215

MRS EYNSFORD HILL I'm sure I hope it wont turn cold.216 Theres so much influenza217 about218. It runs right through our whole family219 regularly every spring.

LIZA [darkly220] My aunt died of221 influenza: so they said.

MRS EYNSFORD HILL [clicks her tongue222 sympathetically] !!!

LIZA [in the same tragic tone] But it's my belief they done the old woman in223.

MRS HIGGINS [puzzled] Done her in?

LIZA Y-e-e-e-es, Lord love you!224 Why should she die of influenza?225 She come through diphtheria right enough226 the year before227. I saw her with my own eyes228. Fairly blue with it, she was.229 They all thought she was dead; but my father he230 kept ladling gin down her throat til she came to231 so sudden that she bit the bowl off the spoon232.

MRS EYNSFORD HILL [startled] Dear me!233

LIZA [piling up the indictment234] What call would a woman with that strength in her have to die of influenza235? What becomeof her new straw hat236 that should have come to me237? Somebody pinched238 it; and what I say is, them as pinched it done her in239.

MRS EYNSFORD HILL What does doing her in mean?240

HIGGINS [hastily] Oh, thats the new small talk241. To do a person in means to kill them.242

MRS EYNSFORD HILL [to Eliza, horrified243] You surely dont believe that your aunt was killed?

LIZA Do I not?244 Them she lived with245 would have killed her for a hat-pin246, let alone a hat247.

MRS EYNSFORD HILL But it cant have been right for your father to pour spirits down her throat like that248. It might have killed her.249

LIZA Not her.250 Gin was mother's milk to her251. Besides, he'd poured so much down his own throat that he knew the good of it252.

MRS EYNSFORD HILL Do you mean that he drank253?

LIZA Drank! My word!254 Something chronic255.

MRS EYNSFORD HILL How dreadful for you!256

LIZA Not a bit.257 It never did him no harm what I could see.258 But then he did not keep it up regular259. [cheerfully] On the burst260, as you might say261, from time to time262. And always more agreeable263 when he had a drop in264. When he was out of work, my mother used to give him fourpence and tell him to go out and not come back265 until he'd drunk himself cheerful266 and loving-like267. Theres lots of women has to make their husbands drunk268 to make them fit to live with269. [now quite at her ease270] You see, it's like this. If a man has a bit of a conscience271, it always takes him272 when he's sober; and then it makes him low-spirited273. A drop of booze just takes that off274 and makes him happy. [to Freddy, who is in convulsions of suppressed laughter275] Here! What are you sniggering276 at?

FREDDY The new small talk. You do it so awfully well.

LIZA If I was doing it proper277, what was you laughing at?278 [to Higgins] Have I said anything I oughtnt279?

MRS HIGGINS [interposing] Not at all, Miss Doolittle.

LIZA Well, thats a mercy280, anyhow. [expansively281] What I always say is –

HIGGINS [rising and looking at his watch] Ahem282!

LIZA [looking round at him; taking the hint283; and rising] Well: I must go. [They all rise. Freddy goes to the door.] So pleased to have met you.284 Goodbye. [She shakes hands with Mrs Higgins.]

MRS HIGGINS Goodbye.

LIZA Goodbye, Colonel Pickering.

PICKERING Goodbye, Miss Doolittle. [They shake hands.]

LIZA [nodding to the others] Goodbye, all.285

FREDDY [opening the door for her] Are you walking across the Park286, Miss Doolittle? If so –

LIZA [with perfectly elegant diction287] Walk!288 Not bloody likely.289 [sensation290] I am going in a taxi. [She goes out.]

Pickering gasps and sits down. Freddy goes out on the balcony to catch another glimpse of Eliza291.

MRS EYNSFORD HILL [suffering from shock292] Well, I really cant get used to the new ways293.

CLARA [throwing herself discontentedly in to the Elizabethan chair] Oh, it's all right, mamma, quite right294. People will think we never go anywhere or see anybody295 if you are so old-fashioned.

MRS EYNSFORD HILL I daresay296 I am very old-fashioned; but I do hope you wont begin using that expression297, Clara. I have got accustomed to hear you talking about men as rotters298, and calling everything filthy and beastly299; though I do think it horrible and unladylike.300 But this last301 is really too much. Dont you think so, Colonel Pickering?

PICKERING Dont ask me. Ive been away in India302 for several years; and manners have changed so much that I sometimes dont know whether I'm at a respectable dinner-table or in a ship's forecastle303.

CLARA It's all a matter of habit.304 Theres no right or wrong in it. Nobody means anything by it.305 And it's so quaint306, and gives such a smart emphasis to things that are not in themselves very witty307. I find the new small talk308 delightful and quite innocent.

MRS EYNSFORD HILL [rising] Well, after that, I think it's time for us to go.

Pickering and Higgins rise.

CLARA [rising] Oh yes: we have three at-homes to go to still309. Goodbye, Mrs Higgins. Goodbye, Colonel Pickering. Goodbye, Professor Higgins.

HIGGINS [coming grimly at her310 from the divan, and accompanying her to the door311] Goodbye. Be sure you try on312 that small talk at the three at-homes. Dont be nervous about it. Pitch it in strong.313

CLARA [all smiles314] I will. Goodbye. Such nonsense, all this early Victorian prudery!315

HIGGINS [tempting her316] Such damned nonsense!

CLARA Such bloody nonsense!

MRS EYNSFORD HILL [convulsively317] Clara!

CLARA Ha! Ha! [She goes out radiant318, conscious of being thoroughly up to date319, and is heard descending the stairs in a stream of silvery laughter320.]

FREDDY [to the heavens at large321] Well, I ask you –322 [He gives it up323, and comes to Mrs Higgins.] Goodbye.

MRS HIGGINS [shaking hands] Goodbye. Would you like to meet Miss Doolittle again?

FREDDY [eagerly] Yes, I should, most awfully324.

MRS HIGGINS Well, you know my days325.

FREDDY Yes. Thanks awfully326. Goodbye. [He goes out.]

MRS EYNSFORD HILL [to Pickering] It's no use.327 I shall never be able to bring myself to use that word328.

PICKERING Don't. It's not compulsory329, you know. Youll get on quiet well without it330.

MRS EYNSFORD HILL Only331, Clara is so down on me332 if I am not positively reeking with the latest slang333. Goodbye .

PICKERING Goodbye. [They shake hands.]

MRS EYNSFORD HILL [to Mrs Higgins] You mustnt mind Clara.334 [Pickering, catching from her lowered tone that this is not meant for him to hear335, discreetly joins Higgins336 at the window.] We are so poor! And she gets so few parties337, poor child! She doesnt quite know338.[Mrs Higgins, seeing that her eyes are moist339, takes her hand sympathetically and goes with her to the door.] But the boy340 is nice. Dont you think so?

MRS HIGGINS Oh, quite nice341. I shall always be delighted to see him.

MRS EYNSFORD HILL Thank you, dear. Goodbye. [She goes out.]

HIGGINS [eagerly] Well? Is Eliza presentable342? [He swoops on his mother and drags her to the ottoman, where she sits down in Eliza's place with her son on her left.]

Pickering returns to his chair on her right.

MRS HIGGINS You silly boy, of course she's not presentable. She's a triumph of your art and of her dressmaker's343; but if you suppose for a moment344 that she doesnt give herself away in every sentence she utters345, you must be perfectly cracked about her346.

PICKERING But dont you think something might be done347? I mean something to eliminate the sanguinary element from her conversation348.

MRS HIGGINS Not as long as she is in Henry's hands.349

HIGGINS [aggrieved] Do you mean that my language is improper?

MRS HIGGINS No, dearest: it would be quite proper – say on a canal barge350; but it would not be proper for her at a garden party.

HIGGINS [deeply injured351] Well I must say –

PICKERING [interrupting him] Come, Higgins: you must learn to know yourself352. I havent heard such language as yours since we used to review the volunteers in Hyde Park353 twenty years ago.

HIGGINS [sulkily] Oh, well, if you say so, I suppose I dont always talk like a bishop354.

MRS HIGGINS [quieting Henry with a touch355] Colonel Pickering: will you tell me what is the exact state of things in Wimpole Street356?

PICKERING [cheerfully: as if this completely changed the subject] Well, I have come to live there with Henry. We work together at my Indian Dialects357; and we think it more convenient –

MRS HIGGINS Quite so.358 I know all about that: it's an excellent arrangement. But where does this girl live?

HIGGINS With us359, of course. Where should she live?

MRS HIGGINS But on what terms?360 Is she a servant? If not, what is she?361

PICKERING [slowly] I think I know what you mean, Mrs Higgins.

HIGGINS Well, dash me if I do!362 Ive had to work at the girl every day for months to get her to her present pitch363. Besides, she's useful. She knows where my things are, and remembers my appointments and so forth364.

MRS HIGGINS How does your housekeeper get on with her365?

HIGGINS Mrs Pearce. Oh, she's jolly366 glad to get so much taken off her hands367; for before Eliza came, she used to have to find things and remind me of my appointments368. But she's got some silly bee in her bonnet369 about Eliza. She keeps saying 'You dont think370, sir': doesnt she, Pick?

PICKERING Yes: thats the formula371. 'You dont think, sir.' Thats the end of every conversation about Eliza.

HIGGINS As if I ever stop thinking the girl and her confounded vowels and consonants.372 I'm worn out373, thinking about her, and watching her lips and her teeth and her tongue, not to mention374 her soul, which is the quaintest of the lot375.

MRS HIGGINS You certainly are a pretty pair of babies376, playing with your live doll377.

HIGGINS Playing! The hardest job I ever tackled: make no mistake378 about that, mother. But you have no idea379 how frightfully380 interesting it is to take a human being381 and change her into a quite different human being by creating a new speech for her382. It's filling up the deepest gulf383 that separates class from class384 and soul from soul.

PICKERING [drawing his chair close to Mrs Higgins and bending over to her385 eagerly] Yes, it's enormously386 interesting. I assure you387, Mrs Higgins, we take Eliza very seriously388. Every week – every day almost – there is some new change389. [closer again] We keep records of every stage390 – dozens of391 gramophone disks and photographs –

HIGGINS [assailing her at the other ear392] Yes, by George: it's the most absorbing experiment I ever tackled393. She regularly fills our lives up394; doesnt she, Pick?

PICKERING We're always talking Eliza395.

HIGGINS Teaching Eliza.

PICKERING Dressing Eliza396.

MRS HIGGINS What!

HIGGINS Inventing new Elizas397.

speaking together　同时一起说话。这里用大括弧括在一起的上下两部分的说话，好比两部重唱的乐谱。括弧内的上半部和上半部连读，下半部同下半部连读。如HIGGINS You know, she has the most extraordinary quickness of ear连下去接读just like a parrot ...；PICKERING I assure you, my dear Mrs Higgins, that girl连下去接读is a genius ...。

quickness of ear耳朵灵；听觉灵敏；这里的ear指分辨各种发音的能力，是抽象名词。

classical concerts [ˈkɒnsəts]演奏古典音乐的音乐会。

Continental [ˌkɒntɪˈnentl]欧洲大陆的；首字母用大写。

Hottentot [ˈhɒtntɒt] （西南非洲的）霍屯督（人，也指其语言）。霍屯督语言多有如啧舌的倒吸气音，所以称Hottentot clicks。

halls　指music halls；表演歌舞和杂耍的游艺场；在美国往往指“音乐厅”。it's all the same to her这对她都一样。it指无论带她到古典音乐会还是游艺场去。

she plays everything she hears right off　马上弹出她听到的一切（乐曲）。she hears前面省略关系代词（that），she hear修饰everything。right off = right away.马上；立刻；是习语；这里修饰plays，不是修饰hears。

things it took me years to get hold of　我费了几年功夫才掌握的东西（指各种语言）。things后面省略关系代词，作get hold of的宾语。it虚词，引出和它同位的不定式短语to get hold of (that)。

picks them up like a shot　一下就学会它们（指各种语言）。like a shot一下子；口语中也作“欣然”解；是习语。

as if she had been at it all her life　仿佛她一生都学这一行。at it从事于此；是习语。it无所专指，是所谓it的泛用（the vague use of "it"）。

Beethoven [ˈbeɪtəʊvn] and Brahms [brɑːmz]贝多芬和布拉姆斯（都是古典音乐家）。Beethoven Ludwig van Beethoven（1770–1827），德国作曲家。Brahms Johannes Brahms（1833–1897），德国作曲家。Lehar [ˈleɪhɑː] and Lionel [ˈlaɪənl] Monckton [ˈmʌŋktən]两个当时著名的音乐家。Lehar Franz Lehar（1870–1948），匈牙利歌剧作曲家。Lionel Monckton （1861–1924）英国作家，歌剧作曲家。

she'd never as much as touched a piano　她连碰也没碰过钢琴。not（或never）as（或so）much as = not（或never）even；这个习语中的much是副词。

MRS HIGGINS [putting her fingers in her ears, as they are by this time398 shouting one another down399 with an intolerable noise] Sh–sh–sh–sh!400 [They stop.]

PICKERING I beg your pardon. [He draws his chair back apologetically.]

HIGGINS Sorry. When Pickering starts shouting nobody can get a word in edgeways401.

MRS HIGGINS Be quiet, Henry. Colonel Pickering: dont you realize that when Eliza walked into Wimpole Street, something walked in with her402?

PICKERING Her father did. But Henry soon got rid of403 him.

MRS HIGGINS It would have been more to the point if her mother had.404 But as her mother didnt something else did405.

PICKERING But what?406

MRS HIGGINS [unconsciously dating herself by the word407] A problem.

PICKERING Oh, I see. The problem of how to pass her off as a lady.

HIGGINS I'll solve that problem. Ive half solved it already.

MRS HIGGINS No, you two infinitely stupid male creatures408: the problem of what is to be done with her afterwards409.

HIGGINS I dont see anything in that.410 She can go her own way411, with all the advantages I have given her412.

MRS HIGGINS The advantages of that poor woman who was here just now!413 The manners and habits that disqualify a fine lady from earning her own living without giving her a fine lady's income!414End} Is that what you mean?415

PICKERING [indulgently, being rather bored416] Oh, that will be all right417, Mrs Higgins. [He rises to go.]

HIGGINS [rising also] We'll find her some light employment418.

PICKERING She's happy enough. Dont you worry about her.419 Goodbye. [He shakes hands as if he were consoling a frightened child, and makes for the door.]

HIGGINS Anyhow, theres no good bothering now420. The thing's done.421 Goodbye, mother. [He kissed her, and follows Pickering.]

PICKERING [turning for a final consolation422] There are plenty of openings423. We'll do whats right. Goodbye.

HIGGINS [to Pickering as they go out together] Lets take her to the Shakespear exhibition at Earls Court424.

PICKERING Yes: lets425. Her remarks will be delicious.

HIGGINS She'll mimic all the people for us426 when we get home.

PICKERING Ripping.427 [Both are heard laughing as they go downstairs.]

MRS HIGGINS [rises with an impatient bounce, and returns to her work at the writing-table. She sweeps a litter of disarranged papers out of the way428; snatches a sheet of paper from her stationery case429, and tries resolutely to write. At the third line she gives it up430; flings down her pen; grips the table angrily and exclaims.] Oh, men! men!! men!!!

Clearly Eliza will not pass as a duchess yet; and Higgins's bet remains unwon431. But the six months are not yet exhausted432; and just in time Eliza does actually pass as a princess433. For a glimpse of how she did it imagine434 an Embassy in London435 one summer evening after dark436. The hall door has an awning437 and a carpet across the sidewalk to the kerb438, because a grand reception is in progress439. A small crowd is lined up440 to see the guests arrive.

A Rolls-Royce car drives up441. Pickering in evening dress, with medals and orders442, alights, and hands out Eliza443, in opera cloak444, evening dress, diamonds, fan, flowers and all accessories445. Higgins follows. The car drives off; and the three go up the steps and into the house, the door opening for them as they approach446.

Inside the house they find themselves in447 a spacious hall from which the grand staircase rises. On the left are the arrangements for the gentlemen's cloaks448. The male guests are depositing their hats and wraps449 there.

On the right is a door leading to the ladies' cloakroom. Ladies are going in cloaked and coming out in splendor450. Pickering whispers to Eliza and points out the ladies' room451. She goes into it. Higgins and Pickering take off their overcoats and take tickets for them from the attendant452.

One of the guests, occupied in the same way453, has his back turned454. Having taken his ticket, he turns round and reveals himself as an important looking455 young man with an astonishingly hairy face456. He has an enormous moustache, flowing out into luxuriant whiskers457. Waves of hair cluster on his brow.458

His hair is cropped closely at the back459, and glows with oil. Otherwise460 he is very smart. He wears several worthless orders461. He is evidently a foreigner, guessable as a whiskered Pandour from Hungary462; but in spite of the ferocity463 of his moustache he is amiable and genially voluble464.

Recognizing Higgins465, he flings his arms wide apart466 and approaches him enthusiastically.

WHISKERS467 Maestro468, maestro. [He embraces Higgins and kisses him on both cheeks.] You remember me?

HIGGINS No I dont. Who the devil are you?

WHISKERS I am your pupil: your first pupil, your best and greatest pupil. I am little Nepommuck469, the marvellous boy. I have made your name famous throughout Europe. You teach me phonetic470. You cannot forget ME471.

HIGGINS Why dont you shave?

NEPOMMUCK I have not your imposing appearance472, your chin, your brow. Nobody notice me when I shave473. Now I am famous: they call me Hairy Faced Dick474.

HIGGINS And what are you doing here among all these swells475?

NEPOMMUCK I am interpreter476. I speak 32 languages. I am indispensable at these international parties477. You are great cockney specialist478: you place a man anywhere in London the moment he open his mouth479. I place any man in Europe.

A footman hurries down the grand staircase and comes to Nepommuck.

FOOTMAN You are wanted upstairs.480 Her Excellency481 cannot understand the Greek gentleman.

NEPOMMUCK Thank you, yes, immediately482.

The footman goes and is lost in the crowd483.

NEPOMMUCK [to Higgins] This Greek diplomatist pretends he cannot speak nor understand484 English. He cannot deceive me.485 He is the son of a Clerkenwell watchmaker486. He speaks English so villainously487 that he dare not utter a word of it without betraying his origin488. I help him to pretend489; but I make him pay through the nose490. I make them all pay491. Ha ha! [He hurries upstairs.]

PICKERING Is this fellow really an expert? Can he find out Eliza492 and blackmail her?

HIGGINS We shall see.493 If he find her out I lose my bet.

Eliza comes from the cloakroom and joins them.

PICKERING Well, Eliza, now for it494. Are you ready?

LIZA Are you nervous, Colonel?495

PICKERING Frightfully.496 I feel exactly as I felt before my first battle497. It's the first time that frightens.498

LIZA It is not the first time for me499, Colonel. I have done this fifty times – hundreds of times – in my little piggery in Angel Court in my day-dreams500. I am in a dream now.501 Promise me not to let Professor Higgins wake me; for if he does I shall forget everything and talk as I used to in Drury Lane502.

PICKERING Not a word503, Higgins. [to Eliza] Now, ready?504

LIZA Ready.

PICKERING Go.

They mount the stairs, Higgins last.505 Pickering whispers to the footman on the first landing506.

FIRST LANDING FOOTMAN Miss Doolittle507, Colonel Pickering, Professor Higgins.

SECOND LANDING FOOTMAN Miss Doolittle, Colonel Pickering, Professor Higgins.

At the top of the staircase the Ambassador and his wife, with Nepommuck at her elbow508, are receiving509.

HOSTESS510 [taking Eliza's hand] How d'ye do?511

HOST [same play512] How d'ye do? How d'ye do, Pickering?

LIZA [with a beautiful gravity that awes her hostess513] How do you do?[She passes on to the drawing room.]

HOSTESS Is that your adopted daughter514, Colonel Pickering? She will make a sensation515.

PICKERING Most kind of you to invite her for me.516 [He passes on.]

HOSTESS [to Nepommuck] Find out all about her.517

NEPOMMUCK [bowing] Excellency518 – [He goes into the crowd.]

HOST How d'ye do, Higgins? You have a rival519 here tonight. He introduced himself as your pupil.520 Is he any good?521

HIGGINS He can learn a language in a fortnight – knows dozens of them. A sure mark of a fool.522 As a phonetician, no good whatever.523

HOSTESS How d'ye do, Professor?

HIGGINS How do you do? Fearful bore for you this sort of thing524. Forgive my part in it.525 [He passes on.]

In the drawing room and its suite of salons the reception is in full swing.526 Eliza passes through. She is so intent on her ordeal that she walks like a somnambulist in a desert instead of a débutante in a fashionable crowd.527 They stop talking to look at her, admiring her dress528, her jewels, and her strangely attractive self529. Some of the younger ones at the back530 stand on their chairs to see.

The Host and Hostess come in from the staircase and mingle with their guests. Higgins, gloomy and contemptuous of the whole business531, comes into the group where they are chatting532.

HOSTESS Ah, here is Professor Higgins: he will tell us. Tell us about the wonderful young lady, Professor.

HIGGINS [almost morosely533] What wonderful young lady?

HOSTESS You know very well. They tell me there has been nothing like her in London since534 people stood on their chairs to look at Mrs Langtry535.

Nepommuck joins the group, full of news536.

HOSTESS Ah, here you are at last, Nepommuck. Have you found out all about the Doolittle lady?

NEPOMMUCK I have found out all about her. She is a fraud537.

HOSTESS A fraud! Oh, no.

NEPOMMUCK YES538, yes. She cannot deceive me539. Her name cannot be Doolittle.540

HIGGINS Why?

NEPOMMUCK Because Doolittle is an English name. And she isnot English.

HOSTESS Oh, nonsense! She speaks English perfectly.

NEPOMMUCK Too perfectly. Can you shew me any English woman who speaks English as it should be spoken541? Only foreigners who have been taught to speak it well.

HOSTESS Certainly she terrified me by the way she said How d'ye do542. I had a schoolmistress who talked like that; and I was mortally afraid of her543. But if she is not English what is she?544

NEPOMMUCK Hungarian545.

ALL THE REST Hungarian!

NEPOMMUCK Hungarian. And of royal blood546. I am Hungarian. My blood is royal.

HIGGINS Did you speak to her in Hungarian?

NEPOMMUCK I did. She was very clever547. She said 'Please speak to me in English: I do not understand French.548' French! She pretend not to know the difference between Hungarian and French. Impossible: she knows both.549

HIGGINS And the blood royal550? How did you find that out?551

NEPOMMUCK Instinct552, maestro, instinct. Only the Magyar races553 can produce that air of the divine right, those resolute eyes554. She is a princess555.

HOST What do you say556, Professor?

HIGGINS I say an ordinary London girl557 out of the gutter558 and taught559 to speak by an expert. I place her in Drury Lane560.

NEPOMMUCK Ha ha ha! Oh, maestro, maestro, you are mad on the subject of cockney dialects561. The London gutter is the whole world for you562.

HIGGINS [to the Hostess] What does Your Excellency say?

HOSTESS Oh, of course I agree with Nepommuck. She must be a princess at least563.

HOST Not necessarily legitimate564, of course. Morganatic perhaps.565 But that is undoubtedly her class.566

HIGGINS I stick to my opinion.567

HOSTESS Oh, you are incorrigible568.

The group breaks up569, leaving Higgins isolated570. Pickering joins him.

PICKERING Where is Eliza? We must keep an eye on her571.

Eliza joins them.

LIZA I dont think I can bear much more572. The people all stare so at me.573 An old lady has just told me that I speak exactly like Queen Victoria574. I am sorry if I have lost your bet575. I have done my best; but nothing can make me the same as these people.

PICKERING You have not lost it576, my dear. You have won it ten times over577.

HIGGINS Let us get out of this578. I have had enough of chattering579 to these fools.

PICKERING Eliza is tired; and I am hungry. Let us clear out580 and have supper somewhere.

1 at-home day　接待客人的日子；会客日。at home除“在家”、“在国内”等解释外，还可作“在家接待客人”解，如：I am not at home today.我今天不会客。at-home对客人的接待；家庭招待会。

2 a flat on Chelsea Embankment　Chelsea堤岸上的（一幢房子中的）一套公寓。flat一套公寓；这是英国说法，美国人叫apartment。Chelsea伦敦西南部、泰晤士河北岸的一个区，过去画家、作家多居此。Chelsea堤岸实是Chelsea区里的泰晤士河岸。注意：Chelsea Embankment前面没有the；介词用on。

3 windows looking on the river　临河的窗；河指泰晤士河。

4 an older house of the same pretension　同样气派的较老的房子。过去房子的天花板较高，现在的低。

5 giving access [ˈækses] to通往。

6 close to the corner nearest the windows　贴近靠窗的角落。close是形容词，修饰door。

7 was brought up on Morris [ˈmɒrɪs] and Burne Jones [bɜ:nˈdʒәʊnz]是受着Morris和Burne Jones的影响而形成她的艺术修养的。bring up使成长；教育；培养。Morris William Morris（1834–1896），英国诗人兼工艺美术家，信奉社会主义，主张社会主义改革，曾开设工厂，所制工艺美术品，风行一时。Burne Jones是复姓，其全名为Edward Burne Jones（1833–1898），英国画家，美术设计家，拉斐尔前派（Pre-Raphaelite）的巨匠，Morris的终身好友。

8 very unlike her son's room in Wimpole Street　跟她儿子（Henry Higgins）在Wimpole Street的房间（见第二幕）大不相同。儿子是语言学家，房间里放满了家具和实验仪器等，而他母亲的房间布置得很雅致，处处显现出她崇尚时新的拉斐尔前派的艺术情趣。母子性格迥然不同。

9 crowded with furniture　放满家具。furniture家具的集称，是不可数名词。“一件家具”应作a piece（或an article或a bit）of furniture。

10 nicknacks [ˈnɪknæks]零星的装饰物（家具或服装等）；小摆设。nicknack也作knicknack。

11 ottoman [ˈɒtəmən]没有靠背和扶手的厚垫长榻或长凳，也指厚垫脚凳；这里指前者。

12 this　指ottoman。

13 Morris wallpapers Morris和他的几个同伴们设计的那种糊墙纸。下面Morris chintz中的Morris也是这个意思。

14 brocade [brəˈkeɪd] covers织锦缎套子。

15 its cushions [ˈkʊʃnz] ottoman上放的垫子。

16 supply all the ornament构成全部的装饰。这里supply的主语是this (= ottoman)，按语法应改用supplies；但this后面用介词with引起一连串其他东西，所以在概念上supply的主体是多样东西，而不是一个ottoman；也可以说这里的介词with实际上起了连词and的作用。

17 are much too handsome to be hidden by odds and ends of useless things = are so handsome that they should not be hidden ...（这些家具和装饰）太漂亮了，不能让没用的零星东西来遮盖掉；加上零星东西反而要遮盖原已非常漂亮的布置。这里are和supply并列，主语同样是this，按语法应改用is，如上条所注。odds and ends零零碎碎的东西（或事情）；是习语。

18 exhibitions[ˌeksɪˈbɪʃnz] in the Grosvenor [ˈgrәʊvnә] Gallery thirty years ago三十年前在Grosvenor画廊展出的展品。the Grosvenor Gallery在伦敦Bond Street，创立于1876年，是著名的美术展览馆，也称the Grosvenor；注意前面有the, Grosvenor中的s不发音。

19 the Burne Jones, not the Whistler [ˈwɪslә] side of them它们（这些油画展品）属于Burne Jones（拉斐尔前派）的，而不属于Whistler（印象派）的。Burne Jones后面省略side of them；或按照严格的标点法，在Whistler后面应加逗号，表示Burne Jones和Whistler并列地修饰side。Whistler James Abbott McNeill Whistler（1834–1903），居住在英国的美国旧派油画家兼蚀刻画家。

20 The only landscape [ˈlændskeɪp] is a Cecil ([ˈsesl]或[ˈsɪsl]) Lawson [ˈlɔːsn]唯一的一幅风景画是Cecil Lawson的作品。landscape风景画；一片风景；是可数名词。Cecil Lawson英国风景画家，善作巨幅风景画；前面用a，表示他的作品；下面a Rubens也指作品。

21 on the scale of a Rubens [ˈru:bɪnz]像Rubens的画那种画幅的。Rubens Peter Paul Rubens（1577–1640），欧洲中世纪佛兰德（Flanders）画家，所作多为巨幅画。

22 a portrait [ˈpɔːtreɪt] of Mrs Higgins as she was一张Mrs Higgins当时模样的肖像。比较：a portrait of Mrs Higgins's: Mrs Higgins所有（或所画、所拍摄）的肖像画（或照片）。

23 when she defied the fashion in her youth　在她年轻时与当时流行的服装式样相对抗；敢于打扮得新奇；修饰as she was的was。

24 Rossettian [rɒˈsetɪən] costumes [ˈkɒstjuːmz] Rossetti画派风格的服装。Rossetti Dante Gabriel Rossetti（1828–1882），英国诗人兼画家，和他妹妹Christina Georgina Rossetti及上述Burne Jones等，都是拉斐尔前派的中心人物。costumes （某一时代、地区、或某一类人等特殊的）服装。

25 when caricatured [ˈkærɪkətʃʊəd] by people who did not understand一经被不懂的人们歪曲地模仿；修饰led；这个插入的状语从句把which和led（主语和谓语）隔开了。when caricatured = when the Rossettian costumes were caricatured.

26 led to the absurdities of popular estheticism [iːsˈθetɪsɪzəm] in the eighteen-seventies导致19世纪70年代（1870–1879）流行的唯美主义的种种荒唐行为。led to通往；导致。estheticism = aestheticism.

27 diagonally [daɪˈæɡənəli] opposite the door斜对着门的。diagonally成对角线地；斜对面地。

28 long past taking the trouble to dress out of the fashion　早过了费心定要穿得不同于流行服装的年纪；不再别出心裁，务求穿得不同流俗。past是介词；这个介词短语和上面的now over sixty并列，都修饰Mrs Higgins。

29 elegantly [ˈelɪɡəntli] simple典雅朴素的；朴素雅致的。

30 with a bell button within reach of her hand有个伸手可以按到的电铃按钮。

31 Chippendale [ˈtʃɪpəndeɪl] chair Chippendale风格的椅子。Chippendale Thomas Chippendale（1718–1779），英国著名的家具设计家。

32 further back in the room　在房间的再往后处；在舞台上更靠近后台处。下面further forward正与此相反。
