228 Damn Mrs Pearce Mrs Pearce见鬼去。Higgins刚说从来不大发脾气，这下又大发雷霆了。

229 lavished my hard-earned knowledge and the treasure of my regard and intimacy [ˈɪntɪməsi] on a heartless guttersnipe把我辛苦得来的知识和我珍贵的关心及亲切浪费在一个没心肝的小叫花子身上。Higgins在第二幕中就骂Eliza是draggletailed guttersnipe。

230 impressive decorum [dɪˈkɔːrəm]给人深刻印象的文雅仪态。

231 spoils it by slamming the door savagely粗野地砰的一声猛力关门而破坏了这个（文雅的仪态）。

232 Eliza goes down on her knees　跪下；是习语。

233 what to do with it　如何处置它（戒指）。

234 in a tearing [ˈteərɪŋ] rage愤怒欲裂地。tearing形容词，原义“撕裂的”，转义“强烈的”。

235 sumptuous [ˈsʌmptʃʊəs] dressing-table豪华的梳妆台。

236 walking dress　妇女（平常出门穿的）便服。which指walking dress, hat和shoes。

237 padded hanger [ˈhæŋə]有装垫的衣架。

238 adjusts it carefully in the evening dress把它（衣架）小心地安放在晚礼服里；把晚礼服小心挂在衣架上。

239 walking shoes　（平常出门穿的）轻便鞋。

240 fastens it on　把它（手表）带上。

241 pulls on her gloves戴上手套。戴手套、穿袜子的动词用pull on，这是习语。

242 vanity bag　（妇女携带化妆品等用的）手提包。也称vanity case, vanity box。

243 to see that her purse is there查看一下，确认她的钱包在那里（手提包里）；看看钱包是否在那里。习语see (to it) that ...表示“留心做到”、“务使”的意思。

244 before hanging [ˈhæŋɪŋ] it on her wrist在把它（手提包）挂在手腕上之前。这个状语短语修饰looks。

245 makes for　向……走去；是习语。

246 furious resolution　强烈的决心。furious原义“盛怒的”，也作“狂暴的”、“喧闹的”解。

247 puts out her tongue at herself　对（在镜子里的）自己伸出舌头；做鬼脸自嘲。

248 Freddy Eynsford Hill　在第一幕开场就和他的母亲及妹妹一起在教堂廊下避雨和叫车的Freddy。

249 lovelorn失恋的；因相思而憔悴的。

250 the second floor　英国用法，指我国所说的“三楼”，美国用法指“二楼”；按第二幕场景说明，Higgins的实验室在the first floor，而Eliza住的当是实验室楼上一层。

251 in which which当然代替the second floor，这里指的是这一层的房间，所以前面介词用in，表示“在房间里”。

252 giving the door a considerable bang behind her　（走出房门）把背后的房门狠狠地砰的一声（关上）。

253 Whatever are you doing here?　你在这里到底干什么？这里的whatever严格应作What ever，在口语中加强what的语气；通常whatever作“无论什么”解，例如：Read whatever you like.你喜欢读什么就读什么。

254 most of my nights　我大多数的夜晚。

255 Liza's good enough for me.　叫我Liza就好了。

256 breaks down　失去自制；是习语。

257 the loveliest, dearest –　破折号表示话未说完，一时说不出是最亲爱的什么。

258 smothers her with kisses　连连吻她，弄得她透不过气来。

259 hungry for comfort　如饥似渴地需要安慰。

260 responds作出反应；回应他的吻。

261 in one another's arms互相拥抱。one another和each other可以通用，虽然旧语法家认为each other用于两者之间，one another用于两者以上之间。

262 elderly　上了年纪的；中年以上的；是形容词。

263 police constable [ˈkʌnstəbl]警察；也单称constable，见下文。

264 scandalized　被使感到震惊（或反感），即“震惊（或反感）”；是过去分词。

265 Now then!比较缓和地向人抗议或叫人注意时用语，也作Now now!犹言“喂，喂！”

266 release one another　彼此松开。

267 only just become engaged　只是刚订了婚的；engaged是表语，也称主语补足语。

268 run away　逃跑；潜逃；是习语。

269 reflecting on his own courtship and on the vanity of human hopes　想到自己追求情人的情况和人生希望的空虚。

270 moves off in the opposite [ˈɒpəzɪt] direction with slow professional steps用缓慢的（警察的）职业（所习惯）的步子向（Eliza和Freddy逃走的）相反方向走去。

271 The flight of the lovers takes them to Cavendish [ˈkævəndɪʃ] Square.这对情人的逃跑把他们带到了Cavendish广场；他们一逃逃到了那个广场。

272 next move　下一步行动；也就是“再往哪里走”。

273 out of breath　喘不过气来；是习语。

274 He didnt half give me a fright, that copper.那个警察，他让我吃了个不小的惊讶（把我吓得不轻）。not half是习语，有两个相反的意思：一是“没有一半”、“一半也没有”、“极不”；一是“不止一半”、“极其”；这里是用作后一义；用作前一义的例如：That's not half bad.那是好透了。that copper是He的同位语；copper或cop作“警察”解是俚语。

275 answered him proper　回答得恰当；proper是副词。

276 taken you out of your way　把你带到了离开你要去的路；使你走到了不是你原来要走的路上。

277 What for?　= What are you going to the river for?你去河边干什么？

278 To make a hole in it.　= I'm going to the river to make a hole in it. “在河上打个窟窿”就是“跳入河中”。

279 It doesnt matter now.　现在（我跳不跳河）都不重要了。（因为她已打消这个念头。）

280 There's nobody in the world now but you and me现在除了你和我之外没有任何人。in the world是习语，无具体意思，只是强调nobody。but = except.

281 Not a soul　指“人”，一般用于not a soul, a dear old soul, 500（大数）souls等语中；原来用soul（灵魂）指人，在修辞学上称为synecdoche（举偶法），现在用得很普遍，可直接作“人”解。

282 What's this?　这是怎么回事？这算什么？

283 Move along here　从这里往前走；离开这里。along向前。

284 double quick速度加倍地，迅速地。double作副词，是口语，修饰quick; quick作副词，也是口语；常用在被修饰的动词后面，如Come quick，不能说Quick come。

285 As you say　依你所说的，指“(Move along here) double quick”。

286 Hanover [ˈhænəvə] Square伦敦广场名。

287 conference　商量，此词也作“（正式）会议”解，这里当然不是。

288 had no idea　没想到过。

289 devilishly prudish [ˈpruːdɪʃ]十分一本（假）正经的。devilishly在口语中也用devilish，是“非常”的意思。

290 It's their business　这是他们的职责。

291 hunt girls off the streets在街上寻找女孩子，发现有女孩子就把她们赶走。

292 wander about the streets all night　整夜在各条街上游荡。这里about是介词；下文wander about for ever中的about是副词。all night是习语，也作all the night, all night long。

293 oblivious [əˈblɪvɪəs]不在意的；不觉；此词也作“忘却的”解；后面介词用of。

294 crawling taxi爬行（一般慢慢开着）的出租汽车；在街上慢慢开着当是在兜揽乘客。

295 Can I drive you and the lady anywhere, sir?　先生，要我把你和这位小姐开到什么地方去吗？决不是“我有没有能力把你们开到任何地方去”的意思，Can I = May I; anywhere的用法也要体会，实际上等于somewhere。

296 They start asunder　他们（原来拥抱着，听见声音）连忙分开。

297 The very thing　= It's (A taxi is) the very thing.正是（我）需要的东西。

298 damn it　见鬼去吧，这是个常用的诅咒语。it并不是实指taxi的代词。

299 plenty　= plenty of money. plenty of后面可数名词和不可数名词都可以用。

300 you should never go out without ten pounds in your pocket你决不该袋里不装十英镑出门。这里的you和your都是指任何人，不是指说话的对方Freddy；“不带十镑不能出门”的说法反映Pickering上校的绅士派头。

301 Listen.　听我说（叫人注意用语）。

302 drive about开来开去。drive作不及物动词时可作“开车”解，也可如文中作“乘车”解；作及物动词时，可作“开（车）”解，也可作“开车送（人）”解，如：I'll drive you home.我开车送你回家。

303 in the morning　（第二天）早上。

304 old Mrs Higgins指Higgins教授的母亲，见第三幕。

305 the police wont touch us there　那里（在汽车里）警察不会来碰我们（干涉我们）。the police警察局；警务人员的总称；前面往往有the。

306 Righto!　= Righto oh!好！行！是英国俚语，表示赞同。

307 Ripping好极的；是英国俚语。

308 Wimbledon[ˈwɪmbldən] Common伦敦西南郊有名的公地。“...Common（公地）”前面不用the。

第五幕　剧情提要

Higgins和Pickering来到Higgins母亲家，告诉她Liza在半夜里走掉了。其实Liza此刻就在Higgins太太家，Higgins太太叫她躲在楼上。

Liza的父亲Doolittle也来了。这回他不再是衣衫褴褛的垃圾工人，而是一个非常体面的绅士。他穿着漂亮的礼服，钮孔里还插着一朵花，又是大礼帽，又是漆皮皮鞋，完全是新郎的打扮。连自己的女儿下楼来，一看见都失声惊叫起来。

他有钱了，却埋怨Higgins害他陷入了他一向嫌恶的中产阶级。原来美国有个大富翁，捐巨款要在全世界建立道德改进会，曾请Higgins发明一种世界语，以利宣传，而Higgins在给他的回信中提到，英国有个独特的道德家，他就是倒垃圾的Alfred Doolittle。那个富翁死了，遗赠给Doolittle一笔财产，每年可有四千镑收入。

现在Doolittle自己也不得不受中产阶级道德的束缚。他和他的“老伴”都无可奈何地只能正式结婚。这一天正是他结婚的日子。他邀请Higgins太太和大家一起去参加他的婚礼。

Liza呢，她不要那对她呼幺喝六的Higgins教授，也不要那对她彬彬有礼的绅士Pickering上校。她挑中了和她真诚相爱的穷小子Freddy。

ACT V

Mrs Higgins's drawing room. She is at her writing-table as before1. The parlormaid comes in.

THE PARLORMAID [at the door] Mr Henry2, maam3, is downstairs with Colonel Pickering.

MRS HIGGINS Well, shew them up4.

THE PARLORMAID Theyre using the telephone5, maam. Telephoning to the police, I think.

MRS HIGGINS What!

THE PARLORMAID [coming further in6 and lowering her voice] Mr Henry is in a state7, maam. I thought I'd better tell you8.

MRS HIGGINS If you had told me that Mr Henry was not in a state it would have been more surprising9. Tell them to come up when theyve finished with the police10. I suppose he's lost something.

THE PARLORMAID Yes, maam [going].

MRS HIGGINS Go upstairs and tell Miss Doolittle that Mr Henry and the Colonel are here. Ask her not to come down til I send for her11.

THE PARLORMAID Yes, maam.

Higgins bursts in12. He is, as the parlormaid has said, in a state.

HIGGINS Look here13, mother: heres a confounded14 thing!

MRS HIGGINS Yes, dear. Good morning. [He checks his impatience15 and kisses her, whilst16 the parlormaid goes out.] What is it?17

HIGGINS Eliza's bolted18.

MRS HIGGINS [calmly continuing her writing] You must have frightened her19.

HIGGINS Frightened her! nonsense! She was left last night, as usual, to turn out the lights and all that20; and instead of going to bed she changed her clothes and went right off21: her bed wasnt slept in22. She came in a cab for her things23 before seven this morning; and that fool Mrs Pearce24 let her have them without telling me a word about it25. What am I to do?26

MRS HIGGINS Do without, I'm afraid27, Henry. The girl has a perfect right to leave if she chooses28.

HIGGINS [wandering distractedly across the room29] But I cant find anything.30 I dont know what appointments Ive got31. I'm –32 [Pickering comes in. Mrs Higgins puts down her pen and turns away from the writing-table.]

PICKERING [shaking hands] Good morning, Mrs Higgins. Has Henry told you?33 [He sits down on the ottoman.]

HIGGINS What does that ass of an inspector34 say? Have you offered a reward35?

MRS HIGGINS [rising in indignant amazement36] You dont mean to say37 you have set the police after Eliza38.

HIGGINS Of course. What are the police for?39 What else could we do? [He sits in the Elizabethan chair.]

PICKERING The inspector made a lot of difficulties40. I really think he suspected us of some improper purpose41.

MRS HIGGINS Well, of course he did42. What right have you43 to go to the police and give the girl's name as if she were a thief, or a lost umbrella, or something44? Really!45 [She sits down again, deeply vexed46.]

HIGGINS But we want to find her.

PICKERING We cant let her go like this, you know, Mrs Higgins. What were we to do?47

MRS HIGGINS You have no more sense48, either of you49, than two children. Why –

The parlormaid comes in and breaks off the conversation50.

THE PARLORMAID Mr Henry: a gentleman wants to see you very particular51. He's been sent on from Wimpole Street.52

HIGGINS Oh, bother! I cant see anyone53 now. Who is it?54

THE PARLORMAID A Mr Doolittle55, sir.

PICKERING Doolittle! Do you mean the dustman?

THE PARLORMAID Dustman! Oh no, sir: a gentleman56.

HIGGINS [springing up excitedly] By George, Pick, it's some relative of hers that she's gone to57. Somebody we know nothing about.58[to the parlormaid] Send him up, quick.59

THE PARLORMAID Yes, sir. [She goes.]

HIGGINS [eagerly, going to his mother] Genteel relatives60! Now we shall hear something61. [He sits down in the Chippendale chair.]

MRS HIGGINS Do you know any of her people62?

PICKERING Only her father: the fellow we told you about.

THE PARLORMAID [announcing] Mr Doolittle. [She withdraws.]

Doolittle enters. He is resplendently dressed as for a fashionable wedding63 and might, in fact, be the bridegroom64. A flower in his buttonhole, a dazzling silk hat, and patent leather shoes65 complete the effect66. He is too concerned with the business he has come on to notice Mrs Higgins.67 He walks straight to Higgins68, and accosts him with vehement reproach69.

DOOLITTLE [indicating his own person70] See here! Do you see this? You done this.71

HIGGINS Done what, man?72

DOOLITTLE This, I tell you.73 Look at it.74 Look at this hat. Look at this coat.

PICKERING Has Eliza been buying you clothes?75

DOOLITTLE Eliza! Not she.76 Why would she buy me clothes?

MRS HIGGINS Good morning, Mr Doolittle. Wont you sit down?

DOOLITTLE [taken aback77 as he becomes conscious that he has forgotten his hostess78] Asking your pardon79, maam. [He approaches her and shakes her proffered hand80.] Thank you.81 [He sits down on the ottoman, on Pickering's right.] I am that full of what has happened to me that I cant think of anything else.82

HIGGINS What the dickens83 has happened to you?

DOOLITTLE I shouldnt mind if it had only happened to me.84 Anything might happened to anybody and nobody to blame but Providence, as you might say85. But this is something that you done to me: yes, you86, Enry Iggins.

HIGGINS Have you found Eliza?

DOOLITTLE Have you lost her?

HIGGINS Yes.

DOOLITTLE You have all the luck, you have.87 I aint found88 her; but she'll find me quick enough now89 after what you done to me.

MRS HIGGINS But what has my son done to you, Mr Doolittle?

DOOLITTLE Done to me!90 Ruined me.91 Destroyed my happiness. Tied me up92 and delivered me into the hands of middle class morality93.

HIGGINS [rising intolerantly94 and standing over Doolittle] Youre raving95. Youre drunk. Youre mad. I gave you five pounds.After that I had two conversations with you, at half-a-crown an hour.96 Ive never seen you since97.

DOOLITTLE Oh! Drunk am I?98 Mad am I? Tell me this.99 Did you or did you not100 write a letter to an old blighter101 in America that was giving five millions to found Moral Reform Societies all over the world102, and that wanted you to invent a universal language for him103?

HIGGINS What! Ezra D. Wannafeller104! He's dead. [He sits down again carelessly.]

DOOLITTLE Yes: he's dead; and I'm done for.105 Now did you or did you not write a letter to him to say that the most original moralist106 at present in England, to the best of your knowledge107, was Alfred Doolittle, a common dustman?

HIGGINS Oh, after your first visit I remember making some silly joke of the kind108.

DOOLITTLE Ah! You may well109 call it a silly joke. It put the lid on me right enough110. Just give him the chance he wanted to shew111 that Americans is not like us: that they reckonize and respect merit112 in every class of life, however humble113. Them words is114 in his blooming will115, in which, Henry Higgins, thanks to116 your silly joking, he leaves me a share in his Pre-digested Cheese Trust117 worth four thousand a year118 on condition that119 I lecture for his Wannafeller Moral Reform World Lecture120 as often as they ask me up to six times a year121.

HIGGINS The devil he does!122 Whew!123 [brightening124 suddenly] What a lark!125

PICKERING A safe thing126 for you, Doolittle. They wont ask you twice.127

DOOLITTLE It aint the lecturing I mind.128 I'll lecture them blue in the face129, I will, and not turn a hair130. It's making a gentleman of me that I object to131. Who asked him to make a gentleman of me? I was happy. I was free. I touched pretty nigh everybody for money132 when I wanted it, same as133 I touched you, Enry Iggins. Now I am worrited134; tied neck and heels135; and everybody touches me for money. It's a fine thing for you, says my solicitor. Is it? says I136. You mean it's a good thing for you137, I says. When I was a poor man and had a solicitor138 once when they found a pram in the dust cart139, he got me off, and got shut of me and got me shut of him140 as quick as he could. Same with the doctors141: used to shove me out of the hospital before I could hardly stand on my legs, and nothing to pay142. Now they finds out that I'm not a healthy man and cant live unless they looks after me twice a day. In the house143 I'm not let do a hand's turn144 for myself: somebody else must do it and touch me for it. A year ago I hadnt a relative in the world except two or three that wouldnt speak to me. Now Ive fifty, and not a decent week's wages among the lot of them145. I have to live for others146 and not for myself: thats middle class morality. You talk of losing Eliza. Dont you be anxious: I bet147 she's on my doorstep by this148: she149 that could support herself easy150 by selling flowers if I wasnt respectable. And the next one to touch me will be you, Enry Iggins. I'll have to learn to speak middle class language from you, instead of speaking proper English151. Thats where youll come in152; and I daresay153 thats what you done it for154.

MRS HIGGINS But, my dear Mr Doolittle, you need not suffer all this if you are really in earnest155. Nobody can force you to accept this bequest. You can repudiate it. Isnt that so, Colonel Pickering?

PICKERING I believe so.

DOOLITTLE [softening his manner in deference to her sex156] Thats the tragedy of it157, maam. It's easy to say chuck it158; but I havnt the nerve159. Which of us has160? We're all intimidated161. Intimidated, maam: thats what we are. What is there for me if I chuck it but the workhouse in my old age?162 I have to dye my hair already163 to keep my job as a dustman. If I was one of the deserving poor164, and had put by a bit165, I could chuck it; but then166 why should I, acause167 the deserving poor might as well be millionaires for all the happiness they ever has168. They dont know what happiness is.169 But I, as one of the undeserving poor170, have nothing between me and the pauper's uniform but171 this here blasted four thousand a year172 that shoves me into the middle class. (Excuse the expression173, maam; youd use it yourself if you had my provocation174.) Theyve got you every way you turn175: it's a choice between the Skilly of the workhouse and the Char Bydis176 of the middle class; and I havnt the nerve for the workhouse. Intimidated: thats what I am. Broke177. Bought up178. Happier men than me will call for my dust179, and touch me for their tip180; and I'll look on helpless181, and envy them. And thats what your son has brought me to. [He is overcome by emotion182.]

MRS HIGGINS Well, I'm very glad youre not going to anything foolish183, Mr Doolittle. For this184 solves the problem of Eliza's future. You can provide for her now.

DOOLITTLE [with melancholy resignation185] Yes, maam: I'm expected to provide for everyone186 now, out of four thousand a year187.

HIGGINS [jumping up] Nonsense! He cant provide for her188. He shant189 provide for her. She doesnt belong to him. I paid him five pounds for her. Doolittle: either youre an honest man or a rogue190.

DOOLITTLE [tolerantly] A little of both191, Henry, like the rest of us: a little of both.

HIGGINS Well, you took that money for the girl; and you have no right to take her as well192.

MRS HIGGINS Henry: dont be absurd. If you want to know where Eliza is, she is upstairs.

HIGGINS [amazed] Upstairs!!! Then I shall jolly193 soon fetch her downstairs. [He makes resolutely for the door.]

MRS HIGGINS [rising and following him] Be quiet, Henry. Sit down.

HIGGINS I –

MRS HIGGINS Sit down, dear; and listen to me.

HIGGINS Oh very well, very well, very well. [He throws himself ungraciously on the ottoman194, with his face towards the windows.] But Ithink you might have told us this half an hour ago195.

MRS HIGGINS Eliza came to me this morning. She told me of the brutal way you two treated her196.

HIGGINS [bounding up again] What!

PICKERING [rising also] My dear Mrs Higgins, she's been telling you stories197. We didnt treat her brutally. We hardly said a word to her198; and we parted on particularly good terms199. [turning on Higgins] Higgins: did you bully her after I went to bed?

HIGGINS Just the other way about200. She threw my slippers in my face. She behaved in the most outrageous way. I never gave her the slightest provocation201. The slippers came bang into may face202 the moment203 I entered the room – before I had uttered a word. And used perfectly awful language204.

PICKERING [astonished] But why? What did we do to her?

MRS HIGGINS I think I know pretty well what you did. The girl is naturally rather affectionate205, I think. Isnt she, Mr Doolittle?

DOOLITTLE Very tender-hearted, maam. Takes after me206.

MRS HIGGINS Just so207. She had become attached to you both208. She worked very hard for you, Henry. I dont think you quite realize what anything in the nature of brain work means to a girl of her class209. Well, it seems that when the great day of trial210 came, and she did this wonderful thing211 for you withoutmaking a single mistake, you two sat there and never said a word to her212, but talked together of how glad you were that it was all over and how you had been bored with the whole thing213. And then you were surprised because she threw your slippers at you! I should have thrown the fire-irons at you214.

HIGGINS We said nothing except that we were tired and wanted to go to bed. Did we, Pick?

PICKERING [shrugging his shoulders] That was all.

MRS HIGGINS [ironically] Quite sure?215

PICKERING Absolutely216. Really, that was all.

MRS HIGGINS You didnt thank her, or pet her, or admire her, or tell her how splendid217 she'd been.

HIGGINS [impatiently] But she knew all about that218. We didnt make speeches to her, if thats what you mean.219

PICKERING [conscience stricken220] Perhaps we were a little inconsiderate. Is she very angry?

MRS HIGGINS [returning to her place at the writing-table] Well, I'm afraid she wont go back to Wimpole Street, especially now that Mr Doolittle is able to keep up the position you have thrust on her221; but she says she is quite willing to meet you on friendly terms and to let bygones be bygones222.

HIGGINS [furious] Is she223, by George? Ho!224

MRS HIGGINS If you promise to behave yourself225, Henry, I'll ask her to come down. If not226, go home; for you have taken up quite enough of my time227.

HIGGINS Oh, all right. Very well. Pick: you behave yourself228. Letus put on our best Sunday manners229 for this creature that we picked out of the mud230. [He flings himself sulkily into the Elizabethan chair.]

DOOLITTLE [remonstrating] Now, now231, Enry Iggins! Have some consideration for my feelings as a middle class man232.

MRS HIGGINS Remember your promise, Henry. [She presses the bell-button on the writing-table.] Mr Doolittle: will you be so good as to step out233 on the balcony for a moment. I dont want Eliza to have the shock of your news234 until235 she has made it up with236 these two gentlemen. Would you mind?237

DOOLITTLE As you wish238, lady. Anything to help Henry to keep her off my hands.239 [He disappears through the window240.]

The parlormaid answers the bell241. Pickering sits down in Doolittle's place.

MRS HIGGINS Ask Miss Doolittle to come down, please.242

THE PARLORMAID Yes, maam. [She goes out.]

MRS HIGGINS Now, Henry: be good243.

HIGGINS I am behaving myself perfectly.

PICKERING He is doing his best, Mrs Higgins.

A pause. Higgins throws back his head244; stretches out his legs; and begins to whistle.

MRS HIGGINS Henry, dearest, you dont look at all nice245 in thatattitude.

HIGGINS [pulling himself together] I was not trying to look nice, mother.

MRS HIGGINS It doesnt matter246, dear. I only wanted to make you speak.

HIGGINS Why?

MRS HIGGINS Because you cant speak and whistle at the same time.

Higgins groans. Another very trying247 pause.

HIGGINS [springing up, out of patience248] Where the devil is that girl? Are we to wait here all day?

Eliza enters, sunny, self-possessed249, and giving a staggeringly convincing exhibition of ease of manner250. She carries a little work-basket251, and is very much at home252. Pickering is too much taken aback to rise253.

LIZA How do you do, Professor Higgins? Are you quite well?

HIGGINS [chocking] Am I –254 [He can say no more.]

LIZA But of course you are: you are never ill. So glad to see you again, Colonel Pickering. [He rises hastily; and they shake hands.] Quite chilly this morning, isnt it? [She sits down on his left. He sits beside her.]

HIGGINS Dont you dare try this game on me.255 I taught it to you;and it doesnt take me in256. Get up and come home257; and dont be a fool258.

Eliza takes a piece of needlework from her basket, and begins to stitch at it, without taking the least notice of this outburst259.

MRS HIGGINS Very nicely put260, indeed, Henry. No woman could resist such an invitation.261

HIGGINS You let her alone262, mother. Let her speak for herself263. You will jolly soon see264 whether she has an idea that I havnt put into her head or a word that I havnt put into her mouth265. I tell you I have created this thing out of the squashed cabbage leaves of Covent Garden266; and now she pretends to play the fine lady with me267.

MRS HIGGINS [placidly268] Yes, dear; but youll sit down wont you?

Higgins sits down again, savagely.

LIZA [to Pickering, talking no apparent notice of Higgins269, and working away270 deftly] Will you drop me altogether271 now that the experiment is over, Colonel Pickering?

PICKERING Oh dont. You mustnt think of it as an experiment272.It shocks me, somehow.273

LIZA Oh, I'm only a squashed cabbage leaf –

PICKERING [impulsively] No.

LIZA [continuing quietly] – but I owe so much to you274 that I should be very unhappy if you forgot me.

PICKERING It's very kind of you to say so, Miss Doolittle.

LIZA It's not because you paid for my dresses. I know you are generous to everybody with money275. But it was from you that I learnt really nice manners276; and that is what makes one a lady277, isnt it? You see it was so very difficult for me with the example of Professor Higgins always before me278. I was brought up to be just like him279, unable to control myself, and using bad language on the slightest provocation280. And I should never have known that ladies and gentlemen didnt behave like that if you hadnt been there281.

HIGGINS Well!!

PICKERING Oh, thats only his way282, you know. He doesnt mean it283.

LIZA Oh, I didnt mean it either, when I was a flower girl. It was only my way. But you see I did it; and thats what makes the difference after all.284

PICKERING No doubt. Still, he taught you to speak; and I couldnt have done that285, you know.

LIZA [trivially286] Of course: that is his profession.

HIGGINS Damnation!287

LIZA [continuing] It was just like learning to dance in the fashion-able way: there was nothing more than that in it288. But do you know what began may real education?

PICKERING What?

LIZA [stopping her work for a moment] Your calling me Miss Doolittle289 that day when I first came to Wimpole Street. That290 was the beginning of self-respect for me. [She resumes her stitching.] And there were a hundred little things you never noticed291, because they came naturally to you292. Things293 about standing up and taking off your hat and opening doors –294

PICKERING Oh, that was nothing295.

LIZA Yes: things that shewed you thought and felt about me as if I were something better than a scullery-maid296; though of course I know you would have been just the same to a scullery-maid if she had been let into the drawing room297. You never took off your boots in the dining room when I was there298.

PICKERING You mustnt mind that299. Higgins takes off his boots all over the place300.

LIZA I know. I am not blaming him. It is his way, isnt it? But it made such a difference to me that you didnt do it. You see, really and truly301, apart from the things anyone can pick up302 (the dressing303 and the proper way of speaking, and so on), thedifference between a lady and a flower girl is not how she behaves, but how she's treated304. I shall always be a flower girl to Professor Higgins, because he always treats me as a flower girl, and always will305; but I know I can be a lady to you, because you always treat me as a lady, and always will.

MRS HIGGINS Please dont grind your teeth306, Henry.

PICKERING Well, this is really very nice of you, Miss Doolittle.

LIZA I should like you to call me Eliza, now307, if you would.

PICKERING Thank you.308 Eliza, of course.

LIZA And I should like Professor Higgins to call me Miss Doolittle.309

HIGGINS I'll see you damned first.310

MRS HIGGINS Henry! Henry!311

PICKERING [laughing] Why dont you slang back at him312? Dont stand it.313 It would do him a lot of good314.

LIZA I cant. I could have done it once; but now I cant315 go back to it316. You told me, you know, that when a child is brought to a foreign country, it picks up the language in a few weeks317, and forgets its own. Well, I am a child in your country318. I have forgotten my own language, and can speak nothing but yours319. Thats the real break-off320 with the corner of Tottenham Court Road321. Leaving Wimpole Street finishes it.322

PICKERING [much alarmed323] Oh! But youre coming back to Wimpole Street, arent you? Youll forgive Higgins?

HIGGINS [rising] Forgive!324 Will she, by George! Let her go. Let her find out how she can get on without us325. She will relapse into the gutter in three weeks without me at her elbow326.

Doolittle appears at the centre window. With a look of dignified reproach at Higgins327, he comes slowly and silently to his daughter, who, with her back to the window, is unconscious of his approach.

PICKERING He's incorrigible328, Eliza. You wont relapse, will you?

LIZA No: not now329. Never again. I have learnt my lesson330. I dont believe I could utter one of the old sounds if I tried331. [Doolittle touches her on her left shoulder. She drops her work332, losing her self-possession utterly at the spectacle of her father's splendor333]. A-a-a-a-a-ah-ow-ooh!

HIGGINS [with a crow of triumph334] Aha! Just so335. A-a-a-a-ahowooh! A-a-a-a-ahowooh! A-a-a-a-ahowooh! Victory! Victory! [He throws himself on the divan, folding his arms, and spraddling arrogantly336.]

DOOLITTLE Can you blame the girl? Dont look at me like that337, Eliza. It aint my fault.338 Ive come into some money339.

LIZA You must have touched a millionaire this time340, dad341.

DOOLITTLE I have. But I'm dressed something special today342. I'm going to St George's, Hanover Square343. Your stepmother is going to marry me.344

LIZA [angrily] Youre going to let yourself down to marry that low common woman345!

PICKERING [quietly] He ought to346, Eliza. [to Doolittle] Why has she changed her mind?347

DOOLITTLE [sadly] Intimidated, Governor348. Intimidated. Middle class morality claims its victim349. Wont you put on your hat, Liza, and come and see me turned off350?

LIZA If the Colonel says I must, I – I'll [almost sobbing] I'll demean myself351. And get insulted for my pains352, like enough353.

DOOLITTLE Dont be afraid: she never comes to words with354 anyone now, poor woman! Respectability has broke all the spirit out of her355.

PICKERING [squeezing Eliza's elbow gently] Be kind to them, Eliza. Make the best of it356.

LIZA [forcing a little smile for him through her vexation357] Oh well, just to shew theres no ill feeling358. I'll be back in a moment. [She goes out.]

DOOLITTLE [sitting down beside Pickering] I feel uncommon359 nerv-ous about the ceremony, Colonel. I wish youd come and see me through it360.

PICKERING But youve been through it before, man361. You were married to Eliza's mother.362

DOOLITTLE Who told you that, Colonel?

PICKERING Well, nobody told me. But I concluded – naturally –363

DOOLITTLE No: that aint the natural way364, Colonel: it's only the middle class way365. My way was always the undeserving way366. But dont say nothing to Eliza367. She dont know368: I always had a delicacy about telling her369.

PICKERING Quite right. We'll leave it so370, if you dont mind371.

DOOLITTLE And youll come to the church, Colonel, and put me through straight372?

PICKERING With pleasure.373 As far as a bachelor can.374

MRS HIGGINS May I come, Mr Doolittle? I should be very sorry to miss your wedding.375

DOOLITTLE I should indeed be honored by your condescension376, maam; and my poor old woman would take it as a tremenjous compliment377. She's been very low378, thinking of the happy days that are no more379.

MRS HIGGINS [rising] I'll order the carriage380 and get ready. [The men rise, except Higgins.] I shant be more than fifteen minutes.381 [As she goes to the door Eliza comes in, hatted and buttoning her gloves382.]I'm going to the church to see your father married, Eliza. You had better come in the brougham383 with me. Colonel Pickering can go on with the bridegroom384.

Mrs Higgins goes out. Eliza comes to the middle of the room between the centre window and the ottoman. pickering joins her385.

DOOLITTLE Bridegroom! What a word! It makes a man realize his position, somehow.386 [He takes up his hat and goes towards the door.]

PICKERING Before I go, Eliza, do forgive387 Higgins and come back to us.

LIZA I dont think dad would allow me388. Would you, dad?389

DOOLITTLE [sad but magnanimous390] They played you off very cunning391, Eliza, them two sportsmen392. If it had been only one of them, you could have nailed him.393 But you see, there was two394; and one of them chaperoned the other, as you might say395. [to Pickering] It was artful of you396, Colonel; but I bear no malice397: I should have done the same myself. I been398 the victim of one woman after another399 all my life, and I dont grudge you two getting the better of Liza400. I shant interfere. It's time for us to go, Colonel. So long401, Henry. See you in St George's402, Eliza. [He goes out.]

PICKERING [coaxing] Do stay with us, Eliza. [He follows Doolittle.]

Eliza goes out on the balcony403 to avoid being alone with Higgins. He rises and joins her there. She immediately comes back into the room and makes for the door; but he goes along the balcony quickly and gets his back to the door before she reaches it.

HIGGINS Well, Eliza, youve had a bit of your own back404, as you call it405. Have you had enough406? And are you going to be reasonable407? Or do you want any more?

LIZA You want me back408 only to pick up your slippers and put up with your tempers409 and fetch and carry410 for you.

HIGGINS I havnt said I wanted you back at all.

LIZA Oh, indeed. Then what are we talking about?

HIGGINS About you411, not about me. If you come back I shall treat you just as I have always treated you. I cant change my nature412; and I dont intend to change my manners. My manners are exactly the same as Colonel Pickering's.

LIZA Thats not true.413 He treats a flower girl as if she was a duchess.414

HIGGINS And I treat a duchess as if she was a flower girl.

LIZA I see. [She turns away composedly415, and sits on the ottoman, facing the window.] The same to everybody.416

HIGGINS Just so.

LIZA Like father.417

HIGGINS [grinning, a little taken down418] Without accepting the comparison at all points419, Eliza, it's quite true that your father is not a snob, and that he will be quite at home in any station of life to which his eccentric destiny may call him420. [seriously421] The great secret, Eliza, is not having bad manners or good manners422 or any other particular sort of manners, but having the same manner for all human souls423: in short424, behaving as if you were in Heaven425, where there are no third-class carriages426, and one soul is as good as another427.

LIZA Amen.428 You are a born preacher429.

HIGGINS [irritated] The question is not whether I treat you rudely, but whether you ever heard me treat anyone else better430.

LIZA [with sudden sincerity] I dont care how you treat me.431 I dont mind your swearing at me. I shouldnt mind a black eye432: Ive had one before this. But [standing up and facing him] I wont be passed over.433

HIGGINS Then get out of my way434; for I wont stop for you435. You talkabout me as if I were a motor bus.

LIZA So436 you are a motor bus: all bounce and go, and no consideration for anyone437. But I can do without438 you: dont think I cant439.

HIGGINS I know you can. I told you you could.440

LIZA [wounded, getting away from him to the other side of the ottoman with her face to the hearth] I know you did441, you brute. You wanted to get rid of442 me.

HIGGINS Liar443.

LIZA Thank you.444 [She sits down with dignity.]

HIGGINS You never asked yourself, I suppose445, whether I could do without you.

LIZA [earnestly] Dont you try to get round446 me. Youll have to do without me.

HIGGINS [arrogant] I can do without anybody. I have my own soul: my own spark of divine fire447. But [with sudden humility] I shall miss you448, Eliza. [He sits down near her on the ottoman.] I have learnt something from your idiotic notions449: I confess that humbly and gratefully. And I have grown accustomed to450 your voice and appearance. I like them, rather451.

LIZA Well, you have both of them452 on your gramophone and in your book of photographs. When you feel lonely without me, you can turn the machine on453. It's got no feelings to hurt.454

HIGGINS I cant turn your soul on455. Leave me those feelings; andyou can take away the voice and the face456. They are not you.457

LIZA Oh, you are a devil458. You can twist the heart in a girl459 as easy as460 some could twist her arms to hurt her. Mrs Pearce warned461 me. Time and again462 she has wanted to leave you; and you always got round her at the last minute463. And you dont care a bit for her. And you dont care a bit for me.

HIGGINS I care for life, for humanity464; and you are a part of it that has come my way and been built into my house465. What more can you or anyone ask?466

LIZA I wont care for anybody that doesnt care for me.

HIGGINS Commercial principles467, Eliza. Like [reproducing her Covent Garden pronunciation with professional exactness468] s'yollin voylets [selling violets], isnt it?

LIZA Dont sneer at me. It's L-End} mean469 to sneer at me.

HIGGINS I have never sneered in my life.470 Sneering doesnt become either the human face or the human soul.471 I am expressing my righteous contempt for Commercialism472. I dont and wont trade in affection473. You call me a brute because you couldnt buy a claim on me474 by fetching my slippers and finding my spectacles. You were a fool: I think a woman fetching a man's slippers is a disgusting sight475: did I ever fetch your slippers? I think a good deal more of you for throwing them in my face.476 No use slaving for me and then saying you want to be cared for477: who cares for a slave? If you come back, come back for the sake of good fellowship478; for youll get nothing else479. Youve had a thousand times as much out of me as I have out of you480; and if you dare to set up your little dog's tricks of fetching and carrying slippers against my creation of a Duchess Eliza481, I'll slam the door in your silly face482.

LIZA What did you do it483 for if you didnt care for me?

HIGGINS [heartily] Why, because it was my job.

LIZA You never thought of the trouble it would make for me484.

HIGGINS Would the world ever have been made if its maker had been afraid of making trouble485. Making life means making trouble.486 Theres only one way of escaping trouble; and thats killing things487. Cowards, you notice488, are always shrieking to have troublesome people killed489.

LIZA I'm no preacher: I dont notice things like that. I notice that you dont notice me.490

HIGGINS [jumping up and walking about intolerantly] Eliza: youre an idiot. I waste the treasures of my Miltonic mind by spreading them before you.491 Once for all492, understand that I go my way493 and do my work without caring twopence what happens to either of us494. I am not intimidated, like your father and yourstepmother. So you can come back or go to the devil495: which you please496.

LIZA What am I to come back for?

HIGGINS [bouncing up on his knees on the ottoman and leaning over it to her497] For the fun of it498. Thats why I took you on499.

LIZA [with averted face500] And you may throw me out501 tomorrow if I dont do everything you want me to502?

HIGGINS Yes; and you may walk out503 tomorrow if I dont do everything you want me to.

LIZA And live with my stepmother?

HIGGINS Yes, or sell flowers.

LIZA Oh! If I only could go back to my flower basket!504 I should be independent of both you and father and all the world!505 Why did you take my independence from me506? Why did I give it up? I'm a slave now, for all my fine clothes507.

HIGGINS Not a bit.508 I'll adopt you as my daughter509 and settle money on you if you like. Or would you rather510 marry Pickering?

LIZA [looking fiercely round at him511] I wouldnt marry you if you asked me512; and youre nearer my age than what he is513.

HIGGINS [gently] Than he is: not 'than what he is.'

LIZA [losing her temper514 and rising] I'll talk as I like. Youre not my teacher now.

HIGGINS [reflectively] I dont suppose Pickering would, though.515 He's as confirmed an old bachelor as I am.

LIZA Thats not what I want516; and dont you think it517. Ive always had chaps enough wanting me that way518. Freddy Hill519 writes to me twice and three times520 a day, sheets and sheets521.

HIGGINS [disagreeably surprised522] Damn his impudence523! [He recoils and finds himself sitting on his heels524.]

LIZA He has a right to if he likes525, poor lad526. And he does love me.

HIGGINS [getting off the ottoman] You have no right to encourage him.527

LIZA Every girl has a right to be loved.

HIGGINS What! By fools like that?528

LIZA Freddy's not a fool. And if he's weak and poor and wants me529, may be530 he'd make me happier than my betters that bully me and dont want me531.

HIGGINS Can he make anything of you?532 Thats the point.533

LIZA Perhaps I could make something of him534. But I never thought of us making anything of one another; and you never think of anything else535. I only want to be natural536.

HIGGINS In short, you want me to be as infatuated537 about you as Freddy? Is that it?

LIZA No I dont. Thats not the sort of feeling I want from you.538 And dont you be too sure of yourself or of me539. I could have been a bad girl if I'd liked.540 Ive seen more of some things than you541, for all your learning542. Girls like me can drag gentlemen down to make love to them easy enough.543 And they wish each other dead the next minute.544

HIGGINS Of course they do545. Then what in thunder are we quarrelling about?546

LIZA [much troubled] I want a little kindness. I know I'm a common ignorant girl, and you a book-learned547 gentleman; but I'm not dirt under your feet. What I done548 [correcting herself] what I did was not for the dresses549 and the taxis: I did it because we were pleasant together550 and I come – came – to care for you551; not to want you to make love to me552, and not forgetting the difference between us553, but more friendly like554.

HIGGINS Well, of course. Thats just how I feel.555 And how Pickering feels. Eliza: youre a fool.

LIZA Thats not a proper answer to give me556. [She sinks on the chair at the writing-table in tears557.]

HIGGINS It's all youll get558 until you stop being a common idiot. If youre going to be a lady, youll have to give up feeling neglected559 if the men you know dont spend half their time snivelling over you and the other half giving you black eyes560. If you cant stand the coldness of my sort of life561, and the strain of it562, go back to the gutter563. Work til youre more a brute than a human being564; and then cuddle and squabble and drink til you fall asleep565. Oh, it's a fine life, the life of the gutter566. It's real: it's warm: it's violent567: you can feel it through the thickest skin568: you can taste it and smell it without any training or any work569. Not like570 Science and Literature and Classical Music and Philosophy and Art. You find me cold, unfeeling571, selfish, dont you? Very well: be off with you572 to the sort of people you like. Marry some sentimental hog or other573 with lots of money, and a thick pair of lips574 to kiss you with and a thick pair of boots to kick you with575. If you cant appreciate what youve got576, youd better get what you can appreciate577.

LIZA [desperate] Oh, you are a cruel tyrant. I cant talk to you: you turn everything against me578: I'm always in the wrong.579 But you know very well all the time that youre nothing but a bully580. You know I cant go back to the gutter, as you call it, and that I have no real friends in the world but you and the Colonel581. You know well I couldnt bear to live with a low common man after you two582; and it's wicked and cruel of you to insult me by pretending I could583. You think I must go back to Wimpole Street because I have nowhere else to go but father's584. But dont you be too sure that you have me under your feet to be trampled on and talked down585. I'll marry Freddy, I will, as soon as I'm able to support him.

HIGGINS [thunderstruck586] Freddy!!! That young fool! That poor devil who couldnt get a job as an errand boy even if he had the guts to try for it!587 Woman: do you not understand that I have made you a consort for a King588?

LIZA Freddy loves me: that makes him king enough for me589. I dont want him to work: he wasnt brought up to it as I was590. I'll go and be a teacher591.

HIGGINS Whatll you teach, in heaven's name592?

LIZA What you taught me.593 I'll teach phonetics.

HIGGINS Ha! Ha! Ha!

LIZA I'll offer myself594 as an assistant to that hairy faced Hungarian595.

HIGGINS [rising in a fury] What! That impostor! That humbug! That toadying ignoramus596! Teach him my methods! my discoveries!597 You take one step in his direction and I'll wring your neck.598 [He lays hands on599 her.] Do you hear?

LIZA [defiantly non-resistant600] Wring away601. What do I care?602 I knew youd strike me some day603. [He lets her go604, stamping with rage at having forgotten himself605, and recoils so hastily that he stumbles back into his seat on the ottoman.] Aha! Now I know how to deal with you. What a fool I was not to think of it606 before! You cant take away the knowledge you gave me. You said I had a finer ear than you607. And I can608 be civil and kind to people, which is more than you can609. Aha! [purposely dropping her aitches610 to annoy him] Thats done you611, Enry Iggins, it az612. Now I dont care that [snapping her fingers] for your bullying and your big talk613. I'll advertize it in the papers that614 your duchess is only a flower girl that you taught, and that she'll teach anybody to be a duchess just the same615 in six months for a thousand guineas. Oh, when I think of myself crawling under your feet andbeing trampled on and called names616, when all the time I had only to lift up my finger to be as good as617 you, I could just kick myself618.

HIGGINS [wondering at her] You damned impudent slut619, you! But it's better than snivelling620; better than fetching slippers and finding spectacles, isnt it? [rising] By George, Eliza, I said I'd make a woman of you621; and I have622. I like you like this623.

LIZA Yes: you turn round624 and make up to me625 now that626 I'm not afraid of you, and can do without you.

HIGGINS Of course I do627, you little fool. Five minutes ago you were like a millstone round my neck628. Now youre a tower of strength629: a consort battleship. You and I and Pickering will be three old bachelors630 instead of only two men and a silly girl.

Mrs Higgins returns, dressed for the wedding631. Eliza instantly becomes cool and elegant.

MRS HIGGINS The carriage is waiting, Eliza. Are you ready?

LIZA Quite. Is the Professor coming?632

MRS HIGGINS Certainly not. He cant behave himself in church633. He makes remarks out loud all the time on the clergyman's pronunciation634.

LIZA Then I shall not see you again, Professor. Goodbye. [She goes to the door.]

MRS HIGGINS [coming to Higgins] Goodbye, dear.

HIGGINS Goodbye, mother. [He is about to635 kiss her, when636 he recollects something.] Oh, by the way637, Eliza, order a ham and a Stilton cheese638, will you? And buy me a pair of reindeer gloves, number eights639, and a tie to match that new suit of mine640. You can choose the color.641 [His cheerful, careless642, vigorous voice shews that he is incorrigible.]

LIZA [disdainfully] Number eights643 are too small for you if you want them lined with lamb's wool644. You have three new ties that you have forgotten in the drawer of your washstand645. Colonel Pickering prefers double Gloucester646 to Stilton; and you dont notice the difference647. I telephoned Mrs Pearce this morning not to forget the ham. What you are to do without me I cannot imagine648. [She sweeps out649.]

MRS HIGGINS I'm afraid youve spoilt that girl650, Henry. I should be uneasy about you and her if she were less fond of Colonel Pickering.651

HIGGINS Pickering!652 Nonsense: she's going to marry Freddy. Ha ha! Freddy! Freddy!! Ha ha ha ha ha!!!!! [He roars with laughter653 as the play ends.]

1 as before　和先前一样；和在第三幕时一样。

2 Mr Henry　Henry少爷，即Henry Higgins。Mr和Miss一般用在姓或者名和姓的前面；但仆人称呼男女小主人时，在Mr和Miss后用名而不是用姓，如男的小主人年纪很轻，则用Master，同样用名不用姓。

3 maam = ma'am [məm]. madam的缩略；太太；小姐；是仆人对女主人的称呼；用作对女王、公主等的称呼时读[mæm, mɑːm]。

4 shew them up　领他们上楼来。

5 using the telephone　在（借）打电话。

6 coming further in　再走近些。further和farther都是far的比较级形式，farther常指本义，指更远；further转义指“近一步”；这里further修饰in。

7 in a state　处于激动的状态。state在口语中可作“激动”、“不安”解；也可作“不良状态”解，如：His business is in a state.他生意很不好。in a state和习语in state不要混淆，后者是“正式地”、“隆重地”、“堂皇地”的意思。

8 I thought I'd better tell you.我想还是告诉你。在告诉之前先有这想法，所以“想”用过去时thought。'd better = had better.最好……，还是……；是习语。如果这里主句中用I think，从句中还是had better; had better在口语中也常用better，如：You better go.你还是去好。

9 If you had told me that Mr Henry was not in a state it would have been more surprising.　要是你告诉我Henry少爷并不激动，这倒是使人惊异的。这是与过去事实相反的虚拟语气；实际上是“你告诉我Henry少爷很激动，所以这事情一点不使人惊异。”

10 finished with the police　跟警察局通话完毕（打完交道）。

11 send for her　派人去叫她。send for是习语；派人去叫（人）；派人去拿（东西）；派人叫商店送（货）来；反正都是“派人”或者“托人”，而不是亲自去。

12 bursts in　直冲进来；闯进来。

13 Look here　叫人注意的口头语，并不真叫人“看这里”。

14 confounded该死的；诅咒语。confound原义“搅乱”、“挫败”的意思，用作诅咒语，作“使……毁灭”解，如confound you（见你的鬼），confound it（该死）。

15 checks his impatience　抑止他的烦躁。

16 whilst　同时；是古旧英语，现在用while。

17 What is it?这是怎么回事？

18 Eliza's bolted　[ˈbəʊltɪd] Eliza逃跑了。bolted在这里是不及物动词的过去分词作形容词，犹如He is gone中的gone。

19 must have frightened her　准是吓着她了。must，这里是“谅必”的意思。

20 to turn out the lights and all that　关灯以及诸如此类（的工作）。

21 went right off　径自走掉了；溜掉了。right有“就这么”的意思，是副词，修饰off。

22 her bed wasnt slept in她的床没被睡过；没睡在床上。sleep原是不及物动词，后面用了介词，可以同及物动词一样构成被动式。

23 for her things　来拿她的东西。

24 that fool Mrs Pearce　Pearce太太那个笨蛋。Mrs Pearce是that fool的同位语。

25 let her have them without telling me a word about it.让她（Eliza）把它们（东西）拿走，而不把这（Eliza来拿东西的）事情告诉我一声。

26 What am I to do?　我该怎么办？动词be后接不定式，表示“应该”、“安排”、“可能”等意思。

27 Do without, I'm afraid.　恐怕只好没有了。Do without没有也行；是习语；这里without是副词。这句话= I'm afraid that you have to do without (Eliza).倘用了Eliza, without便是介词。

28 has a perfect right to leave if she chooses如果她要（离去），她完全有权利离去。if she chooses = if she likes（或pleases）。

29 wandering distractedly across the room　心烦意躁地在房间里走来走去。across从……的这一边到那一边。

30 But I cant find anything.可是（没有Eliza）我什么东西都找不到（本来Higgins要什么东西都由Eliza拿到他手里）。

31 I dont know what appointments Ive got　（没有Eliza随时提醒）我不知道有什么约会。

32 I'm –话没说完，被Pickering上校的进来打断了。

33 Has Henry told you?　Henry（把Eliza走掉的事）告诉你了吗？

34 that ass of an inspector　那个蠢驴般的警长。a ... of a ...是“……般的……”的意思，见前注。

35 offered a reward答应给奖金；悬奖。

36 rising in indignant amazement　在愤慨的惊讶中站起身来；愤慨而惊讶地站起。

37 You dont mean to say你的意思总不会是说……；难道你是说……

38 set the police after Eliza　叫警察局捉拿她。

39 What are the police for?　要警察做什么？（就为了要他们抓人。）

40 made a lot of difficulties　制造许多麻烦（或刁难）。

41 suspected us of some improper purpose　怀疑我们有什么不正当的企图。suspect后面的介词用of。

42 he did　= he suspected you (of some improper purpose).

43 What right have you你们有什么权利（或理由）。

44 give the girl's name as if she were a thief, or a lost umbrella, or something　把她的名字报（警察局），仿佛她是个贼，或者是把被丢失的伞，或者是什么（别的东西）。or something，表示讲不确切，如：He is a writer or something.他大概是作家。She turned dizzy or something.她大概头晕了。

45 Really!　竟然（你们这样轻举妄动）岂有此理！

46 deeply vexed　= being deeply vexed.

47 What were we to do?　我们本来该怎么办？

48 sense　常识（common sense）。

49 either of you你们两个中的任何一个。与主语You同位。

50 breaks off the conversation　打断谈话。比较：The conversation breaks off. break off是习语，作“打断”、“停止”解，其中的break作及物和不及物动词均可。

51 very particular　专诚地；非（见到）不可；是very particularly之讹。没受过教育的英美人常把形容词作副词用。

52 He's been sent on from Wimpole Street.　他是被Wimpole Street（Higgins的住所）转来的。他（Mr Doolittle）先到Wimpole Street去找Higgins，那里的人（应是Mrs Pearce）叫他到这里来找。

53 cant see anyone　不能接见任何人；任何人来都不接见。

54 Who is it?（来访者）是谁？注意：这里习惯用it而不用he。

55 A Mr Doolittle　一位Doolittle先生；一个名叫Doolittle的先生。

56 a gentleman　垃圾工人已成为绅士。见下文。

57 it's some relative of hers that she's gone to　（来者）是她去找的一位亲属。注意：这里的it和that并不呼应而构成强调语气的句型，并不强调she's gone to some relative of hers。

58 Somebody we know nothing about.　= It's somebody (whom/that) we know nothing about.是一个我们一无所知的什么人。

59 Send him up, quick.　叫他上（楼）来，快。quick可以理解为= be quick，也可理解为= quickly。

60 Genteel [dʒenˈtiːl] relatives上流亲属。genteel上流社会的；有修养的；有时带讽刺，指“假斯文的”；因为Parlormaid说来访的是个绅士，所以Higgins这样说。

61 hear something　= hear something about Eliza.听到一些关于Eliza的消息。

62 her people　她的家属。

63 He is resplendently dressed as for a fashionable wedding　他穿得漂亮得好像要去参加上流社会的婚礼；as后面省略if he is dressed。

64 might, in fact, be the bridegroom　竟然好像就是（这个婚礼中的）新郎。

65 patent [ˈpeɪtnt] leather shoes漆皮皮鞋。

66 complete the effect使（新郎打扮的）效果完备；complete的主语是flower、silk hat和patent leather shoes，有此三者，完全像个出席时髦婚礼的人或者新郎了。

67 He is too concerned with the business he has come on to notice Mrs Higgins.　他太专心于他来此所为的事情，所以没注意到Mrs Higgins。concerned后面介词用with。the business he has come on = the business on which he has come. on business因事；是习语。

68 walks straight to Higgins　径直向Higgins走去；straight，是副词，习惯上不用straightly。

69 accosts [əˈkɒst] him with vehement [ˈviːəmənt] reproach走到他面前就破口大骂。accosts走上前向（人）说话；招呼。

70 indicating his own person　指着他自己身上（这副样子）。

71 You done this.　该说You did（或have done）this。是你造成（我现在）这个（样子）的。

72 Done what, man?　（我）干了什么呢，朋友？Done what是承接前言而问的，= what did I do?或What have I done, man?是对男子极不客气的称呼，常有亲切或戏谑的意味，犹如我们称“朋友”、“老兄”。

73 This, I tell you.　= I tell you that it is this that you have done:我告诉你，这（我现在这样子）就是你造成的。

74 Look at it.瞧这个（我现在的样子）就是你造成的。

75 Has Eliza been buying you clothes?　Eliza一直在买衣服给你吗？

76 Eliza! Not she.　Eliza吗，她才不（买衣服给我）呢！not she是she has not been buying me clothes的强调语气的说法。

77 taken aback　吃了一惊；是习语。

78 becomes conscious that he has forgotten his hostess开始觉察他忘记了（招呼）女主人。

79 Asking your pardon　一般说(I) Beg your pardon.请你原谅。

80 her proffered hand　她已经伸出的手。

81 Thank you.因Mrs Higgins伸手向他握手而致谢。

82 I am that full of what has happened to me that I cant think of anything else.我是满脑子尽想着发生在我头上的事情，弄得任何其他的事情都顾不到了。that full口语，= so full；和后面的that呼应full，（思想中）充满着……，后面介词用of。

83 What the dickens　究竟什么。the dickens是诅咒语the devil的委婉语，用以加强疑问词的语气，没有具体意思。

84 I shouldnt mind if it had only happened to me.　要是这事情只是（自然地）发生在我身上，那我也不介意。这是与事实相反的虚拟语气，实际上这事情不是自然发生，而是Higgins造成的，所以他恼恨。

85 nobody to blame but Providence [ˈprɒvɪdəns], as you might say犹如你们会说，除了老天爷，谁也怪不了。nobody to blame = nobody is (/ might be) to blame. to blame主动态形式的不定式表示被动的意思，习惯不用to be blamed。Providence首字母大写，指“天公”、“上帝”。as you might say （如果你们谈起来）你们会这样说。

86 yes, you　重复强调肯定句中前面的you。

87 You have all the luck, you have.　你真有完全的（极度的）运气；你真是运气好极了（否则你要供养她）；句末重复用you have，强调句首的You have。

88 aint found是俚语用法，= have not found; aint（正常写作ain't），除表示have/has not外，也表示are/am/is not。

89 she'll find me quick enough now　现在她会够快地找到我。

90 Done to me!　是“你问我他对我做了什么吗？”的意思。

91 Ruined me. = Your son has ruined me.（你儿子）毁了我；下面destroyed等都是过去分词，省略Your son has。

92 Tied me up　把我束缚了起来。

93 delivered me into the hands of middle class morality [məˈræləti]把我交到了中产阶级道德的手中。萧伯纳在许多剧本和论文中严厉抨击中产阶级道德的虚伪、庸俗。

94 intolerantly　不容（异说）地；偏执地。区别：intolerant，不容异说的；尤指在宗教、政治方面；intolerable，使人无可忍受的。如：The intolerant religious fanatics are intolerable.那些偏执的宗教狂热分子令人无可容忍。

95 raving　狂乱地说话；激昂地说话。

96 After that I had two conversations with you, at half-a-crown an hour.　在那回（给过你五镑）之后，我跟你谈过两次话，每小时（支付给你）半个克朗；Higgins为研究Doolittle的语言特意付了钱和他谈话。crown英国旧币制的五先令硬币。

97 since　（两次谈话）以后。

98 Drunk am I?　= Am I drunk?倒装句强调句首的Drunk。旧本作Drunk! Am I?下句Mad am I?同。

99 Tell me this.回答我这个（问题）；你说我醉了、疯了吗？

100 Did you or did you not这是雄辩家的强调口吻；Doolittle说话常是这种口气，所以在第二幕中Higgins说他大可当个内阁阁员或威尔士传教士。

101 old blighter　老家伙；blighter，英俚，是对人的鄙称。

102 that was giving five millions to found Moral Reform Societies all over the world他要捐出五百万，在全世界各地建立道德改进会。was giving这里用过去进行时表示过去未来时，= would give。

103 that wanted you to invent a universal language for him　他要你给他创造一种全世界通用的语言（当为便于宣传道德改进）。

104 Ezra [ˈezrə] D. Wannafeller [ˈwɒnəfelə]虚伪的美国富翁名；这个姓氏当是取美国Wannamaker和Rockfeller两个豪门的姓氏的首尾拼合而成。

105 I'm done for.　我完蛋了。be done for完了；不中用了；是习语。

106 original moralist　独创一格的德育家。

107 to the best of your knowledge　= to your knowledge = as far as you know.据你自己所能知道的，三者都是习语。

108 remember making some silly joke of the kind　记得开过一个这一类的无聊的玩笑。

109 may well　满可以；尽可。well有充分理由地。

110 put the lid on me right enough够彻底地毁了我。put the lid on制止；破坏；使到顶；是口语中的习语。right副词；有“正好”、“立刻”、“完全”等意思。

111 Just give him the chance he wanted to shew　（你）正好给了他所需要的机会以显示…… shew后面接两个并列的that从句。Just give，应作You just gave。

112 merit　[ˈmerɪt]长处；才能。

113 every class of life, however humble生活中的每一个等级（的人），不管是怎么低微的。however humble = however humble it is.

114 Them words is应该是These words are。

115 his blooming will　他的该死的遗嘱。blooming原义“开着花的”；这里是诅咒语bloody（该死的）的委婉语；有时只是加强语气，没有具体意义。

116 thanks to幸亏；由于（由于某种原因，其结果可以是好的，也可以是坏的）。这是个习语。

117 he leaves me a share in his Pre-digested Cheese Trust　把他在消化干酪托拉斯的股份遗赠给我。leaves遗赠。因遗嘱的文字在Doolittle说话时还是存在的，所以用现在时。a share可有两种解释，一是股份公司的“一股”，如：He holds 500 shares in the trust.他持有这托拉斯的五百股股票；一是“一份”，如文中，这个份额可能有许多“股”。Pre-digested预先消化好的。digest，动词读作[daɪˈdʒest]或[dɪˈdʒest]，名词读[ˈdaɪdʒest]。

118 worth four thousand a year　值每年四千（镑）。这里的“值”是“可得红利（若干）”的意思；worth，是形容词，只作表语用，后面接的作为它的宾语。

119 on condition that ...以……为条件；如果。

120 Wannafeller Moral Reform World Lecture　Wannafeller道德改进世界同盟。这和上面的Moral Reform Societies都是虚构的团体名称。

121 as often as they ask me up to six times a year次数多到他们每年叫我（演讲）六次。they指那League（中的人们）；up to是习语。

122 The devil he does!　他竟然如此。The devil是诅咒语，无意义，并不与he同位语主语。he does代替he leaves you a share ... on condition that you lecture ... as often as they ask you up to six times a year。

123 Whew!呦！表示惊讶、失望等，常含诙谐意；读作[hwuː]。

124 brightening面露喜色；快活起来。

125 What a lark!多么有趣！lark嬉戏；开心事。

126 A safe thing　安全的事；无害的事。

127 They wont ask you twice.他们（League中人）不会请你（讲演）两次的。这可能有两个解释：一是他们听了Doolittle一次演讲就“不敢领教”，一是那些League不会认真地组织一次次的演讲会；似以前为是。

128 It aint the lecturing I mind.　我所介意的倒不是演说（这件事）。lecturing后省略that。

129 lecture them blue in the face对他们演说得使他们脸上发青；讲得他们脸色发青。

130 and not turn a hair　= and I'll not turn a hair.而我将不动声色。not turn a hair是习语。

131 It's making a gentleman of me that I object to.这是I object to making a gentleman of me的强调句型。这里object是不及物动词，后面用介词to，作“反对”解；object作及物动词，后接that从句时，是“提出……作为反对的理由”的意思，不作“反对”解。

132 touched pretty nigh everybody for money　很接近于（几乎）向每个人弄到钱（讨钱或借钱）。pretty = very. nigh = nearly.

133 same as　= the same as. same是副词。

134 worrited　= worried.（被使）感到烦恼。把worry说成worrit是俚语。

135 tied neck and heels颈项和脚跟都被缚住。tied承接I am，与worrited并列；类似的说法如be tied hand and foot（缚手缚脚；不得自由行动）。

136 says I　是said I的俚语说法。前面says my solicitor的says也该作said。

137 You mean it's a good thing for you你的意思是说这对你是件好事（对你有好处）吧？资本主义社会中律师处理遗产有利可得。

138 had a solicitor　请到了律师；找到了律师。

139 they found a pram in the dust cart　人家在我垃圾车里发现一辆童车（因而指控他偷窃或向他要还）。pram是perambulator [pəˈræmbjʊleɪtə]的简称，主要是英国语。

140 got me off, and got shut of me and got me shut of him三个并列的谓语实际上都是“把我摆脱掉”的意思。Doolittle惯于用这雄辩式的说法。shut（过去分词）在方言和俚语中等于rid，因此got shut of = got rid of（摆脱；去掉）；got me shut of him直解是“使我摆脱掉他”，意谓“律师使我不需要他为我辩护”。

141 Same with the doctors　= It is the same with the doctors.关于医生的情况也是如此（同样不理睬穷人）。the doctors中的the指“（医生）这般（人物）”。

142 used to shove [ʃʌv] me out of the hospital before I could hardly stand on my legs, and nothing to pay （医生们）在我还不能站得稳（病尚未愈），因为没钱支付（医药费），就把我推出医院。used to前面省略they (the doctors)。and nothing to pay按语法该说because I had nothing to pay。

143 In the house　该说At home。

144 I'm not let do a hand's turn不让我动一动手。not do a hand's turn一点也不动手；一点也不帮忙；是习语。let的宾语后面的不定式不用to，用被动式时用to或不用to均可，但通常不用be let (to)而用be allowed to。

145 not a decent week's wages among the lot of them　= there is not a decent week's wages among the lot of them = none among the lot of them（或none of them）get a decent week's wage.他们全都没有像样的星期工资。the lot全部；全体。

146 I have to live for others　我必须为别人而生活（意谓许多人向他要钱，他得拿钱给他们用）。这里的live for others显然不是我们所说的“我为人人”的意思。

147 I bet　我打赌；我可以断定。

148 by this　= by now.（至迟）在此刻。

149 she与前面she's on my doorstep的she同位。

150 easy应作easily。

151 proper English　正当的英语；Doolittle认为他原来说的土语、俚语是正当的英语，而中产阶级说的是不正当的英语。

152 Thats where youll come in　那就有你的份儿。come in是习语，有许多意思，这里作“起作用”、“有份儿”解。

153 I daresay我敢说；我认为；大概。daresay也作dare say，后面接that从句时常省去that。

154 thats what you done it for你做这事情就为此。

155 if you are really in earnest　你要是真正认真的话；要是真的认为得到遗赠使你倒霉的话。in earnest是习语，作“认真地”解。really in earnest也可说in real earnest。

156 in deference [ˈdefərəns] to her sex以示尊重女性。in deference to尊重；服从；是习语。

157 Thats the tragedy of it这就是事情的悲剧性（所在）；it，泛指接受遗赠的事；可悲的是受也不是、不受也不是，见下文。

158 say chuck it　= say "chuck it." chuck it放弃它（指遗赠）；在英国俚语中chuck it（it无所实指）作“停止”、“不干”解。

159 havnt the nerve没有这胆量（或坚强意志）；不敢。

160 has　= has the nerve.

161 intimidated [ɪnˈtɪmɪdeɪtɪd]被威胁（而使服从），被胁迫。

162 What is there for me if I chuck it but the workhouse in my old age?我要是放弃遗赠，老来除孤老院之外还有什么依傍？What is there for me but ...除……之外我还有什么出路。if I chuck it ...插入的条件从句，可移在句首或句末。

163 I have to dye my hair already　= I have already to dye my hair.我已经必须染黑头发（使雇主不嫌我老）。

164 the deserving poor　值得救济的穷人们；符合救济条件的贫民们。

165 put by a bit　积蓄一些钱。put by储存；是习语。

166 but then但是另一方面；是习语。

167 acause　应作because。

168 the deserving poor might as well be millionaires for all the happiness they ever has值得救济的穷人们，尽管他们有得欢乐，也不妨成为百万富翁。这是萧伯纳借Doolittle之口所说的俏皮话，说穷人自由自在，可以向人要钱，而富翁有所拘束，又有各方面来向他要钱——犹如Eliza说她贫困可以卖花，变成贵妇人只能卖她自己，同样是尖刻的讽刺。might as well大可；不妨；是习语。for all ...尽管有了……；也是习语。has该作have。

169 They dont know what happiness is.　他们（指the deserving poor）不知幸福是什么。说他们不知贫穷的自由自在。这句话和上句是同样意义的俏皮话。

170 the undeserving [ˌʌndɪˈzɜ:vɪŋ] poor不值得救济（不符受救济）的穷人们。

171 have nothing between me and the pauper's uniform but ...除……之外，在我和救济所贫民的制服之间一无所有（别无选择，只有进救济所）。pauper乞丐；受救济的贫民。

172 this here blasted four thousand a year　这里的该死的一年四千镑（所遗赠的股份的红利）。this here是俚语用法。this here man = this man here.

173 Excuse the expression　原谅（我用）这个词（指blasted）。这句话也可说成Excuse me for using the expression和Excuse my using the expression。

174 if you had my provocation [ˌprɒvəˈkeɪʃən]要是你有像我那样恼火的事；要是你碰到我这样恼火的情况。这是与事实相反的虚拟语气，事实上你并没有。

175 Theyve got you every way you turn　无论你走向哪边，他们都抓住你；他们已使人走头无路。every way有连词的作用，= wherever。you这里泛指任何人。

176 a choice between the Skilly of the workhouse and the Char Bydis　习语between Scylla [ˈsɪlə] and Charybdis [kəˈrɪbdɪs]是“进退维谷”的意思。Scylla（Doolittle误作Skilly）是意大利本土和西西里之间墨西拿（Messina）海峡中的岩礁，希腊罗马神话中说这岩礁为妖魔所居，它对面是Charybdis（Doolittle误作Char Bydis）大漩涡，因此这两者之间的选择是在妖魔和漩涡之间的选择，总是死路一条。

177 Broke　= I'm broke. broke俚语中作表语用的形容词，是“一文不名的”、“破产了的”意思，这里作“被弄垮了”、“完蛋了”解。

178 Bought up = I am（或have been）bought up.我整个儿被人买去了。

179 Happier men than me will call for my dust　比我快乐的人要来倒我的垃圾；than me应作that I (am)。

180 tip　小费；小账。

181 look on helpless只好眼看着。look on旁观；是习语。helpless无可奈何的。

182 is overcome by emotion　被感情压倒；不胜伤感。

183 youre not going to anything foolish　你不准备做什么蠢事（蠢事指放弃遗赠）。

184 this　指接受遗赠。

185 melancholy [ˈmelənkli] resignation [ˌrezɪɡˈneɪʃən]忧郁的屈从；无可奈何。melancholy是名词，也是形容词，作形容词时，可作“忧郁的”解，也可作“令人忧郁的”解。

186 I'm expected to provide for everyone我被指望供养每个人；人人都要我供养。

187 out of four thousand a year　从一年四千镑（的红利收入）中（拿出钱来）。原本three thousand，今据上文改正。

188 He cant provide for her　他不能养她。这里的cant表示说话人的意志，所以是“不得”的意思。

189 He shant在第二、第三人称后用shall或shant表示说话人的意志，有“我不准他”、即“他不得”的意思。

190 either youre an honest man or a rogue　要么你是个老实人，不然你就是个流氓；你要做老实人，还是做流氓。either和or连结的成分应该是相同的，但这里either后面接的是个从句，而or后接的是个名字，所以最好改作either youre an honest man or youre a rogue，或者youre either an honest man or a rogue。

191 A little of both　= I'm a little of both.我两者（老实人和流氓）都有一点；我既有点是老实人，也有点是流氓。

192 you have no right to take her as well　你（把Eliza交给我们已拿了钱去，）没有权利（或理由）又要把她带去。as well也；又；是习语。

193 jolly　在英国口语中作副词，等于very。

194 throws himself ungraciously on the ottoman　粗鲁地猛力地往软垫长凳上坐下。

195 you might have told us this half an hour ago　你原可以（早该）在半小时前告诉我们（Eliza在楼上）这个情况。这是表示与过去事实相反的虚拟语气，实际上并没有告诉。

196 the brutal way you two treated her　你们俩对待她的粗暴态度。the brutal way后面省略in which（in which也可简略用that）。brutal原义“畜生（brute）般的”，转义为“横蛮的”、“粗暴的”。two是you的同位语。

197 telling you stories　对你说谎。tell a story作为习语有“说谎”的意思，所以story-teller除“讲故事的人”、“小说作家”之外，口语中可作“说谎的人”解。

198 hardly said a word to her　几乎没对她说一句话；几乎没说过她一句话。word话；这里指责骂的话。事实上是骂过她的，见上一幕。

199 parted on particularly good terms　特别友好地分手（各自回房睡觉）。on ... terms处于……（好或坏）的关系。

200 Just the other way about　恰恰相反。about是副词，是“（转向）相反方向”的意思，如“向后转！”说About turn!

201 never gave her the slightest provocation　决没有给她一点（即使是）最微小的惹犯；一点没去激怒她。

202 The slippers came bang into may face　拖鞋砰地（掷）到了我脸上来。bang砰地，突然，作副词；口语中也作“正好”解，所以这句话也可作“拖鞋正朝我脸上（掷）来”。

203 the moment　相当于连词as soon as，由at the moment when衍化而来。

204 used perfectly awful language　用了非常难听的语言；used前面省略she。perfectly（十足地），它与perfect一样，不一定用于褒义作“十全十美”解，也可用于贬义，如a perfect fool（十足的傻瓜）。

205 naturally rather affectionate　本性很温柔。注意：affectionate是“爱（人）的”；dear是“被爱的”；因此He is affectionate to her和She is dear to him都是“他爱她”的意思。

206 Takes after me　= She takes after me.她像我。take after （在面貌、性格等方面）像（自己的父亲或母亲或祖父或祖母）；是习语。

207 Just so.正是如此；是习语。

208 become attached to you both对你们两个都变得有了感情。attached是过去分词作表语。attached to依恋；喜爱。

209 what anything in the nature of brain work means to a girl of her class　任何脑力劳动性质的工作对她这个阶级的姑娘意味着什么（对她有多大的困难）。

210 the great day of trial　重大的试验的日子（指Eliza冒充公爵夫人参加大使馆招待会的那一天）。

211 did this wonderful thing　做了这件了不起的事情（指冒充公爵夫人）。

212 you two sat there and never said a word to her　你们俩坐在那里，一句话也不对她说。这里的there并不是指在招待会上，而是指回家后和Eliza一起在房间里。said a word to her对她说一句话（或者是安慰，或者是赞扬她，反正不是上面Higgins所说的gave her the slightest provocation之有“责骂”之意）。

213 had been bored with the whole thing　已经对整个这件事（从训练Eliza到带她去冒充公爵夫人）感到厌烦了。

214 I should have thrown the fire-irons [ˈfaɪərˌaɪənz] at you （换了我）我准要用火钳扔你。这是表示与过去事实相反的虚拟语气，省略条件从句if I were she或if it had been I。

215 Quite sure?　= Are you sure (that) that was all?

216 Absolutely　一般读[ˈæbsəluːtli]，在加强语气或作Certainly（当然）的意义时，也读作[ˌæbsəˈluːtli]。

217 splendid　出色的；令人非常满意的；是口语。

218 about that　that指how splendid she'd been。

219 We didnt make speeches to her, if thats what you mean.要么你的意思是说我们没有对她发表演说（一般地长篇大论地赞扬她表现得如何出色）。

220 conscience [ˈkɒnʃəns] stricken良心受到打击，感到内疚。

221 Mr Doolittle is able to keep up the position you have thrust on her（Eliza的父亲）Doolittle先生（现在有钱了，）能够维持你（们）强加于她的（贵妇人的）身份。keep up维持；继续；是习语。

222 let bygones be bygones　让过去的事情过去（算了）。

223 Is she　= Is she willing to meet us on friendly terms and let bygones be bygones.

224 Ho!呵！感叹词，表示惊讶，赞美，嘲笑等，常与其他感叹词合用，如heigh-ho [ˈheɪˈhəʊ]、what ho。

225 behave yourself　举止规矩；不胡闹。

226 If not　= If you do not promise to behave yourself.

227 taken up quite enough of my time占用了我够多的时间。

228 you behave yourself　Higgins自己最不讲规矩，却叫Pickering要规矩；这是萧伯纳的幽默之处。

229 put on our best Sunday manners　拿出我们最好的礼拜日的礼貌来。基督徒礼拜天上礼拜堂，需要衣冠整齐、举止文雅，所以有a Sunday suit和in one's Sunday best（穿最好的节日服装）等语。manners礼貌；用复数。

230 for this creature that we picked out of the mud为了我们这个从泥潭里捡出来的东西。creature用以指“人”，含怜爱或轻蔑之意。

231 Now, now　= Now then.见前注。

232 Have some consideration for my feelings as a middle class man.稍为我作为一个中产阶级中人的感情着想。feelings（复数）所说的“感情”是指“感受性”、“心里反应”的意思。

233 will you be so good as to step out　请你走出去。倒装的疑问句形式表示祈使的意思。be so good as to也作be good enough to。step out走开；（短时间或短距离地）离开。

234 have the shock of your news　受到你的新闻的震动；突然听到你有了钱而震惊。

235 until用在否定句中作“不到……（不）”、“到……才……”的意思。如Dont go until he comes （在他到来之前你不要走；到他来了你才走）。

236 made it up with和（某人）和解；是习语。这里的it没有具体意义，有人称为“空泛的it”（vague 'it'）。

237 Would you mind?　你介意吗？好吗？注意在这个常用语中现在习惯用would而不用其他助动词。mind作“反对”、“介意”解是及物动词，这里没有宾语，是及物动词的一种特殊用法，有人称为“绝对用法”（absolute use）。

238 As you wish　你要我怎样做就怎样做。前面省略I'll do。

239 Anything to help Henry to keep her off my hands.我愿做任何事情，以帮助Henry (Higgins)使她不再要我照管；只要Henry让我摆脱她，我什么都愿意效劳；前面也省略I'll do。help的宾语后面接不定式时，英国人常用不定式记号to，美国人一般不用。keep ... off one's hands不管；不负责；摆脱掉；是习语。

240 disappears through the window　从落地长窗（french window）的窗门口隐退。

241 answers the bell　应铃声（而来）。

242 Ask Miss Doolittle to come down, please.　请你请Doolittle小姐（Eliza）下楼来。

243 be good好好的；乖乖的；这是习语。

244 throws back his head　（倏地）把头往后仰。

245 dont look at all nice　= dont look nice at all.看上去一点不好看；太难看。

246 It doesnt matter　这倒无关紧要；it指Higgins不求好看。

247 trying　令人难堪的。

248 out of patience　耐不住；不耐烦。这是个形容词性质的习语作表语用；不能用作状语短语。

249 sunny, self-possessed　= being sunny, self-possessed.喜形于色，从容自若；与下面giving加宾语的分词短语并列。

250 giving a staggeringly convincing exhibition [ˌeksɪˈbɪʃən] of ease of manner作出一副惊人地令人信服的悠闲的姿态。

251 work-basket　针线篮。

252 very much at home非常自由自在。at home是习语。

253 is too much taken aback to rise过分惊讶而忘了站起来。Pickering一向很有礼貌，这回看见Eliza突然以雍容自在的姿态出现在面前，竟忘了应该起立的礼貌。

254 Am I –　Higgins对Eliza装腔作势向他敷衍客套气得话也说不出了。

255 Dont you dare try this game on me.　你不要胆敢向我耍这一套把戏（指敷衍客套）。Dont you比单用Dont语气重。dare现在时形式的dare用在否定句或有否定意思的句子里，往往后面接没有to的不定式。

256 take me in　使我上当；骗到我。take in作“欺骗”解，是这个习语的许多意思之一。

257 Get up and come home站起身来，回家去。这里的“家”是指Higgins在Wimpole Street的家。

258 dont be a fool别做傻瓜；不要愚蠢地装腔作势做这一套。

259 without taking the least notice of this outburst[ˈaʊtbɜːst]对（Higgins的）这突然发作的吵闹一点不理睬。take notice注意；理会；是习语；后面介词用of。

260 Very nicely put　= That's（指Higgins说的话）very nicely put.说得真好（真得体，客气）。显然是讽刺Higgins的无礼。

261 No woman could resist such an invitation.没有一个女人抵制得了这样一个邀请；任何女人都乐于接受这样的邀请呢。这是反语，强烈讽刺Higgins的无礼。

262 You let her alone　你由她去，不要管她。let alone是习语；它另有一个特殊的用法，作“更不用说”解，如：He cannot read, let alone write.他不会读，更不用说写了。

263 speak for herself自己说（发表自己的意见、讲自己的事情或者为自己辩解）；是习语。

264 You will jolly soon see你很快就会看到；你马上就会知道。

265 whether she has an idea that I havnt put into her head or a word that I havnt put into her mouth　她可有点滴不是我灌输给她的思想，可有一个不是我教她说的字；她所想的、说的全都是我教给她的。

266 I tell you I have created [krɪˈeɪtɪd] this thing out of the squashed [skwɒʃt] cabbage leaves of Covent Garden我告诉你，我用Covent Garden那里的压扁的白菜叶子制造出了这个东西（指Eliza）。Higgins自恃清高，任性之至，经常使用粗野的语言辱骂Eliza。I tell you有“相信我的话”的意思。out of用……（制成）；是习语。

267 pretends to play the fine lady with me　现在她倒装腔作势对我作出高雅的贵妇人的样子来。play the fine lady play作“摆出……的架势”、“扮演……的角色”解时，常用play the ...，如play the man（显示丈夫气概），play the hero（逞英雄）。

268 placidly　[ˈplæsɪdli]平静地；心平气和地。

269 talking no apparent notice of Higgins　表面上好像不理会Higgins。apparent [əˈpærənt]含有“表面上明明如此而实际上并非如此”的意思。

270 working away继续（做针线）工作。away表示“继续不停”。

271 Will you drop me altogether　可否请你把我整个儿丢开。这里will you是说话人祈求的口气，而不是表示纯粹的未来或主语you的意向。

272 think of it as an experiment　把这件事情看作是一个试验。think of ... as ...把……当作……；是习语。it指把Eliza训练成一个贵妇人；这在Pickering看来是完成了一件大事，决不只是什么随便的“试验”。

273 It shocks me, somehow.　这话（不知怎么）总觉得刺耳；it指这件事只是个试验的说法。shock除“使震惊”外，还可作“使愤慨”、“使厌恶”等解。

274 owe so much to you　受惠（或得益）于你（Pickering），而该报答你的有那么多。

275 are generous to everybody with money　对每个人都慷慨解囊。注意generous with money一语中介词用with。

276 it was from you that I learnt really nice manners　我是从你这里（而不是从Higgins或其他人那里）学得真正好的礼貌的。

277 that is what makes one a lady　那（指礼貌）是使一个人成为上等女人的东西。that指really nice manners; manners虽是复数形式，却具有抽象名词的性质，被看作为单数。what是关系代词，= that which。

278 so very difficult for me with the example of Professor Higgins always before me一直有Higgins教授的榜样在我面前，我（要学习好礼貌）有那么大的困难（因为Higgins一向不讲礼貌，他的榜样实在太坏）。

279 I was brought up to be just like him　我从小被培养成正像是他这个样子。bring up培养，是习语。

280 unable to control myself, and using bad language on the slightest provocation不能控制自己，碰到了即使极小的冒犯就恶言相向。在语法结构上，由and连接的两个词组都是修饰just like him的那种人（虽然上文并没有“人”）。

281 I should never have known that ladies and gentlemen didnt behave like that if you hadnt been there　要是没有你在那里（在Wimpole Street）我决不会知道贵妇绅士不是（像Higgins）那样（粗野无礼）的。这是与过去事实相反的虚拟语气，事实上是因为看到了你（彬彬有礼的Pickering），我才知道贵妇绅士不是那样（粗野无礼）的。

282 thats only his way　那只是他的与众不同的习惯。

283 doesnt mean it （天生如此而）并非故意如此。it指his way。

284 I did it; and thats what makes the difference after all.　过去我就是那样做的，那毕竟（使上等人和下等人）有区别。

285 I couldnt have done that　我（要教你说也）教不来。done that = taught you to speak.

286 trivially　不作为奇地；口气轻飘飘地。

287 Damnation!　作感叹词，是诅咒语，= Damn!该死。

288 there was nothing more than that in it　这（学上等人的语言）里面没有比那（学时髦的舞蹈）有更多的东西；不过如此而已。

289 Your calling me Miss Doolittle　你称呼我Doolittle小姐（见第二幕）。calling是动名词，既作名词，又有动词的性质，所以后面可有状语短语that day和状语从句when I first came to Wimpole Street修饰它，而calling的谓语began my education却省略了，全句成了只有主语的单部句。

290 That　= Your calling me Miss Doolittle.

291 there were a hundred little things you never noticed　有许多你全没注意到的小事情。a hundred喻其多，并不实指“一百”。little things指生活细节、小节；后面省略which或that。

292 they came naturally to you　它们（这些小事情）自然地在你身上表现出来（你的一举一动都出于自然）。

293 Things承接上句，与上句中a hundred little things并列，进一步列举具体事例。

294 standing up and taking off your hat and opening doors – （见人进来时）起立，（进屋内时）脱帽，以及（为人）开门等等；这都是西方礼仪。

295 that was nothing　那不算什么。上文讲things，且一件件列举，但总的概念是礼仪，所以代词可用单数that。

296 things that shewed you thought and felt about me as if I were something better than a scullery-maid　这些事情显出你把我想作、看作仿佛是高于后厨女仆的某类人；说明你不把我当低三下四的人。things指上面列举的那些细节；前面省略these（或the）。

297 you would have been just the same to a scullery-maid if she had been let into the drawing room　要是后厨女仆被允许进入会客室，你也会（像对待我那样）同样（有礼貌）的。这是与过去事实相反的虚拟语气，实际并非曾有这样的事。

298 took off your boots in the dining room when I was there　在餐厅里当着我脱靴子。在餐室里脱靴子已经够不文雅，还要当着女人的面，更是粗野无礼，而Higgins习以为常。

299 mind that　介意那个（指上述脱靴事）。mind作“反对”解时，后面常接动名词（不可接不定式），也接名词、代词，间或还接that从句。

300 all over the place遍地；到处；随地。

301 really and truly　真正地；千真万确地。

302 apart from the things anyone can pick up除了任何人能随便学会的那些东西之外。apart from和pick都是习语。

303 the dressing　衣装打扮。

304 how she's treated　她是怎样被对待的；人家怎么对待她。

305 and always will　= and will always treat me as a flower girl. always一般放在助动词后面，但像这里省略了助动词后面的部分时，always放在助动词前面。

306 grind your teeth　咬牙切齿；是习语。

307 I should like you to call me Eliza, now　现在我倒喜欢你叫我Eliza（叫Eliza比叫Miss Doolittle亲切）。

308 Thank you.　感谢Eliza叫他更随意地称呼她的亲切表示。

309 And I should like Professor Higgins to call me Miss Doolittle.　而我希望Higgins教授叫我Doolittle小姐（要Higgins尊重她些）。

310 I'll see you damned first.　我要先看你被打入了地狱（才叫你Doolittle小姐）；我才不叫你Doolittle小姐呢。see somebody damned first是习语；这里的damned也作blowed, hanged等，都是诅咒语。

311 Henry! Henry!　Mrs Higgins喝住她儿子不要粗野地口出诅咒语。

312 slang back at him　用粗话骂还他。Pickering是个文雅的绅士，并不真要叫Eliza骂人，他说这话是开玩笑的。

313 Dont stand it.不要忍受它；不要听受他吆五喝六。it，泛指Higgins的诅咒。stand作“忍受”解时，宾语除用名词、代词、动名词外，美国人也用不定式。

314 It would do him a lot of good.这（用粗话骂还他）对他有很大好处。do somebody good对（某人）起好作用（有益）。good是名词，作do的直接宾语，somebody是间接宾语。

315 I cant　= I cant slang back at him.

316 go back to it回头再这样做；（学了文雅的语言之后）重又说粗话。

317 it picks up the language in a few weeks　他（这孩子）几个星期就自然学会了那种（外国的）语言。英语中常用it指婴儿和幼年的孩子，相反常用he、she指喜爱的小动物。

318 I am a child in your country　我是在你们国家里的一个孩子。Eliza把Higgins和Pickering看作是属于另一个国家的，把他们的语言看作是外国语（跟她自己原来所用的语言不同）。

319 can speak nothing but yours　除了你们的语言，说不来任何其他的语言；只会说你们的话。

320 break-off　脱离关系。

321 the corner of Tottenham Court Road Eliza原来卖花处，见第二幕（p.62）。

322 Leaving Wimpole Street finishes it.　离开Wimpole Street（Higgins的实验室），便完成了这件事。犹言“离校毕业”。it指break-off，即Eliza从卖花女变成了贵妇人。

323 much alarmed　大为震动；大惊小怪；这里alarmed没有“惊吓”的意思。

324 Forgive!　要她饶恕！

325 get on without us　没有我们而过下去；不靠我们过活。get on是习语，作“过活”解时，也作get along。

326 relapse into the gutter in three weeks without me at her elbow （倘）没有我在她身旁，三个星期后就又流落街头。in three weeks修饰一时的动作relapse，是“三星期后”的意思；倘若修饰持续的动作，则in是“……之内”的意思，如see him several times in a week、learn another language in three years。at one's elbow在手边；是习语。

327 With a look of dignified reproach at Higgins　用严肃的责备的眼光瞧着Higgins。

328 He's incorrigible　[ɪnˈkɒrɪdʒəbl]他（Higgins）是改不好的（指Higgins的口出恶言）。

329 not now　= I shall not relapse now.现在我不会又流落街头了。

330 learnt my lesson领教了。这里指“学好了（语音）课业”，兼含“得到教训”的双关意思，即学了上流社会的语音，弄得不尴不尬，得到了痛苦的教训。（汉语中“领教”也有正面和反面的两个意思。）

331 if I tried　即使我想要这样做。这是虚拟语气，if = even if; tried后面省略to utter one of the old sounds。

332 She drops her work　她丢下针线活。drop这里是“无意地掉落”的意思。work指needlework，可指抽象的工作，也可指具体的工作物。

333 losing her self-possession utterly at the spectacle of her father's splendor （看见）她父亲华贵的景象，完全失去了自制。因为失去自制，所以原先的A-a-a-a-a-ah-ow-ooh的怪叫声又脱口而出。

334 a crow [krəʊ] of triumph [ˈtraɪʌmf]胜利的欢呼。（因为Higgins听见Eliza果然又发出原来的土音了。）

335 Just so正是如此；是习语。这里的意思是“原先就是这样怪叫的”。

336 spraddling arrogantly　[ˈærəɡəntli]傲慢地张开双腿。spraddle是方言，作“摆开”、“展开”解。

337 Dont look at me like that　别那样瞧着我。Eliza看见她父亲神气的样子，定然目光里露出惊讶的表情。

338 It aint my fault.　这（变了神气的样子）不是我的过错；怪不得我。

339 come into some money得到一些钱。come into得到；继承（财产等）；是习语。

340 You must have touched a millionaire [ˌmɪljəˈneər] this time这回你一定从一个百万富翁那里弄到了钱。

341 dad爹，口语中常用dad或daddy称父亲。

342 I'm dressed something special today　今天我穿得特别（漂亮）了些。something这里是副词；意为“多少”、“几分”，等于somewhat。special这里该用specially修饰am dressed。

343 St George's, Hanover [ˈhænəvə] Square Hanover广场的St George教堂。

344 Your stepmother is going to marry me.你的后娘将嫁给我（和我正式结婚）。所谓“后娘”实则是Doolittle的姘妇，过去一直不肯跟他正式结婚，见第二幕（p.130）。

345 let yourself down to marry that low common woman　降低你自己的身份去娶那个下流的低贱女人。low下等的；低贱的。common粗俗的；低贱的。

346 ought to　= ought to marry her.不能硬扣上文理解为ought to marry that low common woman，更不该理解为ought to let himself down to marry that low common woman。

347 Why has she changed her mind?为什么她（原来不肯结婚而）现在改变宗旨（愿意结婚）了？

348 Governor　英国口语用以尊称男子，见前注。

349 claims its victim　（把我们）作为它（中产阶级道德）的理所应当的牺牲品。

350 come and see me turned off来看我结婚。come and see = come to see. turn off俚语里有“绞死”、“使结婚”等意思，这里turned off是宾语补足语，me和turned off（使被迫结婚）有逻辑上的主谓关系，构成复合宾语。

351 demean myself　降低自己身份。区别：另一词源相当于behave的demean，如demean oneself well/ill，表现良好（或不好）。

352 get insulted for my pains　费了心（去参加婚礼）还要受（后娘的）气。and首字母小写，紧接上句，这里另起一句，表示说话时稍顿的口气。for (all) one's pains尽管费（尽）力气（或心思）；是习语。

353 like enough很可能；大概，常用作插入语，也可作very like，等于very likely。

354 comes to words with　跟……吵起嘴来。words口角；用复数。

355 Respectability has broke all the spirit out of her　（中产阶级的）体面身份使她完全丧气了。这里的respectability含有贬义，讽刺一本正经的市侩习俗。broke应作broken。

356 Make the best of it　随遇而安。make the best of （在不幸等情况中）求最大的幸运（或结果）；是习语。it泛指面临的情况。

357 forcing a little smile for him through her vexation　从她的烦躁中勉强对他作出微笑。

358 just to shew theres no ill feeling即以表示没有恶感。这句话在意义上连接下一句，可以看作是个状语短语。

359 uncommon　应作uncommonly，异常地。

360 see me through it　看看我经历这个（结婚仪式）。see somebody through帮某人渡过（难关）（或维持过……）；是习语。

361 man　用man作为对男子的称呼，有亲密或轻蔑的意思。

362 You were married to Eliza's mother.你跟Eliza的母亲已经是结了婚的。Pickering以为孩子也生下了，当然早已是夫妇。

363 I concluded – naturally –　我自然地得出结论（你们已经结了婚）；或者我得出的结论是（你们）自然（已经结了婚）。两个解释实际的意义是相同的。

364 that aint the natural way　那（结婚）不是自然的做法。

365 it's only the middle class way那（结婚）只是中产阶级的做法；中产阶级才讲究结婚。

366 the undeserving way　= the way of the undeserving poor.

367 dont say nothing to Eliza　这是不正当的“双重否定”，应作dont say anything to Eliza或say nothing to Eliza。

368 She dont know应作She doesnt know。

369 had a delicacy [ˈdelɪkəsi] about telling her告诉她有所不便；不便告诉她（我和她妈妈没有结过婚）。

370 leave it so　让它这样（指不说穿）。

371 if you dont mind如果你不反对。这是常用口语，实际是“好吗？”的意思。

372 put me through straight　帮我从头到底地完成婚礼。put through使做完；使经受；是习语；straight，是副词。

373 With pleasure.　很高兴；好的。是常用应答语。

374 As far as a bachelor can.尽一个单身汉所能地（帮你完成婚礼）。Pickering自己不曾有举行婚礼的经验，故作此语。

375 I should be very sorry to miss your wedding.　这是虚拟语气，= I should be very sorry if I missed your wedding. （如果）我错过你的婚礼，我将十分遗憾；我真想参加你的婚礼。

376 your condescension　你屈尊（参加我的婚礼）。

377 take it as a tremenjous compliment　把它（你屈尊来临）看作是极大的褒奖（赏光）。tremenjous [trəˈmendʒəs]是tremendous [trəˈmendəs]的误读。

378 low　意气消沉；沮丧。

379 the happy days that are no more不再有的（一去不返的）快活的日子。Doolittle总把过去的穷日子说成是快活的，因为不受中产阶级道德的束缚。

380 carriage　四轮马车。

381 I shant be more than fifteen minutes.　我不会超过十五分钟（就来）。

382 hatted and buttoning her gloves　戴着帽子和正在扣上手套的钮子。过去分词hatted和现在分词buttoning同样作修饰词。

383 brougham　[ˈbruːəm]一匹马拉的四轮轿车。上面所说的carriage即指此车。

384 go on with the bridegroom　和新郎同行（上教堂去）。

385 joins her　到她那里，和她在一起。

386 It makes a man realize his position, somehow.　它（bridegroom这个词）总会使人意识到自己所处的地位。somehow，修饰makes。

387 do forgive　是forgive的强势式；do重读。

388 allow me　= allow me to come back to you.

389 Would you, dad? = Would you allow me to come back to them, dad? Eliza以为她父亲有了钱，不会允许她再回到Higgins和Pickering那里，而要她一起回家去的。

390 sad but magnanimous　[mæɡˈnænɪməs]抑郁而宽大。Doolittle以为Higgins和Pickering两个人占了Eliza的便宜，所以他抑郁；但是他不责怪他们，也不领她回家，还是愿意把她交给这两个人，所以他是“宽大”的。

391 played you off very cunning很巧妙地把你对付过去。play off是习语。

392 them two sportsmen　应作these two sportsmen，是主语They的同位语；sportsman，原义“运动家”，运动比赛中有规则、要正大光明，所以引伸为“有（运动家的）正大光明的道德、风格的人”；同时sport原是“娱乐”、“游戏”的意思，所以这里称他们“正人君子”，又含有“（拿Eliza）开玩笑的人”的意思，是双关语。

393 If it had been only one of them, you could have nailed him.要是有关这事情的是他们（two sportsmen）中的一个，你就可以钉住他。nail，口语中作“抓住”、“捕获”解；这句子是虚拟语气，事实上，正因为是两个而不是一个，所以没法抓住哪一个，见下文Doolittle自己对这话的解释。

394 there was two　应作there were two。

395 one of them chaperoned the other, as you might say　犹如你（们）说的，他们两个相互伴随着，社交场中未婚少女常有年长妇女伴随监护，称为chaperone，这个词也作及物动词用。

396 It was artful of you　你们真乖巧。这个句型和It is kind/nice/good of you to come的句型同，这里of you省略to do so；有许多形容词可用这句型，但并不是所有形容词都可用此句型。

397 bear no malice [ˈmælɪs]不怀恶意。bear malice to/towards somebody对某人怀有恶意；是习语。

398 I been　应作I have been。

399 the victim of one woman after another一个又一个女人的牺牲品；吃过许多女人的亏。

400 dont grudge you two getting the better of Liza　不抱怨你们两个占Eliza的便宜。get the better of占……的上风；智胜；是习语。

401 So long　再见；是口语，多用于熟人间。Doolittle用此语，以及用教名Henry称Higgins，都表示他对Higgins已经谬托知己，熟不拘礼。

402 See you in St George's在St George's教堂和你见面。See前面省略I'll。

403 goes along the balcony　顺着阳台走去（兜来兜去追逐Eliza）。

404 had a bit of your own back　报复到了一点。习语get one's own back (on somebody)是“（对某人）进行报复”的意思。back是副词。

405 as you call it如你所说的；所谓。指上面had a bit of your own back这说法。

406 had enough　可理解为had enough of your own back。

407 reasonable合情合理的；不过分的。

408 want me back　要我回（到你那里）去。这里back是宾语补足语，me和back有逻辑上的主谓关系，构成复合宾语。

409 put up with your tempers忍受你的发脾气；受你的气。put up with忍受；是习语。tempers指一次次的发脾气，= fits of temper。

410 fetch and carry　打杂；当听差；是习语。

411 About you　= I'm talking about you.

412 change my nature　改变我的本性。

413 Thats not true.　那（你说的话）不真实（不符实际）。

414 He treats a flower girl as if she was a duchess.他对待一个卖花女，仿佛她是个公爵夫人。这里的as if引起的虚拟语气的从句中，有些语法家认为该用were，但实际上用was很普遍。

415 composedly　[kəmˈpəʊzdli]镇定自若。它的相应的形容词composed读[kəm-ˈpəʊzd]；同样的例子如marked [mɑːkt]，markedly [ˈmɑːktli]（显著）；resigned [rɪˈzaɪnd]，resignedly [rɪˈzaɪnɪdli]（无可奈何）；但determinedly读[dɪˈtɜːmɪndli]，不读[-ɪdli]。

416 The same to everybody.　= You are the same to everybody. = You treat everybody in the same way.
